Kurals.Translation: 'A' leads letters; the Ancient Lord Leads and lords the entire world
Kurals.Explanation: As the letter A is the first of all letters, so the eternal God is first in the world
Kurals.Couplet: A, as its first of letters, every speech maintains;The "Primal Deity" is first through all the world's domains
Kurals.EmotionDetail: deep respect for God’s greatness, wonder at God’s eternal and leading nature,

Kurals.Translation: That lore is vain which does not fall At His good feet who knoweth all
Kurals.Explanation: What Profit have those derived from learning, who worship not the good feet of Him who is possessed of pure knowledge ?
Kurals.Couplet: No fruit have men of all their studied lore,Save they the 'Purely Wise One's' feet adore
Kurals.EmotionDetail: deep respect for the divine, recognition of one’s limitations before divine knowledge, dedication and love towards God

Kurals.Translation: Long they live on earth who gain The feet of God in florid brain
Kurals.Explanation: They who are united to the glorious feet of Him who passes swiftly over the flower of the mind, shall flourish long above all worlds
Kurals.Couplet: His feet, 'Who o'er the full-blown flower hath past,' who gainIn bliss long time shall dwell above this earthly plain
Kurals.EmotionDetail: deep respect and admiration for the divine,

Kurals.Translation: Who hold His feet who likes nor loathes Are free from woes of human births
Kurals.Explanation: To those who meditate the feet of Him who is void of desire or aversion, evil shall never come
Kurals.Couplet: His foot, 'Whom want affects not, irks not grief,' who gainShall not, through every time, of any woes complain
Kurals.EmotionDetail: deep reverence and worship toward God, a sense of freedom from suffering and turmoil, confidence in the protective nature of the divine

Kurals.Translation: God's praise who tell, are free from right And wrong, the twins of dreaming night
Kurals.Explanation: The two-fold deeds that spring from darkness shall not adhere to those who delight in the true praise of God
Kurals.Couplet: The men, who on the 'King's' true praised delight to dwell,Affects not them the fruit of deeds done ill or well
Kurals.EmotionDetail: deep respect and awe for God, sense of moral cleanliness and freedom from wrongdoing,

Kurals.Translation: They prosper long who walk His way Who has the senses signed away
Kurals.Explanation: Those shall long proposer who abide in the faultless way of Him who has destroyed the five desires of the senses
Kurals.Couplet: Long live they blest, who 've stood in path from falsehood freed;His, 'Who quenched lusts that from the sense-gates five proceed'
Kurals.EmotionDetail: reverence and praise toward God, peacefulness from controlling desires, expectation of prosperity and well-being by following the righteous path

Kurals.Translation: His feet, whose likeness none can find, Alone can ease the anxious mind
Kurals.Explanation: Anxiety of mind cannot be removed, except from those who are united to the feet of Him who is incomparable
Kurals.Couplet: Unless His foot, 'to Whom none can compare,' men gain,'Tis hard for mind to find relief from anxious pain
Kurals.emotions: Anxiety – Referenced directly as "anxious mind" and "Anxiety of mind"., Reverence – Shown in the praise and admiration for "Him who

Kurals.Translation: Who swims the sea of vice is he Who clasps the feet of Virtue's sea
Kurals.Explanation: None can swim the sea of vice, but those who are united to the feet of that gracious Being who is a sea of virtue
Kurals.Couplet: Unless His feet 'the Sea of Good, the Fair and Bountiful,' men gain,'Tis hard the further bank of being's changeful sea to attain

Kurals.Translation: Like senses stale that head is vain Which bows not to Eight-Virtued Divine
Kurals.Explanation: The head that worships not the feet of Him who is possessed of eight attributes, is as useless as a sense without the power of sensation
Kurals.Couplet: Before His foot, 'the Eight-fold Excellence,' with unbent head,Who stands, like palsied sense, is to all living functions dead
Kurals.EmotionDetail: deep respect for the divine, recognition of one's own limitations before a higher power,

Kurals.Translation: The sea of births they alone swim Who clench His feet and cleave to Him
Kurals.Explanation: None can swim the great sea of births but those who are united to the feet of God
Kurals.Couplet: They swim the sea of births, the 'Monarch's' foot who gain;None others reach the shore of being's mighty main

Summary: Highlights the divine foundation as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Reverence, Devotion, Humility.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The genial rain ambrosia call: The world but lasts while rain shall fall
Kurals.Explanation: By the continuance of rain the world is preserved in existence; it is therefore worthy to be called ambrosia
Kurals.Couplet: The world its course maintains through life that rain unfailing gives;Thus rain is known the true ambrosial food of all that lives
Kurals.emotions: Gratitude – Appreciation for the essential role of rain in sustaining life., Awe – A sense of wonder at the natural phenomenon and its vital importance., Reverence – Deep respect

Kurals.Translation: The rain begets the food we eat
Kurals.Explanation: Rain produces good food, and is itself food
Kurals.Couplet: The rain makes pleasant food for eaters rise;As food itself, thirst-quenching draught supplies
Kurals.emotions: Gratitude – Appreciation for rain as a provider of food and sustenance, Reverence – Respect and admiration for the natural phenomenon of rain, Contentment – Satisfaction in the

Kurals.Translation: Let clouds their visits stay, and dearth Distresses all the sea-girt earth
Kurals.Explanation: If the cloud, withholding rain, deceive (our hopes) hunger will long distress the sea-girt spacious world
Kurals.Couplet: If clouds, that promised rain, deceive, and in the sky remain,Famine, sore torment, stalks o'er earth's vast ocean-girdled plain
Kurals.EmotionDetail: worry about the absence of rain and potential hunger, distress and hopelessness caused by dearth,

Kurals.Translation: Unless the fruitful shower descend, The ploughman's sacred toil must end
Kurals.Explanation: If the abundance of wealth imparting rain diminish, the labour of the plough must cease
Kurals.Couplet: If clouds their wealth of waters fail on earth to pour,The ploughers plough with oxen's sturdy team no more
Kurals.EmotionDetail: on nature for sustenance, about the possibility of rain failing,

Kurals.Translation: Destruction it may sometimes pour But only rain can life restore
Kurals.Explanation: Rain by its absence ruins men; and by its existence restores them to fortune
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis rain works all: it ruin spreads, then timely aid supplies;As, in the happy days before, it bids the ruined rise
Kurals.emotions: Hope – The rain’s return is associated with the restoration of life and fortune

Kurals.Translation: No grassy blade its head will rear, If from the cloud no drop appear
Kurals.Explanation: If no drop falls from the clouds, not even the green blade of grass will be seen
Kurals.Couplet: If from the clouds no drops of rain are shed'Tis rare to see green herb lift up its head
Kurals.emotions: Dependence – The text expresses how all life relies on the rain to flourish., Humility – It reflects the recognition of human limitations in the face of nature’s power.

Kurals.Translation: The ocean's wealth will waste away, Except the cloud its stores repay
Kurals.Explanation: Even the wealth of the wide sea will be diminished, if the cloud that has drawn (its waters) up gives them not back again (in rain)
Kurals.Couplet: If clouds restrain their gifts and grant no rain,The treasures fail in ocean's wide domain
Kurals.emotions: **Gratitude** – for the nourishing return of rain

Kurals.Translation: The earth, beneath a barren sky, Would offerings for the gods deny
Kurals.Explanation: If the heaven dry up, neither yearly festivals, nor daily worship will be offered in this world, to the celestials
Kurals.Couplet: If heaven grow dry, with feast and offering never more,Will men on earth the heavenly ones adore
Kurals.emotions: **Reverence** – A sense of deep respect for the role of rain and the divine order it maintains., **Dependence** – An awareness of humanity’s reliance on rain

Kurals.Translation: Were heaven above to fail below Nor alms nor penance earth would show
Kurals.Explanation: If rain fall not, penance and alms-deeds will not dwell within this spacious world
Kurals.Couplet: If heaven its watery treasures ceases to dispense,Through the wide world cease gifts, and deeds of 'penitence'
Kurals.EmotionDetail: a sense of reliance on rain for sustenance and spiritual practice, worry or anxiety about the consequences of rain failing,

Kurals.Translation: Water is life that comes from rain Sans rain our duties go in vain
Kurals.Explanation: If it be said that the duties of life cannot be discharged by any person without water, so without rain there cannot be the flowing of water
Kurals.Couplet: When water fails, functions of nature cease, you say;Thus when rain fails, no men can walk in 'duty's ordered way'
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for rain and water as essentials to life, respect and awe for nature’s gifts,

Summary: Highlights the sustaining role of rain as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Dependence, Its Vital Importance., Reverence – Deep Respect.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: No merit can be held so high As theirs who sense and self deny
Kurals.Explanation: The end and aim of all treatise is to extol beyond all other excellence, the greatness of those who, while abiding in the rule of conduct peculiar to their state, have abandoned all desire
Kurals.Couplet: The settled rule of every code requires, as highest good,Their greatness who, renouncing all, true to their rule have stood
Kurals.emotions: Reverence – for those who practice self-denial and asceticism., Admiration – for the greatness and excellence of ascetics., Aspiration – towards virtue

Kurals.Translation: To con ascetic glory here Is to count the dead upon the sphere
Kurals.Explanation: To describe the measure of the greatness of those who have forsaken the two-fold desires, is like counting the dead
Kurals.Couplet: As counting those that from the earth have passed away,'Tis vain attempt the might of holy men to say
Kurals.EmotionDetail: towards the greatness of ascetics, deep respect for those who have forsaken desires,

Kurals.Translation: No lustre can with theirs compare Who know the right and virtue wear
Kurals.Explanation: The greatness of those who have discovered the properties of both states of being, and renounced the world, shines forth on earth (beyond all others)
Kurals.Couplet: Their greatness earth transcends, who, way of both worlds weighed,In this world take their stand, in virtue's robe arrayed
Kurals.emotions: Reverence – Expressing deep respect and admiration for ascetics who possess profound knowledge and virtue., Awe – A sense of wonder and amazement at the greatness and spiritual attainment of those

Kurals.Translation: With hook of firmness to restrain The senses five, is heaven to gain
Kurals.Explanation: He who guides his five senses by the hook of wisdom will be a seed in the world of heaven
Kurals.Couplet: He, who with firmness, curb the five restrains,Is seed for soil of yonder happy plains
Kurals.emotions: Self-discipline – The focus on restraining the senses suggests conscious control and personal discipline., Aspiration – The mention of gaining heaven reflects a longing or aspiration for something higher or better

Kurals.Translation: Indra himself has cause to say How great the power ascetics' sway
Kurals.Explanation: Indra, the king of the inhabitants of the spacious heaven, is himself, a sufficient proof of the strength of him who has subdued his five senses
Kurals.Couplet: Their might who have destroyed 'the five', shall soothly tellIndra, the lord of those in heaven's wide realms that dwell
Kurals.emotions: Reverence – Respect and admiration for the greatness and power of ascetics., Awe – A feeling of wonder inspired by the extraordinary strength of those who have mastered their senses.

Kurals.Translation: The small the paths of ease pursue The great achieve things rare to do
Kurals.Explanation: The great will do those things which is difficult to be done; but the mean cannot do them
Kurals.Couplet: Things hard in the doing will great men do;Things hard in the doing the mean eschew
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – for the greatness and achievements of ascetics or exceptional individuals., Aspiration – the desire to pursue greatness and accomplish difficult tasks., Respect – for those who undertake

Kurals.Translation: They gain the world, who grasp and tell Of taste, sight, hearing, touch and smell
Kurals.Explanation: The world is within the knowledge of him who knows the properties of taste, sight, touch, hearing and smell
Kurals.Couplet: Taste, light, touch, sound, and smell: who knows the wayOf all the five,- the world submissive owns his sway
Kurals.EmotionDetail: at the greatness and mastery of ascetics, for those who understand and control the senses,

Kurals.Translation: Full-worded men by what they say, Their greatness to the world display
Kurals.Explanation: The hidden words of the men whose words are full of effect, will shew their greatness to the world
Kurals.Couplet: The might of men whose word is never vain,The 'secret word' shall to the earth proclaim
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with this text are:, Reverence – The text expresses deep respect for ascetics and those whose words possess depth and meaning., Admiration – There is a sense of admiration for the greatness displayed through

Kurals.Translation: Their wrath, who've climb'd the mount of good, Though transient, cannot be withstood
Kurals.Explanation: The anger of those who have ascended the mountain of goodness, though it continue but for a moment, cannot be resisted
Kurals.Couplet: The wrath 'tis hard e'en for an instant to endure,Of those who virtue's hill have scaled, and stand secure
Kurals.EmotionDetail: towards the power and virtue of ascetics, of the wrath/anger of the ascetics, for the ascetics' moral stature and self-control

Kurals.Translation: With gentle mercy towards all, The sage fulfils the vitue's call
Kurals.Explanation: The virtuous are truly called Anthanar; because in their conduct towards all creatures they are clothed in kindness
Kurals.Couplet: Towards all that breathe, with seemly graciousness adorned they live;And thus to virtue's sons the name of 'Anthanar' men give

Summary: Highlights the ethics of governance as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Awe, Reverence, Asceticism..
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: From virtue weal and wealth outflow; What greater good can mankind know?
Kurals.Explanation: Virtue will confer heaven and wealth; what greater source of happiness can man possess ?
Kurals.Couplet: It yields distinction, yields prosperity; what gainGreater than virtue can a living man obtain
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for virtue and its benefits, for the rewards that virtue brings, from the happiness and fulfillment virtue provides

Kurals.Translation: Virtue enhances joy and gain; Forsaking it is fall and pain
Kurals.Explanation: There can be no greater source of good than (the practice of) virtue; there can be no greater source of evil than the forgetfulness of it
Kurals.Couplet: No greater gain than virtue aught can cause;No greater loss than life oblivious of her laws
Kurals.emotions: **Inspiration** — The text encourages the practice of virtue

Kurals.Translation: Perform good deeds as much you can Always and everywhere, o man!
Kurals.Explanation: As much as possible, in every way, incessantly practise virtue
Kurals.Couplet: To finish virtue's work with ceaseless effort strive,What way thou may'st, where'er thou see'st the work may thrive

Kurals.Translation: In spotless mind virtue is found And not in show and swelling sound
Kurals.Explanation: Let him who does virtuous deeds be of spotless mind; to that extent is virtue; all else is vain show
Kurals.Couplet: Spotless be thou in mind! This only merits virtue's name;All else, mere pomp of idle sound, no real worth can claim

Kurals.Translation: Four ills eschew and virtue reach, Lust, anger, envy, evil-speech
Kurals.Explanation: That conduct is virtue which is free from these four things, viz, malice, desire, anger and bitter speech
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis virtue when, his footsteps sliding not through envy, wrath,Lust, evil speech-these four, man onwards moves in ordered path

Kurals.Translation: Do good enow; defer it not A deathless aid in death if sought
Kurals.Explanation: Defer not virtue to another day; receive her now; and at the dying hour she will be your undying friend
Kurals.Couplet: Do deeds of virtue now Say not, 'To-morrow we'll be wise';Thus, when thou diest, shalt thou find a help that never dies

Kurals.Translation: Litter-bearer and rider say Without a word, the fortune's way
Kurals.Explanation: The fruit of virtue need not be described in books; it may be inferred from seeing the bearer of a palanquin and the rider therein
Kurals.Couplet: Needs not in words to dwell on virtue's fruits: compareThe man in litter borne with them that toiling bear
Kurals.emotions: Humility – The observation of roles (bearer and rider) evokes humility about one's own place in the world and the virtue of recognizing equality or fortune., Reflection – The passage encourages

Kurals.Translation: Like stones that block rebirth and pain Are doing good and good again
Kurals.Explanation: If one allows no day to pass without some good being done, his conduct will be a stone to block up the passage to other births
Kurals.Couplet: If no day passing idly, good to do each day you toil,A stone it will be to block the way of future days of moil
Kurals.EmotionDetail: a sense of moral goodness or righteousness, aspiration for positive transformation and liberation from pain, commitment to consistently doing good deeds

Kurals.Translation: Weal flows only from virtue done The rest is rue and renown gone
Kurals.Explanation: Only that pleasure which flows from domestic virtue is pleasure; all else is not pleasure, and it is without praise
Kurals.Couplet: What from virtue floweth, yieldeth dear delight;All else extern, is void of glory's light
Kurals.EmotionDetail: from doing good and living morally,

Kurals.Translation: Worthy act is virtue done Vice is what we ought to shun
Kurals.Explanation: That is virtue which each ought to do, and that is vice which each should shun
Kurals.Couplet: 'Virtue' sums the things that should be done;'Vice' sums the things that man should shun
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 Human Emotions Associated with the Text:, Responsibility – The text emphasizes what one ought to do

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Hope, Admiration, Contentment For Virtue.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The ideal householder is he Who aids the natural orders there
Kurals.Explanation: He will be called a (true) householder, who is a firm support to the virtuous of the three orders in
Kurals.Couplet: The men of household virtue, firm in way of good, sustainThe other orders three that rule professed maintain

Kurals.Translation: His help the monk and retired share, And celibate students are his care
Kurals.Explanation: He will be said to flourish in domestic virtue who aids the forsaken, the poor, and the dead
Kurals.Couplet: To anchorites, to indigent, to those who've passed away,The man for household virtue famed is needful held and stay
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – Showing care and kindness toward others

Kurals.Translation: By dutiful householder's aid God, manes, kin, self and guests are served
Kurals.Explanation: The chief (duty of the householder) is to preserve the five-fold rule (of conduct) towards the manes, the Gods, his guests, his relations and himself
Kurals.Couplet: The manes, God, guests kindred, self, in due degree,These five to cherish well is chiefest charity
Kurals.EmotionDetail: a sense of responsibility and obligation,

Kurals.Translation: Sin he shuns and food he shares His home is bright and brighter fares
Kurals.Explanation: His descendants shall never fail who, living in the domestic state, fears vice (in the acquisition of property) and shares his food (with others)
Kurals.Couplet: Who shares his meal with other, while all guilt he shuns,His virtuous line unbroken though the ages runs

Kurals.Translation: In grace and gain the home excels, Where love with virtue sweetly dwells
Kurals.Explanation: If the married life possess love and virtue, these will be both its duty and reward
Kurals.Couplet: If love and virtue in the household reign,This is of life the perfect grace and gain

Kurals.Translation: Who turns from righteous family To be a monk, what profits he?
Kurals.Explanation: What will he who lives virtuously in the domestic state gain by going into the other, (ascetic) state ?
Kurals.Couplet: If man in active household life a virtuous soul retain,What fruit from other modes of virtue can he gain
Kurals.emotions: Contentment – Satisfaction with a virtuous domestic life., Curiosity – Wondering about the benefits of leaving domestic life for asceticism., Reflection – Thoughtful consideration of

Kurals.Translation: Of all who strive for bliss, the great Is he who leads the married state
Kurals.Explanation: Among all those who labour (for future happiness) he is greatest who lives well in the household state
Kurals.Couplet: In nature's way who spends his calm domestic days,'Mid all that strive for virtue's crown hath foremost place

Kurals.Translation: Straight in virtue, right in living Make men brighter than monks praying
Kurals.Explanation: The householder who, not swerving from virtue, helps the ascetic in his way, endures more than those who endure penance
Kurals.Couplet: Others it sets upon their way, itself from virtue ne'er declines;Than stern ascetics' pains such life domestic brighter shines
Kurals.emotions: Respect – for the virtue and endurance of the householder., Admiration – towards those who live righteously and support others., Pride – in upholding moral values

Kurals.Translation: Home-life and virtue, are the same; Which spotless monkhood too can claim
Kurals.Explanation: The marriage state is truly called virtue The other state is also good, if others do not reproach it
Kurals.Couplet: The life domestic rightly bears true virtue's name;That other too, if blameless found, due praise may claim

Kurals.Translation: He is a man of divine worth Who lives in ideal home on earth
Kurals.Explanation: He who on earth has lived in the conjugal state as he should live, will be placed among the Gods who dwell in heaven
Kurals.Couplet: Who shares domestic life, by household virtues graced,Shall, mid the Gods, in heaven who dwell, be placed
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for virtuous living and divine worth, from harmonious domestic life,

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Contentment, Virtue, Pride.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: A good housewife befits the house, Spending with thrift the mate's resource
Kurals.Explanation: She who has the excellence of home virtues, and can expend within the means of her husband, is a help in the domestic state
Kurals.Couplet: As doth the house beseem, she shows her wifely dignity;As doth her husband's wealth befit, she spends: help - meet is she

Kurals.Translation: Bright is home when wife is chaste If not all greatness is but waste
Kurals.Explanation: If the wife be devoid of domestic excellence, whatever (other) greatness be possessed, the conjugal state, is nothing
Kurals.Couplet: If household excellence be wanting in the wife,Howe'er with splendour lived, all worthless is the life
Kurals.EmotionDetail: sense of worth/respect, if lacking domestic excellence, in possessing virtue/domestic excellence

Kurals.Translation: What is rare when wife is good What can be there when she is bad?
Kurals.Explanation: If his wife be eminent (in virtue), what does (that man) not possess ? If she be without excellence, what does (he) possess ?
Kurals.Couplet: There is no lack within the house, where wife in worth excels,There is no luck within the house, where wife dishonoured dwells
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for virtue/goodness, with lack of virtue/badness, contemplation about value and worth

Kurals.Translation: What greater fortune is for men Than a constant chaste woman?
Kurals.Explanation: What is more excellent than a wife, if she possess the stability of chastity ?
Kurals.Couplet: If woman might of chastity retain,What choicer treasure doth the world contain
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – for the virtues of a constant

Kurals.Translation: Her spouse before God who adores, Is like rain that at request pours
Kurals.Explanation: If she, who does not worship God, but who rising worships her husband, say, "let it rain," it will rain
Kurals.Couplet: No God adoring, low she bends before her lord;Then rising, serves: the rain falls instant at her word
Kurals.emotions: Devotion – The text emphasizes a wife's deep sense of reverence and commitment toward her husband., Adoration – The husband's adoration is mentioned

Kurals.Translation: The good wife guards herself from blame, She tends her spouse and brings him fame
Kurals.Explanation: She is a wife who unweariedly guards herself, takes care of her husband, and preserves an unsullied fame
Kurals.Couplet: Who guards herself, for husband's comfort cares, her household's fame,In perfect wise with sleepless soul preserves, -give her a woman's name
Kurals.emotions: Duty – The text strongly emphasizes responsibility and the fulfillment of expected roles within marriage., Care – The act of tending to one’s spouse and preserving their well-being reflects care and

Kurals.Translation: Of what avail are watch and ward? Their purity is women's guard
Kurals.Explanation: What avails the guard of a prison ? The chief guard of a woman is her chastity
Kurals.Couplet: Of what avail is watch and ward?Honour's woman's safest guard
Kurals.emotions: **Protectiveness** — The text discusses the desire to safeguard and ensure the well-being or virtue of women., **Respect** — It emphasizes valuing and honoring a woman's chastity

Kurals.Translation: Women who win their husbands' heart Shall flourish where the gods resort
Kurals.Explanation: If women shew reverence to their husbands, they will obtain great excellence in the world where the gods flourish
Kurals.Couplet: If wife be wholly true to him who gained her as his bride,Great glory gains she in the world where gods bliss abide
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , for excellence and flourishing

Kurals.Translation: A cuckold has not the lion-like gait Before his detractors aright
Kurals.Explanation: The man whose wife seeks not the praise (of chastity) cannot walk with lion-like stately step, before those who revile them
Kurals.Couplet: Who have not spouses that in virtue's praise delight,They lion-like can never walk in scorner's sight
Kurals.emotions: Shame – The text reflects the feeling of shame a man experiences due to his wife’s lack of chastity and the subsequent judgment from others., Insecurity – The man feels insecure about

Kurals.Translation: An honest wife is home's delight And children good are jewels abright
Kurals.Explanation: The excellence of a wife is the good of her husband; and good children are the jewels of that goodness
Kurals.Couplet: The house's 'blessing', men pronounce the house-wife excellent;The gain of blessed children is its goodly ornament
Kurals.emotions: Appreciation – for the value and excellence of an honest wife and good children., Love – reflected in the warm relationships within the family., Pride – in having a virtuous wife

Summary: Highlights the virtue of chastity as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Responsibility, Contentment, Appreciation.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The world no higher bliss bestows Than children virtuous and wise
Kurals.Explanation: Among all the benefits that may be acquired, we know no greater benefit than the acquisition of intelligent children
Kurals.Couplet: Of all that men acquire, we know not any greater gain,Than that which by the birth of learned children men obtain
Kurals.emotions: Joy – The text speaks of "bliss" and the happiness that virtuous and wise children bring., Pride – There is a sense of honor and satisfaction in having intelligent children.

Kurals.Translation: No evil comes and no blemish; Noble sons bring all we wish
Kurals.Explanation: The evils of the seven births shall not touch those who abtain children of a good disposition, free from vice
Kurals.Couplet: Who children gain, that none reproach, of virtuous worth,No evils touch them, through the sev'n-fold maze of birth
Kurals.emotions: Hope – The text expresses optimism for a future blessed by good

Kurals.Translation: Children are one's wealth indeed Their wealth is measured by their deed
Kurals.Explanation: Men will call their sons their wealth, because it flows to them through the deeds which they (sons) perform on their behalf
Kurals.Couplet: 'Man's children are his fortune,' say the wise;From each one's deeds his varied fortunes rise
Kurals.emotions: Pride – In reference to the good deeds performed by the children

Kurals.Translation: The food is more than nectar sweet In which one's children hands insert
Kurals.Explanation: The rice in which the little hand of their children has dabbled will be far sweeter (to the parent) than ambrosia
Kurals.Couplet: Than God's ambrosia sweeter far the food before men laid,In which the little hands of children of their own have play'd

Kurals.Translation: Children's touch delights the body Sweet to ears are their words lovely
Kurals.Explanation: The touch of children gives pleasure to the body, and the hearing of their words, pleasure to the ear
Kurals.Couplet: To patent sweet the touch of children dear;Their voice is sweetest music to his ear
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 emotions associated with the text:, Joy – The text describes delight and pleasure experienced through children’s touch and words., Love – There is an affectionate tone toward children

Kurals.Translation: The flute and lute are sweet they say Deaf to baby's babble's lay!
Kurals.Explanation: "The pipe is sweet, the lute is sweet," say those who have not heard the prattle of their own children
Kurals.Couplet: 'The pipe is sweet,' 'the lute is sweet,' by them't will be averred,Who music of their infants' lisping lips have never heard
Kurals.emotions: Joy – The text highlights the unique

Kurals.Translation: A father's duty to his son is To seat him in front of the wise
Kurals.Explanation: The benefit which a father should confer on his son is to give him precedence in the assembly of the learned
Kurals.Couplet: Sire greatest boon on son confers, who makes him meet,In councils of the wise to fill the highest seat

Kurals.Translation: With joy the hearts of parents swell To see their children themselves excel
Kurals.Explanation: That their children should possess knowledge is more pleasing to all men of this great earth than to themselves
Kurals.Couplet: Their children's wisdom greater than their own confessed,Through the wide world is sweet to every human breast

Kurals.Translation: The mother, hearing her son's merit Delights more than when she begot
Kurals.Explanation: The mother who hears her son called "a wise man" will rejoice more than she did at his birth
Kurals.Couplet: When mother hears him named 'fulfill'd of wisdom's lore,'Far greater joy she feels, than when her son she bore

Kurals.Translation: The son to sire this word is debt \"What penance such a son begot!\"
Kurals.Explanation: (So to act) that it may be said "by what great penance did his father beget him," is the benefit which a son should render to his father
Kurals.Couplet: To sire, what best requital can by grateful child be done?To make men say, 'What merit gained the father such a son?'
Kurals.emotions: Gratitude – The text reflects a deep sense of thankfulness for one’s lineage and the blessings bestowed by having a worthy son., Reverence – There is a tone of respect and

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Joy, Love, Pride.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: What bolt can bar true love in fact The tricking tears reveal the heart
Kurals.Explanation: Is there any fastening that can shut in love ? Tears of the affectionate will publish the love that is within
Kurals.Couplet: And is there bar that can even love restrain?The tiny tear shall make the lover's secret plain
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions in the text:, Love, Affection, Vulnerability

Kurals.Translation: To selves belong the loveless ones; To oth'rs the loving e'en to bones
Kurals.Explanation: Those who are destitute of love appropriate all they have to themselves; but those who possess love consider even their bones to belong to others
Kurals.Couplet: The loveless to themselves belong alone;The loving men are others' to the very bone
Kurals.emotions: Selfishness — felt by the loveless ones who keep everything to themselves., Selflessness — shown by the loving individuals who offer even their bones to others., Generosity

Kurals.Translation: Soul is encased in frame of bone To taste the life of love alone
Kurals.Explanation: They say that the union of soul and body in man is the fruit of the union of love and virtue (in a former birth)
Kurals.Couplet: Of precious soul with body's flesh and bone,The union yields one fruit, the life of love alone
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions in the text:, Love, Longing, Reverence

Kurals.Translation: Love yields aspiration and thence Friendship springs up in excellence
Kurals.Explanation: Love begets desire: and that (desire) begets the immeasureable excellence of friendship
Kurals.Couplet: From love fond yearning springs for union sweet of minds;And that the bond of rare excelling friendship binds
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Desire, Affection/Companionship

Kurals.Translation: The crowning joy of home life flows From peaceful psychic love always
Kurals.Explanation: These are the fruits of tranquil life of love
Kurals.Couplet: Sweetness on earth and rarest bliss above,These are the fruits of tranquil life of love
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Tranquility,

Kurals.Translation: \"Love is virtue's friend\" say know-nots It helps us against evil plots
Kurals.Explanation: The ignorant say that love is an ally to virtue only, but it is also a help to get out of vice
Kurals.Couplet: The unwise deem love virtue only can sustain,It also helps the man who evil would restrain

Kurals.Translation: Justice burns the loveless form Like solar blaze the boneless worm
Kurals.Explanation: Virtue will burn up the soul which is without love, even as the sun burns up the creature which is without bone, ie worms
Kurals.Couplet: As sun's fierce ray dries up the boneless things,So loveless beings virtue's power to nothing brings
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Love – The passage centers on the importance of possessing love., Justice – The idea of “justice burns the loveless form” imbues the emotion of justice or righteousness. 3

Kurals.Translation: Life bereft of love is gloom Can sapless tree in desert bloom?
Kurals.Explanation: The domestic state of that man whose mind is without love is like the flourishing of a withered tree upon the parched desert
Kurals.Couplet: The loveless soul, the very joys of life may know,When flowers, in barren soil, on sapless trees, shall blow
Kurals.emotions: Loneliness – The absence of love is described as a state of gloom

Kurals.Translation: Love is the heart which limbs must move, Or vain the outer parts will prove
Kurals.Explanation: Of what avail are all the external members (of the body) to those who are destitute of love, the internal member
Kurals.Couplet: Though every outward part complete, the body's fitly framed;What good, when soul within, of love devoid, lies halt and maimed

Kurals.Translation: The seat of life is love alone; Or beings are but skin and bone!
Kurals.Explanation: That body alone which is inspired with love contains a living soul: if void of it, (the body) is bone overlaid with skin
Kurals.Couplet: Bodies of loveless men are bony framework clad with skin;Then is the body seat of life, when love resides within
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or inspiration, implied by the absence of love

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Love, Affection, Vulnerability.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Men set up home, toil and earn To tend the guests and do good turn
Kurals.Explanation: The whole design of living in the domestic state and laying up (property) is (to be able) to exercise the benevolence of hospitality
Kurals.Couplet: All household cares and course of daily life have this in viewGuests to receive with courtesy, and kindly acts to do

Kurals.Translation: To keep out guests cannot be good Albeit you eat nector-like food
Kurals.Explanation: It is not fit that one should wish his guests to be outside (his house) even though he were eating the food of immortality
Kurals.Couplet: Though food of immortality should crown the board,Feasting alone, the guests without unfed, is thing abhorred
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with this text are:, Generosity, Hospitality, Compassion
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , welcoming,

Kurals.Translation: Who tends his guests day in and out His life in want never wears out
Kurals.Explanation: The domestic life of the man that daily entertains the guests who come to him shall not be laid waste by poverty
Kurals.Couplet: Each day he tends the coming guest with kindly care;Painless, unfailing plenty shall his household share

Kurals.Translation: The goddess of wealth will gladly rest Where smiles welcome the worthy guest
Kurals.Explanation: Lakshmi with joyous mind shall dwell in the house of that man who, with cheerful countenance, entertains the good as guests
Kurals.Couplet: With smiling face he entertains each virtuous guest,'Fortune' with gladsome mind shall in his dwelling rest
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Welcoming,

Kurals.Translation: Should his field be sown who first Feeds the guests and eats the rest?
Kurals.Explanation: Is it necessary to sow the field of the man who, having feasted his guests, eats what may remain ?
Kurals.Couplet: Who first regales his guest, and then himself supplies,O'er all his fields, unsown, shall plenteous harvests rise

Kurals.Translation: Who tends a guest and looks for next Is a welcome guest in heaven's feast
Kurals.Explanation: He who, having entertained the guests that have come, looks out for others who may yet come, will be a welcome guest to the inhabitants of heaven
Kurals.Couplet: The guest arrived he tends, the coming guest expects to see;To those in heavenly homes that dwell a welcome guest is he
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Welcoming, of doing good for others

Kurals.Translation: Worth of the guest of quality Is worth of hospitality
Kurals.Explanation: The advantages of benevolence cannot be measured; the measure (of the virtue) of the guests (entertained) is the only measure
Kurals.Couplet: To reckon up the fruit of kindly deeds were all in vain;Their worth is as the worth of guests you entertain

Kurals.Translation: Who loathe guest-service one day cry: \"We toil and store; but life is dry\"
Kurals.Explanation: Those who have taken no part in the benevolence of hospitality shall (at length lament) saying, "we have laboured and laid up wealth and are now without support."
Kurals.Couplet: With pain they guard their stores, yet 'All forlorn are we,' they'll cry,Who cherish not their guests, nor kindly help supply

Kurals.Translation: The man of wealth is poor indeed Whose folly fails the guest to feed
Kurals.Explanation: That stupidity which excercises no hospitality is poverty in the midst of wealth It is the property of the stupid
Kurals.Couplet: To turn from guests is penury, though worldly goods abound;'Tis senseless folly, only with the senseless found
Kurals.emotions: Shame – The text implies a sense of disgrace or embarrassment at failing to fulfill the moral duty of hospitality., Generosity – There is an underlying value placed on generosity

Kurals.Translation: Anicham smelt withers: like that A wry-faced look withers the guest
Kurals.Explanation: As the Anicham flower fades in smelling, so fades the guest when the face is turned away
Kurals.Couplet: The flower of 'Anicha' withers away, If you do but its fragrance inhale;If the face of the host cold welcome convey, The guest's heart within him will fail

Summary: Highlights the duty of hospitality as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Generosity, Hospitality, Welcoming.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The words of Seers are lovely sweet Merciful and free from deceit
Kurals.Explanation: Sweet words are those which imbued with love and free from deceit flow from the mouth of the virtuous
Kurals.Couplet: Pleasant words are words with all pervading love that burn;Words from his guileless mouth who can the very truth discern
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Mercy, Sincerity

Kurals.Translation: Sweet words from smiling lips dispense More joys than heart's beneficence
Kurals.Explanation: Sweet speech, with a cheerful countenance is better than a gift made with a joyous mind
Kurals.Couplet: A pleasant word with beaming smile's preferred,Even to gifts with liberal heart conferred

Kurals.Translation: Calm face, sweet look, kind words from heart Such is the gracious virtue's part
Kurals.Explanation: Sweet speech, flowing from the heart (uttered) with a cheerful countenance and a sweet look, is true virtue
Kurals.Couplet: With brightly beaming smile, and kindly light of loving eye,And heart sincere, to utter pleasant words is charity

Kurals.Translation: Whose loving words delight each one The woe of want from them is gone
Kurals.Explanation: Sorrow-increasing poverty shall not come upon those who use towards all, pleasure-increasing sweetness of speech
Kurals.Couplet: The men of pleasant speech that gladness breathe around,Through indigence shall never sorrow's prey be found
Kurals.EmotionDetail: due to "loving words" and "sweetness of speech", as speech "delight each one" and brings "pleasure"

Kurals.Translation: To be humble and sweet words speak No other jewel do wise men seek
Kurals.Explanation: Humility and sweetness of speech are the ornaments of man; all others are not (ornaments)
Kurals.Couplet: Humility with pleasant speech to man on earth,Is choice adornment; all besides is nothing worth

Kurals.Translation: His sins vanish, his virtues grow Whose fruitful words with sweetness flow
Kurals.Explanation: If a man, while seeking to speak usefully, speaks also sweetly, his sins will diminish and his virtue increase
Kurals.Couplet: Who seeks out good, words from his lips of sweetness flow;In him the power of vice declines, and virtues grow

Kurals.Translation: The fruitful courteous kindly words Lead to goodness and graceful deeds
Kurals.Explanation: That speech which, while imparting benefits ceases not to please, will yield righteousness (for this world) and merit (for the next world)
Kurals.Couplet: The words of sterling sense, to rule of right that strict adhere,To virtuous action prompting, blessings yield in every sphere
Kurals.emotions: Kindness – The text emphasizes courteous and kindly words., Gratitude – There's an implicit sense of appreciation for goodness and graceful deeds., Contentment – The idea that pleasing and

Kurals.Translation: Kind words free from meanness delight This life on earth and life the next
Kurals.Explanation: Sweet speech, free from harm to others, will give pleasure both in this world and in the next
Kurals.Couplet: Sweet kindly words, from meanness free, delight of heart,In world to come and in this world impart
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Delight, or Harmony

Kurals.Translation: Who sees the sweets of sweetness here To use harsh words how can he dare?
Kurals.Explanation: Why does he use harsh words, who sees the pleasure which sweet speech yields ?
Kurals.Couplet: Who sees the pleasure kindly speech affords,Why makes he use of harsh, repellant words

Kurals.Translation: Leaving ripe fruits the raw he eats Who speaks harsh words when sweet word suits
Kurals.Explanation: To say disagreeable things when agreeable are at hand is like eating unripe fruit when there is ripe
Kurals.Couplet: When pleasant words are easy, bitter words to use,Is, leaving sweet ripe fruit, the sour unripe to choose
Kurals.emotions: Regret – The text conveys a sense of wasted opportunity

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Kindness, Warmth, Calmness.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Unhelped in turn good help given Exceeds in worth earth and heaven
Kurals.Explanation: (The gift of) heaven and earth is not an equivalent for a benefit which is conferred where none had been received
Kurals.Couplet: Assistance given by those who ne'er received our aid,Is debt by gift of heaven and earth but poorly paid

Kurals.Translation: A help rendered in hour of need Though small is greater than the world
Kurals.Explanation: A favour conferred in the time of need, though it be small (in itself), is (in value) much larger than the world
Kurals.Couplet: A timely benefit, -though thing of little worth,The gift itself, -in excellence transcends the earth

Kurals.Translation: Help rendered without weighing fruits Outweighs the sea in grand effects
Kurals.Explanation: If we weigh the excellence of a benefit which is conferred without weighing the return, it is larger than the sea
Kurals.Couplet: Kindness shown by those who weigh not what the return may be:When you ponder right its merit, 'Tis vaster than the sea

Kurals.Translation: Help given though millet-small Knowers count its good palm-tree tall
Kurals.Explanation: Though the benefit conferred be as small as a millet seed, those who know its advantage will consider it as large as a palmyra fruit
Kurals.Couplet: Each benefit to those of actions' fruit who rightly deem,Though small as millet-seed, as palm-tree vast will seem

Kurals.Translation: A help is not the help's measure It is gainer's worth and pleasure
Kurals.Explanation: The benefit itself is not the measure of the benefit; the worth of those who have received it is its measure
Kurals.Couplet: The kindly aid's extent is of its worth no measure true;Its worth is as the worth of him to whom the act you do

Kurals.Translation: Forget not friendship of the pure Forsake not timely helpers sure
Kurals.Explanation: Forsake not the friendship of those who have been your staff in adversity Forget not be benevolence of the blameless
Kurals.Couplet: Kindness of men of stainless soul remember evermoreForsake thou never friends who were thy stay in sorrow sore

Kurals.Translation: Through sevenfold births, in memory fares The willing friend who wiped one's tears
Kurals.Explanation: (The wise) will remember throughout their seven-fold births the love of those who have wiped away their affliction
Kurals.Couplet: Through all seven worlds, in seven-fold birth, Remains in mem'ry of the wiseFriendship of those who wiped on earth, The tears of sorrow from their eyes

Kurals.Translation: To forget good turns is not good Good it is over wrong not to brood
Kurals.Explanation: It is not good to forget a benefit; it is good to forget an injury even in the very moment (in which it is inflicted)
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis never good to let the thought of good things done thee pass away;Of things not good, 'tis good to rid thy memory that very day

Kurals.Translation: Let deadly harms be forgotten While remembering one good-turn
Kurals.Explanation: Though one inflict an injury great as murder, it will perish before the thought of one benefit (formerly) conferred
Kurals.Couplet: Effaced straightway is deadliest injury,By thought of one kind act in days gone by

Kurals.Translation: The virtue-killer may be saved Not benefit-killer who is damned
Kurals.Explanation: He who has killed every virtue may yet escape; there is no escape for him who has killed a benefit
Kurals.Couplet: Who every good have killed, may yet destruction flee;Who 'benefit' has killed, that man shall ne'er 'scape free

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Gratitude, Appreciation, Humility.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Equity is supreme virtue It is to give each man his due
Kurals.Explanation: That equity which consists in acting with equal regard to each of (the three) divisions of men [enemies, strangers and friends] is a pre-eminent virtue
Kurals.Couplet: If justice, failing not, its quality maintain,Giving to each his due, -'tis man's one highest gain
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Fairness, Respect
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , a sense of justice and impartiality,

Kurals.Translation: Wealth of the man of equity Grows and lasts to posterity
Kurals.Explanation: The wealth of the man of rectitude will not perish, but will bring happiness also to his posterity
Kurals.Couplet: The just man's wealth unwasting shall endure,And to his race a lasting joy ensure

Kurals.Translation: Though profitable, turn away From unjust gains without delay
Kurals.Explanation: Forsake in the very moment (of acquisition) that gain which, though it should bring advantage, is without equity
Kurals.Couplet: Though only good it seem to give, yet gainBy wrong acquired, not e'en one day retain

Kurals.Translation: The worthy and the unworthy Are seen in their posterity
Kurals.Explanation: The worthy and unworthy may be known by the existence or otherwise of good offsprings
Kurals.Couplet: Who just or unjust lived shall soon appear:By each one's offspring shall the truth be clear
Kurals.emotions: Judgment (Discernment) – evaluating the worthiness or unworthiness of people., Pride – felt by those whose posterity reflects their worthiness., Concern

Kurals.Translation: Loss and gain by cause arise; Equal mind adorns the wise
Kurals.Explanation: Loss and gain come not without cause; it is the ornament of the wise to preserve evenness of mind (under both)
Kurals.Couplet: The gain and loss in life are not mere accident;Just mind inflexible is sages' ornament

Kurals.Translation: Of perdition let him be sure Who leaves justice to sinful lure
Kurals.Explanation: Let him whose mind departing from equity commits sin well consider thus within himself, "I shall perish."
Kurals.Couplet: If, right deserting, heart to evil turn,Let man impending ruin's sign discern
Kurals.EmotionDetail: of perdition or consequence, for departing from justice, a warning to carefully consider actions

Kurals.Translation: The just reduced to poverty Is not held down by equity
Kurals.Explanation: The great will not regard as poverty the low estate of that man who dwells in the virtue of equity
Kurals.Couplet: The man who justly lives, tenacious of the right,In low estate is never low to wise man's sight
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Sense of Justice/Equity, Acceptance of circumstances, Esteem for virtuous living

Kurals.Translation: Like balance holding equal scales A well poised mind is jewel of the wise
Kurals.Explanation: To incline to neither side, but to rest impartial as the even-fixed scale is the ornament of the wise
Kurals.Couplet: To stand, like balance-rod that level hangs and rightly weighs,With calm unbiassed equity of soul, is sages' praise

Kurals.Translation: Justice is upright, unbending And free from crooked word-twisting
Kurals.Explanation: Freedom from obliquity of speech is rectitude, if there be (corresponding) freedom from bias of mind
Kurals.Couplet: Inflexibility in word is righteousness,If men inflexibility of soul possess

Kurals.Translation: A trader's trade prospers fairly When his dealings are neighbourly
Kurals.Explanation: The true merchandize of merchants is to guard and do by the things of others as they do by their own
Kurals.Couplet: As thriving trader is the trader known,Who guards another's interests as his own

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Fairness, Trust, Respect.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Self-rule leads to realms of gods Indulgence leads to gloomy hades
Kurals.Explanation: Self-control will place (a man) among the Gods; the want of it will drive (him) into the thickest darkness (of hell)
Kurals.Couplet: Control of self does man conduct to bliss th' immortals share;Indulgence leads to deepest night, and leaves him there
Kurals.EmotionDetail: associated with self-control and self-restraint,

Kurals.Translation: No gains with self-control measure Guard with care this great treasure
Kurals.Explanation: Let self-control be guarded as a treasure; there is no greater source of good for man than that
Kurals.Couplet: Guard thou as wealth the power of self-control;Than this no greater gain to living soul

Kurals.Translation: Knowing wisdom who lives controlled Name and fame seek him untold
Kurals.Explanation: Knowing that self-control is knowledge, if a man should control himself, in the prescribed course, such self-control will bring him distinction among the wise
Kurals.Couplet: If versed in wisdom's lore by virtue's law you self restrainYour self-repression known will yield you glory's gain
Kurals.emotions: **Respect** – Self-restraint and wisdom lead to recognition and honor among others., **Admiration** – The controlled individual evokes admiration from the wise., **

Kurals.Translation: Firmly fixed in self serene The sage looks grander than mountain
Kurals.Explanation: More lofty than a mountain will be the greatness of that man who without swerving from his domestic state, controls himself
Kurals.Couplet: In his station, all unswerving, if man self subdue,Greater he than mountain proudly rising to the view
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with this text are:, Serenity

Kurals.Translation: Humility is good for all To the rich it adds a wealth special
Kurals.Explanation: Humility is good in all; but especially in the rich it is (the excellence of) higher riches
Kurals.Couplet: To all humility is goodly grace; but chief to themWith fortune blessed, -'tis fortune's diadem

Kurals.Translation: Who senses five like tortoise hold Their joy prolongs to births sevenfold
Kurals.Explanation: Should one throughout a single birth, like a tortoise keep in his five senses, the fruit of it will prove a safe-guard to him throughout the seven-fold births.
Kurals.Couplet: Like tortoise, who the five restrainsIn one, through seven world bliss obtains
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , regarding the positive results of restraint over multiple lifetimes

Kurals.Translation: Rein the tongue if nothing else Or slips of tongue bring all the woes
Kurals.Explanation: Whatever besides you leave unguarded, guard your tongue; otherwise errors of speech and the consequent misery will ensue
Kurals.Couplet: Whate'er they fail to guard, o'er lips men guard should keep;If not, through fault of tongue, they bitter tears shall weep

Kurals.Translation: Even a single evil word Will turn all good results to bad
Kurals.Explanation: If a man's speech be productive of a single evil, all the good by him will be turned into evil
Kurals.Couplet: Though some small gain of good it seem to bring,The evil word is parent still of evil thing

Kurals.Translation: The fire-burnt wounds do find a cure Tongue-burnt wound rests a running sore
Kurals.Explanation: The wound which has been burnt in by fire may heal, but a wound burnt in by the tongue will never heal
Kurals.Couplet: In flesh by fire inflamed, nature may thoroughly heal the sore;In soul by tongue inflamed, the ulcer healeth never more

Kurals.Translation: Virtue seeks and peeps to see Self-controlled savant anger free
Kurals.Explanation: Virtue, seeking for an opportunity, will come into the path of that man who, possessed of learning and self-control, guards himself against anger
Kurals.Couplet: Who learns restraint, and guards his soul from wrath,Virtue, a timely aid, attends his path

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Caution, Self-Control, Regret.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Decorum does one dignity More than life guard its purity
Kurals.Explanation: Propriety of conduct leads to eminence, it should therefore be preserved more carefully than life
Kurals.Couplet: 'Decorum' gives especial excellence; with greater care'Decorum' should men guard than life, which all men share
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for decorum and propriety, valuing one's own honor and standing, for virtues and moral conduct

Kurals.Translation: Virtues of conduct all excel; The soul aid should be guarded well
Kurals.Explanation: Let propriety of conduct be laboriously preserved and guarded; though one know and practise and excel in many virtues, that will be an eminent aid
Kurals.Couplet: Searching, duly watching, learning, 'decorum' still we find;Man's only aid; toiling, guard thou this with watchful mind
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with the provided text are:, Respect – Emphasizing propriety and decorum reflects a sense of respect for oneself and others., Diligence – The text speaks of laboriously preserving and guarding

Kurals.Translation: Good conduct shows good family Low manners mark anomaly
Kurals.Explanation: Propriety of conduct is true greatness of birth, and impropriety will sink into a mean birth
Kurals.Couplet: 'Decorum's' true nobility on earth;'Indecorum's' issue is ignoble birth
Kurals.emotions: Pride – in having good conduct and noble origins, Shame – associated with improper manners and low conduct, Respect – for propriety and those who uphold good manners

Kurals.Translation: Readers recall forgotten lore, But conduct lost returns no more
Kurals.Explanation: A Brahman though he should forget the Vedas may recover it by reading; but, if he fail in propriety of conduct even his high birth will be destroyed
Kurals.Couplet: Though he forget, the Brahman may regain his Vedic lore;Failing in 'decorum due,' birthright's gone for evermore
Kurals.emotions: Regret – The notion of something lost ("conduct lost returns no more") conveys a sense of irreversible loss and sadness over what cannot be reclaimed., Reverence – Emphasis on the

Kurals.Translation: The envious prosper but ill The ill-behaved sinks lower still
Kurals.Explanation: Just as the envious man will be without wealth, so will the man of destitute of propriety of conduct be without greatness
Kurals.Couplet: The envious soul in life no rich increase of blessing gains,So man of 'due decorum' void no dignity obtains

Kurals.Translation: The firm from virtue falter not They know the ills of evil thought
Kurals.Explanation: Those firm in mind will not slacken in their observance of the proprieties of life, knowing, as they do, the misery that flows from the transgression from them
Kurals.Couplet: The strong of soul no jot abate of 'strict decorum's' laws,Knowing that 'due decorum's' breach foulest disgrace will cause
Kurals.EmotionDetail: determination to maintain virtue and propriety, awareness of the misery resulting from wrongdoing,

Kurals.Translation: Conduct good ennobles man, Bad conduct entails disgrace mean
Kurals.Explanation: From propriety of conduct men obtain greatness; from impropriety comes insufferable disgrace
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis source of dignity when 'true decorum' is preserved;Who break 'decorum's' rules endure e'en censures undeserved
Kurals.emotions: Pride – associated with possessing good conduct and obtaining greatness., Shame – linked to bad conduct and experiencing disgrace., Aspiration – the desire or motivation to maintain good behavior for personal

Kurals.Translation: Good conduct sows seeds of blessings Bad conduct endless evil brings
Kurals.Explanation: Propriety of conduct is the seed of virtue; impropriety will ever cause sorrow
Kurals.Couplet: 'Decorum true' observed a seed of good will be;'Decorum's breach' will sorrow yield eternally
Kurals.EmotionDetail: towards good conduct and virtue, that good conduct brings blessings, of sorrow and evil resulting from bad conduct

Kurals.Translation: Foul words will never fall from lips Of righteous men even by slips
Kurals.Explanation: Those who study propriety of conduct will not speak evil, even forgetfully
Kurals.Couplet: It cannot be that they who 'strict decorum's' law fulfil,E'en in forgetful mood, should utter words of ill
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for righteousness and propriety, restraint from using foul words, commitment to virtuous conduct

Kurals.Translation: Though read much they are ignorant Whose life is not world-accordant
Kurals.Explanation: Those who know not how to act agreeably to the world, though they have learnt many things, are still ignorant
Kurals.Couplet: Who know not with the world in harmony to dwell,May many things have learned, but nothing well

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Propriety, Respect, Dignity.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Who know the wealth and virtue's way After other's wife do not stray
Kurals.Explanation: The folly of desiring her who is the property of another will not be found in those who know (the attributes of) virtue and (the rights of) property
Kurals.Couplet: Who laws of virtue and possession's rights have known,Indulge no foolish love of her by right another's own

Kurals.Translation: He is the worst law breaking boor Who haunts around his neighbour's door
Kurals.Explanation: Among all those who stand on the outside of virtue, there are no greater fools than those who stand outside their neighbour's door
Kurals.Couplet: No fools, of all that stand from virtue's pale shut out,Like those who longing lurk their neighbour's gate without
Kurals.emotions: Envy – The text discusses coveting another’s wife and comparing oneself to others

Kurals.Translation: The vile are dead who evil aim And put faithful friends' wives to shame
Kurals.Explanation: Certainly they are no better than dead men who desire evil towards the wife of those who
Kurals.Couplet: They're numbered with the dead, e'en while they live, -how otherwise?With wife of sure confiding friend who evil things devise

Kurals.Translation: Their boasted greatness means nothing When to another's wife they cling
Kurals.Explanation: However great one may be, what does it avail if, without at all considering his guilt, he goes unto the wife of another ?
Kurals.Couplet: How great soe'er they be, what gain have they of life,Who, not a whit reflecting, seek a neighbour's wife

Kurals.Translation: Who trifles with another's wife His guilty stain will last for life
Kurals.Explanation: He who thinks lightly of going into the wife of another acquires guilt that will abide with him imperishably and for ever
Kurals.Couplet: 'Mere triflel' saying thus, invades the home, so he ensuresA gain of guilt that deathless aye endures
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Guilt, Shame, Regret

Kurals.Translation: Hatred, sin, fear, and shame-these four Stain adulterers ever more
Kurals.Explanation: Hatred, sin, fear, disgrace; these four will never leave him who goes in to his neighbour's wife
Kurals.Couplet: Who home ivades, from him pass nevermore,Hatred and sin, fear, foul disgrace; these four

Kurals.Translation: He is the righteous householder His neighbour's wife who covets never
Kurals.Explanation: He who desires not the womanhood of her who should walk according to the will of another will be praised as a virtuous house-holder
Kurals.Couplet: Who sees the wife, another's own, with no desiring eyeIn sure domestic bliss he dwelleth ever virtuously
Kurals.emotions: Respect – Acknowledging and honoring the boundaries and relationships of others., Virtue – Aspiring toward moral excellence and righteous conduct., Restraint – Exercising

Kurals.Translation: They lead a high-souled manly life The pure who eye not another's wife
Kurals.Explanation: That noble manliness which looks not at the wife of another is the virtue and dignity of the great
Kurals.Couplet: Manly excellence, that looks not on another's wife,Is not virtue merely, 'tis full 'propriety' of life
Kurals.emotions: Respect – The text talks about honoring boundaries and valuing others' relationships., Self-control – The emphasis is on restraint and not yielding to temptation., Dignity

Kurals.Translation: Good in storm bound earth is with those Who clasp not arms of another's spouse
Kurals.Explanation: Is it asked, "who are those who shall obtain good in this world surrounded by the terror-producing sea ?" Those who touch not the shoulder of her who belongs to another
Kurals.Couplet: Who 're good indeed, on earth begirt by ocean's gruesome tide?The men who touch not her that is another's bride
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Self-control, ,

Kurals.Translation: Sinners breaking virtue's behest Lust not for another's wife at least
Kurals.Explanation: Though a man perform no virtuous deeds and commit (every) vice, it will be well if he desire not the womanhood of her who is within the limit (of the house) of another
Kurals.Couplet: Though virtue's bounds he pass, and evil deeds hath wrought;At least, 'tis good if neighbour's wife he covet not
Kurals.emotions: Desire (Lust) — The text discusses the emotion of desiring someone who belongs to another., Guilt/Shame — Implied for those who act on such desires

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Shame, Guilt, Respect.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: As earth bears up with diggers too To bear revilers is prime virtue
Kurals.Explanation: To bear with those who revile us, just as the earth bears up those who dig it, is the first of virtues
Kurals.Couplet: As earth bears up the men who delve into her breast,To bear with scornful men of virtues is the best

Kurals.Translation: Forgive insults is a good habit Better it is to forget it
Kurals.Explanation: Bear with reproach even when you can retaliate; but to forget it will be still better than that
Kurals.Couplet: Forgiving trespasses is good always;Forgetting them hath even higher praise;

Kurals.Translation: Neglect the guest is dearth of dearth To bear with fools is strength of strength
Kurals.Explanation: To neglect hospitality is poverty of poverty To bear with the ignorant is might of might
Kurals.Couplet: The sorest poverty is bidding guest unfed depart;The mightiest might to bear with men of foolish heart

Kurals.Translation: Practice of patient quality Retains intact itegrity
Kurals.Explanation: If you desire that greatness should never leave, you preserve in your conduct the exercise of patience
Kurals.Couplet: Seek'st thou honour never tarnished to retain;So must thou patience, guarding evermore, maintain

Kurals.Translation: Vengeance is not in esteem held Patience is praised as hidden gold
Kurals.Explanation: (The wise) will not at all esteem the resentful They will esteem the patient just as the gold which they lay up with care
Kurals.Couplet: Who wreak their wrath as worthless are despised;Who patiently forbear as gold are prized
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , respect/admirational feeling

Kurals.Translation: Revenge accords but one day's joy Patience carries its praise for aye
Kurals.Explanation: The pleasure of the resentful continues for a day The praise of the patient will continue until (the final destruction of) the world
Kurals.Couplet: Who wreak their wrath have pleasure for a day;Who bear have praise till earth shall pass away
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , or Admiration — for the patient person

Kurals.Translation: Though others cause you wanton pain Grieve not; from unjust harm refrain
Kurals.Explanation: Though others inflict injuries on you, yet compassionating the evil (that will come upon them) it will be well not to do them anything contrary to virtue
Kurals.Couplet: Though others work thee ill, thus shalt thou blessing reap;Grieve for their sin, thyself from vicious action keep

Kurals.Translation: By noble forbearance vanquish The proud that have caused you anguish
Kurals.Explanation: Let a man by patience overcome those who through pride commit excesses
Kurals.Couplet: With overweening pride when men with injuries assail,By thine own righteous dealing shalt thou mightily prevail
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Patience, Resilience, Humility

Kurals.Translation: More than ascetics they are pure Who bitter tongues meekly endure
Kurals.Explanation: Those who bear with the uncourteous speech of the insolent are as pure as the ascetics
Kurals.Couplet: They who transgressors' evil words endureWith patience, are as stern ascetics pure

Kurals.Translation: Who fast are great to do penance Greater are they who bear offence
Kurals.Explanation: Those who endure abstinence from food are great, next to those who endure the uncourteous speech of others
Kurals.Couplet: Though 'great' we deem the men that fast and suffer pain,Who others' bitter words endure, the foremost place obtain

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Patience, Tolerance, Endurance.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Deem your heart as virtuous When your nature is not jealous
Kurals.Explanation: Let a man esteem that disposition which is free from envy in the same manner as propriety of conduct
Kurals.Couplet: As 'strict decorum's' laws, that all men bind,Let each regard unenvying grace of mind

Kurals.Translation: No excellence excels the one That by nature envies none
Kurals.Explanation: Amongst all attainable excellences there is none equal to that of being free from envy towords others
Kurals.Couplet: If man can learn to envy none on earth,'Tis richest gift, -beyond compare its worth

Kurals.Translation: Who envies others' good fortune Can't prosper in virtue of his own
Kurals.Explanation: Of him who instead of rejoicing in the wealth of others, envies it, it will be said "he neither desires virtue not wealth."
Kurals.Couplet: Nor wealth nor virtue does that man desire 'tis plain,Whom others' wealth delights not, feeling envious pain

Kurals.Translation: The wise through envy don't others wrong Knowing that woes from evils throng
Kurals.Explanation: (The wise) knowing the misery that comes from transgression will not through envy commit unrighteous deeds
Kurals.Couplet: The wise through envy break not virtue's laws,Knowing ill-deeds of foul disgrace the cause
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , of consequences/misery

Kurals.Translation: Man shall be wrecked by envy's whim Even if enemies spare him
Kurals.Explanation: To those who cherish envy that is enough Though free from enemies that (envy) will bring destruction
Kurals.Couplet: Envy they have within! Enough to seat their fate!Though foemen fail, envy can ruin consummate
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Self-destruction,

Kurals.Translation: Who envies gifts shall suffer ruin Without food and clothes with his kin
Kurals.Explanation: He who is envious at a gift (made to another) will with his relations utterly perish destitute of food and rainment
Kurals.Couplet: Who scans good gifts to others given with envious eye,His kin, with none to clothe or feed them, surely die
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , or Despair

Kurals.Translation: Fortune deserts the envious Leaving misfortune omnious
Kurals.Explanation: Lakshmi envying (the prosperity) of the envious man will depart and introduce him to her sister
Kurals.Couplet: From envious man good fortune's goddess turns away,Grudging him good, and points him out misfortune's prey
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , associated feeling: sadness,

Kurals.Translation: Caitiff envy despoils wealth And drags one into evil path
Kurals.Explanation: Envy will destroy (a man's) wealth (in his world) and drive him into the pit of fire (in the world to come.)
Kurals.Couplet: Envy, embodied ill, incomparable bane,Good fortune slays, and soul consigns to fiery pain
Kurals.emotions: Envy – The central emotion explicitly discussed., Destruction/Self-ruin – Implied through the consequences of envy (loss and downfall)., Fear – Ev

Kurals.Translation: Why is envy rich, goodmen poor People with surprise think over
Kurals.Explanation: The wealth of a man of envious mind and the poverty of the righteous will be pondered
Kurals.Couplet: To men of envious heart, when comes increase of joy,Or loss to blameless men, the 'why' will thoughtful hearts employ
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , thoughtfulness

Kurals.Translation: The envious prosper never The envyless prosper ever
Kurals.Explanation: Never have the envious become great; never have those who are free from envy been without greatness
Kurals.Couplet: No envious men to large and full felicity attain;No men from envy free have failed a sure increase to gain

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Envy, Contentment, Fear.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Who covets others' honest wealth That greed ruins his house forthwith
Kurals.Explanation: If a man departing from equity covet the property (of others), at that very time will his family be destroyed and guilt be incurred
Kurals.Couplet: With soul unjust to covet others' well-earned store,Brings ruin to the home, to evil opes the door
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , of loss or destruction

Kurals.Translation: Who shrink with shame from sin, refrain From coveting which brings ruin
Kurals.Explanation: Those who blush at the want of equity will not commit disgraceful acts through desire of the profit that may be gained
Kurals.Couplet: Through lust of gain, no deeds that retribution bring,Do they, who shrink with shame from every unjust thing
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , self-control, as something to be avoided

Kurals.Translation: For spiritual bliss who long For fleeting joy commit no wrong
Kurals.Explanation: Those who desire the higher pleasures (of heaven) will not act unjustly through desire of the trifling joy. (in this life.)
Kurals.Couplet: No deeds of ill, misled by base desire,Do they, whose souls to other joys aspire
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for spiritual bliss/higher pleasures, controlling desires for fleeting joy, choosing not to act unjustly

Kurals.Translation: The truth-knowers of sense-control Though in want covet not at all
Kurals.Explanation: The wise who have conquered their senses and are free from crime, will not covet (the things of others), with the thought "we are destitute."
Kurals.Couplet: Men who have conquered sense, with sight from sordid vision freed,Desire not other's goods, e'en in the hour of sorest need

Kurals.Translation: What is one's subtle wisdom worth If it deals ill with all on earth
Kurals.Explanation: What is the advantage of extensive and accurate knowledge if a man through covetousness act senselessly towards all ?
Kurals.Couplet: What gain, though lore refined of amplest reach he learn,His acts towards all mankind if covetous desire to folly turn

Kurals.Translation: Who seeks for grace on righteous path Suffers by evil covetous wealth
Kurals.Explanation: If he, who through desire of the virtue of kindness abides in the domestic state ie, the path in which it may be obtained, covet (the property of others) and think of evil methods (to obtain it), he will perish
Kurals.Couplet: Though, grace desiring, he in virtue's way stand strong,He's lost who wealth desires, and ponders deeds of wrong
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , or moral unease/remorse

Kurals.Translation: Shun the fruit of covetousness All its yield is inglorious
Kurals.Explanation: Desire not the gain of covetousness In the enjoyment of its fruits there is no glory
Kurals.Couplet: Seek not increase by greed of gain acquired;That fruit matured yields never good desired
Kurals.emotions: Contentment – Promoting satisfaction with what one has rather than desiring more., Caution – Warning against the negative consequences of greed or covetousness.

Kurals.Translation: The mark of lasting wealth is shown By not coveting others' own
Kurals.Explanation: If it is weighed, "what is the indestructibility of wealth," it is freedom from covetousness
Kurals.Couplet: What saves prosperity from swift decline?Absence of lust to make another's cherished riches thine

Kurals.Translation: Fortune seeks the just and wise Who are free from coveting vice
Kurals.Explanation: Lakshmi, knowing the manner (in which she may approach) will immediately come to those wise men who, knowing that it is virtue, covet not the property of others
Kurals.Couplet: Good fortune draws anigh in helpful time of need,To him who, schooled in virtue, guards his soul from greed
