Kurals.emotions: Contentment – Emphasized by being free from coveting and being satisfied with what one has., Wisdom – Linked to the virtue and knowledge referenced as desirable qualities. 3

Kurals.Translation: Desireless, greatness conquers all; Coveting misers ruined fall
Kurals.Explanation: To covet (the wealth of another) regardless of consequences will bring destruction That greatness (of mind) which covets not will give victory
Kurals.Couplet: From thoughtless lust of other's goods springs fatal ill,Greatness of soul that covets not shall triumph still
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Desire/Coveting, Desirelessness/Non-coveting,

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Greed, Guilt, Restraint.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Though a man from virtue strays, To keep from slander brings him praise
Kurals.Explanation: Though one do not even speak of virtue and live in sin, it will be well if it be said of him "he does not backbite."
Kurals.Couplet: Though virtuous words his lips speak not, and all his deeds are illIf neighbour he defame not, there's good within him still

Kurals.Translation: Who bite behind, and before smile Are worse than open traitors vile
Kurals.Explanation: To smile deceitfully (in another's presence) after having reviled him to his destruction (behind his back) is a greater evil than the commission of (every other) sin and the destruction of (every) virtue
Kurals.Couplet: Than he who virtue scorns, and evil deeds performs, more vile,Is he that slanders friend, then meets him with false smile

Kurals.Translation: Virtue thinks it better to die, Than live to backbite and to lie
Kurals.Explanation: Death rather than life will confer upon the deceitful backbiter the profit which (the treatises on) virtue point out
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis greater gain of virtuous good for man to die,Than live to slander absent friend, and falsely praise when nigh
Kurals.EmotionDetail: as an aspiration or value, towards deceit and backbiting,

Kurals.Translation: Though harsh you speak in one's presence Abuse is worse in his absence
Kurals.Explanation: Though you speak without kindness before another's face speak not in his absence words which regard not the evil subsequently resulting from it
Kurals.Couplet: In presence though unkindly words you speak, say notIn absence words whose ill result exceeds your thought

Kurals.Translation: Who turns to slander makes it plain His praise of virtue is in vain
Kurals.Explanation: The emptiness of that man's mind who (merely) praises virtue will be seen from the meanness of reviling another behind his back
Kurals.Couplet: The slanderous meanness that an absent friend defames,'This man in words owns virtue, not in heart,' proclaims
Kurals.EmotionDetail: towards those who slander or speak ill of others, of hypocrisy and insincerity,

Kurals.Translation: His failings will be found and shown, Who makes another's failings known
Kurals.Explanation: The character of the faults of that man who publishes abroad the faults of others will be sought out and published
Kurals.Couplet: Who on his neighbours' sins delights to dwell,The story of his sins, culled out with care, the world will tell

Kurals.Translation: By pleasing words who make not friends Sever their hearts by hostile trends
Kurals.Explanation: Those who know not to live in friendship with amusing conversation will by back-biting estrange even their relatives
Kurals.Couplet: With friendly art who know not pleasant words to say,Speak words that sever hearts, and drive choice friends away
Kurals.emotions: Alienation – The text discusses how negative actions (back-biting) can isolate a person even from close relations., Distrust – The mention of severed hearts and speaking ill highlights

Kurals.Translation: What will they not to strangers do Who bring their friends' defects to view?
Kurals.Explanation: What will those not do to strangers whose nature leads them to publish abroad the faults of their intimate friends ?
Kurals.Couplet: Whose nature bids them faults of closest friends proclaimWhat mercy will they show to other men's good name

Kurals.Translation: The world in mercy bears his load Who rants behind words untoward
Kurals.Explanation: The world through charity supports the weight of those who reproach others observing their absence
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis charity, I ween, that makes the earth sustain their loadWho, neighbours' absence watching, tales or slander tell abroad
Kurals.emotions: Compassion (or Mercy) – The text speaks of the world acting mercifully and charitably., Reproach (or Disapproval) – It mentions people who

Kurals.Translation: No harm would fall to any man If each his own defect could scan
Kurals.Explanation: If they observed their own faults as they observe the faults of others, would any evil happen to men ?
Kurals.Couplet: If each his own, as neighbours' faults would scan,Could any evil hap to living man
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with this text are:, Self-reflection, Humility, Compassion

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Betrayal, Disapproval, Restraint.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: With silly words who insults all Is held in contempt as banal
Kurals.Explanation: He who to the disgust of many speaks useless things will be despised by all
Kurals.Couplet: Words without sense, while chafe the wise,Who babbles, him will all despise
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , a strong form of dislike or disdain

Kurals.Translation: Vain talk before many is worse Than doing to friends deeds adverse
Kurals.Explanation: To speak useless things in the presence of many is a greater evil than to do unkind things towards friends
Kurals.Couplet: Words without sense, where many wise men hear, to pourThan deeds to friends ungracious done offendeth more
Kurals.emotions: Shame – Speaking vainly in public is implied to be something to be ashamed of

Kurals.Translation: The babbler's hasty lips proclaim That \"good-for-nothing\" is his name
Kurals.Explanation: That conversation in which a man utters forth useless things will say of him "he is without virtue."
Kurals.Couplet: Diffusive speech of useless words proclaimsA man who never righteous wisdom gains
Kurals.emotions: Disapproval – The language expresses a clear sense of condemnation toward vain or useless speech., Shame – The implication that being labeled “good-for-nothing” brings embarrassment or loss of reputation

Kurals.Translation: Vain words before an assembly Will make all gains and goodness flee
Kurals.Explanation: The words devoid of profit or pleasure which a man speaks will, being inconsistent with virtue,
Kurals.Couplet: Unmeaning, worthless words, said to the multitude,To none delight afford, and sever men from good

Kurals.Translation: Glory and grace will go away When savants silly nonsense say
Kurals.Explanation: If the good speak vain words their eminence and excellence will leave them
Kurals.Couplet: Gone are both fame and boasted excellence,When men of worth speak of words devoid of sense

Kurals.Translation: Call him a human chaff who prides Himself in weightless idle words
Kurals.Explanation: Call not him a man who parades forth his empty words Call him the chaff of men
Kurals.Couplet: Who makes display of idle words' inanity,Call him not man, -chaff of humanity
Kurals.emotions: Contempt – The text expresses disdain for those who use idle

Kurals.Translation: Let not men of worth vainly quack Even if they would roughly speak
Kurals.Explanation: Let the wise if they will, speak things without excellence; it will be well for them not to speak useless things
Kurals.Couplet: Let those who list speak things that no delight afford,'Tis good for men of worth to speak no idle word
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – Emphasizes the value of discernment in speech and the virtue of thoughtful communication., Restraint – Suggests self-control in avoiding unnecessary or vain words.

Kurals.Translation: The wise who weigh the worth refrain From words that have no grain and brain
Kurals.Explanation: The wise who seek after rare pleasures will not speak words that have not much weight in them
Kurals.Couplet: The wise who weigh the worth of every utterance,Speak none but words of deep significance

Kurals.Translation: The wise of spotless self-vision Slip not to silly words-mention
Kurals.Explanation: Those wise men who are without faults and are freed from ignorance will not even forgetfully speak things that profit not
Kurals.Couplet: The men of vision pure, from wildering folly free,Not e'en in thoughtless hour, speak words of vanity

Kurals.Translation: To purpose speak the fruitful word And never indulge in useless load
Kurals.Explanation: Speak what is useful, and speak not useless words
Kurals.Couplet: If speak you will, speak words that fruit afford,If speak you will, speak never fruitless word

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Wisdom, Regret, Self-Control.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Sinners fear not the pride of sin The worthy dread the ill within
Kurals.Explanation: Those who have experience of evil deeds will not fear, but the excellent will fear the pride of sin
Kurals.Couplet: With sinful act men cease to feel the dread of ill within,The excellent will dread the wanton pride of cherished sin
Kurals.EmotionDetail: dread, ,

Kurals.Translation: Since evil begets evil dire Fear ye evil more than fire
Kurals.Explanation: Because evil produces evil, therefore should evil be feared more than fire
Kurals.Couplet: Since evils new from evils ever grow,Evil than fire works out more dreaded woe

Kurals.Translation: The wisest of the wise are those Who injure not even their foes
Kurals.Explanation: So shalt thou wisdom's highest law, 'tis said, fulfil
Kurals.Couplet: Even to those that hate make no return of ill;So shalt thou wisdom's highest law, 'tis said, fulfil

Kurals.Translation: His ruin virtue plots who plans The ruin of another man's
Kurals.Explanation: Even though forgetfulness meditate not the ruin of another Virtue will meditate the ruin of him who thus meditates
Kurals.Couplet: Though good thy soul forget, plot not thy neighbour's fall,Thy plans shall 'virtue's Power' by ruin to thyself forestall
Kurals.EmotionDetail: fear/anxiety, ill will, anticipation of justice/consequence

Kurals.Translation: Who makes poverty plea for ill Shall reduce himself poorer still
Kurals.Explanation: Commit not evil, saying, "I am poor": if you do, you will become poorer still
Kurals.Couplet: Make not thy poverty a plea for ill;Thy evil deeds will make thee poorer still

Kurals.Translation: From wounding others let him refrain Who would from harm himself remain
Kurals.Explanation: Let him not do evil to others who desires not that sorrows should pursue him
Kurals.Couplet: What ranks as evil spare to do, if thou would'st shunAffliction sore through ill to thee by others done
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with the provided text are:, Fear (dread) – Concern about harm or negative consequences., Empathy – Considering others' feelings and well-being., Caution – Wariness about causing

Kurals.Translation: Men may escape other foes and live But sin its deadly blow will give
Kurals.Explanation: However great be the enmity men have incurred they may still live The enmity of sin will incessantly pursue and kill
Kurals.Couplet: From every enmity incurred there is to 'scape, a way;The wrath of evil deeds will dog men's steps, and slay
Kurals.emotions: Fear – The text emphasizes the "dread" and inevitability of harm from sin

Kurals.Translation: Ruin follows who evil do As shadow follows as they go
Kurals.Explanation: Destruction will dwell at the heels of those who commit evil even as their shadow that leaves them not
Kurals.Couplet: Man's shadow dogs his steps where'er he wends;Destruction thus on sinful deeds attends
Kurals.emotions: Fear – There is an underlying sense of dread and anxiety about the consequences of evil actions., Guilt – Implicit feeling that wrongdoing is followed by inescapable negative consequences.

Kurals.Translation: Let none who loves himself at all Think of evil however small
Kurals.Explanation: If a man love himself, let him not commit any sin however small
Kurals.Couplet: Beware, if to thyself thyself is dear,Lest thou to aught that ranks as ill draw near

Kurals.Translation: He is secure, know ye, from ills Who slips not right path to do evils
Kurals.Explanation: Know ye that he is freed from destruction who commits no evil, going to neither side of the right path
Kurals.Couplet: The man, to devious way of sin that never turned aside,From ruin rests secure, whatever ills betide
Kurals.EmotionDetail: dread of evil deeds and destruction, feeling safe when avoiding evil, carefulness in staying on the right path

Summary: Highlights fear of wrongdoing as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Fear, Caution, Dread.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Duty demands nothing in turn; How can the world recompense rain?
Kurals.Explanation: Benevolence seeks not a return. What does the world give back to the clouds ?
Kurals.Couplet: Duty demands no recompense; to clouds of heaven,By men on earth, what answering gift is given
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , Sense of Responsibility

Kurals.Translation: All the wealth that toils give Is meant to serve those who deserve
Kurals.Explanation: All the wealth acquired with perseverance by the worthy is for the exercise of benevolence
Kurals.Couplet: The worthy say, when wealth rewards their toil-spent hours,For uses of beneficence alone 'tis ours

Kurals.Translation: In heav'n and earth 'tis hard to find A greater good than being kind
Kurals.Explanation: It is difficult to obtain another good equal to benevolence either in this world or in that of the gods
Kurals.Couplet: To 'due beneficence' no equal good we know,Amid the happy gods, or in this world below
Kurals.emotions: Kindness, Admiration, Aspiration Explanation: - Kindness: The text centers around benevolence

Kurals.Translation: He lives who knows befitting act Others are deemed as dead in fact
Kurals.Explanation: He truly lives who knows (and discharges) the proper duties (of benevolence) He who knows them not will be reckoned among the dead
Kurals.Couplet: Who knows what's human life's befitting grace,He lives; the rest 'mongst dead men have their place
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – The emphasis on "knowing befitting act" and "knows the proper duties" evokes the value and emotion connected to wisdom or knowledge., Responsibility – The idea of

Kurals.Translation: The wealth that wise and kind do make Is like water that fills a lake
Kurals.Explanation: The wealth of that man of eminent knowledge who desires to exercise the benevolence approved of by the world, is like the full waters of a city-tank
Kurals.Couplet: The wealth of men who love the 'fitting way,' the truly wise,Is as when water fills the lake that village needs supplies
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – The text highlights being "wise" and possessing "eminent knowledge.", Benevolence – There is a strong focus on kindness

Kurals.Translation: Who plenty gets and plenty gives Is like town-tree teeming with fruits
Kurals.Explanation: The wealth of a man (possessed of the virtue) of benevolence is like the ripening of a fruitful tree in the midst of a town
Kurals.Couplet: A tree that fruits in th' hamlet's central mart,Is wealth that falls to men of liberal heart

Kurals.Translation: The wealth of a wide-hearted soul Is a herbal tree that healeth all
Kurals.Explanation: If wealth be in the possession of a man who has the great excellence (of benevolence), it is like a tree which as a medicine is an infallible cure for disease
Kurals.Couplet: Unfailing tree that healing balm distils from every part,Is ample wealth that falls to him of large and noble heart

Kurals.Translation: Though seers may fall on evil days Their sense of duty never strays
Kurals.Explanation: The wise who know what is duty will not scant their benevolence even when they are without wealth
Kurals.Couplet: E'en when resources fall, they weary not of 'kindness due,'-They to whom Duty's self appears in vision true
Kurals.emotions: Integrity — The adherence to moral and ethical principles

Kurals.Translation: The good man's poverty and grief Is want of means to give relief
Kurals.Explanation: The poverty of a benevolent man, is nothing but his inability to exercise the same
Kurals.Couplet: The kindly-hearted man is poor in this alone,When power of doing deeds of goodness he finds none
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – Empathy for the benevolent man's desire to help others., Frustration – The man's inability to offer relief despite his good intentions., Sadness – The grief

Kurals.Translation: By good if ruin comes across Sell yourself to save that loss
Kurals.Explanation: If it be said that loss will result from benevolence, such loss is worth being procured even by the sale of one's self
Kurals.Couplet: Though by 'beneficence,' the loss of all should come,'Twere meet man sold himself, and bought it with the sum
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Compassion,

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Benevolence, Admiration, Selflessness.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: To give the poor is charity The rest is loan and vanity
Kurals.Explanation: To give to the destitute is true charity All other gifts have the nature of (what is done for) a measured return
Kurals.Couplet: Call that a gift to needy men thou dost dispense,All else is void of good, seeking for recompense

Kurals.Translation: To beg is bad e'en from the good To give is good, were heaven forbid
Kurals.Explanation: To beg is evil, even though it were said that it is a good path (to heaven) To give is good, even though it were said that those who do so cannot obtain heaven
Kurals.Couplet: Though men declare it heavenward path, yet to receive is ill;Though upper heaven were not, to give is virtue still
Kurals.emotions: **Shame** – The idea that begging is "bad" or "evil" carries a sense of shame or humiliation related to asking for help., **Generosity** – The

Kurals.Translation: No pleading, \"I am nothing worth,\" But giving marks a noble birth
Kurals.Explanation: (Even in a low state) not to adopt the mean expedient of saying "I have nothing," but to give, is the characteristic of the mad of noble birth
Kurals.Couplet: 'I've nought' is ne'er the high-born man's reply;He gives to those who raise themselves that cry

Kurals.Translation: The cry for alms is painful sight Until the giver sees him bright
Kurals.Explanation: To see men begging from us in disagreeable, until we see their pleasant countenance
Kurals.Couplet: The suppliants' cry for aid yields scant delight,Until you see his face with grateful gladness bright
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – The mention of giving to those who cry for alms invokes empathy and a desire to help., Discomfort – The text highlights that seeing people beg is a "painful

Kurals.Translation: Higher's power which hunger cures Than that of penance which endures
Kurals.Explanation: The power of those who perform penance is the power of enduring hunger It is inferior to the power of those who remove the hunger (of others)
Kurals.Couplet: 'Mid devotees they're great who hunger's pangs sustain,Who hunger's pangs relieve a higher merit gain
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – The text highlights the value of alleviating others' suffering and hunger

Kurals.Translation: Drive from the poor their gnawing pains If room you seek to store your gains
Kurals.Explanation: The removal of the killing hunger of the poor is the place for one to lay up his wealth
Kurals.Couplet: Let man relieve the wasting hunger men endure;For treasure gained thus finds he treasure-house secure

Kurals.Translation: Who shares his food with those who need Hunger shall not harm his creed
Kurals.Explanation: The fiery disease of hunger shall never touch him who habitually distributes his food to others
Kurals.Couplet: Whose soul delights with hungry men to share his meal,The hand of hunger's sickness sore shall never feel

Kurals.Translation: The joy of give and take they lose Hard-hearted rich whose hoarding fails
Kurals.Explanation: Do the hard-eyed who lay up and lose their possessions not know the happiness which springs from the pleasure of giving ?
Kurals.Couplet: Delight of glad'ning human hearts with gifts do they not know.Men of unpitying eye, who hoard their wealth and lose it so
Kurals.EmotionDetail: from the happiness and pleasure of giving,

Kurals.Translation: Worse than begging is that boarding Alone what one's greed is hoarding
Kurals.Explanation: Solitary and unshared eating for the sake of filling up one's own riches is certainly much more
Kurals.Couplet: They keep their garners full, for self alone the board they spread;-'Tis greater pain, be sure, than begging daily bread
Kurals.emotions: Greed – The focus on "hoarding" and accumulating for oneself indicates greed., Selfishness – The act of not sharing and "solitary and unshared eating" shows

Kurals.Translation: Nothing is more painful than death Yet more is pain of giftless dearth
Kurals.Explanation: Nothing is more unpleasant than death: yet even that is pleasant where charity cannot be exercised
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis bitter pain to die, 'Tis worse to live.For him who nothing finds to give

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Generosity, Compassion, Humility.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: They gather fame who freely give The greatest gain for all that live
Kurals.Explanation: Give to the poor and live with praise There is no greater profit to man than that
Kurals.Couplet: See that thy life the praise of generous gifts obtain;Save this for living man exists no real gain

Kurals.Translation: The glory of the alms-giver Is praised aloud as popular
Kurals.Explanation: Whatsoever is spoken in the world will abide as praise upon that man who gives alms to the poor
Kurals.Couplet: The speech of all that speak agrees to crownThe men that give to those that ask, with fair renown

Kurals.Translation: Nothing else lasts on earth for e'er Saving high fame of the giver!
Kurals.Explanation: There is nothing that stands forth in the world imperishable, except fame, exalted in solitary greatness
Kurals.Couplet: Save praise alone that soars on high,Nought lives on earth that shall not die
Kurals.emotions: Aspiration – The desire for enduring recognition or greatness., Pride – The sense of honor and satisfaction derived from achieving high fame., Transience/Sadness – The awareness and

Kurals.Translation: From hailing gods heavens will cease To hail the men of lasting praise
Kurals.Explanation: If one has acquired extensive fame within the limits of this earth, the world of the Gods will no longer praise those sages who have attained that world
Kurals.Couplet: If men do virtuous deeds by world-wide ample glory crowned,The heavens will cease to laud the sage for other gifts renowned
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – for those who have acquired extensive fame and praise., Aspiration – the desire to achieve lasting recognition and greatness., Reverence – respect for individuals whose

Kurals.Translation: Fame in fall and life in death Are rare but for the soulful worth
Kurals.Explanation: Prosperity to the body of fame, resulting in poverty to the body of flesh and the stability to the former arising from the death of the latter, are achievable only by the wise
Kurals.Couplet: Loss that is gain, and death of life's true bliss fulfilled,Are fruits which only wisdom rare can yield

Kurals.Translation: Be born with fame if birth you want If not of birth you must not vaunt
Kurals.Explanation: If you are born (in this world), be born with qualities conductive to fame From those who are destitute of them it will be better not to be born
Kurals.Couplet: If man you walk the stage, appear adorned with glory's grace;Save glorious you can shine, 'twere better hide your face
Kurals.emotions: Ambition – The desire for fame and recognition is emphasized., Pride – There is value placed on being born with qualities worthy of fame., Inferiority – A sense of un

Kurals.Translation: Why grieve at those who blame the shame Of those who cannot live in fame?
Kurals.Explanation: Why do those who cannot live with praise, grieve those who despise them, instead of grieving themselves for their own inability
Kurals.Couplet: If you your days will spend devoid of goodly fame,When men despise, why blame them? You've yourself to blame

Kurals.Translation: To men on earth it is a shame Not to beget the child of fame
Kurals.Explanation: Not to beget fame will be esteemed a disgrace by the wise in this world
Kurals.Couplet: Fame is virtue's child, they say; if, then,You childless live, you live the scorn of men

Kurals.Translation: The land will shrink in yield if men O'erburden it without renown
Kurals.Explanation: The ground which supports a body without fame will diminish in its rich produce
Kurals.Couplet: The blameless fruits of fields' increase will dwindle down,If earth the burthen bear of men without renown
Kurals.EmotionDetail: the sense of being unrecognized or living without honor, the desire for fame or renown,

Kurals.Translation: They live who live without blemish The blameful ones do not flurish
Kurals.Explanation: Those live who live without disgrace Those who live without fame live not
Kurals.Couplet: Who live without reproach, them living men we deem;Who live without renown, live not, though living men they seem
Kurals.emotions: Pride – The text emphasizes living without disgrace

Summary: Highlights honour and lasting fame as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Pride, Shame, Generosity.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The wealth of wealth is wealth of grace Earthly wealth e'en the basest has
Kurals.Explanation: The wealth of kindness is wealth of wealth, in as much as the wealth of property is possessed by the basest of men
Kurals.Couplet: Wealth 'mid wealth is wealth 'kindliness';Wealth of goods the vilest too possess

Kurals.Translation: Seek by sound ways good compassion; All faiths mark that for-salvation
Kurals.Explanation: (Stand) in the good path, consider, and be kind. Even considering according to the conflicting tenets of the different sects, kindness will be your best aid, (in the acquisition of heavenly bliss.)
Kurals.Couplet: The law of 'grace' fulfil, by methods good due trial made,Though many systems you explore, this is your only aid

Kurals.Translation: The hearts of mercy shall not go Into dark worlds of gruesome woe
Kurals.Explanation: They will never enter the world of darkness and wretchedness whose minds are the abode of kindness
Kurals.Couplet: They in whose breast a 'gracious kindliness' resides,See not the gruesome world, where darkness drear abides

Kurals.Translation: His soul is free from dread of sins Whose mercy serveth all beings
Kurals.Explanation: (The wise) say that the evils, which his soul would dread, will never come upon the man who exercises kindness and protects the life (of other creatures)
Kurals.Couplet: Who for undying souls of men provides with gracious zeal,In his own soul the dreaded guilt of sin shall never feel
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , or Serenity

Kurals.Translation: The wide wind-fed world witness bears: Men of mercy meet not sorrows
Kurals.Explanation: This great rich earth over which the wind blows, is a witness that sorrow never comes upon the kindhearted
Kurals.Couplet: The teeming earth's vast realm, round which the wild winds blow,Is witness, men of 'grace' no woeful want shall know

Kurals.Translation: Who grace forsake and graceless act The former loss and woes forget
Kurals.Explanation: (The wise) say that those who neglect kindness and practise cruelties, neglected virtue (in their former birth), and forgot (the sorrows which they must suffer.)
Kurals.Couplet: Gain of true wealth oblivious they eschew,Who 'grace' forsake, and graceless actions do
Kurals.EmotionDetail: highlighted in both the chapter title and the underlying message, implied through the context of neglecting kindness and forgetting past sorrows,

Kurals.Translation: This world is not for weathless ones That world is not for graceless swines
Kurals.Explanation: As this world is not for those who are without wealth, so that world is not for those who are without kindness
Kurals.Couplet: As to impoverished men this present world is not;The 'graceless' in you world have neither part nor lot

Kurals.Translation: The wealthless may prosper one day; The graceless never bloom agay
Kurals.Explanation: Those who are without wealth may, at some future time, become prosperous; those who are destitute of kindness are utterly destitute; for them there is no change
Kurals.Couplet: Who lose the flower of wealth, when seasons change, again may bloom;Who lose 'benevolence', lose all; nothing can change their doom
Kurals.emotions: Hope – The possibility that those without wealth may prosper one day conveys hope., Compassion – The emphasis on kindness and the consequences of its absence centers on compassion., Resignation/Sadness – The idea that those without kindness will

Kurals.Translation: Like Truth twisted by confused mind Wisdom is vain in hearts unkind
Kurals.Explanation: If you consider, the virtue of him who is without kindness is like the perception of the true being by him who is without wisdom
Kurals.Couplet: When souls unwise true wisdom's mystic vision see,The 'graceless' man may work true works of charity
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Indifference,

Kurals.Translation: Think how you feel before the strong When to the feeble you do wrong
Kurals.Explanation: When a man is about to rush upon those who are weaker than himself, let him remember how he has stood (trembling) before those who are stronger than himself
Kurals.Couplet: When weaker men you front with threat'ning brow,Think how you felt in presence of some stronger foe

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Compassion, Kindness, Humility.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: What graciousness can one command who feeds his flesh by flesh gourmand
Kurals.Explanation: How can he be possessed of kindness, who to increase his own flesh, eats the flesh of other creatures
Kurals.Couplet: How can the wont of 'kindly grace' to him be known,Who other creatures' flesh consumes to feed his own
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – The text questions the presence of kindness and empathy in those who consume the flesh of other creatures

Kurals.Translation: The thriftless have no property And flesh-eaters have no pity
Kurals.Explanation: As those possess no property who do not take care of it, so those possess no kindness who feed on flesh
Kurals.Couplet: No use of wealth have they who guard not their estate;No use of grace have they with flesh who hunger sate

Kurals.Translation: Who wields a steel is steel-hearted Who tastes body is hard-hearted
Kurals.Explanation: Like the (murderous) mind of him who carries a weapon (in his hand), the mind of him who feasts with pleasure on the body of another (creature), has no regard for goodness
Kurals.Couplet: Like heart of them that murderous weapons bear, his mind,Who eats of savoury meat, no joy in good can find
Kurals.EmotionDetail: lack of empathy, deliberate causing of suffering, emotional toughness or insensitivity

Kurals.Translation: If merciless it is to kill, To kill and eat is disgraceful
Kurals.Explanation: If it be asked what is kindness and what its opposite, the answer would be preservation and destruction of life; and therefore it is not right to feed on the flesh (obtained by taking away life)
Kurals.Couplet: 'What's grace, or lack of grace'? 'To kill' is this, that 'not to kill';To eat dead flesh can never worthy end fulfil

Kurals.Translation: Off with flesh; a life you save The eater hell's mouth shall not waive!
Kurals.Explanation: Not to eat flesh contributes to the continuance of life; therefore if a man eat flesh, hell will not open its mouth (to let him escape out, after he has once fallen in)
Kurals.Couplet: If flesh you eat not, life's abodes unharmed remain;Who eats, hell swallows him, and renders not again
Kurals.emotions: **Fear** – The reference to "hell's mouth" and its unforgiving nature evokes fear of punishment and consequences., **Guilt** – The notion that eating flesh leads to

Kurals.Translation: None would kill and sell the flesh For eating it if they don't wish
Kurals.Explanation: If the world does not destroy life for the purpose of eating, then no one would sell flesh for the sake of money
Kurals.Couplet: 'We eat the slain,' you say, by us no living creatures die;Who'd kill and sell, I pray, if none came there the flesh to buy

Kurals.Translation: From eating flesh men must abstain If they but feel the being's pain
Kurals.Explanation: If men should come to know that flesh is nothing but the unclean ulcer of a body, let them abstain from eating it
Kurals.Couplet: With other beings' ulcerous wounds their hunger they appease;If this they felt, desire to eat must surely cease
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – Empathy for the pain and suffering of living beings., Disgust – Revulsion at the idea of flesh being an “unclean ulcer” of a body.

Kurals.Translation: Whose mind from illusion is freed Refuse on lifeless flesh to feed
Kurals.Explanation: The wise, who have freed themselves from mental delusion, will not eat the flesh which has been severed from an animal
Kurals.Couplet: Whose souls the vision pure and passionless perceive,Eat not the bodies men of life bereave
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – Concern and empathy for the suffering of animals., Wisdom – A sense of clarity and higher understanding

Kurals.Translation: Not to-kill-and-eat, truly Excels thousand pourings of ghee!
Kurals.Explanation: Not to kill and eat (the flesh of) an animal, is better than the pouring forth of ghee etc, in a thousand sacrifices
Kurals.Couplet: Than thousand rich oblations, with libations rare,Better the flesh of slaughtered beings not to share
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – The text emphasizes not killing animals

Kurals.Translation: All lives shall lift their palms to him Who eats not flesh nor kills with whim
Kurals.Explanation: All creatures will join their hands together, and worship him who has never taken away life, nor eaten flesh
Kurals.Couplet: Who slays nought,- flesh rejects- his feet beforeAll living things with clasped hands adore

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Cruelty, Hardness Lack Of Empathy, Disgust.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Pains endure; pain not beings This is the type of true penance
Kurals.Explanation: The nature of religious discipline consists, in the endurance (by the ascetic) of the sufferings which it brings on himself, and in abstaining from giving pain to others
Kurals.Couplet: To bear due penitential pains, while no offenceHe causes others, is the type of 'penitence'
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Endurance, Compassion, Self-discipline

Kurals.Translation: Penance is fit for penitents Not for him who in vain pretends
Kurals.Explanation: Austerities can only be borne, and their benefits enjoyed, by those who have practised them (in a former birth); it will be useless for those who have not done so, to attempt to practise them (now)
Kurals.Couplet: To 'penitents' sincere avails their 'penitence';Where that is not, 'tis but a vain pretence
Kurals.emotions: Regret — The concept of penance and the mention of "penitents" imply an underlying feeling of regret for past actions., Unworthiness — The idea that penance is

Kurals.Translation: Is it to true penitent's aid, That others austere path avoid?
Kurals.Explanation: It is to provide food etc, for the ascetics who have abandoned (the desire of earthly possessions) that other persons have forgotten (to practise) austerity ?
Kurals.Couplet: Have other men forgotten 'penitence' who striveTo earn for penitents the things by which they live
Kurals.emotions: Reverence – for ascetics who renounce worldly possessions., Guilt – for others who have forgotten to practice austerity (penance)., Compassion – in providing aid or

Kurals.Translation: In penance lies the power to save The friends and foil the foe and knave
Kurals.Explanation: If (the ascetic) desire the destruction of his enemies, or the aggrandizement of his friends, it will be effected by (the power of) his austerities
Kurals.Couplet: Destruction to his foes, to friends increase of joyThe 'penitent' can cause, if this his thoughts employ
Kurals.emotions: Determination – Reflecting the resolute commitment involved in penance and austerities., Hope – The expectation that these acts will lead to saving friends or achieving a desired outcome. 3

Kurals.Translation: What they wish as they wish is won Here hence by men penance is done
Kurals.Explanation: Religious dislipline is practised in this world, because it secures the attainment of whatever one may wish to enjoy (in the world to come)
Kurals.Couplet: That what they wish may, as they wish, be won,By men on earth are works of painful 'penance' done
Kurals.emotions: Hope – Anticipation of attaining desires or rewards through penance., Devotion – Commitment to religious discipline and practice., Aspiration – Yearning for better outcomes or spiritual

Kurals.Translation: Who do penance achieve their aim Others desire-rid themselves harm
Kurals.Explanation: Those discharge their duty who perform austerities; all others accomplish their own destruction, through the entanglement of the desire (of riches and sensual pleasure)
Kurals.Couplet: Who works of 'penance' do, their end attain,Others in passion's net enshared, toil but in vain
Kurals.emotions: **Discipline** — Reflects the commitment and self-control required for doing penance and performing austerities., **Desire** — Highlights longing or craving for riches and sensual pleasure

Kurals.Translation: Pure and bright gets the gold in fire; and so the life by pain austere
Kurals.Explanation: Just as gold is purified as heated in the fire, will those shine, who have endured the burning of pain (in frequent austerities)
Kurals.Couplet: The hotter glows the fining fire, the gold the brighter shines;The pain of penitence, like fire, the soul of man refines

Kurals.Translation: He worship wins from every soul Who Master is by soul control
Kurals.Explanation: All other creatures will worship him who has attained the control of his own soul
Kurals.Couplet: Who gains himself in utter self-control,Him worships every other living soul
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , a sense of inner strength/pride,

Kurals.Translation: They can even defy death Who get by penance godly strenth
Kurals.Explanation: Those who have attained the power which religious discipline confers, will be able also to pass the limit of Yama, (the God of death)
Kurals.Couplet: E'en over death the victory he may gain,If power by penance won his soul obtain
Kurals.emotions: Awe – At the extraordinary power attained through penance., Reverence – For the strength and discipline gained through religious practices., Hope – In the possibility of transcending even death

Kurals.Translation: Many are poor and few are rich For they care not for penance much
Kurals.Explanation: Because there are few who practise austerity and many who do not, there are many destitute and few rich in this world
Kurals.Couplet: The many all things lack! The cause is plain,The 'penitents' are few The many shun such pain
Kurals.EmotionDetail: towards penance/austerity, for the lack of penance, to the resulting inequality or reality

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Endurance, Compassion, Self-Discipline.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Elements five of feigned life Of a sly hypocrite within laugh
Kurals.Explanation: The five elements (of his body) will laugh within him at the feigned conduct of the deceitful minded man
Kurals.Couplet: Who with deceitful mind in false way walks of covert sin,The five-fold elements his frame compose, decide within
Kurals.EmotionDetail: as an emotion of self-deception or insincerity, the deceitful person is subtly exposed or ridiculed

Kurals.Translation: Of what avail are sky-high shows When guild the conscience gnaws and knows
Kurals.Explanation: What avails an appearance (of sanctity) high as heaven, if his mind suffers (the indulgence) of conscious sin
Kurals.Couplet: What gain, though virtue's semblance high as heaven his fame exalt,If heart dies down through sense of self-detected fault

Kurals.Translation: Vaunting sainthood while week within Seems a grazer with tiger skin
Kurals.Explanation: The assumed appearance of power, by a man who has no power (to restrain his senses and perform austerity), is like a cow feeding on grass covered with a tiger's skin
Kurals.Couplet: As if a steer should graze wrapped round with tiger's skin,Is show of virtuous might when weakness lurks within
Kurals.emotions: Hypocrisy – The act of pretending to possess virtues or morality that one actually lacks., Deception – The intent to mislead others by adopting a false appearance of strength or sain

Kurals.Translation: Sinning in saintly show is like Fowlers in ambush birds to strike
Kurals.Explanation: He who hides himself under the mask of an ascetic and commits sins, like a sportsman who conceals himself in the thicket to catch birds
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis as a fowler, silly birds to snare, in thicket lurksWhen, clad in stern ascetic garb, one secret evil works
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 emotions associated with the text:, Hypocrisy – The text discusses the act of pretending to be virtuous while secretly committing wrongs., Deception – It describes someone hiding their true intentions and actions

Kurals.Translation: Who false within but freedom feign Shall moan \"What have we done\" with pain
Kurals.Explanation: The false conduct of those who say they have renounced all desire will one day bring them sorrows that will make them cry out, "Oh! what have we done, what have we done."
Kurals.Couplet: 'Our souls are free,' who say, yet practise evil secretly,'What folly have we wrought!' by many shames o'er-whelmed, shall cry
Kurals.emotions: Guilt – The regret and self-reproach felt when realizing one’s own hypocrisy or wrongdoing., Sorrow – The deep sadness that arises from the consequences of false actions. 3

Kurals.Translation: Vilest is he who seems a saint Cheating the world without restraint
Kurals.Explanation: Amongst living men there are none so hard-hearted as those who without to saking (desire) in their heart, falsely take the appearance of those who have forsaken (it)
Kurals.Couplet: In mind renouncing nought, in speech renouncing every tie,Who guileful live,- no men are found than these of 'harder eye'
Kurals.EmotionDetail: as an emotion: duplicity/insincerity, towards deceitful behavior,

Kurals.Translation: Berry-red is his outward view, Black like its nose his inward hue
Kurals.Explanation: (The world) contains persons whose outside appears (as fair) as the (red) berry of the Abrus, but whose inside is as black as the nose of that berry
Kurals.Couplet: Outward, they shine as 'kunri' berry's scarlet bright;Inward, like tip of 'kunri' bead, as black as night
Kurals.emotions: Distrust – The contrast between outward appearance and inward reality evokes suspicion and caution., Disappointment – The revelation that appearances can be deceiving brings about a sense of letdown

Kurals.Translation: Filthy in mind some bathe in streams Hiding sins in showy extremes
Kurals.Explanation: There are many men of masked conduct, who perform their ablutions, and (make a show) of
Kurals.Couplet: Many wash in hollowed waters, living lives of hidden shame;Foul in heart, yet high upraised of men in virtuous fame
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 emotions associated with the text:, Hypocrisy – The text discusses people who display outward morality while hiding inner corruption., Disgust – There is a sense of revulsion toward the insincerity and "filth"

Kurals.Translation: Know men by acts and not by forms Strait arrow kills, bent lute but charms
Kurals.Explanation: As, in its use, the arrow is crooked, and the curved lute is straight, so by their deeds, (and not by their appearance) let (the uprightness or crookedness of) men be estimated
Kurals.Couplet: Cruel is the arrow straight, the crooked lute is sweet,Judge by their deeds the many forms of men you meet
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – The text emphasizes understanding deeper truths about people beyond surface appearances

Kurals.Translation: No balding nor tangling the hair! Abstain from condemned acts with care
Kurals.Explanation: There is no need of a shaven crown, nor of tangled hair, if a man abstain from those deeds which the wise have condemned
Kurals.Couplet: What's the worth of shaven head or tresses long,If you shun what all the world condemns as wrong
Kurals.emotions: Integrity – Emphasizing the importance of moral uprightness over outward appearances., Wisdom – Valuing the guidance and judgment of the wise., Mindfulness – Encouraging careful abst

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Hypocrisy, Shame, Actions.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Let him who would reproachless be From all frauds guard his conscience free
Kurals.Explanation: Let him, who desires not to be despised, keep his mind from (the desire of) defrauding another of the smallest thing
Kurals.Couplet: Who seeks heaven's joys, from impious levity secure,Let him from every fraud preserve his spirit pure
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Desire for moral uprightness and honesty, Value placed on maintaining one's reputation and being above reproach,

Kurals.Translation: \"We will by fraud win other's wealth\" Even this thought is sin and stealth
Kurals.Explanation: Even the thought (of sin) is sin; think not then of crafiily stealing the property of another
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis sin if in the mind man but thought conceive;'By fraud I will my neighbour of his wealth bereave.'

Kurals.Translation: The gain by fraud may overflow But swift to ruin it shall go
Kurals.Explanation: The property, which is acquired by fraud, will entirely perish, even while it seems to increase
Kurals.Couplet: The gain that comes by fraud, although it seems to growWith limitless increase, to ruin swift shall go
Kurals.emotions: Caution — The text warns against fraudulent behavior

Kurals.Translation: The fruit that fraud and greed obtain Shall end in endless grief and pain
Kurals.Explanation: The eager desire of defrauding others will, when it brings forth its fruit, produce undying sorrow
Kurals.Couplet: The lust inveterate of fraudful gain,Yields as its fruit undying pain

Kurals.Translation: Love and Grace are not their worth Who watch to waylay dozer's wealth
Kurals.Explanation: The study of kindness and the exercise of benevolence is not with those who watch for another's forgetfulness, though desire of his property
Kurals.Couplet: 'Grace' is not in their thoughts, nor know they kind affection's power,Who neighbour's goods desire, and watch for his unguarded hour
Kurals.emotions: Greed – The reference to those who "watch for another's forgetfulness

Kurals.Translation: They cannot walk in measured bounds who crave and have covetous ends
Kurals.Explanation: They cannot walk steadfastly, according to rule, who eagerly desire to defraud others
Kurals.Couplet: They cannot walk restrained in wisdom's measured bound,In whom inveterate lust of fraudful gain is found

Kurals.Translation: Men of measured wisdom shun Black art of fraud and what it won
Kurals.Explanation: That black-knowledge which is called fraud, is not in those who desire that greatness which is called rectitude
Kurals.Couplet: Practice of fraud's dark cunning arts they shun,Who long for power by 'measured wisdom' won

Kurals.Translation: Virtue abides in righteous hearts Into minds of frauds deceit darts
Kurals.Explanation: Deceit dwells in the mind of those who are conversant with fraud, even as virtue in the minds of those who are conversant with rectitude
Kurals.Couplet: As virtue dwells in heart that 'measured wisdom' gains;Deceit in hearts of fraudful men established reigns
Kurals.EmotionDetail: or Righteousness, or Suspicion, for virtue

Kurals.Translation: They perish in their perfidy Who know nothing but pilfery
Kurals.Explanation: Those, who are acquainted with nothing but fraud, will perish in the very commission of transgression
Kurals.Couplet: Who have no lore save that which fraudful arts supply,Acts of unmeasured vice committing straightway die
Kurals.emotions: Condemnation – The text expresses strong disapproval of those who commit fraud and pilfery., Warning/Fear – There is a cautionary tone about the consequences

Kurals.Translation: Even the body rejects thieves; The honest men, heaven receives
Kurals.Explanation: Even their body will fail the fraudulent; but even the world of the gods will not fail those who are free from fraud
Kurals.Couplet: The fraudful forfeit life and being here below;Who fraud eschew the bliss of heavenly beings know
Kurals.emotions: Honesty – The text highlights the virtue of being honest and free from fraud., Integrity – It emphasizes the importance of moral uprightness and being genuine., Rejection

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Integrity, Self-Respect, Honesty.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Foes fear not who for toddy craze The addicts daily their glory lose
Kurals.Explanation: Those who always thirst after drink will neither inspire fear (in others) nor retain the light (of their fame)
Kurals.Couplet: Who love the palm's intoxicating juice, each day,No rev'rence they command, their glory fades away
Kurals.emotions: Shame – The text speaks of losing one's glory and light

Kurals.Translation: Drink not liquor; but let them drink Whom with esteem the wise won't think
Kurals.Explanation: Let no liquor be drunk; if it is desired, let it be drunk by those who care not for esteem of the great
Kurals.Couplet: Drink not inebriating draught Let him count well the costWho drinks, by drinking, all good men's esteem is lost

Kurals.Translation: The drunkard's joy pains ev'n mother's face How vile must it look for the wise?
Kurals.Explanation: Intoxication is painful even in the presence of (one's) mother; what will it not then be in that of the wise ?
Kurals.Couplet: The drunkard's joy is sorrow to his mother's eyes;What must it be in presence of the truly wise

Kurals.Translation: Good shame turns back from him ashamed Who is guilty of wine condemned
Kurals.Explanation: The fair maid of modesty will turn her back on those who are guilty of the great and abominable crime of drunkenness
Kurals.Couplet: Shame, goodly maid, will turn her back for aye on themWho sin the drunkard's grievous sin, that all condemn

Kurals.Translation: To pay and drink and lose the sense Is nothing but rank ignorance
Kurals.Explanation: To give money and purchase unconsciousness is the result of one's ignorance of (one's own actions)
Kurals.Couplet: With gift of goods who self-oblivion buys,Is ignorant of all that man should prize
Kurals.emotions: Regret – There is a sense of remorse implied for losing awareness and acting unwisely., Disapproval – The text expresses strong criticism against drinking and the resulting ignorance.

Kurals.Translation: They take poison who take toddy And doze ev'n like a dead body
Kurals.Explanation: They that sleep resemble the deed; (likewise) they that drink are no other than poison-eaters
Kurals.Couplet: Sleepers are as the dead, no otherwise they seem;Who drink intoxicating draughts, they poison quaff, we deem
Kurals.emotions: Warning/Fear – The text warns about the dangers of drinking liquor

Kurals.Translation: The secret drunkards' senses off Make the prying public laugh
Kurals.Explanation: Those who always intoxicate themselves by a private (indulgence in) drink; will have their secrets detected and laughed at by their fellow-townsmen
Kurals.Couplet: Who turn aside to drink, and droop their heavy eye,Shall be their townsmen's jest, when they the fault espy
Kurals.emotions: Shame – The text discusses the exposure and ridicule of private indulgence

Kurals.Translation: Don't say I'm not a drunkard hard The hidden fraud is known abroad
Kurals.Explanation: Let (the drunkard) give up saying "I have never drunk"; (for) the moment (he drinks) he will simply betray his former attempt to conceal
Kurals.Couplet: No more in secret drink, and then deny thy hidden fraud;What in thy mind lies hid shall soon be known abroad
Kurals.emotions: Shame – The text refers to the attempt to conceal a behavior
Kurals.EmotionDetail: drinking

Kurals.Translation: Can torch search one in water sunk? Can reason reach the raving drunk?
Kurals.Explanation: Reasoning with a drunkard is like going under water with a torch in search of a drowned man
Kurals.Couplet: Like him who, lamp in hand, would seek one sunk beneath the waveIs he who strives to sober drunken man with reasonings grave
Kurals.emotions: **Frustration** – There is a sense of futility and exasperation when trying to reason with someone who is drunk., **Helplessness** – The analogy of

Kurals.Translation: The sober seeing the drunkard's plight On selves can't they feel same effect?
Kurals.Explanation: When (a drunkard) who is sober sees one who is not, it looks as if he remembered not the evil effects of his (own) drink
Kurals.Couplet: When one, in sober interval, a drunken man espies,Does he not think, 'Such is my folly in my revelries'

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Regret, Disapproval, Shame.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: If \"What is truth\"? the question be, It is to speak out evil-free
Kurals.Explanation: Truth is the speaking of such words as are free from the least degree of evil (to others)
Kurals.Couplet: You ask, in lips of men what 'truth' may be;'Tis speech from every taint of evil free

Kurals.Translation: E'en falsehood may for truth suffice, When good it brings removing vice
Kurals.Explanation: Even falsehood has the nature of truth, if it confer a benefit that is free from fault
Kurals.Couplet: Falsehood may take the place of truthful word,If blessing, free from fault, it can afford
Kurals.emotions: Justification – The text expresses the emotional reasoning for accepting actions (even falsehood) when they lead to good outcomes and the absence of harm., Acceptance – There’s an

Kurals.Translation: Let not a man knowingly lie; Conscience will scorch and make him sigh
Kurals.Explanation: Let not a man knowingly tell a lie; for after he has told the lie, his mind will burn him (with the memory of his guilt)
Kurals.Couplet: Speak not a word which false thy own heart knowsSelf-kindled fire within the false one's spirit glows

Kurals.Translation: He lives in loving hearts of all Who serves the Truth serene in soul
Kurals.Explanation: He who, in his conduct, preserves a mind free from deceit, will dwell in the minds of all men
Kurals.Couplet: True to his inmost soul who lives,- enshrinedHe lives in souls of all mankind

Kurals.Translation: To speak the truth from heart sincere Is more than giving and living austere
Kurals.Explanation: He, who speaks truth with all his heart, is superior to those who make gifts and practise austerities
Kurals.Couplet: Greater is he who speaks the truth with full consenting mindThan men whose lives have penitence and charity combined

Kurals.Translation: Not to lie brings all the praise All virtues from Truth arise
Kurals.Explanation: There is no praise like the praise of never uttering a falsehood: without causing any suffering, it will lead to every virtue
Kurals.Couplet: No praise like that of words from falsehood free;This every virtue yields spontaneously
Kurals.EmotionDetail: a sense of honesty and moral uprightness, being esteemed and valued by others, a sense of inner calm and absence of guilt or suffering

Kurals.Translation: Lie not lie not Naught else you need All virtues are in Truth indeed
Kurals.Explanation: If a man has the power to abstain from falsehood, it will be well with him, even though he practise no other virtue
Kurals.Couplet: If all your life be utter truth, the truth alone,'Tis well, though other virtuous acts be left undone

Kurals.Translation: Water makes you pure outward Truth renders you pure inward
Kurals.Explanation: Purity of body is produced by water and purity of mind by truthfulness
Kurals.Couplet: Outward purity the water will bestow;Inward purity from truth alone will flow

Kurals.Translation: All lights are not lights for the wise; Truth light is light bright like Sun-light
Kurals.Explanation: All lamps of nature are not lamps; the lamp of truth is the lamp of the wise
Kurals.Couplet: Every lamp is not a lamp in wise men's sight;That's the lamp with truth's pure radiance bright
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – The text emphasizes discernment and the value of true knowledge (truth light) over superficial appearances., Reverence – There is respect and admiration for the deeper

Kurals.Translation: Of all the things we here have seen Nothing surpasses Truth serene!
Kurals.Explanation: Amidst all that we have seen (described) as real (excellence), there is nothing so good as truthfulness
Kurals.Couplet: Of all good things we've scanned with studious care,There's nought that can with truthfulness compare
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for truthfulness, ,

Summary: Highlights truth as an eternal value as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Integrity, Respect, Honesty.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Anger against the weak is wrong It is futile against the strong
Kurals.Explanation: He restrains his anger who restrains it when it can injure; when it cannot injure, what does it matter whether he restrain it, or not ?
Kurals.Couplet: Where thou hast power thy angry will to work, thy wrath restrain;Where power is none, what matter if thou check or give it rein
Kurals.emotions: Anger – The chapter and text revolve around the experience and expression of anger., Restraint – The focus is on the ability to control or suppress one’s anger.

Kurals.Translation: Vain is wrath against men of force Against the meek it is still worse
Kurals.Explanation: Anger is bad, even when it cannot injure; when it can injure; there is no greater evil
Kurals.Couplet: Where power is none to wreak thy wrath, wrath importent is ill;Where thou hast power thy will to work, 'tis greater, evil still
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Helplessness,

Kurals.Translation: Off with wrath with any one It is the source of sin and pain
Kurals.Explanation: Forget anger towards every one, as fountains of evil spring from it
Kurals.Couplet: If any rouse thy wrath, the trespass straight forget;For wrath an endless train of evils will beget
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , from letting go of anger

Kurals.Translation: Is there a foe like harmful ire Which kills the smile and joyful cheer?
Kurals.Explanation: What other foe to man works such annoy?
Kurals.Couplet: Wrath robs the face of smiles, the heart of joy,What other foe to man works such annoy

Kurals.Translation: Thyself to save, from wrath away! If not thyself the wrath will slay
Kurals.Explanation: If a man would guard himself, let him guard against anger; if he do not guard it, anger will kill him
Kurals.Couplet: If thou would'st guard thyself, guard against wrath alway;'Gainst wrath who guards not, him his wrath shall slay
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or fear for one’s own safety, or wariness

Kurals.Translation: Friend-killer is the fatal rage It burns the helpful kinship-barge
Kurals.Explanation: The fire of anger will burn up even the pleasant raft of friendship
Kurals.Couplet: Wrath, the fire that slayeth whose draweth near,Will burn the helpful 'raft' of kindred dear

Kurals.Translation: The wrath-lover to doom is bound Like failless-hand that strikes the ground
Kurals.Explanation: Destruction will come upon him who ragards anger as a good thing, as surely as the hand of him who strikes the ground will not fail
Kurals.Couplet: The hand that smites the earth unfailing feels the sting;So perish they who nurse their wrath as noble thing
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , fear/consequence,

Kurals.Translation: Save thy soul from burning ire Though tortured like the touch of fire
Kurals.Explanation: Though one commit things against you as painful (to bear) as if a bundle of fire had been thrust upon you, it will be well, to refrain, if possible, from anger
Kurals.Couplet: Though men should work thee woe, like touch of tongues of fire'Tis well if thou canst save thy soul from burning ire
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , or Restraint

Kurals.Translation: Wishes he gains as he wishes If man refrains from rage vicious!
Kurals.Explanation: If a man never indulges anger in his heart, he will at once obtain whatever he has thought of
Kurals.Couplet: If man his soul preserve from wrathful fires,He gains with that whate'er his soul desires
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with the given text are:, Calmness (or Serenity) – reflecting the state of refraining from anger., Desire (or Aspiration) – relating to the wishes or goals a person hopes to

Kurals.Translation: Dead are they who are anger-fed Saints are they from whom wrath has fled
Kurals.Explanation: Those, who give way to excessive anger, are no better than dead men; but those, who are freed from it, are equal to those who are freed (from death)
Kurals.Couplet: Men of surpassing wrath are like the men who've passed away;Who wrath renounce, equals of all-renouncing sages they
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Peace, or Respect

Summary: Highlights the need to restrain anger as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Anger, Regret, Pain.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The pure by faith mean pain to none Though princely wealth by that is won
Kurals.Explanation: It is the determination of the spotless not to cause sorrow to others, although they could (by so causing) obtain the wealth which confers greatness
Kurals.Couplet: Though ill to neighbour wrought should glorious pride of wealth secure,No ill to do is fixed decree of men in spirit pure
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – Concern for not causing pain or sorrow to others., Integrity – Commitment to moral principles

Kurals.Translation: The spotless hearts seek not revenge Though Malice does the worst in rage
Kurals.Explanation: It is the determination of the spotless not to do evil, even in return, to those who have cherished enmity and done them evil
Kurals.Couplet: Though malice work its worst, planning no ill return, to endure,And work no ill, is fixed decree of men in spirit pure
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , self-control,

Kurals.Translation: Revenging even causeless hate Bad-blood breeds and baneful heat
Kurals.Explanation: In an ascetic inflict suffering even on those who hate him, when he has not done them any evil, it will afterwards give him irretrievable sorrow
Kurals.Couplet: Though unprovoked thy soul malicious foes should sting,Retaliation wrought inevitable woes will bring

Kurals.Translation: Doing good-turns, put them to shame Thus chide the evil who do harm
Kurals.Explanation: The (proper) punishment to those who have done evil (to you), is to put them to shame by showing them kindness, in return and to forget both the evil and the good done on both sides
Kurals.Couplet: To punish wrong, with kindly benefits the doers ply;Thus shame their souls; but pass the ill unheeded by

Kurals.Translation: What does a man from wisdom gain If he pines not at other's pain?
Kurals.Explanation: What benefit has he derived from his knowledge, who does not endeavour to keep off pain from another as much as from himself ?
Kurals.Couplet: From wisdom's vaunted lore what doth the learner gain,If as his own he guard not others' souls from pain

Kurals.Translation: What you feel as pain to yourself Do it not to the other-self
Kurals.Explanation: Let not a man consent to do those things to another which, he knows, will cause sorrow
Kurals.Couplet: What his own soul has felt as bitter pain,From making others feel should man abstain
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , as an emotion to be avoided by not causing sorrow to others

Kurals.Translation: Any, anywhere injure not At any time even in thought
Kurals.Explanation: It is the chief of all virtues not knowingly to do any person evil, even in the lowest degree, and at any time
Kurals.Couplet: To work no wilful woe, in any wise, through all the days,To any living soul, is virtue's highest praise
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – The emphasis on not injuring others

Kurals.Translation: How can he injure other souls Who in his life injury feels
Kurals.Explanation: Why does a man inflict upon other creatures those sufferings, which he has found by experience are sufferings to himself ?
Kurals.Couplet: Whose soul has felt the bitter smart of wrong, how canHe wrongs inflict on ever-living soul of man
Kurals.emotions: Empathy — The text reflects understanding the feelings and sufferings of others based on one’s own experience., Compassion — There is concern about the pain of others and a desire to avoid

Kurals.Translation: Harm others in the forenoon Harm seeks thee in afternoon
Kurals.Explanation: If a man inflict sorrow upon others in the morning, it will come upon him unsought in the very evening
Kurals.Couplet: If, ere the noontide, you to others evil do,Before the eventide will evil visit you
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with this text are:, Guilt – Awareness or anticipation of wrongdoing towards others., Fear – Concern or anxiety that harmful actions will lead to negative consequences., Retribution – Expectation of justice

Kurals.Translation: No harm is done by peace-lovers For pains rebound on pain-givers
Kurals.Explanation: Sorrow will come upon those who cause pain to others; therfore those, who desire to be free from sorrow, give no pain to others
Kurals.Couplet: O'er every evil-doer evil broodeth still;He evil shuns who freedom seeks from ill
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – The text emphasizes caring for others and avoiding harm., Empathy – It reflects understanding and sharing the feelings of others

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Compassion, Empathy, Forgiveness.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: What is Virtue? 'Tis not to kill For killing causes every ill
Kurals.Explanation: Never to destroy life is the sum of all virtuous conduct The destruction of life leads to every evil
Kurals.Couplet: What is the work of virtue? 'Not to kill';For 'killing' leads to every work of ill

Kurals.Translation: Share the food and serve all lives This is the law of all the laws
Kurals.Explanation: The chief of all (the virtues) which authors have summed up, is the partaking of food that has been shared with others, and the preservation of the mainfold life of other creatures
Kurals.Couplet: Let those that need partake your meal; guard every-thing that lives;This the chief and sum of lore that hoarded wisdom gives
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , for life

Kurals.Translation: Not to kill is unique good The next, not to utter falsehood
Kurals.Explanation: Not to destroy life is an incomparably (great) good next to it in goodness ranks freedom from falsehood
Kurals.Couplet: Alone, first of goods things, is 'not to slay';The second is, no untrue word to say
Kurals.EmotionDetail: related to not killing and valuing life, related to not uttering falsehood, for life and truth

Kurals.Translation: What way is good? That we can say The way away from heat to slay
Kurals.Explanation: Good path is that which considers how it may avoid killing any creature
Kurals.Couplet: You ask, What is the good and perfect way?'Tis path of him who studies nought to slay

Kurals.Translation: Of saints who renounce birth-fearing The head is he who dreads killing
Kurals.Explanation: Of all those who, fearing the permanence of earthly births, have abandoned desire, he is the chief who, fearing (the guilt of) murder, considers how he may avoid the destruction of life
Kurals.Couplet: Of those who 'being' dread, and all renounce, the chief are they,Who dreading crime of slaughter, study nought to slay
Kurals.EmotionDetail: specifically fear of guilt and the consequences of killing, a desire to avoid the destruction of life,

Kurals.Translation: Life-eating-Death shall spare the breath Of him who no life puts to death
Kurals.Explanation: Yama, the destroyer of life, will not attack the life of him, who acts under the determination of never destroying life
Kurals.Couplet: Ev'n death that life devours, their happy days shall spare,Who law, 'Thou shall not kill', uphold with reverent care
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – Emphasizing the value of sparing life and the resolve to not harm others., Peacefulness – Reflecting a state of harmony and non-violence towards all beings

Kurals.Translation: Kill not life that others cherish Even when your life must perish
Kurals.Explanation: Let no one do that which would destroy the life of another, although he should by so doing, lose his own life
Kurals.Couplet: Though thine own life for that spared life the price must pay,Take not from aught that lives gift of sweet life away
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , for life

Kurals.Translation: The gain of slaughter is a vice Though deemed good in sacrifice
Kurals.Explanation: The advantage which might flow from destroying life in sacrifice, is dishonourable to the wise (who renounced the world), even although it should be said to be productive of great good
Kurals.Couplet: Though great the gain of good should seem, the wiseWill any gain by staughter won despise
Kurals.emotions: Disapproval – The text expresses moral rejection of killing

Kurals.Translation: Those who live by slaying are Eaters of carrion bizarre!
Kurals.Explanation: To minds of them who what is vileness know
Kurals.Couplet: Whose trade is 'killing', always vile they show,To minds of them who what is vileness know
Kurals.emotions: Disgust – The phrase "Eaters of carrion bizarre" and the reference to vileness evoke a strong sense of revulsion or disgust., Moral Judgment – The text implies a

Kurals.Translation: The loathsome poor sickly and sore Are killers stained by blood before
Kurals.Explanation: (The wise) will say that men of diseased bodies, who live in degradation and in poverty, are those who separated the life from the body of animals (in a former birth)
Kurals.Couplet: Who lead a loathed life in bodies sorely pained,Are men, the wise declare, by guilt of slaughter stained
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Compassion,

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Compassion, Reverence, Caution.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The worst of follies it is told The fleeting as lasting to hold
Kurals.Explanation: That ignorance which considers those things to be stable which are not so, is dishonourable (to the wise)
Kurals.Couplet: Lowest and meanest lore, that bids men trust secure,In things that pass away, as things that shall endure

Kurals.Translation: Like a drama-crowd wealth gathers Like passing show its pride too goes
Kurals.Explanation: The acquisition of wealth is like the gathering together of an assembly for a theatre; its expenditure is like the breaking up of that assembly
Kurals.Couplet: As crowds round dancers fill the hall, is wealth's increase;Its loss, as throngs dispersing, when the dances cease
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with this text:, Impermanence / Transience, Detachment, Reflection / Cont
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , reflecting awareness that situations and wealth are temporary, recognizing the fleeting nature of pride and material possessions,

Kurals.Translation: Wealth wanes away; but when it comes Take care to do enduring things
Kurals.Explanation: Wealth is perishable; let those who obtain it immediately practise those (virtues) which are imperishable
Kurals.Couplet: Unenduring is all wealth; if you wealth enjoy,Enduring works in working wealth straightway employ
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text urges readers to be careful with the fleeting nature of wealth., Impermanence (Transience) – There is an underlying awareness of the temporary

Kurals.Translation: The showy day is but a saw Your life, know that, to file and gnaw
Kurals.Explanation: Time, which shows itself (to the ignorant) as if it were something (real) is in the estimation of the wise (only) a saw which cuts down life
Kurals.Couplet: As 'day' it vaunts itself; well understood, 'tis knife',That daily cuts away a portion from thy life
Kurals.EmotionDetail: of impermanence, about the fleeting nature of life,

Kurals.Translation: Ere tongue benumbs and hiccough comes Rise up to do good deeds betimes
Kurals.Explanation: Let virtuous deeds be done quickly, before the hiccup comes making the tongue silent
Kurals.Couplet: Before the tongue lie powerless, 'mid the gasp of gurgling breath,Arouse thyself, and do good deeds beyond the power of death
Kurals.emotions: Urgency – There is a strong feeling of needing to act quickly., Awareness (of mortality) – The text conveys recognition of life’s fragility and the inevitability of death.

Kurals.Translation: One was yesterday; not today!
Kurals.Explanation: This world possesses the greatness that one who yesterday was is not today
Kurals.Couplet: Existing yesterday, today to nothing hurled!-Such greatness owns this transitory world
Kurals.emotions: Impermanence (or Transience) – Awareness that things and people change or may no longer be present., Uncertainty – A sense of instability about life’s unpredictability. 3

Kurals.Translation: Man knows not his next moment On crores of things he is intent
Kurals.Explanation: Innumerable are the thoughts which occupy the mind of (the unwise), who know not that they shall live another moment
Kurals.Couplet: Who know not if their happy lives shall last the day,In fancies infinite beguile the hours away

Kurals.Translation: The soul from body any day Like bird from egg-shell flies away
Kurals.Explanation: The love of the soul to the body is like (the love of) a bird to its egg which it flies away from and leaves empty
Kurals.Couplet: Birds fly away, and leave the nest deserted bare;Such is the short-lived friendship soul and body share
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with this text are:, Detachment – The imagery of the soul leaving the body like a bird leaving its eggshell suggests a profound sense of separation or letting go., Transience – The description evokes awareness

Kurals.Translation: Death is like a slumber deep And birth like waking from that sleep
Kurals.Explanation: Death is like sleep; birth is like awaking from it
Kurals.Couplet: Death is sinking into slumbers deep;Birth again is waking out of sleep
Kurals.EmotionDetail: reflectiveness about life and death, peacefulness in accepting the natural cycle,

Kurals.Translation: The life berthed in this body shows A fixed home it never knows
Kurals.Explanation: It seems as if the soul, which takes a temporary shelter in a body, had not attained a home
Kurals.Couplet: The soul in fragile shed as lodger courts repose:-Is it because no home's conclusive rest it knows

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Anxiety, Uncertainty, Restlessness.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: From what from what a man is free From that, from that his torments flee
Kurals.Explanation: Whatever thing, a man has renounced, by that thing; he cannot suffer pain
Kurals.Couplet: From whatever, aye, whatever, man gets free,From what, aye, from that, no more of pain hath he

Kurals.Translation: Give up all to gain the True And endless joys shall hence seek you
Kurals.Explanation: After a man has renounced (all things), there will still be many things in this world (which he may enjoy); if he should desire them, let him, while it is time abandon (the world)
Kurals.Couplet: 'Renunciation' made- ev'n here true pleasures men acquire;'Renounce' while time is yet, if to those pleasures you aspire
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , for higher joy or truth, from renunciation and giving up desires

Kurals.Translation: Curb the senses five and renounce The carving desires all at once
Kurals.Explanation: Let the five senses be destroyed; and at the same time, let everything be abandoned that (the ascetic) has (formerly) desired
Kurals.Couplet: 'Perceptions of the five' must all expire;-Relinquished in its order each desire

Kurals.Translation: To have nothing is law of vows Having the least deludes and snares
