Kurals.Explanation: To be altogether destitute is the proper condition of those who perform austerities; if they possess anything, it will change (their resolution) and bring them back to their confused state
Kurals.Couplet: 'Privation absolute' is penance true;'Possession' brings bewilderment anew

Kurals.Translation: Why add to bonds while this body Is too much for saints to be birth-free
Kurals.Explanation: What means the addition of other things those who are attempting to cut off (future) births, when even their body is too much (for them)
Kurals.Couplet: To those who sev'rance seek from being's varied strife,Flesh is burthen sore; what then other bonds of life
Kurals.emotions: Detachment – The passage discusses renouncing worldly bonds and emphasizes letting go

Kurals.Translation: Who curbs the pride of I and mine Gets a world rare for gods to gain
Kurals.Explanation: Shall enter realms above the powers divine
Kurals.Couplet: Who kills conceit that utters 'I' and 'mine',Shall enter realms above the powers divine
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , for spiritual elevation or transcendence

Kurals.Translation: Grief clings on and on to those Who cling to bonds without release
Kurals.Explanation: Sorrows will never let go their hold of those who give not up their hold of desire
Kurals.Couplet: Who cling to things that cling and eager clasp,Griefs cling to them with unrelaxing grasp

Kurals.Translation: Who renounce all are free from care Others suffer delusive snare
Kurals.Explanation: Those who have entirely renounced (all things and all desire) have obtained (absorption into God); all others wander in confusion, entangled in the net of (many) births
Kurals.Couplet: Who thoroughly 'renounce' on highest height are set;The rest bewildered, lie entangled in the net
Kurals.EmotionDetail: or Renunciation, or Serenity, for those who have not renounced

Kurals.Translation: Bondage cut off, rebirth is off The world then seems instable stuff
Kurals.Explanation: At the moment in which desire has been abandoned, (other) births will be cut off; when that has not been done, instability will be seen
Kurals.Couplet: When that which clings falls off, severed is being's tie;All else will then be seen as instability
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Relief, or Instability

Kurals.Translation: Bind Thyself to the unbound one That binding breaks all bonds anon
Kurals.Explanation: Desire the desire of Him who is without desire; in order to renounce desire, desire that desire
Kurals.Couplet: Cling thou to that which He, to Whom nought clings, hath bid thee cling,Cling to that bond, to get thee free from every clinging thing
Kurals.emotions: Longing – There is a yearning or deep desire for spiritual connection or transcendence., Detachment – A sense of wanting to let go of worldly desires and attachments. 3

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Detachment, Peace, Resolve.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: That error entails ignoble birth Which deems vain things as things of worth
Kurals.Explanation: Inglorious births are produced by the confusion (of mind) which considers those things to be real which are not real
Kurals.Couplet: Of things devoid of truth as real things men deem;-Cause of degraded birth the fond delusive dream

Kurals.Translation: Men of spotless pure insight Enjoy delight devoid of night
Kurals.Explanation: A clear, undimmed vision of things will deliver its possessors from the darkness of future births, and confer the felicity (of heaven)
Kurals.Couplet: Darkness departs, and rapture springs to men who see,The mystic vision pure, from all delusion free

Kurals.Translation: To doubtless minds whose heart is clear More than earth heaven is near
Kurals.Explanation: Heaven is nearer than earth to those men of purified minds who are freed from from doubt
Kurals.Couplet: When doubts disperse, and mists of error rollAway, nearer is heav'n than earth to sage's soul

Kurals.Translation: Knowledge of five senses is vain Without knowing the Truth within
Kurals.Explanation: Even those who have all the knowledge which can be attained by the five senses, will derive no benefit from it, if they are without a knowledge of the true nature of things
Kurals.Couplet: Five-fold perception gained, what benefits accrueTo them whose spirits lack perception of the true
Kurals.emotions: Enlightenment, Hopelessness (in relation to the futility or emptiness of sense-based knowledge alone) 3
Kurals.EmotionDetail: a sense of realization or awakening to a deeper truth,

Kurals.Translation: Knowledge is truth of things to find In every case of every kind
Kurals.Explanation: (True) knowledge is the perception concerning every thing of whatever kind, that that thing is the true thing
Kurals.Couplet: Whatever thing, of whatsoever kind it be,'Tis wisdom's part in each the very thing to see
Kurals.emotions: Curiosity — The desire to seek and uncover truth and knowledge is evident., Enlightenment — The realization or understanding of truth implies a sense of illumination and clarity., Assurance —

Kurals.Translation: Who learn and here the Truth discern Enter the path of non-return
Kurals.Explanation: They, who in this birth have learned to know the True Being, enter the road which returns not into this world
Kurals.Couplet: Who learn, and here the knowledge of the true obtain,Shall find the path that hither cometh not again

Kurals.Translation: One-minded sage sees inner-truth He is free from thoughts of rebirth
Kurals.Explanation: Let it not be thought that there is another birth for him whose mind having thoroughly considered (all it has been taught) has known the True Being
Kurals.Couplet: The mind that knows with certitude what is, and ponders well,Its thoughts on birth again to other life need not to dwell

Kurals.Translation: It is knowledge to know Self-Truth And remove the folly of birth
Kurals.Explanation: True knowledge consists in the removal of ignorance; which is (the cause of) births, and the perception of the True Being who is (the bestower of) heaven
Kurals.Couplet: When folly, cause of births, departs; and soul can viewThe truth of things, man's dignity- 'tis wisdom true
Kurals.emotions: Enlightenment – The realization of Self-Truth and the removal of ignorance implies a sense of awakening and deeper understanding., Liberation – The removal of the “folly of birth” suggests

Kurals.Translation: Know the Refuge; off with bondage Be free from ills of thraldom, O sage
Kurals.Explanation: He who so lives as to know Him who is the support of all things and abandon all desire, will be freed from the evils which would otherwise cleave to him and destroy (his efforts after absorption)
Kurals.Couplet: The true 'support' who knows- rejects 'supports' he sought before-Sorrow that clings all destroys, shall cling to him no more
Kurals.emotions: Liberation (Freedom) – The desire and experience of breaking free from bondage and ills is central to the passage., Realization (Enlightenment) – The text speaks of knowing

Kurals.Translation: Woes expire when lust, wrath, folly Expire even to name, fully
Kurals.Explanation: If the very names of these three things, desire, anger, and confusion of mind, be destroyed, then will also perish evils (which flow from them)
Kurals.Couplet: When lust and wrath and error's triple tyranny is o'er,Their very names for aye extinct, then pain shall be no more
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Lust, Wrath, Folly / Bewilderment

Summary: Highlights truth as an eternal value as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Enlightenment, Peace, Confusion.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Desire to all, always is seed From which ceaseless births proceed
Kurals.Explanation: (The wise) say that the seed, which produces unceasing births, at all times, to all creatures, is desire
Kurals.Couplet: The wise declare, through all the days, to every living thingThat ceaseless round of birth from seed of strong desire doth spring
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , from the understanding or acknowledgment of desire’s role

Kurals.Translation: If long thou must, long for non-birth It comes by longing no more for earth
Kurals.Explanation: If anything be desired, freedom from births should be desired; that (freedom from births) will be attained by desiring to be without desire
Kurals.Couplet: If desire you feel, freedom from changing birth require!'I' will come, if you desire to 'scape, set free from all desire
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , for liberation/freedom,

Kurals.Translation: No such wealth is here and there As peerless wealth of non-desire
Kurals.Explanation: There is in this world no excellence equal to freedom from desire; and even in that world, there is nothing like it
Kurals.Couplet: No glorious wealth is here like freedom from desire;To bliss like this not even there can soul aspire

Kurals.Translation: To nothing crave is purity That is the fruit of verity
Kurals.Explanation: Purity (of mind) consists in freedom from desire; and that (freedom from desire) is the fruit of the love of truth
Kurals.Couplet: Desire's decease as purity men know;That, too, from yearning search for truth will grow
Kurals.emotions: Purity, Detachment, Truthfulness (Sincerity) Explanation: The text discusses achieving purity by eradicating desire

Kurals.Translation: The free are those who desire not The rest not free in bonds are caught
Kurals.Explanation: They are said to be free (from future birth) who are freed from desire; all others (who, whatever else they may be free from, are not freed from desire) are not thus free
Kurals.Couplet: Men freed from bonds of strong desire are free;None other share such perfect liberty
Kurals.EmotionDetail: or Liberation, or Longing, leading to Bondage

Kurals.Translation: Dread desire; Virtue is there To every soul desire is snare!
Kurals.Explanation: It is the chief duty of (an ascetic) to watch against desire with (jealous) fear; for it has power to deceive (and destroy) him
Kurals.Couplet: Desire each soul beguiles;True virtue dreads its wiles
Kurals.EmotionDetail: specifically dread and jealous fear regarding desire,

Kurals.Translation: Destroy desire; deliverance Comes as much as you aspire hence
Kurals.Explanation: If a man thoroughly cut off all desire, the deeds, which confer immortality, will come to him, in the path in which he seeks them
Kurals.Couplet: Who thoroughly rids his life of passion-prompted deed,Deeds of unfailing worth shall do, which, as he plans, succeed
Kurals.emotions: Aspiration – The text references aspiration and striving for deliverance

Kurals.Translation: Desire extinct no sorrow-taints Grief comes on grief where it pretends
Kurals.Explanation: There is no sorrow to those who are without desire; but where that is, (sorrow) will incessantly come, more and more
Kurals.Couplet: Affliction is not known where no desires abide;Where these are, endless rises sorrow's tide
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , longing/craving, as a result of being free from desire

Kurals.Translation: Desire, the woe of woes destroy Joy of joys here you enjoy
Kurals.Explanation: Even while in this body, joy will never depart (from the mind, in which) desire, that sorrow of sorrows, has been destroyed
Kurals.Couplet: When dies away desire, that woe of woesEv'n here the soul unceasing rapture knows
Kurals.emotions: Joy, Relief, Peace These emotions are reflected in the emphasis on happiness after eradicating desire
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , which is linked to sorrow

Kurals.Translation: Off with desire insatiate You gain the native blissful state
Kurals.Explanation: The removal of desire, whose nature it is never to be satisfied, will immediately confer a nature that can never be changed
Kurals.Couplet: Drive from thy soul desire insatiate;Straight'way is gained the moveless blissful state

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Desire, Freedom, Peace.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Efforts succeed by waxing star Wealth-losing brings waning star
Kurals.Explanation: Perseverance comes from a prosperous fate, and idleness from an adverse fate
Kurals.Couplet: Wealth-giving fate power of unflinching effort brings;From fate that takes away idle remissness springs
Kurals.emotions: Hope – Suggests that effort and perseverance can lead to success and prosperity., Resignation – Reflects acceptance that adverse fate can lead to inactivity or loss., Determ

Kurals.Translation: Loss-fate makes a dull fool of us Gain-fate makes us prosperous, wise!
Kurals.Explanation: An adverse fate produces folly, and a prosperous fate produces enlarged knowledge
Kurals.Couplet: The fate that loss ordains makes wise men's wisdom foolishness;The fate that gain bestows with ampler powers will wisdom bless
Kurals.EmotionDetail: acceptance of fate, with loss and folly, linked to gain and improvement

Kurals.Translation: What matters subtle study deep? Levels of innate wisdom-keep
Kurals.Explanation: Although (a man) may study the most polished treatises, the knowledge which fate has decreed to him will still prevail
Kurals.Couplet: In subtle learning manifold though versed man be,'The wisdom, truly his, will gain supremacy
Kurals.emotions: Acceptance – There is a sense of accepting that fate or destiny ultimately governs one's knowledge

Kurals.Translation: Two natures in the world obtain Some wealth and others wisdom gain
Kurals.Explanation: There are (through fate) two different natures in the world, hence the difference (observable in men) in (their acquisition of) wealth, and in their attainment of knowledge
Kurals.Couplet: Two fold the fashion of the world: some live in fortune's light;While other some have souls in wisdom's radiance bright
Kurals.emotions: Acceptance – Recognition of the differences in people’s fate

Kurals.Translation: In making wealth fate changes mood; The good as bad and bad as good
Kurals.Explanation: In the acquisition of property, every thing favourable becomes unfavourable, and (on the other hand) everything unfavourable becomes favourable, (through the power of fate)
Kurals.Couplet: All things that good appear will oft have ill success;All evil things prove good for gain of happiness
Kurals.emotions: Uncertainty – Reflecting the unpredictable nature of fate and fortune., Acceptance – Recognizing and coming to terms with the power of fate over circumstances., Hope – The possibility that

Kurals.Translation: Things not thine never remain Things destined are surely thine
Kurals.Explanation: Whatever is not conferred by fate cannot be preserved although it be guarded with most painful care; and that, which fate has made his, cannot be lost, although one should even take it and throw it away
Kurals.Couplet: Things not your own will yield no good, howe'er you guard with pain;Your own, howe'er you scatter them abroad, will yours remain
Kurals.emotions: Acceptance – Recognizing and coming to terms with fate and what is destined., Resignation – Letting go of personal control or effort in the face of fate.

Kurals.Translation: Who crores amass enjoy but what The Dispenser's decrees allot
Kurals.Explanation: Even those who gather together millions will only enjoy them, as it has been determined by the disposer (of all things)
Kurals.Couplet: Save as the 'sharer' shares to each in due degree,To those who millions store enjoyment scarce can be
Kurals.emotions: Acceptance – Acknowledging that outcomes are ultimately determined by fate., Resignation – Recognizing that one's efforts are limited by forces beyond their control., Humility – Realizing

Kurals.Translation: The destitute desire will quit If fate with ills visit them not
Kurals.Explanation: The destitute will renounce desire (and become ascetics), if (fate) do not make them suffer the hindrances to which they are liable, and they pass away
Kurals.Couplet: The destitute with ascetics merit share,If fate to visit with predestined ills would spare
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , for release or relief

Kurals.Translation: Who good in time of good perceive In evil time why should they grieve?
Kurals.Explanation: How is it that those, who are pleased with good fortune, trouble themselves when evil comes, (since both are equally the decree of fate) ?
Kurals.Couplet: When good things come, men view them all as gain;When evils come, why then should they complain
Kurals.emotions: Acceptance – The text encourages accepting both good and bad times as decreed by fate., Resignation – There is a sense of surrendering or yielding to the inevitability of fortune's

Kurals.Translation: What power surpasses fate? Its will Persists against the human skill
Kurals.Explanation: What is stronger than fate ? If we think of an expedient (to avert it), it will itself be with us before (the thought)
Kurals.Couplet: What powers so great as those of Destiny? Man's skillSome other thing contrives; but fate's beforehand still
Kurals.EmotionDetail: or Helplessness, or Resignation, or Reverence

Summary: Highlights the light of wisdom as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Resignation, Acceptance – Recognizing, Prosperity..
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: People, troops, wealth, forts, council, friends Who owns these six is lion of kings
Kurals.Explanation: He who possesses these six things, an army, a people, wealth, ministers, friends and a fortress, is a lion among kings
Kurals.Couplet: An army, people, wealth, a minister, friends, fort: six things-Who owns them all, a lion lives amid the kings
Kurals.EmotionDetail: in possessing great power and resources, desire to achieve greatness and dominance, feeling of safety and strength from having support and defenses

Kurals.Translation: Courage, giving, knowledge and zeal Are four failless features royal
Kurals.Explanation: Never to fail in these four things, fearlessness, liberality, wisdom, and energy, is the kingly character
Kurals.Couplet: Courage, a liberal hand, wisdom, and energy: these fourAre qualities a king adorn for evermore
Kurals.EmotionDetail: fearlessness, liberality/giving, zeal/energy

Kurals.Translation: Alertness, learning bravery Are adjuncts three of monarchy
Kurals.Explanation: These three things, viz, vigilance, learning, and bravery, should never be wanting in the ruler of a country
Kurals.Couplet: A sleepless promptitude, knowledge, decision strong:These three for aye to rulers of the land belong

Kurals.Translation: A brave noble king refrains from vice Full of virtue and enterprise
Kurals.Explanation: He is a king who, with manly modesty, swerves not from virtue, and refrains from vice
Kurals.Couplet: Kingship, in virtue failing not, all vice restrains,In courage failing not, it honour's grace maintains
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for the noble and virtuous king, for the king's bravery and enterprise, the king's strong adherence to virtue

Kurals.Translation: The able king gets, stores and guards And spends them for people's safeguards
Kurals.Explanation: He is a king who is able to acquire (wealth), to lay it up, to guard, and to distribute it
Kurals.Couplet: A king is he who treasure gains, stores up, defends,And duly for his kingdom's weal expends

Kurals.Translation: That land prospers where the king is Easy to see, not harsh of words
Kurals.Explanation: The whole world will exalt the country of the king who is easy of access, and who is free from harsh language
Kurals.Couplet: Where king is easy of access, where no harsh word repels,That land's high praises every subject swells

Kurals.Translation: The world commends and acts his phrase Who sweetly speaks and gives with grace
Kurals.Explanation: The world will praise and submit itself to the mind of the king who is able to give with affability, and to protect all who come to him
Kurals.Couplet: With pleasant speech, who gives and guards with powerful liberal hand,He sees the world obedient all to his command
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – The world commends and praises the king., Gratitude – People feel thankful for his generosity and protection., Respect – There is submission and reverence

Kurals.Translation: He is the Lord of men who does Sound justice and saves his race
Kurals.Explanation: That king, will be esteemed a God among men, who performs his own duties, and protects (his subjects).
Kurals.Couplet: Who guards the realm and justice strict maintains,That king as god o'er subject people reigns

Kurals.Translation: Under his shelter thrives the world Who bears remarks bitter and bold
Kurals.Explanation: The whole world will dwell under the umbrella of the king, who can bear words that embitter the ear
Kurals.Couplet: The king of worth, who can words bitter to his ear endure,Beneath the shadow of his power the world abides secure
Kurals.EmotionDetail: the ability to bear bitter or bold remarks, for a king who shelters and protects the world,

Kurals.Translation: He is the Light of Kings who has Bounty, justice, care and grace
Kurals.Explanation: He is the light of kings who has there four things, beneficence, benevolence, rectitude, and care for his people
Kurals.Couplet: Gifts, grace, right sceptre, care of people's weal;These four a light of dreaded kings reveal
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – The text reveres and respects the qualities of the king

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Respect, Admiration, Generosity.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Lore worth learning, learn flawlessly Live by that learning thoroughly
Kurals.Explanation: Let a man learn thoroughly whatever he may learn, and let his conduct be worthy of his learning
Kurals.Couplet: So learn that you may full and faultless learning gain,Then in obedience meet to lessons learnt remain
Kurals.emotions: Aspiration – The text encourages striving for knowledge and self-improvement., Respect – It emphasizes honoring knowledge through flawless learning and conduct., Determination – There is a call for

Kurals.Translation: Letter, number, art and science Of living kind both are the eyes
Kurals.Explanation: Letters and numbers are the two eyes of man
Kurals.Couplet: The twain that lore of numbers and of letters giveAre eyes, the wise declare, to all on earth that live
Kurals.emotions: Curiosity – The text invokes a sense of interest in learning and discovery., Appreciation – There is a respect or valuing of knowledge

Kurals.Translation: The learned alone have eyes on face The ignorant two sores of disgrace
Kurals.Explanation: The learned are said to have eyes, but the unlearned have (merely) two sores in their face
Kurals.Couplet: Men who learning gain have eyes, men say;Blockheads' faces pairs of sores display
Kurals.EmotionDetail: towards the learned and the value of knowledge, towards ignorance and lack of learning,

Kurals.Translation: To meet with joy and part with thought Of learned men this is the art
Kurals.Explanation: It is the part of the learned to give joy to those whom they meet, and on leaving, to make them think (Oh! when shall we meet them again.)
Kurals.Couplet: You meet with joy, with pleasant thought you part;Such is the learned scholar's wonderous art
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , or yearning

Kurals.Translation: Like poor before rich they yearn: For knowledge: the low never learn
Kurals.Explanation: The unlearned are inferior to the learned, before whom they stand begging, as the destitute before the wealthy
Kurals.Couplet: With soul submiss they stand, as paupers front a rich man's face;Yet learned men are first; th'unlearned stand in lowest place
Kurals.emotions: Inferiority – The unlearned are described as inferior to the learned., Yearning – There is a desire for knowledge

Kurals.Translation: As deep you dig the sand spring flows As deep you learn the knowledge grows
Kurals.Explanation: Water will flow from a well in the sand in proportion to the depth to which it is dug, and knowledge will flow from a man in proportion to his learning
Kurals.Couplet: In sandy soil, when deep you delve, you reach the springs below;The more you learn, the freer streams of wisdom flow
Kurals.emotions: Curiosity – The desire to learn and discover more

Kurals.Translation: All lands and towns are learner's own Why not till death learning go on!
Kurals.Explanation: How is it that any one can remain without learning, even to his death, when (to the learned man) every country is his own (country), and every town his own (town) ?
Kurals.Couplet: The learned make each land their own, in every city find a home;Who, till they die; learn nought, along what weary ways they roam
Kurals.emotions: Curiosity – The text encourages continual learning and an inquisitive attitude towards knowledge., Inspiration – It motivates individuals to embrace learning throughout their lives

Kurals.Translation: The joy of learning in one birth Exalts man upto his seventh
Kurals.Explanation: The learning, which a man has acquired in one birth, will yield him pleasure during seven births
Kurals.Couplet: The man who store of learning gains,In one, through seven worlds, bliss attains
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , in achievement, for lasting impact

Kurals.Translation: The learned foster learning more On seeing the world enjoy their lore
Kurals.Explanation: The learned will long (for more learning), when they see that while it gives pleasure to themselves, the world also derives pleasure from it
Kurals.Couplet: Their joy is joy of all the world, they see; thus moreThe learners learn to love their cherished lore
Kurals.emotions: Curiosity – The desire and eagerness to learn more., Joy – The pleasure and satisfaction experienced from both learning and sharing knowledge., Motivation – The drive or inspiration to

Kurals.Translation: Learning is wealth none could destroy Nothing else gives genuine joy
Kurals.Explanation: Learning is the true imperishable riches; all other things are not riches
Kurals.Couplet: Learning is excellence of wealth that none destroy;To man nought else affords reality of joy
Kurals.emotions: Joy – The text mentions "genuine joy" as a result of learning., Admiration – There is a sense of reverence and high regard for learning as it is described as

Summary: Highlights the pursuit of learning as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Joy, Self-Improvement., Conduct..
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Like play of chess on squareless board Vain is imperfect loreless word
Kurals.Explanation: To speak in an assembly (of the learned) without fullness of knowledge, is like playing at chess (on a board) without squares
Kurals.Couplet: Like those at draughts would play without the chequered square,Men void of ample lore would counsels of the learned share

Kurals.Translation: Unlearned man aspiring speech Is breastless lady's love-approach
Kurals.Explanation: The desire of the unlearned to speak (in an assembly), is like a woman without breasts desiring (the enjoyment of ) woman-hood
Kurals.Couplet: Like those who doat on hoyden's undeveloped charms are they,Of learning void, who eagerly their power of words display
Kurals.emotions: Ambition – The unlearned man aspires to speak

Kurals.Translation: Ev'n unread men are good and wise If before the wise, they hold their peace
Kurals.Explanation: The unlearned also are very excellent men, if they know how to keep silence before the learned
Kurals.Couplet: The blockheads, too, may men of worth appear,If they can keep from speaking where the learned hear

Kurals.Translation: The unread's wit though excellent Is not valued by the savant
Kurals.Explanation: Although the natural knowledge of an unlearned man may be very good, the wise will not accept for true knowledge
Kurals.Couplet: From blockheads' lips, when words of wisdom glibly flow,The wise receive them not, though good they seem to show

Kurals.Translation: A man untaught when speech he vaunts Sadly fails before savants
Kurals.Explanation: The self-conceit of an unlearned man will fade away, as soon as he speaks in an assembly (of
Kurals.Couplet: As worthless shows the worth of man unlearned,When council meets, by words he speaks discerned
Kurals.emotions: Embarrassment – The unlearned man's self-conceit fading when he speaks among the learned suggests a feeling of embarrassment or shame., Inferiority – The contrast between the un

Kurals.Translation: People speak of untaught minds \"They just exist like barren lands\"
Kurals.Explanation: The unlearned are like worthless barren land: all that can be said of them is, that they exist
Kurals.Couplet: 'They are': so much is true of men untaught;But, like a barren field, they yield us nought

Kurals.Translation: Like painted clay-doll is his show Grand subtle lore who fails to know
Kurals.Explanation: The beauty and goodness of one who is destitute of knowledge by the study of great and exquisite works, is like (the beauty and goodness) of a painted earthen doll
Kurals.Couplet: Who lack the power of subtle, large, and penetrating sense,Like puppet, decked with ornaments of clay, their beauty's vain pretence
Kurals.emotions: Ignorance – The central theme is lack of knowledge and understanding., Emptiness – There is a sense of hollowness or superficiality

Kurals.Translation: Wealth in the hand of fools is worse Than a learned man's empty purse
Kurals.Explanation: Wealth, gained by the unlearned, will give more sorrow than the poverty which may come upon the learned
Kurals.Couplet: To men unlearned, from fortune's favour greater-evil springsThan poverty to men of goodly wisdom brings

Kurals.Translation: Lower are fools of higher birth Than low-born men of learning's worth
Kurals.Explanation: The unlearned, though born in a high caste, are not equal in dignity to the learned; though they may have been born in a low caste
Kurals.Couplet: Lower are men unlearned, though noble be their race,Than low-born men adorned with learning's grace
Kurals.emotions: Humility – Recognition of the value of learning over status by birth., Respect – For the learned

Kurals.Translation: Like beasts before men, dunces are Before scholars of shining lore
Kurals.Explanation: As beasts by the side of men, so are other men by the side of those who are learned in celebrated works
Kurals.Couplet: Learning's irradiating grace who gain,Others excel, as men the bestial train
Kurals.emotions: Inferiority – The comparison of the ignorant to beasts and the learned to men suggests a feeling of being lesser or inadequate., Admiration – There is a clear respect or admiration for those

Summary: Highlights the pursuit of learning as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Confusion, Inadequacy, Frustration.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Wealth of wealths is listening's wealth It is the best of wealths on earth
Kurals.Explanation: Wealth (gained) by the ear is wealth of wealth; that wealth is the chief of all wealth
Kurals.Couplet: Wealth of wealth is wealth acquired be ear attent;Wealth mid all wealth supremely excellent
Kurals.emotions: Appreciation – recognizing and valuing the importance of listening., Wisdom – understanding that intangible knowledge is more valuable than material wealth., Reverence – holding the act of listening in high

Kurals.Translation: Some food for the stomach is brought When the ear gets no food for thought
Kurals.Explanation: When there is no food for the ear, give a little also to the stomach
Kurals.Couplet: When 'tis no longer time the listening ear to feedWith trifling dole of food supply the body's need
Kurals.emotions: Disappointment – The lack of "food for the ear" suggests unmet expectations or the absence of stimulating conversation or learning., Acceptance – There is a sense of making do with what is

Kurals.Translation: Whose ears get lots of wisdom-food Equal gods on oblations fed
Kurals.Explanation: Those who in this world enjoy instruction which is the food of the ear, are equal to the Gods, who enjoy the food of the sacrifices
Kurals.Couplet: Who feed their ear with learned teachings rare,Are like the happy gods oblations rich who share
Kurals.emotions: Reverence – The comparison to gods and the sacredness of wisdom evoke deep respect., Curiosity – The value placed on instruction and listening inspires a desire to learn., Grat

Kurals.Translation: Though not learned, hear and heed That serves a staff and stay in need
Kurals.Explanation: Although a man be without learning, let him listen (to the teaching of the learned); that will be to him a staff in adversity
Kurals.Couplet: Though learning none hath he, yet let him hear alway:In weakness this shall prove a staff and stay
Kurals.EmotionDetail: recognizing one’s own lack of knowledge and being open to learning, having faith that listening can help in difficult times,

Kurals.Translation: Virtuous men's wisdom is found A strong staff on slippery ground
Kurals.Explanation: The words of the good are like a staff in a slippery place
Kurals.Couplet: Like staff in hand of him in slippery ground who straysAre words from mouth of those who walk in righteous ways

Kurals.Translation: Lend ear to good words however few That much will highly exalt you
Kurals.Explanation: Let a man listen, never so little, to good (instruction), even that will bring him great dignity
Kurals.Couplet: Let each man good things learn, for e'en as heShall learn, he gains increase of perfect dignity

Kurals.Translation: Who listen well and learn sharply Not ev'n by slip speak foolishly
Kurals.Explanation: Not even when they have imperfectly understood (a matter), will those men speak foolishly, who have profoundly studied and diligently listened (to instruction)
Kurals.Couplet: Not e'en through inadvertence speak they foolish word,With clear discerning mind who've learning's ample lessons heard
Kurals.emotions: Attentiveness – The emphasis on listening well and learning sharply reflects careful attention and focus., Wisdom – The idea of not speaking foolishly after diligent study conveys wisdom and discernment.

Kurals.Translation: That ear though hearing is dulled Which is not by wisdom drilled
Kurals.Explanation: The ear which has not been bored by instruction, although it hears, is deaf
Kurals.Couplet: Where teaching hath not oped the learner's ear,The man may listen, but he scarce can hear
Kurals.emotions: Ignorance – The text reflects the state of being untouched by wisdom or instruction., Frustration – There is an implicit sense of exasperation at the ear
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , person

Kurals.Translation: A modest mouth is hard for those Who care little to counsels wise
Kurals.Explanation: It is a rare thing to find modesty, a reverend mouth- with those who have not received choice instruction
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis hard for mouth to utter gentle, modest word,When ears discourse of lore refined have never heard
Kurals.emotions: Humility – The text emphasizes the virtue of modesty

Kurals.Translation: Who taste by mouth and not by ear What if they fare ill or well here?
Kurals.Explanation: What does it matter whether those men live or die, who can judge of tastes by the mouth, and not by the ear ?
Kurals.Couplet: His mouth can taste, but ear no taste of joy can give!What matter if he die, or prosperous live
Kurals.emotions: Indifference – The text expresses a sense of detachment or lack of concern about the fate (“fare ill or well”) of certain people., Judgment – There is an evaluative tone

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Humility, Respect, Grat.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Wisdom's weapon wards off all woes It is a fort defying foes
Kurals.Explanation: Wisdom is a weapon to ward off destruction; it is an inner fortress which enemies cannot destroy
Kurals.Couplet: True wisdom wards off woes, A circling fortress high;Its inner strength man's eager foes Unshaken will defy

Kurals.Translation: Wisdom checks the straying senses Expels evils, impels goodness
Kurals.Explanation: Not to permit the mind to go where it lists, to keep it from evil, and to employ it in good, this is wisdom
Kurals.Couplet: Wisdom restrains, nor suffers mind to wander where it would;From every evil calls it back, and guides in way of good

Kurals.Translation: To grasp the Truth from everywhere From everyone is wisdom fair
Kurals.Explanation: To discern the truth in every thing, by whomsoever spoken, is wisdom
Kurals.Couplet: Though things diverse from divers sages' lips we learn,'Tis wisdom's part in each the true thing to discern
Kurals.emotions: Curiosity – The pursuit to grasp truth from everywhere reflects a yearning to know and understand., Openness – The willingness to accept wisdom from anyone

Kurals.Translation: Speaking out thoughts in clear trends Wisdom subtle sense comprehends
Kurals.Explanation: To speak so as that the meaning may easily enter the mind of the hearer, and to discern the subtlest thought which may lie hidden in the words of others, this is wisdom
Kurals.Couplet: Wisdom hath use of lucid speech, words that acceptance win,And subtle sense of other men's discourse takes in
Kurals.emotions: Clarity – The text values the importance of clear communication and understanding., Wisdom – There is appreciation and reverence for insight

Kurals.Translation: The wise-world the wise befriend They bloom nor gloom, equal in mind
Kurals.Explanation: To secure the friendship of the great is true wisdom; it is (also) wisdom to keep (that friendship unchanged, and) not opening and closing (like the lotus flower)
Kurals.Couplet: Wisdom embraces frank the world, to no caprice exposed;Unlike the lotus flower, now opened wide, now petals strictly closed
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , steadiness/equanimity

Kurals.Translation: As moves the world so move the wise In tune with changing times and ways
Kurals.Explanation: To live as the world lives, is wisdom
Kurals.Couplet: As dwells the world, so with the world to dwellIn harmony- this is to wisely live and well
Kurals.emotions: Adaptability – The text emphasizes adjusting to changing times

Kurals.Translation: The wise foresee what is to come The unwise lack in that wisdom
Kurals.Explanation: The wise are those who know beforehand what will happen; those who do not know this are the unwise
Kurals.Couplet: The wise discern, the foolish fail to see,And minds prepare for things about to be
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – The text emphasizes the value and presence of wisdom in foreseeing the future., Confidence – The wise possess confidence due to their knowledge and foresight., Insecurity –

Kurals.Translation: Fear the frightful and act wisely Not to fear the frightful's folly
Kurals.Explanation: Not to fear what ought to be feared, is folly; it is the work of the wise to fear what should be feared
Kurals.Couplet: Folly meets fearful ills with fearless heart;To fear where cause of fear exists is wisdom's part
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , Foolishness

Kurals.Translation: No frightful evil shocks the wise Who guard themselves against surprise
Kurals.Explanation: No terrifying calamity will happen to the wise, who (foresee) and guard against coming evils
Kurals.Couplet: The wise with watchful soul who coming ills foresee;From coming evil's dreaded shock are free

Kurals.Translation: Who have wisdom they are all full Whatev'r they own, misfits are nil
Kurals.Explanation: Those who possess wisdom, possess every thing; those who have not wisdom, whatever they may possess, have nothing
Kurals.Couplet: The wise is rich, with ev'ry blessing blest;The fool is poor, of everything possessed
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for wisdom and the wise, recognizing the value of wisdom over material possessions, desire to attain wisdom

Summary: Highlights the light of wisdom, fear of wrongdoing as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Wisdom, Security, Confidence.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Plenty is their prosperity Who're free from wrath pride lust petty
Kurals.Explanation: Truly great is the excellence of those (kings) who are free from pride, anger, and lust
Kurals.Couplet: Who arrogance, and wrath, and littleness of low desire restrain,To sure increase of lofty dignity attain
Kurals.EmotionDetail: opposite of pride, opposite of anger/wrath,

Kurals.Translation: Mean pride, low pleasure, avarice These add blemishes to a prince
Kurals.Explanation: Avarice, undignified pride, and low pleasures are faults in a king
Kurals.Couplet: A niggard hand, o'erweening self-regard, and mirthUnseemly, bring disgrace to men of kingly brith
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Avarice, stemming from engaging in low pleasures or being blemished

Kurals.Translation: Though millet-small their faults might seem Men fearing disgrace, Palm-tall deem
Kurals.Explanation: Those who fear guilt, if they commit a fault small as a millet seed, will consider it to be as large as a palmyra tree
Kurals.Couplet: Though small as millet-seed the fault men deem;As palm tree vast to those who fear disgrace 'twill seem
Kurals.EmotionDetail: of guilt and disgrace, recognizing and feeling a fault, worrying about small faults being significant

Kurals.Translation: Watch like treasure freedom from fault Our fatal foe is that default
Kurals.Explanation: Guard against faults as a matter (of great consequence; for) faults are a deadly enemy
Kurals.Couplet: Freedom from faults is wealth; watch heedfully'Gainst these, for fault is fatal enmity
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text urges carefulness and vigilance in avoiding faults

Kurals.Translation: Who fails to guard himself from flaw Loses his life like flame-lit straw
Kurals.Explanation: The prosperity of him who does not timely guard against faults, will perish like straw before fire
Kurals.Couplet: His joy who guards not 'gainst the coming evil day,Like straw before the fire shall swift consume away

Kurals.Translation: What fault can be the king's who cures First his faults, then scans others
Kurals.Explanation: What fault will remain in the king who has put away his own evils, and looks after the evils of others
Kurals.Couplet: Faultless the king who first his own faults cures, and thenPermits himself to scan faults of other men

Kurals.Translation: That miser's wealth shall waste and end Who would not for a good cause spend
Kurals.Explanation: The wealth of the avaricious man, who does not expend it for the purposes for which he ought to expend it will waste away and not continue
Kurals.Couplet: Who leaves undone what should be done, with niggard mind,His wealth shall perish, leaving not a wrack behind
Kurals.emotions: Greed (Avarice) – Highlighted by the miser’s unwillingness to spend wealth for good causes., Regret – Implied as the ultimate result when wealth is wasted

Kurals.Translation: The gripping greed of miser's heart Is more than fault the worst apart
Kurals.Explanation: Griping avarice is not to be reckoned as one among other faults; (it stands alone - greater than all)
Kurals.Couplet: The greed of soul that avarice men call,When faults are summed, is worst of all
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Avarice, from recognizing fault, in human nature or moral failing

Kurals.Translation: Never boast yourself in any mood Nor do a deed that does no good
Kurals.Explanation: Let no (one) praise himself, at any time; let him not desire to do useless things
Kurals.Couplet: Never indulge in self-complaisant mood,Nor deed desire that yields no gain of good

Kurals.Translation: All designs of the foes shall fail If one his wishes guards in veil
Kurals.Explanation: If (a king) enjoys, privately the things which he desires, the designs of his enemies will be useless
Kurals.Couplet: If, to your foes unknown, you cherish what you love,Counsels of men who wish you harm will harmless prove
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text emphasizes carefulness and the importance of guarding one's desires to prevent adversaries from exploiting them., Prudence – It suggests wise self-restraint and foresight in

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Greed, Shame, Humility.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

chapters.Chapter: Seeking the Aid of the Great

Kurals.Translation: Weigh their worth and friendship gain Of men of virtue and mature brain
Kurals.Explanation: Let (a king) ponder well its value, and secure the friendship of men of virtue and of mature knowledge
Kurals.Couplet: As friends the men who virtue know, and riper wisdom share,Their worth weighed well, the king should choose with care
Kurals.emotions: Respect – The text emphasizes valuing and seeking the friendship of virtuous and knowledgeable individuals

Kurals.Translation: Cherish the help of men of skill Who ward and safe-guard you from ill
Kurals.Explanation: Let (a king) procure and kindly care for men who can overcome difficulties when they occur, and guard against them before they happen
Kurals.Couplet: Cherish the all-accomplished men as friends,Whose skill the present ill removes, from coming ill defends
Kurals.emotions: Gratitude – for the help and protection received from skilled individuals., Trust – in the abilities of those who can overcome and prevent difficulties., Security – stemming from proactive

Kurals.Translation: Honour and have the great your own Is rarest of the rare things known
Kurals.Explanation: To cherish great men and make them his own, is the most difficult of all difficult things
Kurals.Couplet: To cherish men of mighty soul, and make them all their own,Of kingly treasures rare, as rarest gift is known
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for great people, desiring to associate with greatness, deep respect for the great

Kurals.Translation: To have betters as intimates Power of all powers promotes
Kurals.Explanation: So to act as to make those men, his own, who are greater than himself is of all powers the highest
Kurals.Couplet: To live with men of greatness that their own excels,As cherished friends, is greatest power that with a monarch dwells

Kurals.Translation: Ministers are the monarch's eyes Round him should be the right and wise
Kurals.Explanation: As a king must use his ministers as eyes (in managing his kingdom), let him well examine their character and qualifications before he engages them
Kurals.Couplet: The king, since counsellors are monarch's eyes,Should counsellors select with counsel wise
Kurals.EmotionDetail: careful judgment, reliance on capable ministers, concern for making wise choices

Kurals.Translation: To move with worthy friends who knows Has none to fear from frightful foes
Kurals.Explanation: There will be nothing left for enemies to do, against him who has the power of acting (so as to secure) the fellowship of worthy men
Kurals.Couplet: The king, who knows to live with worthy men allied,Has nought to fear from any foeman's pride
Kurals.EmotionDetail: a sense of safety through strong alliances, trust in one's protection from harm, reliance on the support of worthy friends

Kurals.Translation: No foe can foil his powers whose friends reprove him when he errs
Kurals.Explanation: Who are great enough to destroy him who has servants that have power to rebuke him ?
Kurals.Couplet: What power can work his fall, who faithful ministersEmploys, that thunder out reproaches when he errs
Kurals.emotions: Gratitude – Appreciation for having friends who care enough to correct one’s mistakes., Humility – The willingness to accept reproach or correction from friends., Security – The sense

Kurals.Translation: The careless king whom none reproves Ruins himself sans harmful foes
Kurals.Explanation: The king, who is without the guard of men who can rebuke him, will perish, even though there be no one to destroy him
Kurals.Couplet: The king with none to censure him, bereft of safeguards all,Though none his ruin work, shall surely ruined fall
Kurals.emotions: Vulnerability – The king is exposed to harm due to a lack of guidance., Neglect – The absence of reproval shows a lack of care or attention.

Kurals.Translation: No capital, no gain in trade No prop secure sans good comrade
Kurals.Explanation: There can be no gain to those who have no capital; and in like manner there can be no permanence to
Kurals.Couplet: Who owns no principal, can have no gain of usury;Who lacks support of friends, knows no stability
Kurals.emotions: Ambition – The desire for gain and success in trade or partnership., Dependence – Recognition of the need for resources

Kurals.Translation: To give up good friends is ten times worse Than being hated by countless foes
Kurals.Explanation: It is tenfold more injurious to abandon the friendship of the good, than to incur the hatred of the many
Kurals.Couplet: Than hate of many foes incurred, works greater woeTen-fold, of worthy men the friendship to forego
Kurals.emotions: Regret — The text reflects a sense of sorrow or remorse that comes from losing good friends., Loyalty — It emphasizes the value and importance of remaining loyal to good friends.

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship, the value of true friendship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Prevent Difficulties., Admiration, Aspiration.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The ignoble the noble fear The mean hold them as kinsmen dear
Kurals.Explanation: (True) greatness fears the society of the base; it is only the low - minded who will regard them as friends
Kurals.Couplet: The great of soul will mean association fear;The mean of soul regard mean men as kinsmen dear
Kurals.EmotionDetail: towards mean or ignoble company, of being associated with the base or ignoble, in nobility and true greatness

Kurals.Translation: With soil changes water's taste With mates changes the mental state
Kurals.Explanation: As water changes (its nature), from the nature of the soil (in which it flows), so will the character of men resemble that of their associates
Kurals.Couplet: The waters' virtues change with soil through which they flow;As man's companionship so will his wisdom show
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text warns about the influence of companionship

Kurals.Translation: Wisdom depends upon the mind The worth of man upon his friend
Kurals.Explanation: The power of knowing is from the mind; (but) his character is from that of his associates
Kurals.Couplet: Perceptions manifold in men are of the mind alone;The value of the man by his companionship is known
Kurals.EmotionDetail: reflecting the importance of knowledge and judgment, about the company one keeps and the effect of associates,

Kurals.Translation: Wisdom seems to come from mind But it truly flows from the kind
Kurals.Explanation: Wisdom appears to rest in the mind, but it really exists to a man in his companions
Kurals.Couplet: Man's wisdom seems the offspring of his mind;'Tis outcome of companionship we find
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text warns about the impact of one’s company

Kurals.Translation: Purity of the thought and deed Comes from good company indeed
Kurals.Explanation: Chaste company is the staff on which come, these two things, viz, purity of mind and purity of conduct
Kurals.Couplet: Both purity of mind, and purity of action clear,Leaning no staff of pure companionship, to man draw near
Kurals.EmotionDetail: desire for self-improvement and purity, reliance on good company for personal growth,

Kurals.Translation: Pure-hearted get good progeny Pure friendship acts with victory
Kurals.Explanation: To the pure-minded there will be a good posterity By those whose associates are pure, no deeds will be done that are not good
Kurals.Couplet: From true pure-minded men a virtuous race proceeds;To men of pure companionship belong no evil deeds

Kurals.Translation: Goodness of mind increases gain Good friendship fosters fame again
Kurals.Explanation: Goodness of mind will give wealth, and good society will bring with it all praise, to men
Kurals.Couplet: Goodness of mind to lives of men increaseth gain;And good companionship doth all of praise obtain

Kurals.Translation: Men of wisdom, though good in mind In friends of worth a new strength find
Kurals.Explanation: Yet good companionship is confirmation strong
Kurals.Couplet: To perfect men, though minds right good belong,Yet good companionship is confirmation strong

Kurals.Translation: Good mind decides the future bliss Good company gains strength to this
Kurals.Explanation: Future bliss is (the result) of goodness of mind; and even this acquires strength from the society of the good
Kurals.Couplet: Although to mental goodness joys of other life belong,Yet good companionship is confirmation strong
Kurals.emotions: Hopefulness – The text conveys anticipation of a positive future ("future bliss") as a result of a good mind and good company., Aspiration – There is a desire for self-im

Kurals.Translation: No help good company exeeds; The bad to untold anguish leads
Kurals.Explanation: There is no greater help than the company of the good; there is no greater source of sorrow than the company of the wicked
Kurals.Couplet: Than good companionship no surer help we know;Than bad companionship nought causes direr woe
Kurals.EmotionDetail: stemming from good company, resulting from bad company,

Summary: Highlights the light of wisdom as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Trust, Purity, Security.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Weigh well output the loss and gain And proper action ascertain
Kurals.Explanation: Let a man reflect on what will be lost, what will be acquired and (from these) what will be his ultimate gain, and (then, let him) act
Kurals.Couplet: Expenditure, return, and profit of the deedIn time to come; weigh these- than to the act proceed

Kurals.Translation: Nothing is hard for him who acts With worthy counsels weighing facts
Kurals.Explanation: There is nothing too difficult to (be attained by) those who, before they act, reflect well themselves, and thoroughly consider (the matter) with chosen friends
Kurals.Couplet: With chosen friends deliberate; next use the private thought;Then act By those who thus proceed all works with ease are wrought

Kurals.Translation: The wise risk not their capital In doubtful gains and lose their all
Kurals.Explanation: Wise men will not, in the hopes of profit, undertake works that will consume their principal
Kurals.Couplet: To risk one's all and lose, aiming at added gain,Is rash affair, from which the wise abstain

Kurals.Translation: They who scornful reproach fear Commence no work not made clear
Kurals.Explanation: Those who fear reproach will not commence anything which has not been (thoroughly considered) and made clear to them
Kurals.Couplet: A work of which the issue is not clear,Begin not they reproachful scorn who fear
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , of reproach,

Kurals.Translation: Who marches without plans and ways His field is sure to foster foes
Kurals.Explanation: One way to promote the prosperity of an enemy, is (for a king) to set out (to war) without having thoroughly weighed his ability (to cope with its chances)
Kurals.Couplet: With plans not well matured to rise against your foe,Is way to plant him out where he is sure to grow
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text emphasizes the importance of careful planning and consideration before taking action., Anxiety – The mention of fostering foes and the risks of acting without preparation suggests underlying worry about negative

Kurals.Translation: Doing unfit action ruins Failing fit-act also ruins
Kurals.Explanation: He will perish who does not what is not fit to do; and he also will perish who does not do what it is fit to
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis ruin if man do an unbefitting thing;Fit things to leave undone will equal ruin bring
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text warns about the dangers of acting without proper consideration

Kurals.Translation: Think and dare a proper deed Dare and think is bad in need
Kurals.Explanation: Consider, and then undertake a matter; after having undertaken it, to say "We will consider," is folly
Kurals.Couplet: Think, and then dare the deed! Who cry,'Deed dared, we'll think,' disgraced shall be

Kurals.Translation: Toil without a plan ahead Is doomed to fall though supported
Kurals.Explanation: The work, which is not done by suitable methods, will fail though many stand to uphold it
Kurals.Couplet: On no right system if man toil and strive,Though many men assist, no work can thrive
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text warns against action without forethought

Kurals.Translation: Attune the deeds to habitude Or ev'n good leads to evil feud
Kurals.Explanation: There are failures even in acting well, when it is done without knowing the various dispositions of men
Kurals.Couplet: Though well the work be done, yet one mistake is made,To habitudes of various men when no regard is paid
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The emphasis on acting with consideration

Kurals.Translation: Do deeds above reproachfulness The world refutes uncomely mess
Kurals.Explanation: Let a man reflect, and do things which bring no reproach; the world will not approve, with him, of things which do not become of his position to adopt
Kurals.Couplet: Plan and perform no work that others may despise;What misbeseems a king the world will not approve as wise

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Prudence, Caution, Confidence.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Judge act and might and foeman's strength The allies' strength and go at length
Kurals.Explanation: Let (one) weigh well the strength of the deed (he purposes to do), his own strength, the strength of his enemy, and the strength of the allies (of both), and then let him act
Kurals.Couplet: The force the strife demands, the force he owns, the force of foes,The force of friends; these should he weigh ere to the war he goes
Kurals.emotions: Caution – There is an emphasis on careful consideration and evaluation before acting., Prudence – The advice reflects wise and thoughtful judgment regarding one’s own and others’ strengths.

Kurals.Translation: Nothing hampers the firm who know What they can and how to go
Kurals.Explanation: There is nothing which may not be accomplished by those who, before they attack (an enemy), make themselves acquainted with their own ability, and with whatever else is (needful) to be known, and
Kurals.Couplet: Who know what can be wrought, with knowledge of the means, on this,Their mind firm set, go forth, nought goes with them amiss

Kurals.Translation: Many know not their meagre might Their pride breaks up in boastful fight
Kurals.Explanation: There are many who, ignorant of their (want of) power (to meet it), have haughtily set out to war, and broken down in the midst of it
Kurals.Couplet: Ill-deeming of their proper powers, have many monarchs striven,And midmost of unequal conflict fallen asunder riven
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , or Shame

Kurals.Translation: Who adapts not, outsteps measure And brags himself-his fall is sure
Kurals.Explanation: He will quickly perish who, ignorant of his own resources flatters himself of his greatness, and does
Kurals.Couplet: Who not agrees with those around, no moderation knows,In self-applause indulging, swift to ruin goes
Kurals.emotions: Pride – The text discusses someone flattering themselves and bragging about their greatness., Ignorance – The person is described as ignorant of their own resources/strength., Downfall

Kurals.Translation: Even the gentle peacock's plume Cart's axle breaks by gross volume
Kurals.Explanation: The axle tree of a bandy, loaded only with peacocks' feathers will break, if it be greatly overloaded
Kurals.Couplet: With peacock feathers light, you load the wain;Yet, heaped too high, the axle snaps in twain
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The warning about overloading (even with something light) creates a sense of caution., Humility – The reminder that even things of beauty or gentleness

Kurals.Translation: Beyond the branches' tip who skips Ends the life as his body rips
Kurals.Explanation: There will be an end to the life of him who, having climbed out to the end of a branch, ventures to go further
Kurals.Couplet: Who daring climbs, and would himself upraiseBeyond the branch's tip, with life the forfeit pays
Kurals.emotions: **Fear** – The imagery of venturing too far and the consequence of death evokes a sense of fear or anxiety about overreaching., **Caution** – The

Kurals.Translation: Know the limit; grant with measure This way give and guard your treasure
Kurals.Explanation: Let a man know the measure of his ability (to give), and let him give accordingly; such giving is the way to preserve his property
Kurals.Couplet: With knowledge of the measure due, as virtue bids you give!That is the way to guard your wealth, and seemly live
Kurals.emotions: Prudence – The text emphasizes careful judgment

Kurals.Translation: The outflow must not be excess No matter how small income is
Kurals.Explanation: Even though the income (of a king) be small, it will not cause his (ruin), if his outgoings be not larger than his income
Kurals.Couplet: Incomings may be scant; but yet, no failure there,If in expenditure you rightly learn to spare

Kurals.Translation: Who does not know to live in bounds His life seems rich but thins and ends
Kurals.Explanation: The prosperity of him who lives without knowing the measure (of his property), will perish, even while it seems to continue
Kurals.Couplet: Who prosperous lives and of enjoyment knows no bound,His seeming wealth, departing, nowhere shall be found
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , about loss or downfall

Kurals.Translation: Wealth amassed quickly vanishes Sans level if one lavishes
Kurals.Explanation: The measure of his wealth will quickly perish, whose liberality weighs not the measure of his property
Kurals.Couplet: Beneficence that measures not its bound of means,Will swiftly bring to nought the wealth on which it leans
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Caution, Prudence, Regret

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Caution, Wisdom, Others’ Strengths..
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: By day the crow defeats the owl Kings need right time their foes to quell
Kurals.Explanation: A crow will overcome an owl in the day time; so the king who would conquer his enemy must have (a suitable) time
Kurals.Couplet: A crow will conquer owl in broad daylight;The king that foes would crush, needs fitting time to fight
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text emphasizes the importance of waiting for the right moment

Kurals.Translation: Well-ordered seasoned act is cord That fortune binds in bon accord
Kurals.Explanation: Acting at the right season, is a cord that will immoveably bind success (to a king)
Kurals.Couplet: The bond binds fortune fast is ordered effort made,Strictly observant still of favouring season's aid

Kurals.Translation: What is hard for him who acts With proper means and time and tacts?
Kurals.Explanation: Is there anything difficult for him to do, who acts, with (the right) instruments at the right time ?
Kurals.Couplet: Can any work be hard in very fact,If men use fitting means in timely act

Kurals.Translation: Choose proper time and act and place Even the world you win with ease
Kurals.Explanation: Though (a man) should meditate (the conquest of) the world, he may accomplish it if he acts in the right time, and at the right place
Kurals.Couplet: The pendant world's dominion may be won,In fitting time and place by action done
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 emotions associated with the text:, Wisdom – The text emphasizes the importance of knowledge

Kurals.Translation: Who want to win the world sublime Wait unruffled biding their time
Kurals.Explanation: They who thoughtfully consider and wait for the (right) time (for action), may successfully meditate (the conquest of) the world
Kurals.Couplet: Who think the pendant world itself to subjugate,With mind unruffled for the fitting time must wait

Kurals.Translation: By self-restraint stalwarts keep fit Like rams retreating but to butt
Kurals.Explanation: The self-restraint of the energetic (while waiting for a suitable opportunity), is like the drawing back of a fighting-ram in order to butt
Kurals.Couplet: The men of mighty power their hidden energies repress,As fighting ram recoils to rush on foe with heavier stress

Kurals.Translation: The wise jut not their vital fire They watch their time with hidden ire
Kurals.Explanation: The wise will not immediately and hastily shew out their anger; they will watch their time, and restrain it within
Kurals.Couplet: The glorious once of wrath enkindled make no outward show,At once; they bide their time, while hidden fires within them glow
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , controlled or suppressed,

Kurals.Translation: Bear with hostiles when you meet them Fell down their head in fateful time
Kurals.Explanation: If one meets his enemy, let him show him all respect, until the time for his destruction is come; when that is come, his head will be easily brought low
Kurals.Couplet: If foes' detested form they see, with patience let them bear;When fateful hour at last they spy,- the head lies there
Kurals.emotions: Patience – The text advises enduring and bearing with hostiles until the appropriate moment., Restraint – It emphasizes showing respect and holding back immediate action

Kurals.Translation: When comes the season ripe and rare Dare and do hard things then and there
Kurals.Explanation: If a rare opportunity occurs, while it lasts, let a man do that which is rarely to be accomplished (but for such an opportunity)
Kurals.Couplet: When hardest gain of opportunity at last is won,With promptitude let hardest deed be done
Kurals.emotions: Anticipation – The text speaks about recognizing and waiting for the right opportunity
Kurals.EmotionDetail: "When comes the season ripe and rare"

Kurals.Translation: In waiting time feign peace like stork In fighting time strike like its peck
Kurals.Explanation: At the time when one should use self-control, let him restrain himself like a heron; and, let him like it, strike, when there is a favourable opportunity
Kurals.Couplet: As heron stands with folded wing, so wait in waiting hour;As heron snaps its prey, when fortune smiles, put forth your power
Kurals.emotions: Patience – Emphasized through the advice to wait and exercise self-control., Restraint – Highlighted by the suggestion to restrain oneself during waiting times. 3

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Patience, Clarity, Assurance.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: No action take, no foe despise Until you have surveyed the place
Kurals.Explanation: Let not (a king) despise (an enemy), nor undertake any thing (against him), until he has obtained (a suitable) place for besieging him
Kurals.Couplet: Begin no work of war, depise no foe,Till place where you can wholly circumvent you know

Kurals.Translation: Many are gains of fortresses Ev'n to kings of power and prowess
Kurals.Explanation: Even to those who are men of power and expedients, an attack in connection with a fortification will yield many advantages
Kurals.Couplet: Though skill in war combine with courage tried on battle-field,The added gain of fort doth great advantage yield
Kurals.emotions: Security – The mention of fortresses and advantages suggests a sense of safety and protection., Confidence – The reference to “kings of power and prowess” implies strength and assured capability.

Kurals.Translation: Weaklings too withstand foe's offence In proper fields of strong defence
Kurals.Explanation: Even the powerless will become powerful and conquer, if they select a proper field (of action), and guard themselves, while they make war on their enemies
Kurals.Couplet: E'en weak ones mightily prevail, if place of strong defence,They find, protect themselves, and work their foes offence
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text emphasizes careful selection of the right place and defensive strategy

Kurals.Translation: If fighters fight in vantage field The plans of foes shall be baffled
Kurals.Explanation: If they who draw near (to fight) choose a suitable place to approach (their enemy), the latter, will have to relinquish the thought which they once entertained, of conquering them
Kurals.Couplet: The foes who thought to triumph, find their thoughts were vain,If hosts advance, seize vantage ground, and thence the fight maintain
Kurals.emotions: Confidence – Fighters choosing a suitable place implies self-assurance and readiness., Caution – The emphasis on strategy and proper positioning suggests careful planning and wariness., Determination –

Kurals.Translation: In water crocodile prevails In land before others it fails
Kurals.Explanation: In deep water, a crocodile will conquer (all other animals); but if it leave the water, other animals will conquer it
Kurals.Couplet: The crocodile prevails in its own flow of water wide,If this it leaves, 'tis slain by anything beside
Kurals.emotions: Vulnerability – The idea that strength is context-dependent evokes a sense of vulnerability when one is out of their element., Humility – Recognizing that one cannot always dominate or prevail inst

Kurals.Translation: Sea-going ship goes not on shore Nor on sea the strong-wheeled car
Kurals.Explanation: Wide chariots, with mighty wheels, will not run on the ocean; neither will ships that the traverse ocean, move on the earth
Kurals.Couplet: The lofty car, with mighty wheel, sails not o'er watery main,The boat that skims the sea, runs not on earth's hard plain
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – The text conveys an understanding of appropriateness and the importance of knowing one’s proper place

Kurals.Translation: No aid but daring dash they need When field is chosen right for deed
Kurals.Explanation: You will need no other aid than fearlessness, if you thoroughly reflect (on what you are to do), and select (a suitable) place for your operations
Kurals.Couplet: Save their own fearless might they need no other aid,If in right place they fight, all due provision made
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Fearlessness, ,

Kurals.Translation: Though force is small, if place is right One quells a foe of well-armed might
Kurals.Explanation: The power of one who has a large army will perish, if he goes into ground where only a small army can act
Kurals.Couplet: If lord of army vast the safe retreat assailOf him whose host is small, his mightiest efforts fail

Kurals.Translation: To face a foe at home is vain Though fort and status are not fine
Kurals.Explanation: It is a hazardous thing to attack men in their own country, although they may neither have power nor a good fortress
Kurals.Couplet: Though fort be none, and store of wealth they lack,'Tis hard a people's homesteads to attack
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , for adversaries, of potential danger

Kurals.Translation: A fox can kill a war tusker Fearless with feet in deep quagmire
Kurals.Explanation: A fox can kill a fearless, warrior-faced elephant, if it go into mud in which its legs sink down
Kurals.Couplet: The jackal slays, in miry paths of foot-betraying fen,The elephant of fearless eye and tusks transfixing armed men
Kurals.emotions: Vulnerability – The powerful being brought low due to circumstances

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Caution, Confidence, Respect.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Pleasure, gold, fear of life Virtue- Test by these four and trust the true
Kurals.Explanation: Let (a minister) be chosen, after he has been tried by means of these four things, viz,-his virtue, (love of) money, (love of) sexual pleasure, and tear of (losing) life
Kurals.Couplet: How treats he virtue, wealth and pleasure? How, when life's at stake,Comports himself? This four-fold test of man will full assurance make

Kurals.Translation: Spotless name of noble birth Shamed of stain-that choice is worth
Kurals.Explanation: (The king's) choice should (fall) on him, who is of good family, who is free from faults, and who has the modesty which fears the wounds (of sin)
Kurals.Couplet: Of noble race, of faultless worth, of generous prideThat shrinks from shame or stain; in him may king confide
Kurals.EmotionDetail: in noble birth and spotless name,

Kurals.Translation: Though deep scholars of stainless sense Rare is freedom from ignorance
Kurals.Explanation: When even men, who have studied the most difficult works, and who are free from faults, are (carefully) examined, it is a rare thing to find them without ignorance
Kurals.Couplet: Though deeply learned, unflecked by fault, 'tis rare to see,When closely scanned, a man from all unwisdom free
Kurals.emotions: Humility – The acknowledgment that even great scholars are not free from ignorance evokes humility., Acceptance – The realization that ignorance is a common human trait fosters a sense of acceptance.

Kurals.Translation: Good and evil in man weigh well Judge him by virtues which prevail
Kurals.Explanation: Let (a king) consider (a man's) good qualities, as well as his faults, and then judge (of his character) by that which prevails
Kurals.Couplet: Weigh well the good of each, his failings closely scan,As these or those prevail, so estimate the man
Kurals.emotions: Fairness (or Justice) – Emphasizing judging someone by both their good and bad qualities and weighing them fairly., Thoughtfulness (or Mindfulness) – Encouraging

Kurals.Translation: By the touchstone of deeds is seen If any one is great or mean
Kurals.Explanation: A man's deeds are the touchstone of his greatness and littleness
Kurals.Couplet: Of greatness and of meanness too,The deeds of each are touchstone true
Kurals.emotions: Judgement (discernment) – Evaluating people based on their actions., Respect – Admiring greatness achieved through good deeds., Humility – Recognizing that one's worth

Kurals.Translation: Choose not those men without kinsmen Without affine or shame of sin
Kurals.Explanation: Let (a king) avoid choosing men who have no relations; such men have no attachment, and thereforehave no fear of crime
Kurals.Couplet: Beware of trusting men who have no kith of kin;No bonds restrain such men, no shame deters from sin
Kurals.emotions: Caution — The text advises careful selection

Kurals.Translation: On favour leaning fools you choose; Folly in all its forms ensues
Kurals.Explanation: Yields all his being up to folly's blind control
Kurals.Couplet: By fond affection led who trusts in men of unwise soul,Yields all his being up to folly's blind control

Kurals.Translation: To trust an untried stranger brings Endless troubles on all our kins
Kurals.Explanation: Sorrow that will not leave even his posterity will come upon him chooses a stranger whose character he has not known
Kurals.Couplet: Who trusts an untried stranger, brings disgrace,Remediless, on all his race

Kurals.Translation: Trust not without testing and then Find proper work for trusted men
Kurals.Explanation: Let (a king) choose no one without previous consideration; after he has made his choice, let him unhesitatingly select for each such duties as are appropriate
Kurals.Couplet: Trust no man whom you have not fully tried,When tested, in his prudence proved confide

Kurals.Translation: Trust without test; The trusted doubt; Both entail troubles in and out
Kurals.Explanation: To make choice of one who has not been examined, and to entertain doubts respecting one who has been chosen, will produce irremediable sorrow
Kurals.Couplet: Trust where you have not tried, doubt of a friend to feel,Once trusted, wounds inflict that nought can heal

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Regret, Sorrow, Fear  Pride.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Employ the wise who will discern The good and bad and do good turn
Kurals.Explanation: He should be employed (by a king), whose nature leads him to choose the good, after having weighed both the evil and the good in any undertaking
Kurals.Couplet: Who good and evil scanning, ever makes the good his joy;Such man of virtuous mood should king employ
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – The text emphasizes discernment and good judgment., Trust – It suggests employing those who can be relied upon to choose rightly., Confidence – There is encouragement to act with

Kurals.Translation: Let him act who resource swells; Fosters wealth and prevents ills
Kurals.Explanation: Let him do (the king's) work who can enlarge the sources (of revenue), increase wealth and considerately prevent the accidents (which would destroy it)
Kurals.Couplet: Who swells the revenues, spreads plenty o'er the land,Seeks out what hinders progress, his the workman's hand
Kurals.emotions: Determination – The emphasis on "acting" and achieving results through resourcefulness reflects a strong sense of purpose and resolve., Responsibility – The text conveys the importance of choosing someone capable and

Kurals.Translation: Trust him in whom these four you see: Love, wit, non-craving, clarity
Kurals.Explanation: Let the choice (of a king) fall upon him who largely possesses these four things, love, knowledge, a clear mind and freedom from covetousness
Kurals.Couplet: A loyal love with wisdom, clearness, mind from avarice free;Who hath these four good gifts should ever trusted be
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , sense of peace/calmness

Kurals.Translation: Though tried and found fit, yet we see Many differ before duty
