Kurals.Explanation: Even when (a king) has tried them in every possible way, there are many men who change, from the nature of the works (in which they may be employed)
Kurals.Couplet: Even when tests of every kind are multiplied,Full many a man proves otherwise, by action tried

Kurals.Translation: Wise able men with power invest Not by fondness but by hard test
Kurals.Explanation: (A king's) work can only be accomplished by a man of wisdom and patient endurance; it is not of a nature to be given to one from mere personal attachment
Kurals.Couplet: No specious fav'rite should the king's commission bear,But he that knows, and work performs with patient care
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Determination, ,

Kurals.Translation: Discern the agent and the deed And just in proper time proceed
Kurals.Explanation: Let (a king) act, after having considered the agent (whom he is to employ), the deed (he desires to do), and the time which is suitable to it
Kurals.Couplet: Let king first ask, 'Who shall the deed perform?' and 'What the deed?'Of hour befitting both assured, let every work proceed
Kurals.emotions: Caution – Reflecting the careful consideration before taking action., Determination – Emphasizing the intent to act decisively once deliberation is complete., Prudence – Highlight

Kurals.Translation: This work, by this, this man can do Like this entrust the duty due
Kurals.Explanation: After having considered, "this man can accomplish this, by these means", let (the king) leave with him the discharge of that duty
Kurals.Couplet: 'This man, this work shall thus work out,' let thoughtful king command;Then leave the matter wholly in his servant's hand
Kurals.emotions: Trust – The act of entrusting a duty to someone implies a sense of confidence and reliance., Determination – The theme of acting with purpose and ensuring a task is completed is central

Kurals.Translation: His fitness for the duty scan Leave him to do the best he can
Kurals.Explanation: Having considered what work a man is fit for, let (the king) employ him in that work
Kurals.Couplet: As each man's special aptitude is known,Bid each man make that special work his own

Kurals.Translation: Who do duty for duty's sake Doubt them; and fortune departs quick
Kurals.Explanation: Prosperity will leave (the king) who doubts the friendship of the man who steadily labours in the discharge of his duties
Kurals.Couplet: Fortune deserts the king who ill can bear,Informal friendly ways of men his tolls who share

Kurals.Translation: Worker straight the world is straight The king must look to this aright
Kurals.Explanation: Let a king daily examine the conduct of his servants; if they do not act crookedly, the world will not act crookedly
Kurals.Couplet: Let king search out his servants' deeds each day;When these do right, the world goes rightly on its way
Kurals.emotions: Responsibility – Emphasizing the duty of leaders (kings) to oversee and guide their servants’ conduct., Determination – Highlighting the importance of consistent and resolute action to

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Trust, Determination, Good Judgment..
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Let fortunes go; yet kinsmen know The old accustomed love to show
Kurals.Explanation: Even when (a man's) property is all gone, relatives will act towards him with their accustomed (kindness)
Kurals.Couplet: When wealth is fled, old kindness still to show,Is kindly grace that only kinsmen know

Kurals.Translation: The gift of loving Kins bestows Fadeless fortune's fresh flowers
Kurals.Explanation: If (a man's) relatives remain attached to him with unchanging love, it will be a source of everincreasing wealth
Kurals.Couplet: The gift of kin's unfailing love bestowsMuch gain of good, like flower that fadeless blows

Kurals.Translation: A kinless wealth is like a tank Which overflows without a bank
Kurals.Explanation: The wealth of one who does not mingle freely with his relatives, will be like the filling of water in a
Kurals.Couplet: His joy of life who mingles not with kinsmen gathered round,Is lake where streams pour in, with no encircling bound
Kurals.emotions: Loneliness – Emphasized by the phrase "kinless wealth" and the lack of mingling with relatives., Emptiness – The analogy of wealth being like a tank overflowing

Kurals.Translation: The fruit of growing wealth is gained When kith and kin are happy found
Kurals.Explanation: To live surrounded by relatives, is the advantage to be derived from the acquisition of wealth
Kurals.Couplet: The profit gained by wealth's increase,Is living compassed round by relatives in peace

Kurals.Translation: Loving words and liberal hand Encircle kith and kin around
Kurals.Explanation: He will be surrounded by numerous relatives who manifests generosity and affability
Kurals.Couplet: Who knows the use of pleasant words, and liberal gifts can give,Connections, heaps of them, surrounding him shall live
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for kith and kin, liberal hand,

Kurals.Translation: Large giver and wrathless man Commands on earth countless kinsmen
Kurals.Explanation: No one, in all the world, will have so many relatives (about him), as he who makes large gift, and does not give way to anger
Kurals.Couplet: Than one who gifts bestows and wrath restrains,Through the wide world none larger following gains
Kurals.emotions: Generosity – Highlighted by the praise for the "large giver" who makes generous gifts., Serenity (or Calmness) – Emphasized by the value placed on

Kurals.Translation: The crows hide not; thy call and eat Welfare abides a man of heart
Kurals.Explanation: The crows do not conceal (their prey), but will call out for others (to share with them) while they eat it; wealth will be with those who show a similar disposition (towards their relatives)
Kurals.Couplet: The crows conceal not, call their friends to come, then eat;Increase of good such worthy ones shall meet
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with this text are:, Generosity, Love, Compassion
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , for kin/relatives,

Kurals.Translation: From public gaze when kings perceive Each one's merits so many thrive
Kurals.Explanation: Many relatives will live near a king, when they observe that he does not look on all alike, but that he looks on each man according to his merit
Kurals.Couplet: Where king regards not all alike, but each in his degree,'Neath such discerning rule many dwell happily
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with the provided text are:, Appreciation, Pride, Motivation
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , for recognizing individual merit, felt by those whose merits are acknowledged, encouragement to demonstrate merit to gain favor

Kurals.Translation: Forsaken friends will come and stay When cause for discord goes away
Kurals.Explanation: Those who have been friends and have afterwards forsaken him, will return and join themselves (to him), when the cause of disagreement is not to be found in him
Kurals.Couplet: Who once were his, and then forsook him, as beforeWill come around, when cause of disagreement is no more

Kurals.Translation: Who leaves and returns with motive The king should test him and receive
Kurals.Explanation: When one may have left him, and for some cause has returned to him, let the king fulfil the object (for which he has come back) and thoughtfully receive him again
Kurals.Couplet: Who causeless went away, then to return, for any cause, ask leave;The king should sift their motives well, consider, and receive

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Love, Security, Gratitude.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Worse than wrath in excess is Forgetfulness in joy-excess
Kurals.Explanation: More evil than excessive anger, is forgetfulness which springs from the intoxication of great joy
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis greater ill, it rapture of o'erweening gladness to the soulBring self-forgetfulness than if transcendent wrath control

Kurals.Translation: Negligence kills renown just as Ceaseless want wisdom destroys
Kurals.Explanation: Forgetfulness will destroy fame, even as constant poverty destroys knowledge
Kurals.Couplet: Perpetual, poverty is death to wisdom of the wise;When man forgets himself his glory dies
Kurals.emotions: Regret – Reflecting on what is lost through negligence or forgetfulness., Concern – Worry about losing reputation or wisdom due to neglect or poverty., Awareness – Realization

Kurals.Translation: Forgetful nature fails of fame All schools of thinkers say the same
Kurals.Explanation: Thoughtlessness will never acquire fame; and this tenet is upheld by all treatises in the world
Kurals.Couplet: 'To self-oblivious men no praise'; this ruleDecisive wisdom sums of every school

Kurals.Translation: The fearful find no fortress here The forgetful find good never
Kurals.Explanation: Just as the coward has no defence (by whatever fortifications ha may be surrounded), so the thoughtless has no good (whatever advantages he may possess)
Kurals.Couplet: 'To cowards is no fort's defence'; e'en soThe self-oblivious men no blessing know
Kurals.emotions: Fear – Referenced by "the fearful" and "the coward

Kurals.Translation: Failing foresight the guardless man Shall rue his folly later on
Kurals.Explanation: The thoughtless man, who provides not against the calamities that may happen, will afterwards repent for his fault
Kurals.Couplet: To him who nought foresees, recks not of anything,The after woe shall sure repentance bring

Kurals.Translation: Forget none; watch with wakeful care Miss none; the gain is sans compare
Kurals.Explanation: There is nothing comparable with the possession of unfailing thoughtfulness at all times; and towards all persons
Kurals.Couplet: Towards all unswerving, ever watchfulness of soul retain,Where this is found there is no greater gain
Kurals.EmotionDetail: or Mindfulness, ,

Kurals.Translation: With cautious care pursue a thing Impossible there is nothing
Kurals.Explanation: There is nothing too difficult to be accomplished, if a man set about it carefully, with unflinching endeavour
Kurals.Couplet: Though things are arduous deemed, there's nought may not be won,When work with mind's unslumbering energy and thought is done
Kurals.emotions: Determination – The emphasis on “cautious care” and “unflinching endeavour” points to a strong sense of resolve., Hope – The assertion that “

Kurals.Translation: Do what the wise commend as worth If not, for seven births no mirth
Kurals.Explanation: Let (a man) observe and do these things which have been praised (by the wise); if he neglects and fails to perform them, for him there will be no (happiness) throughout the seven births
Kurals.Couplet: Let things that merit praise thy watchful soul employ;Who these despise attain through sevenfold births no joy
Kurals.emotions: Duty — Reflects a sense of responsibility to follow what the wise commend., Fear — Implies apprehension about the consequences of neglecting these duties., Regret — Suggest

Kurals.Translation: When joy deludes, their fate recall Whom negligence has made to fall
Kurals.Explanation: Let (a king) think of those who have been ruined by neglect, when his mind is elated with joy
Kurals.Couplet: Think on the men whom scornful mind hath brought to nought,When exultation overwhelms thy wildered thought

Kurals.Translation: Easy it is a thing to get When the mind on it is set
Kurals.Explanation: It is easy for (one) to obtain whatever he may think of, if he can again think of it
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis easy what thou hast in mind to gain,If what thou hast in mind thy mind retain
Kurals.emotions: Determination – The focus on setting one's mind on a goal conveys a sense of resolve and persistence., Hope – The idea that one can obtain what they think of inspires optimism about achieving

Summary: Highlights the light of wisdom as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Awareness – Realization, Regret, Disappointment.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Test and attest impartially Consult and act the laws justly
Kurals.Explanation: To examine into (the crimes which may be committed), to show no favour (to any one), to desire to act with impartiality towards all, and to inflict (such punishments) as may be wisely resolved on,
Kurals.Couplet: Search out, to no one favour show; with heart that justice lovesConsult, then act; this is the rule that right approves

Kurals.Translation: The earth looks up to sky and thrives And mankind to king's rod of justice
Kurals.Explanation: When there is rain, the living creation thrives; and so when the king rules justly, his subjects thrive
Kurals.Couplet: All earth looks up to heav'n whence raindrops fall;All subjects look to king that ruleth all

Kurals.Translation: The Sage's scripture and virtue spring From the sceptre of a stately king
Kurals.Explanation: The sceptre of the king is the firm support of the Vedas of the Brahmin, and of all virtues therein described
Kurals.Couplet: Learning and virtue of the sages spring,From all-controlling sceptre of the king
Kurals.emotions: Respect – The passage shows reverence for justice

Kurals.Translation: The world clings to the ruler's feet Whose sceptre clasps the people's heart
Kurals.Explanation: The world will constantly embrace the feet of the great king who rules over his subjects with love
Kurals.Couplet: Whose heart embraces subjects all, lord over mighty landWho rules, the world his feet embracing stands

Kurals.Translation: Full rains and yields enrich the land Which is ruled by a righteous hand
Kurals.Explanation: Rain and plentiful crops will ever dwell together in the country of the king who sways his sceptre with justice
Kurals.Couplet: Where king, who righteous laws regards, the sceptre wields,There fall the showers, there rich abundance crowns the fields
Kurals.EmotionDetail: from the assurance of stability and prosperity under just rule, towards a just leader bringing benefits to the people,

Kurals.Translation: Not the spear but the sceptre straight That brings success to monarch's might
Kurals.Explanation: It is not the javelin that gives victory, but the king's sceptre, if it do no injustice
Kurals.Couplet: Not lance gives kings the victory,But sceptre swayed with equity
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Fairness: The text emphasizes the importance of just rule and fairness

Kurals.Translation: The king protects the entire earth And justice protects his royal worth
Kurals.Explanation: The king defends the whole world; and justice, when administered without defect, defends the king
Kurals.Couplet: The king all the whole realm of earth protects;And justice guards the king who right respects
Kurals.emotions: Responsibility – The text reflects a deep sense of duty and obligation

Kurals.Translation: Hard of access, the unjust king He shall himself his ruin bring
Kurals.Explanation: The king who gives not facile audience (to those who approach him), and who does not examine and pass judgment (on their complaints), will perish in disgrace
Kurals.Couplet: Hard of access, nought searching out, with partial handThe king who rules, shall sink and perish from the land
Kurals.emotions: **Injustice** – There is a clear emphasis on unfair treatment by the king and the resulting negative consequences., **Disappointment** – The unmet expectations of those seeking justice

Kurals.Translation: Save his subjects and chide the wrong Is flawless duty of a king
Kurals.Explanation: In guarding his subjects (against injury from others), and in preserving them himself; to punish crime
Kurals.Couplet: Abroad to guard, at home to punish, bringsNo just reproach; 'tis work assigned to kings

Kurals.Translation: Killing killers, the king, behold Weeds removes from cropful field
Kurals.Explanation: For a king to punish criminals with death, is like pulling up the weeds in the green corn
Kurals.Couplet: By punishment of death the cruel to restrain,Is as when farmer frees from weeds the tender grain
Kurals.emotions: Justice – The text centers around the concept of delivering appropriate consequences and maintaining moral or social order., Determination – The act of removing "weeds" (criminals) reflects a

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Fairness, Responsibility, Security.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The unjust tyrant oppressor Is worse than cruel murderer
Kurals.Explanation: The king who gives himself up to oppression and acts unjustly (towards his subjects) is more cruel than the man who leads the life of a murderer
Kurals.Couplet: Than one who plies the murderer's trade, more cruel is the kingWho all injustice works, his subjects harassing
Kurals.EmotionDetail: towards oppression and injustice, of tyranny and cruel leadership, for the suffering caused by unjust rulers

Kurals.Translation: Sceptered tyrant exacting gold Is \"give\" of lanced robber bold
Kurals.Explanation: The request (for money) of him who holds the sceptre is like the word of a highway robber who stands with a weapon in hand and says "give up your wealth"
Kurals.Couplet: As 'Give' the robber cries with lance uplift,So kings with sceptred hand implore a gift
Kurals.emotions: Fear – The mention of a tyrant and the comparison to a highway robber evoke a sense of fear or intimidation., Oppression – The text conveys the feeling of being controlled or exploited

Kurals.Translation: Spy wrongs daily and do justice Or day by day the realm decays
Kurals.Explanation: The country of the king who does not daily examine into the wrongs done and distribute justice, will daily fall to ruin
Kurals.Couplet: Who makes no daily search for wrongs, nor justly rules, that kingDoth day by day his realm to ruin bring
Kurals.emotions: **Concern/Worry** – about the decay and ruin of the kingdom., **Injustice** – arising from unchecked wrongs and absence of justice., **Responsibility**

Kurals.Translation: The king shall wealth and subjects lose If his sceptre he dares abuse
Kurals.Explanation: The king, who, without reflecting (on its evil consequences), perverts justice, will lose at once both his wealth and his subjects
Kurals.Couplet: Whose rod from right deflects, who counsel doth refuse,At once his wealth and people utterly shall lose
Kurals.emotions: Warning/Fear – Concern about the negative consequences of abusing power., Injustice/Indignation – Recognition of unfairness or the perversion of justice., Loss/Sadness

Kurals.Translation: Groaning tears caused by tyrant's sway File the royal wealth away
Kurals.Explanation: Will not the tears, shed by a people who cannot endure the oppression which they suffer (from their king), become a saw to waste away his wealth ?
Kurals.Couplet: His people's tears of sorrow past endurance, are not theySharp instruments to wear the monarch's wealth away

Kurals.Translation: Glory endures by sceptre right Without it wanes the royal light
Kurals.Explanation: Righteous government gives permanence to (the fame of) kings; without that their fame will have no endurance
Kurals.Couplet: To rulers' rule stability is sceptre right;When this is not, quenched is the rulers' light
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 emotions associated with the text are:, Reverence, Caution, Admiration
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , for righteous government and enduring fame, about the consequences of tyranny or unrighteous rule, for justice and rightful authority

Kurals.Translation: Dry like the earth without rainfall Is graceless king to creatures all
Kurals.Explanation: As is the world without rain, so live a people whose king is without kindness
Kurals.Couplet: As lack of rain to thirsty lands beneath,Is lack of grace in kings to all that breathe
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Desolation, Hopelessness, Longing

Kurals.Translation: To have is worse than having not If ruler is unjust despot
Kurals.Explanation: Property gives more sorrow than poverty, to those who live under the sceptre of a king without justice
Kurals.Couplet: To poverty it adds a sharper sting,To live beneath the sway of unjust king

Kurals.Translation: The sky withdraws season's shower If the king misuses his power
Kurals.Explanation: If the king acts contrary to justice, rain will become unseasonable, and the heavens will withhold their showers
Kurals.Couplet: Where king from right deflecting, makes unrighteous gain,The seasons change, the clouds pour down no rain
Kurals.emotions: Fear – Fear of the consequences when power is misused and justice is not served., Concern – Concern for the well-being of the people and the impact of tyranny on nature

Kurals.Translation: The *six-functioned forget their lore Cows give less if kings guard no more * the six functions are: learning, teaching, giving, getting, sacrificing, kindling sacrifice These are duties of Vedic savants
Kurals.Explanation: If the guardian (of the country) neglects to guard it, the produce of the cows will fail, and the men of six duties viz, the Brahmins will forget the vedas
Kurals.Couplet: Where guardian guardeth not, udder of kine grows dry,And Brahmans' sacred lore will all forgotten lie
Kurals.emotions: Anxiety – Concern about neglect from guardians leading to societal disorder and loss., Fear – Worry that traditional knowledge and resources (like the Vedas and cows) will diminish., Sadness – Regret or sorrow over the potential

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Injustice, Sorrow, Oppression.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: A king enquires and gives sentence Just to prevent future offence
Kurals.Explanation: He is a king who having equitably examined (any injustice which has been brought to his notice), suitably punishes it, so that it may not be again committed
Kurals.Couplet: Who punishes, investigation made in due degree,So as to stay advance of crime, a king is he
Kurals.EmotionDetail: or Fairness, , or Preventiveness

Kurals.Translation: Wield fast the rod but gently lay This strict mildness prolongs the sway
Kurals.Explanation: Let the king, who desires that his prosperity may long remain, commence his preliminary enquires with strictness, and then punish with mildness
Kurals.Couplet: For length of days with still increasing joys on Heav'n who call,Should raise the rod with brow severe, but let it gently fall

Kurals.Translation: His cruel rod of dreadful deed Brings king's ruin quick indeed
Kurals.Explanation: The cruel-sceptred king, who acts so as to put his subjects in fear, will certainly and quickly come to ruin
Kurals.Couplet: Where subjects dread of cruel wrongs endure,Ruin to unjust king is swift and sure
Kurals.emotions: Fear – The subjects experience fear due to the king's cruel actions., Cruelty – The king's behavior is described as harsh and merciless., Ruin

Kurals.Translation: As men the king a tyrant call His days dwindled, hasten his fall
Kurals.Explanation: The king who is spoken of as cruel will quickly perish; his life becoming shortened
Kurals.Couplet: 'Ah! cruel is our king', where subjects sadly say,His age shall dwindle, swift his joy of life decay
Kurals.EmotionDetail: of tyranny and cruelty, about the king's fate and downfall, of the king's impending fall or retribution

Kurals.Translation: Whose sight is scarce, whose face is foul His wealth seems watched by a ghoul
Kurals.Explanation: The great wealth of him who is difficult of access and possesses a sternness of countenance, is like that which has been obtained by a devil
Kurals.Couplet: Whom subjects scarce may see, of harsh forbidding countenance;His ample wealth shall waste, blasted by demon's glance
Kurals.emotions: Fear – The text uses words such as “terrifying

Kurals.Translation: Whose word is harsh, whose sight is rude His wealth and power quickly fade
Kurals.Explanation: The abundant wealth of the king whose words are harsh and whose looks are void of kindness, will instantly perish instead of abiding long, with him
Kurals.Couplet: The tyrant, harsh in speach and hard of eye,His ample joy, swift fading, soon shall die
Kurals.emotions: Fear – The text conveys a sense of fear or dread regarding the consequences of harshness and unkindness., Disapproval – There is a strong tone of disapproval toward harsh words

Kurals.Translation: Reproofs rough and punishments rude Like files conquering power corrode
Kurals.Explanation: Severe words and excessive punishments will be a file to waste away a king's power for destroying (his enemies)
Kurals.Couplet: Harsh words and punishments severe beyond the right,Are file that wears away the monarch's conquering might

Kurals.Translation: The king who would not take counsels Rages with wrath-his fortune fails
Kurals.Explanation: The prosperity of that king will waste away, who without reflecting (on his affairs himself), commits them to his ministers, and (when a failure occurs) gives way to anger, and rages against them
Kurals.Couplet: Who leaves the work to those around, and thinks of it no more;If he in wrathful mood reprove, his prosperous days are o'er
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with this text are:, Anger – The king "rages with wrath" and gives way to anger., Frustration – The king is upset when failures occur and reacts negatively.

Kurals.Translation: The king who builds not fort betimes Fears his foes in wars and dies
Kurals.Explanation: The king who has not provided himself with a place of defence, will in times of war be seized with fear and quickly perish
Kurals.Couplet: Who builds no fort whence he may foe defy,In time of war shall fear and swiftly die
Kurals.emotions: Fear – The threat of war and lack of defense causes the king to be afraid., Anxiety – The anticipation of danger due to unpreparedness creates worry and unease.

Kurals.Translation: The crushing burden borne by earth Is tyrants bound to fools uncouth
Kurals.Explanation: The earth bears up no greater burden than ignorant men whom a cruel sceptre attaches to itself (as the ministers of its evil deeds)
Kurals.Couplet: Tyrants with fools their counsels share:Earth can no heavier burthen bear

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Responsibility, Justice, Caution Or Fairness.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Living in the world implies The bounteous dame of benign eyes
Kurals.Explanation: The world exists through that greatest ornament (of princes), a gracious demeanour
Kurals.Couplet: Since true benignity, that grace exceeding great, residesIn kingly souls, world in happy state abides

Kurals.Translation: World lives by looks of lovely worth Who lack them are burdens of earth
Kurals.Explanation: The prosperity of the world springs from the kindliness, the existence of those who have no (kindliness) is a burden to the earth
Kurals.Couplet: The world goes on its wonted way, since grace benign is there;All other men are burthen for the earth to bear

Kurals.Translation: Of tuneless song what is the use? Without gracious looks what are eyes?
Kurals.Explanation: Of what avail is a song if it be inconsistent with harmony ? what is the use of eyes which possess no kindliness
Kurals.Couplet: Where not accordant with the song, what use of sounding chordsWhat gain of eye that no benignant light affords

Kurals.Translation: Except that they are on the face What for are eyes sans measured grace
Kurals.Explanation: Beyond appearing to be in the face, what good do they do, those eyes in which is no well-regulated kindness ?
Kurals.Couplet: The seeming eye of face gives no expressive light,When not with duly meted kindness bright
Kurals.EmotionDetail: or lack thereof, ,

Kurals.Translation: Kind looks are jewels for eyes to wear Without them they are felt as sore
Kurals.Explanation: Kind looks are the ornaments of the eyes; without these they will be considered (by the wise) to be merely two sores
Kurals.Couplet: Benignity is eyes' adorning grace;Without it eyes are wounds disfiguring face
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , in the absence of kindness

Kurals.Translation: Like trees on inert earth they grow Who don't eye to eye kindness show
Kurals.Explanation: They resemble the trees of the earth, who although they have eyes, never look kindly (on others)
Kurals.Couplet: Whose eyes 'neath brow infixed diffuse no rayOf grace; like tree in earth infixed are they

Kurals.Translation: Ungracious men lack real eyes Men of real eyes show benign grace
Kurals.Explanation: Men without kind looks are men without eyes; those who (really) have eyes are also not devoid of kind looks
Kurals.Couplet: Eyeless are they whose eyes with no benignant lustre shine;Who've eyes can never lack the light of grace benign
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , Understanding/Perception

Kurals.Translation: Who gracious are but dutiful Have right for this earth beautiful
Kurals.Explanation: The world is theirs (kings) who are able to show kindness, without injury to their affairs, (administration of justice)
Kurals.Couplet: Who can benignant smile, yet leave no work undone;By them as very own may all the earth be won
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Sense of Responsibility,

Kurals.Translation: To be benign and bear with foes Who vex us is true virtue's phase
Kurals.Explanation: Patiently to bear with, and show kindness to those who grieve us, is the most excellent of all dispositions
Kurals.Couplet: To smile on those that vex, with kindly face,Enduring long, is most excelling grace

Kurals.Translation: Men of graceful courtesy Take hemlock and look cheerfully
Kurals.Explanation: Those who desire (to cultivate that degree of) urbanity which all shall love, even after swallowing the poison served to them by their friends, will be friendly with them
Kurals.Couplet: They drink with smiling grace, though poison interfused they see,Who seek the praise of all-esteemed courtesy
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , graciousness

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Kindness, Compassion, Grace.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: A king should treat these two as eyes The code of laws and careful spies
Kurals.Explanation: Let a king consider as his eyes these two things, a spy and a book (of laws) universally esteemed
Kurals.Couplet: These two: the code renowned and spies,In these let king confide as eyes

Kurals.Translation: All that happens, always, to all The king should know in full detail
Kurals.Explanation: 'Tis duty of the king to learn with speed
Kurals.Couplet: Each day, of every subject every deed,'Tis duty of the king to learn with speed

Kurals.Translation: Conquests are not for the monarch Who cares not for the Spy's remark
Kurals.Explanation: There is no way for a king to obtain conquests, who knows not the advantage of discoveries made by a spy
Kurals.Couplet: By spies who spies, not weighing things they bring,Nothing can victory give to that unwary king
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text highlights the importance of vigilance and being careful through gathering information., Curiosity – The use of spies and the pursuit of discoveries indicate a strong sense of wanting to

Kurals.Translation: His officers, kinsmen and foes Who watch keenly are worthy spies
Kurals.Explanation: He is a spy who watches all men, to wit, those who are in the king's employment, his relatives, and his enemies
Kurals.Couplet: His officers, his friends, his enemies,All these who watch are trusty spies
Kurals.emotions: Suspicion — stemming from the act of watching others closely., Caution — the careful observation of people suggests a need to be cautious., Distrust — watching even one’s

Kurals.Translation: Fearless gaze, suspectless guise Guarding secrets mark the spies
Kurals.Explanation: A spy is one who is able to assume an appearance which may create no suspicion (in the minds of others), who fears no man's face, and who never reveals (his purpose)
Kurals.Couplet: Of unsuspected mien and all-unfearing eyes,Who let no secret out, are trusty spies
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Courage, ,

Kurals.Translation: Guised as monks they gather secrets They betray them not under threats
Kurals.Explanation: He is a spy who, assuming the appearance of an ascetic, goes into (whatever place he wishes), examines into (all, that is needful), and never discovers himself, whatever may be done to him
Kurals.Couplet: As monk or devotee, through every hindrance making way,A spy, whate'er men do, must watchful mind display
Kurals.emotions: Caution (or wariness) – The spy is careful

Kurals.Translation: A spy draws out other's secrets Beyond a doubt he clears his facts
Kurals.Explanation: A spy is one who is able to discover what is hidden and who retains no doubt concerning what he has known
Kurals.Couplet: A spy must search each hidden matter out,And full report must render, free from doubt
Kurals.emotions: Curiosity – The desire to discover and uncover hidden information., Suspicion – An atmosphere of doubt and seeking hidden motives., Confidence – Certainty and clarity upon understanding the uncovered

Kurals.Translation: The reports given by one spy By another spy verify
Kurals.Explanation: Let not a king receive the information which a spy has discovered and made known to him, until he has examined it by another spy
Kurals.Couplet: Spying by spies, the things they tellTo test by other spies is well
Kurals.emotions: **Caution** – The advice to verify information from one spy with another reflects a careful

Kurals.Translation: Engage the spies alone, apart When three agree confirm report
Kurals.Explanation: Let a king employ spies so that one may have no knowledge of the other; and when the information of three agrees together, let him receive it
Kurals.Couplet: One spy must not another see: contrive it so;And things by three confirmed as truth you know
Kurals.emotions: Suspicion – The need for spies to work independently and the verification of information indicates distrust and a need for caution., Caution – The careful approach in handling intelligence

Kurals.Translation: Give not the spy open reward It would divulge the secret heard!
Kurals.Explanation: Let not a king publicly confer on a spy any marks of his favour; if he does, he will divulge his own secret
Kurals.Couplet: Reward not trusty spy in others' sight,Or all the mystery will come to light

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Caution, Vigilance, Wisdom.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: To own is to own energy All others own but lethargy
Kurals.Explanation: Energy makes out the man of property; as for those who are destitute of it, do they (really) possess what they possess ?
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis energy gives men o'er that they own a true control;They nothing own who own not energy of soul
Kurals.emotions: Ambition – Reflecting the value and pursuit of energy as an important asset., Pride – Relating to the sense of accomplishment and status derived from possessing energy.

Kurals.Translation: Psychic heart is wealth indeed Worldly wealth departs in speed
Kurals.Explanation: The possession of (energy of) mind is true property; the possession of wealth passes away and abides not
Kurals.Couplet: The wealth of mind man owns a real worth imparts,Material wealth man owns endures not, utterly departs
Kurals.EmotionDetail: from valuing inner wealth over material possessions, recognition of what is truly lasting and important,

Kurals.Translation: The strong in will do not complain The loss of worldly wealth and gain
Kurals.Explanation: They who are possessed of enduring energy will not trouble themselves, saying, "we have lost our property."
Kurals.Couplet: 'Lost is our wealth,' they utter not this cry distressed,The men of firm concentred energy of soul possessed

Kurals.Translation: Fortune enquires, enters with boom Where tireless strivers have their home
Kurals.Explanation: Wealth will find its own way to the man of unfailing energy
Kurals.Couplet: The man of energy of soul inflexible,Good fortune seeks him out and comes a friend to dwell

Kurals.Translation: Water depth is lotus height Mental strength is men's merit
Kurals.Explanation: The stalks of water-flowers are proportionate to the depth of water; so is men's greatness proportionate to their minds
Kurals.Couplet: With rising flood the rising lotus flower its stem unwinds;The dignity of men is measured by their minds
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – The text reflects upon deep understanding of human nature and the relationship between external and internal qualities., Inspiration – It encourages self-improvement by highlighting the value of strong mental character.

Kurals.Translation: Let thoughts be always great and grand Though they fail their virtues stand
Kurals.Explanation: In all that a king thinks of, let him think of his greatness; and if it should be thrust from him (by fate), it will have the nature of not being thrust from him
Kurals.Couplet: Whate'er you ponder, let your aim be loftly still,Fate cannot hinder always, thwart you as it will
Kurals.emotions: **Aspiration** – The text encourages one to keep thoughts lofty and grand

Kurals.Translation: Elephants are firm when arrows hit Great minds keep fit ev'n in defeat
Kurals.Explanation: The strong minded will not faint, even when all is lost; the elephant stands firm, even when wounded by a shower of arrows
Kurals.Couplet: The men of lofty mind quail not in ruin's fateful hour,The elephant retains his dignity mind arrows' deadly shower

Kurals.Translation: Heartless persons cannot boast \"We are liberal to our best\"
Kurals.Explanation: Those who have no (greatness of) mind, will not acquire the joy of saying in the world, "we have excercised liaberality"
Kurals.Couplet: The soulless man can never gainTh' ennobling sense of power with men
Kurals.emotions: Regret – Reflecting on the inability to experience the joy of giving., Emptiness – Feeling a lack of generosity or greatness of mind., Longing –

Kurals.Translation: Huge elephant sharp in tusk quails When tiger, less in form, assails
Kurals.Explanation: Although the elephant has a large body, and a sharp tusk, yet it fears the attack of the tiger
Kurals.Couplet: Huge bulk of elephant with pointed tusk all armed,When tiger threatens shrinks away alarmed

Kurals.Translation: Mental courage is true manhood Lacking that man is like a wood
Kurals.Explanation: Energy is mental wealth; those men who are destitute of it are only trees in the form of men
Kurals.Couplet: Firmness of soul in man is real excellance;Others are trees, their human form a mere pretence
Kurals.emotions: Motivation – The text emphasizes the importance of mental courage and energy

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Determination, Contentment, Wisdom.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Quenchless lamp of ancestry goes When foul idleness encloses
Kurals.Explanation: By the darkness, of idleness, the indestructible lamp of family (rank) will be extinguished
Kurals.Couplet: Of household dignity the lustre beaming bright,Flickers and dies when sluggish foulness dims its light
Kurals.emotions: Shame – The idea that idleness can lead to the downfall of one’s family or ancestry brings a sense of dishonor., Anxiety – There is worry or fear about the consequences of

Kurals.Translation: To make your home an ideal home Loath sloth as sloth; refuse it room
Kurals.Explanation: Let those, who desire that their family may be illustrious, put away all idleness from their conduct
Kurals.Couplet: Let indolence, the death of effort, die,If you'd uphold your household's dignity
Kurals.EmotionDetail: associated with the desire to improve and make one's home/family illustrious, toward sloth/ idleness,

Kurals.Translation: The fool who fosters sluggishness Before he dies ruins his house
Kurals.Explanation: The (lustre of the) family of the ignorant man, who acts under the influence of destructive laziness will perish, even before he is dead
Kurals.Couplet: Who fosters indolence within his breast, the silly elf!The house from which he springs shall perish ere himself
Kurals.emotions: Regret – for wasted time and missed potential due to laziness., Fear – of loss and destruction of one's home or family because of inaction., Shame – about ignorance

Kurals.Translation: Who strive not high, sunk deep in sloth Ruin their house by evil growth
Kurals.Explanation: Family (greatness) will be destroyed, and faults will increase, in those men who give way to laziness, and put forth no dignified exertions
Kurals.Couplet: His family decays, and faults unheeded thrive,Who, sunk in sloth, for noble objects doth not strive
Kurals.EmotionDetail: sloth,

Kurals.Translation: To lag, forget, idle and doze These four are pleasure boats of loss
Kurals.Explanation: Procrastination, forgetfulness, idleness, and sleep, these four things, form the vessel which is desired by those destined to destruction
Kurals.Couplet: Delay, oblivion, sloth, and sleep: these fourAre pleasure-boat to bear the doomed to ruin's shore
Kurals.emotions: Regret – Suggested by the consequences of procrastination and idleness leading to loss., Caution – Implied as a warning against indulging in these behaviors., Shame –

Kurals.Translation: With all the wealth of lords of earth The slothful gain nothing of worth
Kurals.Explanation: It is a rare thing for the idle, even when possessed of the riches of kings who ruled over the whole earth, to derive any great benefit from it
Kurals.Couplet: Though lords of earth unearned possessions gain,The slothful ones no yield of good obtain
Kurals.EmotionDetail: of idleness/laziness, encouragement for diligence, at wasted potential/wealth

Kurals.Translation: The slothful lacking noble deeds Subject themselves to scornful words
Kurals.Explanation: Those who through idleness, and do not engage themselves in dignified exertion, will subject
Kurals.Couplet: Who hug their sloth, nor noble works attempt,Shall bear reproofs and words of just contempt

Kurals.Translation: If sloth invades a noble house It will become a slave of foes
Kurals.Explanation: If idleness take up its abode in a king of high birth, it will make him a slave of his enemies
Kurals.Couplet: If sloth a dwelling find mid noble family,Bondsmen to them that hate them shall they be
Kurals.emotions: Warning/Fear – The text cautions about the negative consequences
Kurals.EmotionDetail: becoming a slave to foes

Kurals.Translation: The blots on race and rule shall cease When one from sloth gets his release
Kurals.Explanation: When a man puts away idleness, the reproach which has come upon himself and his family will disappear
Kurals.Couplet: Who changes slothful habits savesHimself from all that household rule depraves
Kurals.emotions: Determination – The focus on putting away idleness reflects a strong will to take action., Hope – The expectation that shame or reproach will disappear brings a sense of optimism. 3

Kurals.Translation: The slothless king shall gain en masse *All regions trod by Lord apace * Hindu mythology holds that Lord Vishnu measured with his feet the three worlds
Kurals.Explanation: The king who never gives way to idleness will obtain entire possession of (the whole earth) passed over by him who measured (the worlds) with His foot
Kurals.Couplet: The king whose life from sluggishness is rid,Shall rule o'er all by foot of mighty god bestrid
Kurals.emotions: **Diligence** – The emphasis on avoiding idleness and being active., **Ambition** – The aspiration to obtain and achieve dominion (entire possession) over all

Summary: Highlights the sanctity of family life as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Disapproval, Laziness, Determination.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Feel not frustrate saying This hard Who tries attains striving's reward
Kurals.Explanation: Yield not to the feebleness which says, "this is too difficult to be done"; labour will give the greatness (of mind) which is necessary (to do it)
Kurals.Couplet: Say not, 'Tis hard', in weak, desponding hour,For strenuous effort gives prevailing power
Kurals.emotions: Determination – The text encourages striving and not yielding to the idea that something is too hard

Kurals.Translation: In doing work don't break and shirk The world will quit who quits his work
Kurals.Explanation: Take care not to give up exertion in the midst of a work; the world will abandon those who abandon their unfinished work
Kurals.Couplet: In action be thou, 'ware of act's defeat;The world leaves those who work leave incomplete
Kurals.emotions: Determination — Emphasizing persistence and the importance of continuing efforts., Responsibility — Highlighting the need to complete tasks and not abandon duties., Anxiety

Kurals.Translation: On excellence of industry Depends magnanimous bounty
Kurals.Explanation: The lustre of munificence will dwell only with the dignity of laboriousness or efforts
Kurals.Couplet: In strenuous effort doth resideThe power of helping others: noble pride
Kurals.EmotionDetail: desire for achievement and excellence, spirit of giving or munificence, sense of dignity associated with hard work and effort

Kurals.Translation: Bounty of man who never strives Like sword in eunuch's hand it fails
Kurals.Explanation: The liberality of him, who does not labour, will fail, like the manliness of a hermaphrodite, who has a sword in its hand
Kurals.Couplet: Beneficent intent in men by whom no strenuous work is wrought,Like battle-axe in sexless being's hand availeth nought
Kurals.emotions: Frustration – The text describes the futility of effort or gifts without the necessary ability

Kurals.Translation: Work who likes and not pleasure Wipes grief of friends, pillar secure
Kurals.Explanation: He who desires not pleasure, but desires labour, will be a pillar to sustain his relations, wiping away their sorrows
Kurals.Couplet: Whose heart delighteth not in pleasure, but in action finds delight,He wipes away his kinsmen's grief and stands the pillar of their might

Kurals.Translation: Industry adds prosperity Indolence brings but poverty
Kurals.Explanation: Labour will produce wealth; idleness will bring poverty
Kurals.Couplet: Effort brings fortune's sure increase,Its absence brings to nothingness
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Motivation – The text encourages hard work and diligence

Kurals.Translation: Illuck abides with sloth they say *Laxmi's gifts with labourers stay *Laxmi the Goddes of wealth and prosperity
Kurals.Explanation: They say that the black Mudevi (the goddess of adversity) dwells with laziness, and the Latchmi (the goddess of prosperity) dwells with the labour of the industrious
Kurals.Couplet: In sluggishness is seen misfortune's lurid form, the wise declare;Where man unslothful toils, she of the lotus flower is there
Kurals.emotions: Motivation — The text encourages hard work and industriousness as a way to achieve success and prosperity., Hope — The association of prosperity (through the goddess Laxmi) with labor

Kurals.Translation: Misfortune is disgrace to none The shame is nothing learnt or done
Kurals.Explanation: Adverse fate is no disgrace to any one; to be without exertion and without knowing what should be known, is disgrace
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis no reproach unpropitious fate should ban;But not to do man's work is foul disgrace to man
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Determination,

Kurals.Translation: Though fate is against fulfilment Hard labour has ready payment
Kurals.Explanation: Although it be said that, through fate, it cannot be attained, yet labour, with bodily exertion, will yield its reward
Kurals.Couplet: Though fate-divine should make your labour vain;Effort its labour's sure reward will gain

Kurals.Translation: Tireless Toiler's striving hand Shall leave even the fate behind
Kurals.Explanation: They who labour on, without fear and without fainting will see even fate (put) behind their back
Kurals.Couplet: Who strive with undismayed, unfaltering mind,At length shall leave opposing fate behind

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Determination, Not Abandon Duties., Anxiety.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Laugh away troubles; there is No other way to conquer woes
Kurals.Explanation: If troubles come, laugh; there is nothing like that, to press upon and drive away sorrow
Kurals.Couplet: Smile, with patient, hopeful heart, in troublous hour;Meet and so vanquish grief; nothing hath equal power

Kurals.Translation: Deluging sorrows come to nought When wise men face them with firm thought
Kurals.Explanation: A flood of troubles will be overcome by the (courageous) thought which the minds of the wise will entertain, even in sorrow
Kurals.Couplet: Though sorrow, like a flood, comes rolling on,When wise men's mind regards it,- it is gone

Kurals.Translation: Grief they face and put to grief Who grieve not grief by mind's relief
Kurals.Explanation: They give sorrow to sorrow, who in sorrow do not suffer sorrow
Kurals.Couplet: Who griefs confront with meek, ungrieving heart,From them griefs, put to grief, depart

Kurals.Translation: Who pulls like bulls patiently on Causes grief to grieve anon
Kurals.Explanation: Troubles will vanish (i.e., will be troubled) before the man who (struggles against difficulties) as a
Kurals.Couplet: Like bullock struggle on through each obstructed way;From such an one will troubles, troubled, roll away
Kurals.EmotionDetail: or Determination, ,

Kurals.Translation: Before the brave grief grieves and goes Who dare a host of pressing woes
Kurals.Explanation: The troubles of that man will be troubled (and disappear) who, however thickly they may come upon him, does not abandon (his purpose)
Kurals.Couplet: When griefs press on, but fail to crush the patient heart,Then griefs defeated, put to grief, depart
Kurals.emotions: Resilience – The text describes enduring and facing adversity without abandoning purpose., Courage – There is an emphasis on bravery in the face of grief and pressing woes., Determination –

Kurals.Translation: The wise that never gloat in gain Do not fret in fateful ruin
Kurals.Explanation: Will those men ever cry out in sorrow, "we are destitute" who, (in their prosperity), give not way to (undue desire) to keep their wealth
Kurals.Couplet: Who boasted not of wealth, nor gave it all their heart,Will not bemoan the loss, when prosperous days depart

Kurals.Translation: The wise worry no more of woes Knowing body's butt of sorrows
Kurals.Explanation: The great will not regard trouble as trouble, knowing that the body is the butt of trouble
Kurals.Couplet: 'Man's frame is sorrow's target', the noble mind reflects,Nor meets with troubled mind the sorrows it expects
Kurals.emotions: Resilience – Enduring hardship without being overwhelmed., Wisdom – Understanding the transient nature of troubles., Detachment – Maintaining distance from suffering by realizing its source.

Kurals.Translation: Who seek not joy, deem grief norm By sorrows do not come to harm
Kurals.Explanation: That man never experiences sorrow, who does not seek for pleasure, and who considers distress to be natural (to man)
Kurals.Couplet: He seeks not joy, to sorrow man is born, he knows;Such man will walk unharmed by touch of human woes

Kurals.Translation: In joy to joy who is not bound In grief he grieves not dual round!
Kurals.Explanation: He does not suffer sorrow, in sorrow who does not look for pleasure in pleasure
Kurals.Couplet: Mid joys he yields not heart to joys' controlMid sorrows, sorrow cannot touch his soul

Kurals.Translation: His glory is esteemed by foes Who sees weal in wanton woes!
Kurals.Explanation: The elevation, which even his enemies will esteem, will be gained by him, who regards pain as pleasure
Kurals.Couplet: Who pain as pleasure takes, he shall acquireThe bliss to which his foes in vain aspire

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Resilience, Courage, Sorrow.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: He is minister who chooses Right means, time, mode and rare ventures
Kurals.Explanation: The minister is one who can make an excellent choice of means, time, manner of execution, and the difficult undertaking (itself)
Kurals.Couplet: A minister is he who grasps, with wisdom large,Means, time, work's mode, and functions rare he must discharge
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – The emphasis on choosing the right means

Kurals.Translation: With these he guards people, -by his Knowledge, firmness and manliness
Kurals.Explanation: The minister is one who in addition to the aforesaid five things excels in the possession of firmness,
Kurals.Couplet: A minister must greatness own of guardian power, determined mind,Learn'd wisdom, manly effort with the former five combined

Kurals.Translation: A minister cherishes friends Divides foes and the parted blends
Kurals.Explanation: The minister is one who can effect discord (among foes), maintain the good-will of his friends and restore to friendship those who have seceded (from him)
Kurals.Couplet: A minister is he whose power can foes divide,Attach more firmly friends, of severed ones can heal the breaches wide
Kurals.emotions: Loyalty – The text emphasizes cherishing friends and maintaining good-will., Reconciliation – It highlights efforts to restore friendships and blend those who have parted., Strategic Caution –

Kurals.Translation: A minister must sift reflect Select and say surely one fact
Kurals.Explanation: The minister is one who is able to comprehend (the whole nature of an undertaking), execute it in the best manner possible, and offer assuring advice (in time of necessity)
Kurals.Couplet: A minister has power to see the methods help afford,To ponder long, then utter calm conclusive word
Kurals.emotions: Responsibility – The text emphasizes the duty and weight of making decisions and offering guidance., Confidence – There is an underlying sense of assurance in offering advice and executing plans effectively., Prud

Kurals.Translation: Have him for help who virtue knows Right wisdom speaks, ever apt in acts
Kurals.Explanation: He is the best helper (of the king) who understanding the duties, of the latter, is by his special learning, able to tender the fullest advice, and at all times conversant with the best method (of
Kurals.Couplet: The man who virtue knows, has use of wise and pleasant wordsWith plans for every season apt, in counsel aid affords
Kurals.emotions: Trust – in seeking advice from wise

Kurals.Translation: Which subtler brain can stand before The keen in brain with learned love?
Kurals.Explanation: What (contrivances) are there so acute as to resist those who possess natural acuteness in addition to learning ?
Kurals.Couplet: When native subtilty combines with sound scholastic lore,'Tis subtilty surpassing all, which nothing stands before
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for natural acuteness and learned intelligence, recognition that few can stand against such intelligence,

Kurals.Translation: Albeit you know to act from books Act after knowing world's outlooks
Kurals.Explanation: Though you are acquainted with the (theoretical) methods (of performing an act), understand the ways of the world and act accordingly
Kurals.Couplet: Though knowing all that books can teach, 'tis truest tactTo follow common sense of men in act
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The advice to act only after understanding the world implies carefulness and a desire to avoid mistakes., Wisdom – The emphasis on combining book knowledge with practical worldly understanding suggests valu

Kurals.Translation: The man in place must tell the facts Though the ignorant king refutes
Kurals.Explanation: Although the king be utterly ignorant, it is the duty of the minister to give (him) sound advice
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis duty of the man in place aloud to sayThe very truth, though unwise king may cast his words away

Kurals.Translation: Seventy crores of foes are better Than a minister with mind bitter
Kurals.Explanation: Far better are seventy crores of enemies (for a king) than a minister at his side who intends (his) ruin
Kurals.Couplet: A minister who by king's side plots evil thingsWorse woes than countless foemen brings
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Caution,

Kurals.Translation: The unresolved, though well designed To fulfil an act they have no mind
Kurals.Explanation: Those ministers who are destitute of (executive) ability will fail to carry out their projects, although they may have contrived aright
Kurals.Couplet: For gain of end desired just counsel nought availsTo minister, when tact in execution fails
Kurals.emotions: Frustration – Due to the inability to execute well-devised plans., Disappointment – From seeing good intentions and preparations not translating into action., Helplessness – Feeling

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Trust, Admiration, Maintaining Good-Will..
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The goodness called goodness of speech Is goodness which nothing can reach
Kurals.Explanation: The possession of that goodness which is called the goodness of speech is (even to others) better than any other goodness
Kurals.Couplet: A tongue that rightly speaks the right is greatest gain,It stands alone midst goodly things that men obtain
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – for the virtue and impact of good speech., Respect – toward those who possess the goodness of speech., Inspiration – to cultivate and value goodness in one’s own

Kurals.Translation: Since gain or ruin speeches bring Guard against the slips of tongue
Kurals.Explanation: Since (both) wealth and evil result from (their) speech, ministers should most carefully guard themselves against faultiness therein
Kurals.Couplet: Since gain and loss in life on speech depend,From careless slip in speech thyself defend
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , about possible negative consequences of speech

Kurals.Translation: A speech is speech that holds ears And attracts ev'n those that are averse
Kurals.Explanation: The (minister's) speech is that which seeks (to express) elements as bind his friends (to himself) and is so delivered as to make even his enemies desire (his friendship)
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis speech that spell-bound holds the listening ear,While those who have not heard desire to hear
Kurals.EmotionDetail: the emotion of being moved or influenced by words,

Kurals.Translation: Weigh thy words and speak; because No wealth or virtue words surpass
Kurals.Explanation: Understand the qualities (of your hearers) and (then) make your speech; for superior to it, there is neither virtue nor wealth
Kurals.Couplet: Speak words adapted well to various hearers' state;No higher virtue lives, no gain more surely great
Kurals.emotions: Thoughtfulness – The text highlights the importance of careful consideration before speaking., Respect – It encourages understanding others' qualities before addressing them

Kurals.Translation: Speak out thy world so that no word Can win it and say untoward
Kurals.Explanation: Deliver your speech, after assuring yourself that no counter speech can defeat your own
Kurals.Couplet: Speak out your speech, when once 'tis past disputeThat none can utter speech that shall your speech refute
Kurals.emotions: Confidence – The text encourages the speaker to be assured and steadfast before speaking

Kurals.Translation: Spotless men speak what is sweet And grasp in others what is meet
Kurals.Explanation: It is the opinion of those who are free from defects in diplomacy that the minister should speak so as to make his hearers desire (to hear more) and grasp the meaning of what he hears himself
Kurals.Couplet: Charming each hearer's ear, of others' words to seize the sense,Is method wise of men of spotless excellence
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – Reflecting the value and admiration for insightful and thoughtful communication., Respect – Toward those who use speech skillfully and without defects., Appreciation – For clarity

Kurals.Translation: No foe defies the speaker clear Flawless, puissant, and free from fear
Kurals.Explanation: It is impossible for any one to conquer him by intrique who possesses power of speech, and is neither faulty nor timid
Kurals.Couplet: Mighty in word, of unforgetful mind, of fearless speech,'Tis hard for hostile power such man to overreach

Kurals.Translation: The world will quickly carry out The words of counsellors astute
Kurals.Explanation: If there be those who can speak on various subjects in their proper order and in a pleasing manner, the world would readily accept them
Kurals.Couplet: Swiftly the listening world will gather round,When men of mighty speech the weighty theme propound
Kurals.emotions: Respect – for those who are wise and speak astutely., Admiration – for the skill of speaking effectively and pleasantly., Trust – in the words and counsel of articulate and

Kurals.Translation: They overspeak who do not seek A few and flawless words to speak
Kurals.Explanation: They will desire to utter many words, who do not know how to speak a few faultless ones
Kurals.Couplet: Who have not skill ten faultless words to utter plain,Their tongues will itch with thousand words man's ears to pain
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – The text reflects a desire for thoughtful and prudent speech., Humility – It implies an awareness of one’s limitations in communication., Restraint – There is an

Kurals.Translation: Who can't express what they have learnt Are bunch of flowers not fragrant
Kurals.Explanation: Those who are unable to set forth their acquirements (before others) are like flowers blossoming in a cluster and yet without fragrance
Kurals.Couplet: Like scentless flower in blooming garland boundAre men who can't their lore acquired to other's ears expound
Kurals.emotions: Frustration – At being unable to express oneself or one's knowledge., Regret – For not being able to share or demonstrate one's abilities., Insignificance – Feeling unnoticed

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Impact Of Good Speech., Caution, Responsibility.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Friendship brings gain; but action pure Does every good thing we desire
Kurals.Explanation: The efficacy of support will yield (only) wealth; (but) the efficacy of action will yield all that is desired
Kurals.Couplet: The good external help confers is worldly gain;By action good men every needed gift obtain
Kurals.emotions: Hope – The text emphasizes that pure actions can lead to all desires being fulfilled

Kurals.Translation: Eschew always acts that do not Bring good nor glory on their part
Kurals.Explanation: Ministers should at all times avoid acts which, in addition to fame, yield no benefit (for the future)
Kurals.Couplet: From action evermore thyself restrainOf glory and of good that yields no gain

Kurals.Translation: Those in the world desire for fame Should shun the deed that dims their name
Kurals.Explanation: Those who say, "we will become (better)" should avoid the performance of acts that would destroy (their fame)
Kurals.Couplet: Who tell themselves that nobler things shall yet be wonAll deeds that dim the light of glory must they shun
Kurals.emotions: Pride – There is a sense of valuing one's reputation and seeking to maintain or improve it., Caution – The advice to avoid actions that could tarnish one's name reflects

Kurals.Translation: Though perils press the faultless wise Shun deeds of mean, shameful device
Kurals.Explanation: Those who have infallible judgement though threatened with peril will not do acts which have brought disgrace (on former ministers)
Kurals.Couplet: Though troubles press, no shameful deed they do,Whose eyes the ever-during vision view

Kurals.Translation: Do not wrong act and grieve, \"Alas\" If done, do not repeat it twice
Kurals.Explanation: Let a minister never do acts of which he would have to grieve saying, "what is this I have done"; (but) should he do (them), it were good that he grieved not
Kurals.Couplet: Do nought that soul repenting must deplore,If thou hast sinned, 'tis well if thou dost sin no more
Kurals.emotions: Regret – The text discusses feeling sorrowful with the word "grieve" and the exclamation "Alas" over a wrongful action., Guilt – It suggests a sense

Kurals.Translation: Though she who begot thee hungers Shun acts denounced by ancient seers
Kurals.Explanation: Though a minister may see his mother starve; let him do not act which the wise would (treat with contempt)
Kurals.Couplet: Though her that bore thee hung'ring thou behold, no deedDo thou, that men of perfect soul have crime decreed
Kurals.EmotionDetail: or Responsibility, or Moral Uprightness, or Concern

Kurals.Translation: Pinching poverty of the wise Is more than wealth hoarded by Vice
Kurals.Explanation: Far more excellent is the extreme poverty of the wise than wealth obtained by heaping up of sinful
Kurals.Couplet: Than store of wealth guilt-laden souls obtain,The sorest poverty of perfect soul is richer gain
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for wisdom and virtue, for wealth gained through vice,

Kurals.Translation: Those who dare a forbidden deed Suffer troubles though they succeed
Kurals.Explanation: The actions of those, who have not desisted from doing deeds forbidden (by the great), will, even if they succeed, cause them sorrow
Kurals.Couplet: To those who hate reproof and do forbidden thingWhat prospers now, in after days shall anguish bring

Kurals.Translation: Gains from weeping, weeping go Though lost, from good deeds blessings flow
Kurals.Explanation: All that has been obtained with tears (to the victim) will depart with tears (to himself); but what has been by fair means; though with loss at first, will afterwards yield fruit
Kurals.Couplet: What's gained through tears with tears shall go;From loss good deeds entail harvests of blessings grow
Kurals.emotions: Regret – The text refers to gains obtained through weeping

Kurals.Translation: The wealth gathered in guilty ways Is water poured in wet clay vase
Kurals.Explanation: (For a minister) to protect (his king) with wealth obtained by foul means is like preserving a vessel of wet clay by filling it with water
Kurals.Couplet: In pot of clay unburnt he water pours and would retain,Who seeks by wrong the realm in wealth and safety to maintain

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Caution, Integrity, Responsibility.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: A powerful mind does powerful act And all the rest are imperfect
Kurals.Explanation: Firmness in action is (simply) one's firmness of mind; all other (abilities) are not of this nature
Kurals.Couplet: What men call 'power in action' know for 'power of mind'Externe to man all other aids you find

Kurals.Translation: Shun failing fuss; fail not purpose These two are maxims of the wise
Kurals.Explanation: Not to perform a ruinous act, and not to be discouraged by the ruinous termination of an act, are the two maxims which, the wise say, from the principles of those who have investigated the subject
Kurals.Couplet: 'Each hindrance shun', 'unyielding onward press, If obstacle be there,'These two define your way, so those that search out truth declare
Kurals.emotions: Determination – Emphasizes not being discouraged by setbacks and staying resolute in purpose., Wisdom – Reflects careful decision-making and learning from both success and failure., Perse

Kurals.Translation: The strong achieve and then display Woe unto work displayed midway
Kurals.Explanation: So to perform an act as to publish it (only) at its termination is (true) manliness; for to announce it beforehand, will cause irremediable sorrow
Kurals.Couplet: Man's fitting work is known but by success achieved;In midst the plan revealed brings ruin ne'er to be retrieved
Kurals.emotions: Determination – Emphasized by firmness in action and the achievement of goals., Pride – Displaying accomplishments after completion reflects a sense of satisfaction and pride in one's work.

Kurals.Translation: Easy it is to tell a fact But hard it is to know and act
Kurals.Explanation: To say (how an act is to be performed) is (indeed) easy for any one; but far difficult it is to do according to what has been said
Kurals.Couplet: Easy to every man the speech that shows the way;Hard thing to shape one's life by words they say
Kurals.emotions: Determination – The emphasis on acting according to one’s word highlights the need for resolve and firmness., Frustration – The difficulty in matching actions to words suggests a struggle that can

Kurals.Translation: Dynamic deeds of a doughty soul Shall win the praise of king and all
Kurals.Explanation: The firmness in action of those who have become great by the excellence (of their counsel) will, by attaining its fulfilment in the person of the king, be esteemed (by all).
Kurals.Couplet: The power in act of men renowned and great,With king acceptance finds and fame through all the state

Kurals.Translation: The will-to-do achieves the deed When mind that wills is strong indeed
Kurals.Explanation: If those who have planned (an undertaking) possess firmness (in executing it) they will obtain what they have desired even as they have desired it
Kurals.Couplet: Whate'er men think, ev'n as they think, may men obtain,If those who think can steadfastness of will retain

Kurals.Translation: Scorn not the form: for men there are Like linchpin of big rolling car
Kurals.Explanation: Let none be despised for (their) size; (for) the world has those who resemble the linch-pin of the big rolling car
Kurals.Couplet: Despise not men of modest bearing; Look not at form, but what men are:For some there live, high functions sharing, Like linch-pin of the mighty car
Kurals.emotions: Respect — The text emphasizes the importance of respecting everyone regardless of their outward appearance or size., Humility — It cautions against arrogance or looking down on others

Kurals.Translation: Waver not; do wakefully The deed resolved purposefully
Kurals.Explanation: An act that has been firmly resolved on must be as firmly carried out without delay
Kurals.Couplet: What clearly eye discerns as right, with steadfast will,And mind unslumbering, that should man fulfil

Kurals.Translation: Do with firm will though pains beset The deed that brings delight at last
Kurals.Explanation: Though it should cause increasing sorrow (at the outset), do with firmness the act that yield bliss (in the end)
Kurals.Couplet: Though toil and trouble face thee, firm resolve hold fast,And do the deeds that pleasure yield at last

Kurals.Translation: The world merits no other strength But strength of will-to-do at length
Kurals.Explanation: The great will not esteem those who esteem not firmness of action, whatever other abilities the latter may possess
Kurals.Couplet: The world desires not men of every power possessed,Who power in act desire not,- crown of all the rest
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , towards those lacking firmness or resolve

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Determination, Confidence, Resolve.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: When counsel takes a resolve strong Weak delay of action is wrong
Kurals.Explanation: Consultation ends in forming a resolution (to act); (but) delay in the execution of that resolve is an evil
Kurals.Couplet: The Resolve is counsel's end, If resolutions halt In weak delays, still unfulfilled, 'tis grievous fault

Kurals.Translation: Delay such acts as need delay Delay not acts that need display
Kurals.Explanation: Sleep over such (actions) as may be slept over; (but) never over such as may not be slept over
Kurals.Couplet: Slumber when sleepy work's in hand beware Thou slumber not when action calls for sleepless care
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text advises careful consideration before acting

Kurals.Translation: It's best to act when feasible If not see what is possible
Kurals.Explanation: Whenever it is possible (to overcome your enemy) the act (of fighting) is certainly good; if not, endeavour to employ some more successful method
Kurals.Couplet: When way is clear, prompt let your action be; When not, watch till some open path you see
Kurals.EmotionDetail: a practical approach to problems and affairs, being careful and considering alternatives,

Kurals.Translation: Work or foe left unfinished Flare up like fire unextinguished
Kurals.Explanation: When duly considered, the incomplete execution of an undertaking and hostility will grow and destroy one like the (unextinguished) remnant of a fire
Kurals.Couplet: With work or foe, when you neglect some little thing, If you reflect, like smouldering fire, 'twill ruin bring
Kurals.EmotionDetail: stemming from the sense of unfinished work or unresolved hostility, related to leaving tasks or conflicts incomplete,

Kurals.Translation: Money and means, time, place and deed Decide these five and then proceed
Kurals.Explanation: Do an act after a due consideration of the (following) five, viz money, means, time, execution and place
Kurals.Couplet: Treasure and instrument and time and deed and place of act These five, till every doubt remove, think o'er with care exact

Kurals.Translation: Weigh well the end, hindrance, profit And then pursue a fitting act
Kurals.Explanation: An act is to be performed after considering the exertion required, the obstacles to be encountered, and the great profit to be gained (on its completion)
Kurals.Couplet: Accomplishment, the hindrances, large profits won By effort these compare,- then let the work be done

Kurals.Translation: Know first the secret from experts That is the way of fruitful acts
Kurals.Explanation: The method of performance for one who has begun an act is to ascertain the mind of him who knows the secret thereof
Kurals.Couplet: Who would succeed must thus begin first let him ask The thoughts of them who thoroughly know the task
Kurals.emotions: Curiosity – The desire to know the "secret from experts" and understand deeper knowledge., Respect – Recognition of value in "him who knows the secret

Kurals.Translation: Lure a tusker by a tusker Achieve a deed by deed better
Kurals.Explanation: To make one undertaking the means of accomplishing another (similar to it) is like making one rutting elephant the means of capturing another
Kurals.Couplet: By one thing done you reach a second work's accomplishment; So furious elephant to snare its fellow brute is sent

Kurals.Translation: Than doing good to friends it is More urgent to befriend the foes
Kurals.Explanation: One should rather hasten to secure the alliance of the foes (of one's foes) than perform good offices to one's friends
Kurals.Couplet: Than kindly acts to friends more urgent thing to do, Is making foes to cling as friends attached to you
Kurals.emotions: Strategic Concern – The text reflects a thoughtful

Kurals.Translation: Small statesmen fearing people's fear Submit to foes superior
Kurals.Explanation: Ministers of small states, afraid of their people being frightened, will yield to and acknowledge their superior foes, if the latter offer them a chance of reconciliation
Kurals.Couplet: The men of lesser realm, fearing the people's inward dread, Accepting granted terms, to mightier ruler bow the head
Kurals.emotions: Fear – Small statesmen and their ministers are primarily motivated by fear for their people and the consequences of conflict., Submission – The act of yielding or submitting to superior foes indicates a sense

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Determination, Caution, Prudence.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Love, noble birth, good courtesy Pleasing kings mark true embassy
Kurals.Explanation: The qualification of an ambassador are affection (for his relations) a fitting birth, and the possession of attributes pleasing to royalty
Kurals.Couplet: Benevolence high birth, the courtesy kings love:-These qualities the envoy of a king approve
Kurals.EmotionDetail: affection,

Kurals.Translation: Envoys must bear love for their prince Knowledge and learned eloquence
Kurals.Explanation: Love (to his sovereign), knowledge (of his affairs), and a discriminating power of speech (before other sovereigns) are the three sine qua non qualifications of an ambassador
Kurals.Couplet: Love, knowledge, power of chosen words, three things,Should he possess who speaks the words of kings
Kurals.EmotionDetail: stemming from love for one's sovereign, related to understanding and valuing the sovereign's affairs,

Kurals.Translation: Savant among savants, he pleads Before lanced king, triumphant words
Kurals.Explanation: To be powerful in politics among those who are learned (in ethics) is the character of him who speaks
Kurals.Couplet: Mighty in lore amongst the learned must he be,Midst jav'lin-bearing kings who speaks the words of victory
Kurals.emotions: Confidence – The speaker is described as triumphant and powerful among learned individuals

Kurals.Translation: Who has these three: good form, sense, lore Can act as bold ambassador
