Kurals.Explanation: He may go on a mission (to foreign rulers) who has combined in him all these three viz, (natural) sense, an attractive bearing and well-tried learning
Kurals.Couplet: Sense, goodly grace, and knowledge exquisiteWho hath these three for envoy's task is fit
Kurals.emotions: Confidence – Embodied by the qualities needed to act as an ambassador., Respect – Toward those possessing sense

Kurals.Translation: Not harsh, the envoy's winsome ways Does good by pleasant words concise
Kurals.Explanation: He is an ambassador who (in the presence of foreign rulers) speaks briefly, avoids harshness, talks so as to make them smile, and thus brings good (to his own sovereign)
Kurals.Couplet: In terms concise, avoiding wrathful speech, who utters pleasant word,An envoy he who gains advantage for his lord

Kurals.Translation: Learned; fearless, the envoy tends Convincing words which time demands
Kurals.Explanation: He is an ambassador who having studied (politics) talks impressively, is not afraid of angry looks, and knows (to employ) the art suited to the time
Kurals.Couplet: An envoy meet is he, well-learned, of fearless eyeWho speaks right home, prepared for each emergency
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 emotions associated with the text:, Confidence – The ambassador is described as fearless and able to talk impressively., Wisdom – The ambassador is learned

Kurals.Translation: Knowing duty time and place The envoy employs mature phrase
Kurals.Explanation: He is chief (among ambassadors) who understands the proper decorum (before foreign princes), seeks the (proper) occasion, knows the (most suitable) place, and delivers his message after (due)
Kurals.Couplet: He is the best who knows what's due, the time considered well,The place selects, then ponders long ere he his errand tell
Kurals.emotions: Respect – Emphasized through understanding proper decorum and conveying messages appropriately., Caution – Reflected in the envoy’s care to choose the right time and place

Kurals.Translation: The true envoy of three virtues Is pure helpful and bold in views
Kurals.Explanation: The qualifications of him who faithfully delivers his (sovereign's) message are purity, the support (of foreign ministers), and boldness, with truthfulness in addition to the (aforesaid) three
Kurals.Couplet: Integrity, resources, soul determined, truthfulnessWho rightly speaks his message must these marks possess

Kurals.Translation: The envoy who ports the king's message Has flawless words and heart's courage
Kurals.Explanation: He alone is fit to communicate (his sovereign's) reply, who possesses the firmness not to utter even inadvertently what may reflect discredit (on the latter)
Kurals.Couplet: His faltering lips must utter no unworthy thing,Who stands, with steady eye, to speak the mandates of his king
Kurals.emotions: Loyalty – The text emphasizes fidelity to the king and the responsibility to protect his reputation., Courage – It highlights the necessity of bravery (“heart’s courage”) in an envoy.

Kurals.Translation: Braving death the bold envoy Assures his king's safety and joy
Kurals.Explanation: He is the ambassador who fearlessly seeks his sovereign's good though it should cost him his life (to deliver his message)
Kurals.Couplet: Death to the faithful one his embassy may bring;To envoy gains assured advantage for his king

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Loyalty, Respect, Courage.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

chapters.Chapter: Conduct in the Presence of the King

Kurals.Translation: Move with hostile kings as with fire Not coming close nor going far
Kurals.Explanation: Thus let them act who dwell beneath of warlike kings the palace-roof
Kurals.Couplet: Who warm them at the fire draw not too near, nor keep too much aloof;Thus let them act who dwell beneath of warlike kings the palace-roof

Kurals.Translation: Crave not for things which kings desire This brings thee their fruitful favour
Kurals.Explanation: For ministers not to cover the things desired by their kings will through the kings themselves yield them everlasting wealth
Kurals.Couplet: To those who prize not state that kings are wont to prize,The king himself abundant wealth supplies
Kurals.emotions: **Desire** – The text discusses craving and coveting

Kurals.Translation: Guard thyself from petty excess Suspected least, there's no redress
Kurals.Explanation: Ministers who would save themselves should avoid (the commission of) serious errors for if the king's suspicion is once roused, no one can remove it
Kurals.Couplet: Who would walk warily, let him of greater faults beware;To clear suspicions once aroused is an achievement rare
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The advice centers on being careful and vigilant

Kurals.Translation: Whisper not; nor smile exchange Amidst august men's assemblage
Kurals.Explanation: While in the presence of the sovereign, ministers should neither whisper to nor smile at others
Kurals.Couplet: All whispered words and interchange of smiles repress,In presence of the men who kingly power possess

Kurals.Translation: Hear not, ask not the king's secret Hear only when he lets it out
Kurals.Explanation: (When the king is engaged) in secret counsel (with others), ministers should neither over-hear anything whatever nor pry into it with inquisitive questions, but (wait to) listen when it is divulged (by
Kurals.Couplet: Seek not, ask not, the secret of the king to hear;But if he lets the matter forth, give ear
Kurals.EmotionDetail: towards authority,

Kurals.Translation: Discern his mood and time and tell No dislikes but what king likes well
Kurals.Explanation: Knowing the (king's disposition and seeking the right time, (the minister) should in a pleasing manner suggest things such as are desirable and not disagreeable
Kurals.Couplet: Knowing the signs, waiting for fitting time, with courteous care,Things not displeasing, needful things, declare

Kurals.Translation: Tell pleasing things; and never tell Even if pressed what is futile
Kurals.Explanation: Ministers should (always) give agreeable advice but on no occasion recommend useless actions, though requested (to do so)
Kurals.Couplet: Speak pleasant things, but never utter idle word;Not though by monarch's ears with pleasure heard
Kurals.emotions: Respect – The text emphasizes appropriate conduct in the presence of the king

Kurals.Translation: As young and kinsman do not slight; Look with awe king's light and might
Kurals.Explanation: Ministers should behave in accordance with the (Divine) light in the person of kings and not despise them saying, "He is our junior (in age) and connected with our family!"
Kurals.Couplet: Say not, 'He's young, my kinsman,' despising thus your king;But reverence the glory kingly state doth bring
Kurals.emotions: Respect – The text emphasizes the need for showing respect towards the king

Kurals.Translation: The clear-visioned do nothing base Deeming they have the monarch's grace
Kurals.Explanation: Those whose judgement is firm will not do what is disagreeable (to the sovereign) saying (within
Kurals.Couplet: 'We've gained his grace, boots nought what graceless acts we do',So deem not sages who the changeless vision view
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for authority and the monarch, in one's actions and judgment, toward the king or sovereign

Kurals.Translation: Worthless acts based on friendship old Shall spell ruin and woe untold
Kurals.Explanation: The (foolish) claim with which a minister does unbecoming acts because of his (long) familiarity (with the king) will ensure his ruin
Kurals.Couplet: Who think 'We're ancient friends' and do unseemly things;To these familiarity sure ruin brings
Kurals.emotions: **Caution** – The text warns against negligent behavior

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Caution, Respect, Fear.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Who reads the mind by look, untold Adorns the changeless sea-girt world
Kurals.Explanation: The minister who by looking (at the king) understands his mind without being told (of it), will be a perpetual ornament to the world which is surrounded by a never-drying sea
Kurals.Couplet: Who knows the sign, and reads unuttered thought, the gem is he,Of earth round traversed by the changeless sea
Kurals.emotions: **Understanding** – The text values the deep comprehension and intuitive grasp of another’s unspoken thoughts., **Admiration** – There is reverence for the rare quality in a minister

Kurals.Translation: Take him as God who reads the thought Of another man with without a doubt
Kurals.Explanation: He is to be esteemed a god who is able to ascertain without a doubt what is within (one's mind)
Kurals.Couplet: Undoubting, who the minds of men can scan,As deity regard that gifted man

Kurals.Translation: By sign who scans the sign admit At any cost in cabinet
Kurals.Explanation: The king should ever give whatever (is asked) of his belongings and secure him who, by the indications (of his own mind) is able to read those of another
Kurals.Couplet: Who by the sign the signs interprets plain,Give any member up his aid to gain
Kurals.emotions: Curiosity – The focus on scanning and understanding signs

Kurals.Translation: Untold, he who divines the thought Though same in form is quite apart
Kurals.Explanation: Those who understand one's thoughts without being informed (thereof) and those who do not, may (indeed) resemble one another bodily; still are they different (mentally)
Kurals.Couplet: Who reads what's shown by signs, though words unspoken be,In form may seem as other men, in function nobler far is he
Kurals.emotions: Insightfulness – The text concerns understanding unspoken thoughts and the depth of perception

Kurals.Translation: Among senses what for is eye If thought by thought one can't descry?
Kurals.Explanation: Of what use are the eyes amongst one's members, if they cannot by their own indications dive those of another ?
Kurals.Couplet: By sign who knows not sings to comprehend, what gain,'Mid all his members, from his eyes does he obtain
Kurals.emotions: Curiosity – The text ponders the deeper purpose of our senses

Kurals.Translation: What throbs in mind the face reflects Just as mirror nearby objects
Kurals.Explanation: As the mirror reflects what is near so does the face show what is uppermost in the mind
Kurals.Couplet: As forms around in crystal mirrored clear we find,The face will show what's throbbing in the mind

Kurals.Translation: Than face what is subtler to tell First if the mind feels well or ill
Kurals.Explanation: Is there anything so full of knowledge as the face ? (No) it precedes the mind, whether (the latter is) pleased or vexed
Kurals.Couplet: Than speaking countenance hath aught more prescient skill?Rejoice or burn with rage, 'tis the first herald still
Kurals.EmotionDetail: about understanding subtle signs and knowledge, about the state of mind and what is being communicated,

Kurals.Translation: Just standing in front would suffice For those who read the mind on face
Kurals.Explanation: If the king gets those who by looking into his mind can understand (and remove) what has occurred (to him) it is enough that he stand looking at their face
Kurals.Couplet: To see the face is quite enough, in presence brought,When men can look within and know the lurking thought

Kurals.Translation: Friend or foe the eyes will show To those who changing outlooks know
Kurals.Explanation: If a king gets ministers who can read the movements of the eye, the eyes (of foreign kings) will (themselves) reveal (to him) their hatred or friendship
Kurals.Couplet: The eye speaks out the hate or friendly soul of man;To those who know the eye's swift varying moods to scan

Kurals.Translation: The scale of keen discerning minds Is eye and eye that secrets finds
Kurals.Explanation: The measuring-rod of those (ministers) who say "we are acute" will on inquiry be found to be their (own) eyes and nothing else
Kurals.Couplet: The men of keen discerning soul no other test apply(When you their secret ask) than man's revealing eye

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Trust, Awe, Reverence.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The pure in thought and eloquence Adapt their words to audience
Kurals.Explanation: Let the pure who know the arrangement of words speak with deliberation after ascertaining (the nature of) the court (then assembled)
Kurals.Couplet: Men pure in heart, who know of words the varied force,Should to their audience known adapt their well-arranged discourse
Kurals.emotions: Thoughtfulness – The emphasis on speaking with deliberation and understanding the audience reflects careful consideration., Respect – There is an underlying respect for the assembled audience and the context in which words are

Kurals.Translation: Who know the art of speech shall suit Their chosen words to time in fact
Kurals.Explanation: Let the good who know the uses of words speak with a clear knowledge after ascertaining the time (suited to the court)
Kurals.Couplet: Good men to whom the arts of eloquence are known,Should seek occasion meet, and say what well they've made their own

Kurals.Translation: They speak in vain at length who talk Words unversed which ears don't take
Kurals.Explanation: Those who undertake to speak without knowing the (nature of the) court are ignorant of the quality of words as well as devoid of the power (of learning)
Kurals.Couplet: Unversed in councils, who essays to speakKnows not the way of suasive words,- and all is weak
Kurals.emotions: Ignorance – Highlighting a lack of understanding and knowledge., Frustration – Implied by the futility of speaking without comprehension or preparation., Wisdom

Kurals.Translation: Before the bright be brilliant light Before the muff be mortar white
Kurals.Explanation: Ministers should be lights in the assembly of the enlightned, but assume the pure whiteness of mortar (ignorance) in that of fools
Kurals.Couplet: Before the bright ones shine as doth the lightBefore the dull ones be as purest stucco white
Kurals.emotions: Disappointment – The text implies a letdown

Kurals.Translation: Modest restraint all good excels Which argues not before elders
Kurals.Explanation: The modesty by which one does not rush forward and speak in (an assembly of) superiors is the best among all (one's) good qualities
Kurals.Couplet: Midst all good things the best is modest grace,That speaks not first before the elders' face

Kurals.Translation: Tongue-slip before the talented wise is like slipping from righteous ways
Kurals.Explanation: (For a minister) to blunder in the presence of those who have acquired a vast store of learning and know (the value thereof) is like a good man stumbling (and falling away) from the path (of virtue)
Kurals.Couplet: As in the way one tottering falls, is slip beforeThe men whose minds are filled with varied lore
Kurals.emotions: **Embarrassment** – The mention of "tongue-slip" and "blunder" points to feelings of awkwardness or shame when making a mistake before knowledgeable people., **

Kurals.Translation: The learning of the learned shines Valued by flawless scholar-minds
Kurals.Explanation: The learning of those who have read and understood (much) will shine in the assembly of those who faultlessly examine (the nature of) words
Kurals.Couplet: The learning of the learned sage shines brightTo those whose faultless skill can value it aright
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for knowledge and scholars, for learned individuals and their wisdom, desire to learn and be valued in scholarly assemblies

Kurals.Translation: To address understanding ones Is to water beds of growing grains
Kurals.Explanation: Lecturing to those who have the ability to understand (for themselves) is like watering a bed of plants that are growing (of themselves)
Kurals.Couplet: To speak where understanding hearers you obtain,Is sprinkling water on the fields of growing grain
Kurals.emotions: Respect – for the capability and growth of others., Appreciation – for self-sufficiency and independent understanding., Humility – in recognizing when input may not be necessary or could

Kurals.Translation: O ye who speak before the keen Forgetful, address not the mean
Kurals.Explanation: Those who are able to speak good things impressively in an assembly of the good should not even forgetfully speak them in that of the low
Kurals.Couplet: In councils of the good, who speak good things with penetrating power,In councils of the mean, let them say nought, e'en in oblivious hour
Kurals.emotions: Caution — Advising carefulness about where and to whom one speaks., Disdain — Implicitly expressing a negative judgment toward "the low" or mean

Kurals.Translation: To hostiles who wise words utters Pours ambrosia into gutters
Kurals.Explanation: To utter (a good word) in the assembly of those who are of inferior rank is like dropping nectar on the ground
Kurals.Couplet: Ambrosia in the sewer spilt, is wordSpoken in presence of the alien herd
Kurals.emotions: Frustration – The sense that wise or beneficial words are wasted or unappreciated when offered to those who cannot value them., Disappointment – The feeling that efforts or wisdom

Summary: Highlights the pursuit of learning as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Respect, Thoughtfulness, Caution.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The pure fail not in power of words Knowing grand council's moods and modes
Kurals.Explanation: The pure who know the classification of words having first ascertained the nature (of the court) will not (through fear) falter in their speech before the powerful body
Kurals.Couplet: Men, pure in heart, who know of words the varied force,The mighty council's moods discern, nor fail in their discourse

Kurals.Translation: Among scholars he is scholar Who holds scholars with learned lore
Kurals.Explanation: Those who can agreeably set forth their acquirements before the learned will be regarded as the most learned among the learned
Kurals.Couplet: Who what they've learned, in penetrating words heve learned to say,Before the learn'd among the learn'd most learn'd are they
Kurals.emotions: Confidence – The text highlights fearlessness and the ability to present one's knowledge boldly before others., Respect – It discusses earning esteem and regard among other learned people., Pride – There's

Kurals.Translation: Many brave foes and die in fields The fearless few face wise councils
Kurals.Explanation: Many indeed may (fearlessly) die in the presence of (their) foes; (but) few are those who are fearless in the assembly (of the learned)
Kurals.Couplet: Many encountering death in face of foe will hold their ground;Who speak undaunted in the council hall are rarely found
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Fearlessness, Apprehension in front of a learned assembly, for wisdom or authority in an assembly

Kurals.Translation: Impress the learned with your lore From greater savants learn still more
Kurals.Explanation: (Ministers) should agreeably set forth their acquirements before the learned and acquire more (knowledge) from their superiors (in learning)
Kurals.Couplet: What you have learned, in penetrating words speak out beforeThe learn'd; but learn what men more learn'd can teach you more
Kurals.emotions: Confidence – The text emphasizes presenting one’s knowledge before learned people

Kurals.Translation: Grammar and logic learn so that Foes you can boldly retort
Kurals.Explanation: In order to reply fearlessly before a foreign court, (ministers) should learn logic according to the rules (of grammar)
Kurals.Couplet: By rule, to dialectic art your mind apply,That in the council fearless you may make an apt reply

Kurals.Translation: To cowards what can sword avail And books to those who councils fail?
Kurals.Explanation: What have they to do with a sword who are not valiant, or they with learning who are afraid of an intelligent assembly ?
Kurals.Couplet: To those who lack the hero's eye what can the sword availOr science what, to those before the council keen who quail
Kurals.emotions: Fear (or Cowardice): The text discusses the lack of courage and the consequences of fearfulness., Shame (or Embarrassment): Implied as those who are afraid or

Kurals.Translation: Like eunuch's sword in field, is vain His lore who fears men of brain
Kurals.Explanation: The learning of him who is diffident before an assembly is like the shining sword of an hermaphrodite in the presence of his foes
Kurals.Couplet: As shining sword before the foe which 'sexless being' bears,Is science learned by him the council's face who fears

Kurals.Translation: Though learned much his lore is dead Who says no good before the good
Kurals.Explanation: Those who cannot agreeably speak good things before a good assembly are indeed unprofitable persons inspite of all their various acquirements
Kurals.Couplet: Though many things they've learned, yet useless are they all,To man who cannot well and strongly speak in council hall
Kurals.emotions: Fear or Anxiety, Unworthiness or Inadequacy, Respect or Reverence
Kurals.EmotionDetail: about speaking before an assembly, feeling unprofitable or lacking if unable to speak,

Kurals.Translation: Who fear to face good assembly Are learned idiots, certainly
Kurals.Explanation: They who, though they have learned and understood, are yet afraid of the assembly of the good, are said to be inferior (even) to the illiterate
Kurals.Couplet: Who, though they've learned, before the council of the good men quake,Than men unlearn'd a lower place must take
Kurals.emotions: Fear – Refers to the anxiety or apprehension felt when facing an assembly or group., Inferiority – A sense of inadequacy or low self-worth

Kurals.Translation: They are breathing dead who dare not Empress before the wise their art
Kurals.Explanation: Those who through fear of the assembly are unable to set forth their learning in an interesting manner, though alive, are yet like the dead
Kurals.Couplet: Who what they've learned, in penetrating words know not to say,The council fearing, though they live, as dead are they

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Courage, Confidence, Composure.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: It's country which has souls of worth Unfailing yields and ample wealth
Kurals.Explanation: A kingdom is that in which (those who carry on) a complete cultivation, virtuous persons, and
Kurals.Couplet: Where spreads fertility unfailing, where resides a band,Of virtuous men, and those of ample wealth, call that a 'land'
Kurals.EmotionDetail: in the worth and virtues of the country/kingdom, from ample wealth and unfailing yields, for virtuous persons within the society

Kurals.Translation: The Land has large luring treasure Where pests are nil and yields are sure
Kurals.Explanation: A kingdom is that which is desire for its immense wealth, and which grows greatly in prosperity, being free from destructive causes
Kurals.Couplet: That is a 'land' which men desire for wealth's abundant share,Yielding rich increase, where calamities are rare
Kurals.emotions: Desire – The text speaks of land that is "luring" and "desired for its immense wealth.", Security – The mention of "pests are nil" and "yields

Kurals.Translation: It's land that bears pressing burdens And pays its tax which king demands
Kurals.Explanation: A kingdom is that which can bear any burden that may be pressed on it (from adjoining kingdoms) and (yet) pay the full tribute to its sovereign
Kurals.Couplet: When burthens press, it bears; Yet, With unfailing handTo king due tribute pays: that is the 'land'
Kurals.emotions: **Resilience** – The text highlights the ability to withstand and bear heavy burdens., **Duty** – There is emphasis on fulfilling obligations and paying demands/tribute., **

Kurals.Translation: It is country which is free from Fierce famine, plague and foemen's harm
Kurals.Explanation: A kingdom is that which continues to be free from excessive starvation, irremediable epidemics, and destructive foes
Kurals.Couplet: That is a 'land' whose peaceful annals know,Nor famine fierce, nor wasting plague, nor ravage of the foe
Kurals.emotions: Security – The emphasis on freedom from famine

Kurals.Translation: Sects and ruinous foes are nil No traitors in a land tranquil
Kurals.Explanation: A kingdom is that which is without various (irregular) associations, destructive internal enemies, and murderous savages who (sometimes) harass the sovereign
Kurals.Couplet: From factions free, and desolating civil strife, and bandOf lurking murderers that king afflict, that is the 'land'
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Security – The emphasis on the absence of enemies and traitors evokes a sense of safety and protection., Peacefulness – Describing the land as tranquil and free from turmoil suggests a

Kurals.Translation: The land of lands no ruin knows Even in grief its wealth yet grows
Kurals.Explanation: The learned say that the best kingdom is that which knows no evil (from its foes), and, if injured (at all), suffers no diminution in its fruitfulness
Kurals.Couplet: Chief of all lands is that, where nought disturbs its peace;Or, if invaders come, still yields its rich increase
Kurals.emotions: **Pride** – There is a sense of pride in one's country for its enduring strength and resilience., **Hope** – The idea that even in grief

Kurals.Translation: Waters up and down, hills and streams With strong forts as limbs country beams
Kurals.Explanation: The constituents of a kingdom are the two waters (from above and below), well situated hills and an undestructible fort
Kurals.Couplet: Waters from rains and springs, a mountain near, and waters thence;These make a land, with fortress' sure defence
Kurals.emotions: Security – The reference to strong forts and natural features fosters a sense of safety and protection., Pride – The description of a well-endowed country evokes admiration and pride in one’s land

Kurals.Translation: Rich yield, delight, defence and wealth Are jewels of lands with blooming health
Kurals.Explanation: Freedom from epidemics, wealth, produce, happiness and protection (to subjects); these five, the learned, say, are the ornaments of a kingdom
Kurals.Couplet: A country's jewels are these five: unfailing health,Fertility, and joy, a sure defence, and wealth

Kurals.Translation: A land is land which yields unsought Needing hard work the land is nought
Kurals.Explanation: The learned say that those are kingdom whose wealth is not laboured for, and those not, whose wealth is only obtained through labour
Kurals.Couplet: That is a land that yields increase unsought,That is no land whose gifts with toil are bought
Kurals.emotions: Contentment – The text expresses appreciation for effortless abundance

Kurals.Translation: Though a land has thus every thing It is worthless without a king
Kurals.Explanation: Although in possession of all the above mentioned excellences, these are indeed of no use to a country, in the absence of harmony between the sovereign and the sujects
Kurals.Couplet: Though blest with all these varied gifts' increase,A land gains nought that is not with its king at peace
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for leadership or unity, due to lack of a king or harmony, on authority and harmonious relationships

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Protection., Pride, Security.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The fort is vital for offence Who fear the foes has its defence
Kurals.Explanation: A fort is an object of importance to those who march (against their foes) as well as to those who through fear (of pursuers) would seek it for shelter
Kurals.Couplet: A fort is wealth to those who act against their foes;Is wealth to them who, fearing, guard themselves from woes
Kurals.emotions: Fear – The text discusses seeking a fort for shelter due to fear of pursuers., Security – The fort provides a sense of protection and safety for those defending or seeking refuge

Kurals.Translation: A crystal fount, a space a mount Thick woods form a fort paramount
Kurals.Explanation: A fort is that which has everlasting water, plains, mountains and cool shady forests
Kurals.Couplet: A fort is that which owns fount of waters crystal clear,An open space, a hill, and shade of beauteous forest near
Kurals.emotions: Security/Safety – The description of a fort protected by woods

Kurals.Translation: An ideal fort's so says science: High, broad, strong and hard for access
Kurals.Explanation: The learned say that a fortress is an enclosure having these four (qualities) viz, height, breadth, strength and inaccessibility
Kurals.Couplet: Height, breadth, strength, difficult access:Science declares a fort must these possess
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with this text:, Security – The emphasis on strength

Kurals.Translation: Ample in space, easy to hold The fort foils enemies bold
Kurals.Explanation: A fort is that which has an extensive space within, but only small places to be guarded, and such as can destroy the courage of besieging foes
Kurals.Couplet: A fort must need but slight defence, yet ample be,Defying all the foeman's energy
Kurals.emotions: Security – The description of the fort as "ample in space

Kurals.Translation: Impregnable with stores of food Cosy to live-That fort is good
Kurals.Explanation: A fort is that which cannot be captured, which abounds in suitable provisions, and affords a position of easy defence to its inmates
Kurals.Couplet: Impregnable, containing ample stores of food,A fort for those within, must be a warlike station good

Kurals.Translation: A fort is full of stores and arms And brave heroes to meet alarms
Kurals.Explanation: A fort is that which has all (needful) things, and excellent heroes that can help it against destruction (by foes)
Kurals.Couplet: A fort, with all munitions amply stored,In time of need should good reserves afford
Kurals.emotions: Security – The fort is described as being full of stores

Kurals.Translation: Besieging foes a fort withstands Darts and mines of treacherous hands
Kurals.Explanation: A fort is that which cannot be captured by blockading, assaulting, or undermining it
Kurals.Couplet: A fort should be impregnable to foes who gird it round,Or aim there darts from far, or mine beneath the ground
Kurals.emotions: Security – Emphasizing the sense of safety and protection provided by a strong fort., Confidence – Reflecting the feeling of assurance in the strength and resilience of the fort.

Kurals.Translation: A fort holds itself and defies The attacks of encircling foes
Kurals.Explanation: That is a fort whose inmates are able to overcome without losing their ground, even abler men who have besieged it
Kurals.Couplet: Howe'er the circling foe may strive access to win,A fort should give the victory to those who guard within
Kurals.emotions: Courage – The text speaks of holding firm and not yielding to attacks

Kurals.Translation: A fort it is that fells the foes And gains by deeds a name glorious
Kurals.Explanation: A fort is that which derives excellence from the stratagems made (by its inmates) to defeat their enemies in the battlefield
Kurals.Couplet: At outset of the strife a fort should foes dismay;And greatness gain by deeds in every glorious day
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions:, Pride – associated with achieving glory and excellence through deeds., Determination – linked to strategizing and overcoming foes., Confidence – stemming from the belief in successfully defeating enemies and gaining a good reputation.

Kurals.Translation: But a fort however grand Is nil if heroes do not stand
Kurals.Explanation: Although a fort may possess all (the above-said) excellence, it is, as it were without these, if its inmates possess not the excellence of action
Kurals.Couplet: Howe'er majestic castled walls may rise,To craven souls no fortress strength supplies
Kurals.EmotionDetail: about strength without true support, for heroic action and virtue, awareness of the limitations of material assets without good people

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Security, Comfort, Confidence.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Naught exists that can, save wealth Make the worthless as men of worth
Kurals.Explanation: Besides wealth there is nothing that can change people of no importance into those of (some) importance
Kurals.Couplet: Nothing exists save wealth, that canChange man of nought to worthy man
Kurals.emotions: Ambition – reflects the desire for wealth and upward mobility., Envy – stems from the implication that wealth confers value and importance., Insecurity – suggested by the idea

Kurals.Translation: The have-nothing poor all despise The men of wealth all raise and praise
Kurals.Explanation: All despise the poor; (but) all praise the rich
Kurals.Couplet: Those who have nought all will despise;All raise the wealthy to the skies

Kurals.Translation: Waneless wealth is light that goes To every land and gloom removes
Kurals.Explanation: The imperishable light of wealth goes into regions desired (by its owner) and destroys the darkness (of enmity therein)
Kurals.Couplet: Wealth, the lamp unfailing, speeds to every land,Dispersing darkness at its lord's command
Kurals.emotions: Hope – The reference to wealth as "light" that removes "gloom" suggests an uplifting sense of hope for improvement., Desire – The yearning for wealth to reach "regions desired

Kurals.Translation: The blameless wealth from fairest means Brings good virtue and also bliss
Kurals.Explanation: The wealth acquired with a knowledge of the proper means and without foul practices will yield virtue and happiness
Kurals.Couplet: Their wealth, who blameless means can use aright,Is source of virtue and of choice delight

Kurals.Translation: Riches devoid of love and grace Off with it; it is disgrace!
Kurals.Explanation: (Kings) should rather avoid than seek the accumulation of wealth which does not flow in with mercy and love
Kurals.Couplet: Wealth gained by loss of love and grace,Let man cast off from his embrace
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for wealth without love and grace, of acquiring wealth devoid of compassion, valuing mercy and love over material gain

Kurals.Translation: Escheats, derelicts; spoils of war Taxes duties are king's treasure
Kurals.Explanation: The spoils of slaughtered foes; these are the royal revenues
Kurals.Couplet: Wealth that falls to him as heir, wealth from the kingdom's dues,
Kurals.emotions: Ambition – The pursuit of acquiring wealth and power is rooted in ambition., Greed – The emphasis on spoils

Kurals.Translation: Grace the child of love is nourished By the wet-nurse of wealth cherished
Kurals.Explanation: The child mercy which is borne by love grows under the care of the rich nurse of wealth
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis love that kindliness as offspring bears:And wealth as bounteous nurse the infant rears
Kurals.emotions: Nurturing – The text speaks of "nourishing" and "cherishing

Kurals.Translation: Treasures in hand fulfil all things Like hill-tuskers the wars of kings
Kurals.Explanation: An undertaking of one who has wealth in one's hands is like viewing an elephant-fight from a hill-top
Kurals.Couplet: As one to view the strife of elephants who takes his stand,On hill he's climbed, is he who works with money in his hand
Kurals.emotions: Security – The text conveys a sense of safety and confidence that comes from possessing wealth., Superiority – There is an implied feeling of advantage or elevation over others

Kurals.Translation: Make wealth; there is no sharper steel The insolence of foes to quell
Kurals.Explanation: Accumulate wealth; it will destroy the arrogance of (your) foes; there is no weapon sharper than it
Kurals.Couplet: Make money! Foeman's insolence o'ergrownTo lop away no keener steel is known
Kurals.emotions: Ambition – The encouragement to accumulate wealth reflects a strong desire to achieve and succeed., Power – Possessing wealth is portrayed as a means of gaining control or influence

Kurals.Translation: They have joy and virtue at hand Who acquire treasures abundant
Kurals.Explanation: To those who have honestly acquired an abundance of riches, the other two, (virtue and pleasure) are things easy (of acquisition)
Kurals.Couplet: Who plenteous store of glorious wealth have gained,By them the other two are easily obtained
Kurals.emotions: Joy – The text explicitly mentions “joy” as something available to those who acquire wealth honestly., Virtue – There is an emphasis on acquiring wealth in a virtuous

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Disdain, Upward Mobility., Importance..
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The daring well-armed winning force Is king's treasure and main resource
Kurals.Explanation: The army which is complete in (its) parts and conquers without fear of wounds is the chief wealth of the king
Kurals.Couplet: A conquering host, complete in all its limbs, that fears no wound,Mid treasures of the king is chiefest found

Kurals.Translation: Through shots and wounds brave heroes hold Quailing not in fall, the field
Kurals.Explanation: Ancient army can alone have the valour which makes it stand by its king at the time of defeat, fearless of wounds and unmindful of its reduced strength
Kurals.Couplet: In adverse hour, to face undaunted might of conquering foe,Is bravery that only veteran host can show
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Bravery, ,

Kurals.Translation: Sea-like ratfoes roar What if? They perish at a cobra's whiff
Kurals.Explanation: What if (a host of) hostile rats roar like the sea ? They will perish at the mere breath of the cobra
Kurals.Couplet: Though, like the sea, the angry mice send forth their battle cry;What then? The dragon breathes upon them, and they die
Kurals.emotions: Confidence – The text conveys assuredness in the strength or superiority of the “cobra” over its foes., Contempt – It expresses a dismissive attitude toward the “ratfoes

Kurals.Translation: The army guards its genial flame Not crushed, routed nor marred in name
Kurals.Explanation: That indeed is an army which has stood firm of old without suffering destruction or deserting (to the enemy)
Kurals.Couplet: That is a host, by no defeats, by no desertions shamed,For old hereditary courage famed

Kurals.Translation: The real army with rallied force Resists even Death-God fierce
Kurals.Explanation: That indeed is an army which is capable of offering a united resistance, even if Yama advances against it with fury
Kurals.Couplet: That is a 'host' that joins its ranks, and mightily withstands,Though death with sudden wrath should fall upon its bands

Kurals.Translation: Manly army has merits four:- Stately-march, faith, honour, valour
Kurals.Explanation: Valour, honour, following in the excellent-footsteps (of its predecessors) and trust-worthiness; these four alone constitute the safeguard of an army
Kurals.Couplet: Valour with honour, sure advance in glory's path, with confidence;To warlike host these four are sure defence
Kurals.emotions: Pride – Reflecting the sense of honour and the greatness of the army’s qualities., Trust – Emphasizing faith and trustworthiness as critical merits., Courage – Highlighting

Kurals.Translation: Army sets on to face to foes Knowing how the trend of war goes
Kurals.Explanation: That is an army which knowing the art of warding off an impending struggle, can bear against the dust-van (of a hostile force)
Kurals.Couplet: A valiant army bears the onslaught, onward goes,Well taught with marshalled ranks to meet their coming foes
Kurals.emotions: Courage – Facing hostile forces and impending struggle implies bravery., Determination – The army advances with a clear understanding of war

Kurals.Translation: Army gains force by grand array Lacking in stay or dash in fray
Kurals.Explanation: Though destitute of courage to fight and strength (to endure), an army may yet gain renown by the splendour of its appearance
Kurals.Couplet: Though not in war offensive or defensive skilled;An army gains applause when well equipped and drilled
Kurals.emotions: Pride – Reflecting the "splendour of its appearance" and the sense of renown., Insecurity – Due to being "destitute of courage to fight

Kurals.Translation: Army shall win if it is free From weakness, aversion, poverty
Kurals.Explanation: An army can triumph (over its foes) if it is free from diminution; irremediable aversion and poverty
Kurals.Couplet: Where weakness, clinging fear and povertyAre not, the host will gain the victory
Kurals.EmotionDetail: stemming from the idea of strength and preparedness, related to overcoming adversity and weaknesses, resulting from freedom from poverty and aversion

Kurals.Translation: With troops in large numbers on rolls Army can't march missing gen'rals
Kurals.Explanation: Though an army may contain a large number of permanent soldiers, it cannot last if it has no generals
Kurals.Couplet: Though men abound, all ready for the war,No army is where no fit leaders are
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for the well-being and effectiveness of the group/army, on leadership and guidance, awareness of insufficiency without key leaders

Summary: Highlights the responsibility of kingship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Courage, Pride, Loyalty.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Stand not before my chief, O foes! Many who stood, in stones repose
Kurals.Explanation: O my foes, stand not before my leader; (for) many are those who did so but afterwards stood (in the shape of) statues
Kurals.Couplet: Ye foes stand not before my lord for many a oneWho did my lord withstand, now stands in stone
Kurals.emotions: Pride – The text strongly conveys admiration and pride for the leader's strength and reputation., Intimidation – There’s an element of warning and fear directed towards the foes.

Kurals.Translation: To lift a lance that missed a tusker Is prouder than shaft that hit a hare
Kurals.Explanation: It is more pleasant to hold the dart that has missed an elephant than that which has hit hare in the forest
Kurals.Couplet: Who aims at elephant, though dart should fail, has greater praiseThan he who woodland hare with winged arrow slays

Kurals.Translation: Valour is fight with fierce courage Mercy to the fallen is its edge
Kurals.Explanation: The learned say that fierceness (incontest with a foe) is indeed great valour; but to become a benefactor in case of accident (to a foe) is the extreme (limit) of that valour
Kurals.Couplet: Fierceness in hour of strife heroic greatness shows;Its edge is kindness to our suffering foes
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Courage – Highlighted by "fierce courage" and "great valour" in the face of adversity., Compassion – Shown through "mercy to the fallen"

Kurals.Translation: At the tusker he flings his lance One in body smiles another chance
Kurals.Explanation: The hero who after casting the lance in his hand on an elephant, comes (in search of another) will pluck the one (that sticks) in his body and laugh (exultingly)
Kurals.Couplet: At elephant he hurls the dart in hand; for weapon pressed,He laughs and plucks the javelin from his wounded breast
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with the provided text are:, Pride, Courage, Exultation
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , , Triumphant joy

Kurals.Translation: When lances dart if heroes wink \"It is a rout\" the world will think
Kurals.Explanation: Is it not a defeat to the valiant to wink and destroy their ferocious look when a lance in cast at them (by their foe) ?
Kurals.Couplet: To hero fearless must it not defeat appear,If he but wink his eye when foemen hurls his spear
Kurals.emotions: Pride – Stemming from the concept of "military pride" and the expectation to maintain a brave appearance., Shame – Associated with the idea of "defeat" or

Kurals.Translation: The brave shall deem the days as vain Which did not battle-wounds sustain
Kurals.Explanation: The hero will reckon among wasted days all those on which he had not received severe wounds
Kurals.Couplet: The heroes, counting up their days, set down as vainEach day when they no glorious wound sustain

Kurals.Translation: Their anklets aloud jingle their name Who sacrifice their life for fame
Kurals.Explanation: The fastening of ankle-ring by those who disire a world-wide renown and not (the safety of) their lives is like adorning (themselves)
Kurals.Couplet: Who seek for world-wide fame, regardless of their life,The glorious clasp adorns, sign of heroic strife
Kurals.emotions: Pride – The text centers on "military pride" and describes the honor sought by those who risk their lives for fame and renown., Courage – It refers to individuals who sacrifice

Kurals.Translation: The king may chide, they pursue strife; They fear loss of glory; not life
Kurals.Explanation: The heroes who are not afraid of losing their life in a contest will not cool their ardour, even if the king prohibits (their fighting)
Kurals.Couplet: Fearless they rush where'er 'the tide of battle rolls';The king's reproof damps not the ardour of their eager souls
Kurals.EmotionDetail: specifically military/heroic pride, fearlessness in the face of danger, steadfastness despite opposition or prohibition

Kurals.Translation: Who will blame the heroes that lose Their lives in war to keep their vows?
Kurals.Explanation: Who would reproach with failure those who seal their oath with their death ?
Kurals.Couplet: Who says they err, and visits them scorn,Who die and faithful guard the vow they've sworn

Kurals.Translation: Such a death shall be prayed for Which draws the the tears of the ruler
Kurals.Explanation: If (heroes) can so die as to fill with tears the eyes of their rulers, such a death deserves to be obtained even by begging
Kurals.Couplet: If monarch's eyes o'erflow with tears for hero slain,Who would not beg such boon of glorious death to gain
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions in the text:, Honor — The text reflects deep respect for a death that earns the admiration and sorrow of rulers., Pride — There is pride in the idea of a death so noble that it is mourned

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Pride, Courage, Reputation..
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Like friendship what's so hard to gain? That guards one against acts villain?
Kurals.Explanation: What so sure defence 'gainst all that foe can do?
Kurals.Couplet: What so hard for men to gain as friendship trueWhat so sure defence 'gainst all that foe can do

Kurals.Translation: Good friendship shines like waxing moon, The bad withers like waning moon
Kurals.Explanation: The friendship of the wise waxes like the new moon; (but) that of fools wanes like the full moon
Kurals.Couplet: Friendship with men fulfilled of good Waxes like the crescent moon;Friendship with men of foolish mood, Like the full orb, waneth soon

Kurals.Translation: Like taste in books good friendship grows The more one moves the more he knows
Kurals.Explanation: Like learning, the friendship of the noble, the more it is cultivated, the more delightful does it become
Kurals.Couplet: Learned scroll the more you ponder, Sweeter grows the mental food;So the heart by use grows fonder, Bound in friendship with the good
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Delight/Joy – relating to the increasing pleasure and happiness as friendship grows., Appreciation – for noble character and the value found in cultivating good friendships., Curiosity – par

Kurals.Translation: Not to laugh is friendship made But to hit when faults exceed
Kurals.Explanation: Friendship is to be practised not for the purpose of laughing but for that of being beforehand in giving one another sharp rebukes in case of transgression
Kurals.Couplet: Nor for laughter only friendship all the pleasant day,But for strokes of sharp reproving, when from right you stray
Kurals.emotions: Loyalty – Emphasizing the true value of friendship in correcting and supporting each other., Responsibility – Highlighting the duty to guide friends when they make mistakes., Concern – Reflect

Kurals.Translation: No close living nor clasping grip Friendship's feeling heart's fellowship
Kurals.Explanation: Living together and holding frequent intercourse are not necessary (for friendship); (mutual) understanding can alone create a claim for it
Kurals.Couplet: Not association constant, not affection's token bind;'Tis the unison of feeling friends unites of kindred mind

Kurals.Translation: Friendship is not more smile on face It is the smiling heart's embrace
Kurals.Explanation: The love that dwells (merely in the smiles of the face is not friendship; (but) that which dwells deep in the smiles of the heart is true friendship
Kurals.Couplet: Not the face's smile of welcome shows the friend sincere,But the heart's rejoicing gladness when the friend is near

Kurals.Translation: From ruin friendship saves and shares The load of pain and right path shows
Kurals.Explanation: (True) friendship turns aside from evil (ways) makes (him) walk in the (good) way, and, in case of loss if shares his sorrow (with him)
Kurals.Couplet: Friendship from ruin saves, in way of virtue keeps;In troublous time, it weeps with him who weeps

Kurals.Translation: Friendship hastens help in mishaps Like hands picking up dress that slips
Kurals.Explanation: (True) friendship hastens to the rescue of the afflicted (as readily) as the hand of one whose garment is loosened (before an assembly)
Kurals.Couplet: As hand of him whose vesture slips away,Friendship at once the coming grief will stay

Kurals.Translation: Friendship is enthroned on the strength That always helps with utmost warmth
Kurals.Explanation: Friendship may be said to be on its throne when it possesses the power of supporting one at all times and under all circumstances, (in the practice or virtue and wealth)
Kurals.Couplet: And where is friendship's royal seat? In stable mind,Where friend in every time of need support may find

Kurals.Translation: \"Such we are and such they are!\" Ev'n this boast will friendship mar
Kurals.Explanation: Though friends may praise one another saying, "He is so intimate with us, and we so much (with him)"; (still) such friendship will appear mean
Kurals.Couplet: Mean is the friendship that men blazon forth,'He's thus to me' and 'such to him my worth'

Summary: Highlights the value of true friendship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Affection, Compassion, Support.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Than testless friendship nought is worse For contacts formed will scarcely cease
Kurals.Explanation: As those who are of a friendly nature will not forsake (a friend) after once loving (him), there is no evil so great as contracting a friendship without due inquiry
Kurals.Couplet: To make an untried man your friend is ruin sure;For friendship formed unbroken must endure
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text warns about forming friendships without proper evaluation., Betrayal – The fear or risk of being let down by a friend not properly chosen is implied.

Kurals.Translation: Friendship made without frequent test Shall end in grief and death at last
Kurals.Explanation: The friendship contracted by him who has not made repeated inquiry will in the end grieve (him) to death
Kurals.Couplet: Alliance with the man you have not proved and proved again,In length of days will give you mortal pain

Kurals.Translation: Temper, descent, defects and kins Trace well and take companions
Kurals.Explanation: Make friendship (with one) after ascertaining (his) character, birth, defects and the whole of one's relations
Kurals.Couplet: Temper, descent, defects, associations freeFrom blame: know these, then let the man be friend to thee
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text emphasizes careful consideration before forming friendships

Kurals.Translation: Take as good friend at any price The nobly born who shun disgrace
Kurals.Explanation: The friendship of one who belongs to a (good) family and is afraid of (being charged with) guilt, is worth even purchasing
Kurals.Couplet: Who, born of noble race, from guilt would shrink with shame,Pay any price so you as friend that man may claim

Kurals.Translation: Who make you weep and chide wrong trends And lead you right are worthy friends
Kurals.Explanation: You should examine and secure the friendship of those who can speak so as to make you weep over a crime (before its commission) or rebuke you severely (after you have done it) and are able to teach
Kurals.Couplet: Make them your chosen friend whose words repentance move,With power prescription's path to show, while evil they reprove
Kurals.emotions: Gratitude – Appreciation for friends who guide and correct us for our own good., Remorse – The feeling of regret or sorrow when confronted with one’s mistakes or wrongdoings.

Kurals.Translation: Is there a test like misfortune A rod to measure out kinsmen?
Kurals.Explanation: Even in ruin there is some good; (for) it is a rod by which one may measure fully (the affection of one's) relations
Kurals.Couplet: Ruin itself one blessing lends:'Tis staff that measures out one's friends
Kurals.emotions: Disappointment – The mention of "misfortune" and "ruin" suggests an experience of hardship or letdown., Gratitude – Recognizing some good even in adversity

Kurals.Translation: Keep off contacts with fools; that is The greatest gain so say the wise
Kurals.Explanation: It is indead a gain for one to renounce the friendship of fools
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis gain to any man, the sages say,Friendship of fools to put away

Kurals.Translation: Off with thoughts that depress the heart Off with friends that in woe depart
Kurals.Explanation: Do not think of things that discourage your mind, nor contract friendship with those who would forsake you in adversity
Kurals.Couplet: Think not the thoughts that dwarf the soul; nor takeFor friends the men who friends in time of grief forsake

Kurals.Translation: Friends who betray at ruin's brink Burn our mind ev'n at death to think
Kurals.Explanation: The very thought of the friendship of those who have deserted one at the approach of adversity will burn one's mind at the time of death
Kurals.Couplet: Of friends deserting us on ruin's brink,'Tis torture e'en in life's last hour to think
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Mental Anguish, or Sadness

Kurals.Translation: The blameless ones as friends embarace; Give something and give up the base
Kurals.Explanation: Continue to enjoy the friendship of the pure; (but) renounce even with a gift, the friendship of those who do not agree (with the world)
Kurals.Couplet: Cling to the friendship of the spotless one's; whate'er you payRenounce alliance with the men of evil way
Kurals.emotions: Trust – Emphasized by valuing the friendship of the pure or blameless., Disappointment – Felt when recognizing or renouncing unworthy or disagreeable friends

Summary: Highlights the value of true friendship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Caution, Grief, Regret.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: That friendship is good amity Which restrains not one's liberty
Kurals.Explanation: Imtimate friendship is that which cannot in the least be injured by (things done through the) right (of longstanding intimacy)
Kurals.Couplet: Familiarity is friendship's silent pact,That puts restraint on no familiar act

Kurals.Translation: Friendship's heart is freedom close; Wise men's duty is such to please
Kurals.Explanation: The constituents of friendship are (things done through) the right of intimacy; to be pleased with such a right is the duty of the wise
Kurals.Couplet: Familiar freedom friendship's very frame supplies;To be its savour sweet is duty of the wise
Kurals.emotions: Trust – stemming from familiarity and the "right of intimacy" in friendship., Pleasure – expressed as being "pleased with such a right.", Respect – towards the duties and

Kurals.Translation: Of long friendship what is the use Righteous freedom if men refuse?
Kurals.Explanation: Of what avail is long-standing friendship, if friends do not admit as their own actions done through the right of intimacy ?
Kurals.Couplet: When to familiar acts men kind response refuse,What fruit from ancient friendship's use
Kurals.emotions: Disappointment – The text expresses a sense of letdown regarding friendships that do not fulfill expectations of closeness., Longing – There is a yearning for true intimacy and recognition in friendship

Kurals.Translation: Things done unasked by loving friends Please the wise as familiar trends!
Kurals.Explanation: If friends, through the right of friendship, do (anything) without being asked, the wise will be pleased with them on account of its desirability
Kurals.Couplet: When friends unbidden do familiar acts with loving heart,Friends take the kindly deed in friendly part
Kurals.emotions: Gratitude – The wise feel grateful when friends act kindly without being asked., Trust – There is a sense of trust in the bond between friends., Affection – Acts of

Kurals.Translation: Offence of friends feel it easy As folloy or close intimacy
Kurals.Explanation: If friends should perform what is painful, understand that it is owing not only to ignorance, but also to the strong claims of intimacy
Kurals.Couplet: Not folly merely, but familiar carelessness,Esteem it, when your friends cause you distress
Kurals.emotions: Hurt (or Offense) – The feeling of being pained by a friend’s actions., Understanding – Recognizing the reasons behind a friend's actions

Kurals.Translation: They forsake not but continue In friendship's bounds though loss ensue
Kurals.Explanation: Those who stand within the limits (of true friendship) will not even in adversity give up the intimacy of long-standing friends
Kurals.Couplet: Who stand within the bounds quit not, though loss impends,Association with the old familiar friends

Kurals.Translation: Comrades established in firm love Though ruin comes waive not their vow
Kurals.Explanation: Those who have (long) stood in the path of affection will not give it up even if their friends cause (them) their ruin
Kurals.Couplet: True friends, well versed in loving ways,Cease not to love, when friend their love betrays

Kurals.Translation: Fast friends who list not tales of ill Though wronged they say \"that day is well\"
Kurals.Explanation: To those who understand that by which they should not listen to (tales about) the faults of their friends, that is a (profitable) day on which the latter may commit a fault
Kurals.Couplet: In strength of friendship rare of friend's disgrace who will not hear,The day his friend offends will day of grace to him appear
Kurals.emotions: Forgiveness — The text highlights overlooking the faults of friends and not listening to negative tales about them., Loyalty — It emphasizes staying true and supportive to friends despite their mistakes.

Kurals.Translation: To love such friends the world desires Whose friendship has unbroken ties
Kurals.Explanation: They will be loved by the world, who have not forsaken the friendship of those with whom they have kept up an unbroken long-standing intimacy
Kurals.Couplet: Friendship of old and faithful friends,Who ne'er forsake, the world commends

Kurals.Translation: Even foes love for better ends Those who leave not long-standing friends
Kurals.Explanation: Even enemies will love those who have never changed in their affection to their long-standingfriends
Kurals.Couplet: Ill-wishers even wish them well, who guardFor ancient friends, their wonted kind regard

Summary: Highlights the value of true friendship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Loyalty, Trust, Affection.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Swallowing love of soulless men Had better wane than wax anon
Kurals.Explanation: The decrease of friendship with those who look as if they would eat you up (through excess of love) while they are really destitute of goodness is far better than its increase
Kurals.Couplet: Though evil men should all-absorbing friendship show,Their love had better die away than grow
Kurals.emotions: Distrust — Reflecting suspicion toward those whose apparent affection is insincere., Disillusionment — Feeling let down by the realization that someone's friendship is hollow or self-serving. 3

Kurals.Translation: Who fawn in wealth and fail in dearth Gain or lose; such friends have no worth
Kurals.Explanation: Of what avail is it to get or lose the friendship of those who love when there is gain and leave when there is none ?
Kurals.Couplet: What though you gain or lose friendship of men of alien heart,Who when you thrive are friends, and when you fail depart
Kurals.emotions: Disappointment – in friends who are only present in times of gain and not in hardship., Distrust – towards friendships based on self-interest rather than loyalty., Sadness –

Kurals.Translation: Cunning friends who calculate Are like thieves and whores wicked
Kurals.Explanation: Friendship who calculate the profits (of their friendship), prostitutes who are bent on obtaining their gains, and thieves are (all) of the same character
Kurals.Couplet: These are alike: the friends who ponder friendship's gainThose who accept whate'er you give, and all the plundering train

Kurals.Translation: Better be alone than trust in those That throw in field like faithless horse
Kurals.Explanation: Solitude is more to be desired than the society of those who resemble the untrained horses which throw down (their riders) in the fields of battle
Kurals.Couplet: A steed untrained will leave you in the tug of war;Than friends like that to dwell alone is better far

Kurals.Translation: Friends low and mean that give no help- Leave them is better than to keep
Kurals.Explanation: It is far better to avoid that to contract the evil friendship of the base who cannot protect (their friends) even when appointed to do so
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis better not to gain than gain the friendship profitlessOf men of little minds, who succour fails when dangers press

Kurals.Translation: Million times the wise man's hate Is better than a fool intimate
Kurals.Explanation: The hatred of the wise is ten-million times more profitable than the excessive intimacy of the fool
Kurals.Couplet: Better ten million times incur the wise man's hate,Than form with foolish men a friendship intimate
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , for wisdom

Kurals.Translation: Ten-fold crore you gain from foes Than from friends who are vain laughers
Kurals.Explanation: What comes from enemies is a hundred million times more profitable than what comes from the friendship of those who cause only laughter
Kurals.Couplet: From foes ten million fold a greater good you gain,Than friendship yields that's formed with laughers vain
Kurals.emotions: Disappointment – towards unworthy or vain friends who offer only shallow amusement., Caution – in choosing friends versus foes

Kurals.Translation: Without a word those friends eschew Who spoil deeds which they can do
Kurals.Explanation: Gradually abandon without revealing (beforehand) the friendship of those who pretend inability to carry out what they (really) could do
Kurals.Couplet: Those men who make a grievous toil of what they doOn your behalf, their friendship silently eschew

Kurals.Translation: Even in dreams the tie is bad With those whose deed is far from word
Kurals.Explanation: The friendship of those whose actions do not agree with their words will distress (one) even in (one's) dreams
Kurals.Couplet: E'en in a dream the intercourse is bitternessWith men whose deeds are other than their words profess

Kurals.Translation: Keep aloof from those that smile At home and in public revile
Kurals.Explanation: Avoid even the least approach to a contraction of friendship with those who would love you in private but ridicule you in public
Kurals.Couplet: In anywise maintain not intercourse with those,Who in the house are friends, in hall are slandering foes

Summary: Highlights the value of true friendship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Distrust, Caution, Disappointment.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The friendship by an enemy shown Is anvil in time, to strike you down
Kurals.Explanation: The friendship of those who behave like friends without inward affection is a weapon that may be thrown when a favourable opportunity presents itself
Kurals.Couplet: Anvil where thou shalt smitten be, when men occasion find,Is friendship's form without consenting mind

Kurals.Translation: Who pretend kinship but are not Their friendship's fickle like woman's heart
Kurals.Explanation: The friendship of those who seem to be friends while they are not, will change like the love of women
Kurals.Couplet: Friendship of those who seem our kin, but are not really kindWill change from hour to hour like woman's mind

Kurals.Translation: They may be vast in good studies But heartfelt-love is hard for foes
Kurals.Explanation: Though (one's) enemies may have mastered many good books, it will be impossible for them to become truly loving at heart
Kurals.Couplet: To heartfelt goodness men ignoble hardly may attain,Although abundant stores of goodly lore they gain

Kurals.Translation: Fear foes whose face has winning smiles Whose heart is full of cunning guiles
Kurals.Explanation: One should fear the deceitful who smile sweetly with their face but never love with their heart
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis fitting you should dread dissemblers' guile,Whose hearts are bitter while their faces smile

Kurals.Translation: Do not trust in what they tell Whose mind with your mind goes ill
Kurals.Explanation: In nothing whatever is it proper to rely on the words of those who do not love with their heart
Kurals.Couplet: When minds are not in unison, 'its never; just,In any words men speak to put your trust

Kurals.Translation: The words of foes is quickly seen Though they speak like friends in fine
Kurals.Explanation: Though (one's) foes may utter good things as though they were friends, once will at once understand (their evil, import)
Kurals.Couplet: Though many goodly words they speak in friendly tone,The words of foes will speedily be known

Kurals.Translation: Trust not the humble words of foes Danger darts from bending bows
Kurals.Explanation: Since the bending of the bow bespeaks evil, one should not accept (as good) the humiliating speeches of one's foes
Kurals.Couplet: To pliant speech from hostile lips give thou no ear;'Tis pliant bow that show the deadly peril near

Kurals.Translation: Adoring hands of foes hide arms Their sobbing tears have lurking harms
Kurals.Explanation: A weapon may be hid in the very hands with which (one's) foes adore (him) (and) the tears they shed are of the same nature
Kurals.Couplet: In hands that worship weapon ten hidden lies;Such are the tears that fall from foeman's eyes

Kurals.Translation: In open who praise, at heart despise Cajole and crush them in friendly guise
Kurals.Explanation: It is the duty of kings to affect great love but make it die (inwardly); as regard those foes who shew them great friendship but despise them (in their heart)
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis just, when men make much of you, and then despise,To make them smile, and slap in friendship's guise

Kurals.Translation: When foes, in time, play friendship's part Feign love on face but not in heart
Kurals.Explanation: When one's foes begin to affect friendship, one should love them with one's looks, and, cherishing no love in the heart, give up (even the former)
Kurals.Couplet: When time shall come that foes as friends appear,Then thou, to hide a hostile heart, a smiling face may'st wear

Summary: Highlights the value of true friendship as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Distrust, Caution, Disappointment.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: This is folly's prominent vein To favour loss and forego gain
Kurals.Explanation: Folly is one (of the chief defects); it is that which (makes one) incur loss and forego gain
Kurals.Couplet: What one thing merits folly's special name.Letting gain go, loss for one's own to claim
Kurals.emotions: Regret – The focus on incurring loss and foregoing gain hints at feelings of missed opportunities and consequences., Foolishness – The chapter centers on folly

Kurals.Translation: Folly of follies is to lead A lewd and lawless life so bad
Kurals.Explanation: The greatest folly is that which leads one to take delight in doing what is forbidden
Kurals.Couplet: 'Mid follies chiefest folly is to fix your loveOn deeds which to your station unbefitting prove
Kurals.emotions: Regret – A sense of sorrow for poor choices or misbehavior., Shame – Feeling dishonor or disgrace from immoral or lawless actions., Warning (Caution) –

Kurals.Translation: Shameless, aimless, callous, listless Such are the marks of foolishness
Kurals.Explanation: Shamelessness indifference (to what must be sought after), harshness, and aversion for everything (that ought to be desired) are the qualities of the fool
Kurals.Couplet: Ashamed of nothing, searching nothing out, of loveless heart,Nought cherishing, 'tis thus the fool will play his part
Kurals.emotions: Indifference, Apathy, Shamelessness These emotions are conveyed through descriptors such as "shameless
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , listlessness,

Kurals.Translation: No fool equals the fool who learns Knows, teaches, but self-control spurns
Kurals.Explanation: There are no greater fools than he who, though he has read and understood (a great deal) and even taught it to others, does not walk according to his own teaching
Kurals.Couplet: The sacred law he reads and learns, to other men expounds,-Himself obeys not; where can greater fool be found
Kurals.emotions: Shame – The text implies a sense of self-embarrassment or dishonor for not practicing what one preaches., Hypocrisy – There is an underlying feeling of

Kurals.Translation: The fool suffers seven fold hells In single birth of hellish ills
Kurals.Explanation: A fool can procure in a single birth a hell into which he may enter and suffer through all the seven births
Kurals.Couplet: The fool will merit hell in one brief life on earth,In which he entering sinks through sevenfold round of birth

Kurals.Translation: A know-nothing fool daring a deed Not only fails but feels fettered
Kurals.Explanation: If the fool, who knows not how to act undertakes a work, he will (certainly) fail (But) is it all ? He will even adorn himself with fetters
Kurals.Couplet: When fool some task attempts with uninstructed pains,It fails; nor that alone, himself he binds with chains
Kurals.EmotionDetail: ignorance/stupidity, due to failure and entrapment, realizing the cost of unwise action

Kurals.Translation: Strangers feast and kinsmen fast When fools mishandle fortunes vast
Kurals.Explanation: If a fool happens to get an immense fortune, his neighbours will enjoy it while his relations starve
Kurals.Couplet: When fools are blessed with fortune's bounteous store,Their foes feed full, their friends are prey to hunger sore
Kurals.emotions: Frustration – at the mismanagement of wealth by fools., Envy – as kinsmen watch others benefit while they suffer., Regret – over the

Kurals.Translation: Fools possessing something on hand Like dazed and drunken stupids stand
Kurals.Explanation: A fool happening to possess something is like the intoxication of one who is (already) giddy
Kurals.Couplet: When folly's hand grasps wealth's increase, 'twill beAs when a mad man raves in drunken glee
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , misplaced or excessive self-importance

Kurals.Translation: Friendship with fools is highly sweet For without a groan we part
Kurals.Explanation: The friendship between fools is exceedingly delightful (to each other): for at parting there will be
Kurals.Couplet: Friendship of fools is very pleasant thing,Parting with them will leave behind no sting
Kurals.EmotionDetail: or Delight/Joy, or Folly, or Lack of Sorrow

Kurals.Translation: Entrance of fools where Savants meet Looks like couch trod by unclean feet
Kurals.Explanation: The appearance of a fool in an assembly of the learned is like placing (one's) unwashed feet on a bed
Kurals.Couplet: Like him who seeks his couch with unwashed feet,Is fool whose foot intrudes where wise men meet

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Foolishness, Consequences., Warning (Caution) –.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

chapters.Chapter: Ignorance of What Should Be Known

Kurals.Translation: Want of wisdom is want of wants Want of aught else the world nev'r counts
Kurals.Explanation: The want of wisdom is the greatest of all wants; but that of wealth the world will not regard as such
Kurals.Couplet: Want of knowledge, 'mid all wants the sorest want we deem;Want of other things the world will not as want esteem
Kurals.emotions: **Ignorance** – Reflecting a lack of awareness or knowledge., **Regret** – A sense of loss or deficiency for not possessing wisdom., **Disapproval

Kurals.Translation: When fool bestows with glee a gift It comes but by getter's merit
Kurals.Explanation: (The cause of) a fool cheerfully giving (something) is nothing else but the receiver's merit (in a former birth)
Kurals.Couplet: The gift of foolish man, with willing heart bestowed, is nought,But blessing by receiver's penance bought
Kurals.emotions: Ignorance – The text describes the actions of a "fool

Kurals.Translation: The self-torments of fools exceed Ev'n tortures of their foes indeed
Kurals.Explanation: The suffering that fools inflict upon themselves is hardly possible even to foes
Kurals.Couplet: With keener anguish foolish men their own hearts wring,Than aught that even malice of their foes can bring

Kurals.Translation: Stupidity is vanity That cries \"We have sagacity\"
Kurals.Explanation: What is called want of wisdom is the vanity which says, "We are wise"
Kurals.Couplet: What is stupidity? The arrogance that cries,'Behold, we claim the glory of the wise.'

Kurals.Translation: Feigning knowledge that one has not Leads to doubt ev'n that he has got
Kurals.Explanation: Fools pretending to know what has not been read (by them) will rouse suspicion even as to what they have thoroughly mastered
Kurals.Couplet: If men what they have never learned assume to know,Upon their real learning's power a doubt 'twill throw
Kurals.emotions: Insecurity – The fear of being exposed or doubted about one’s true level of knowledge., Shame – Embarrassment from pretending or being suspected of pretense.

Kurals.Translation: Fools their nakedness conceal And yet their glaring faults reveal
Kurals.Explanation: Even to cover one's nakedness would be folly, if (one's) faults were not covered (by forsaking them)
Kurals.Couplet: Fools are they who their nakedness conceal,And yet their faults unveiled reveal
Kurals.emotions: Shame – Related to the idea of nakedness and the desire to hide one's flaws or shortcomings., Folly (Foolishness) – Implied by the reference to "f

Kurals.Translation: The fool that slights sacred counsels Upon himself great harm entails
Kurals.Explanation: The fool who neglects precious counsel does, of his own accord, a great injury to himself
Kurals.Couplet: From out his soul who lets the mystic teachings die,Entails upon himself abiding misery
Kurals.emotions: Regret – The text reflects the negative consequences faced due to neglecting wise counsel

Kurals.Translation: He listens not nor himself knows Plague is his life until it goes
Kurals.Explanation: The fool will not perform (his duties) even when advised nor ascertain them himself; such a soul is a
Kurals.Couplet: Advised, he heeds not; of himself knows nothing wise;This man's whole life is all one plague until he dies
Kurals.emotions: Frustration – Reflecting irritation at someone’s refusal to listen or learn., Ignorance – A lack of awareness or understanding., Regret – A sense of sorrow

Kurals.Translation: Sans Self-sight in vain one opens Sight To the blind who bet their sight as right
Kurals.Explanation: One who would teach a fool will (simply) betray his folly; and the fool would (still) think himself "wise in his own conceit"
Kurals.Couplet: That man is blind to eyes that will not see who knowledge shows;-The blind man still in his blind fashion knows
Kurals.emotions: Frustration – Stemming from the inability to teach or change the mind of someone who is ignorant but convinced of their own wisdom., Arrogance – Reflected in

Kurals.Translation: To people's \"Yes\" who proffer \"No\" Deemed as ghouls on earth they go
Kurals.Explanation: He who denies the existence of what the world believes in will be regarded as a demon on earth
Kurals.Couplet: Who what the world affirms as false proclaim,O'er all the earth receive a demon's name
Kurals.emotions: Alienation – A sense of being isolated or ostracized due to differing beliefs., Disapproval – Judgment or condemnation from others for rejecting commonly held truths., Fear

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as **Disapproval, Regret, Ignorance.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.
