Kurals.Translation: Hatred is a plague that divides And rouses illwill on all sides
Kurals.Explanation: The disease which fosters the evil of disunion among all creatures is termed hatred by the wise
Kurals.Couplet: Hostility disunion's plague will bring,That evil quality, to every living thing
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , resentment

Kurals.Translation: Rouse not hatred and confusion Though foes provoke disunion
Kurals.Explanation: Though disagreeable things may be done from (a feeling of) disunion, it is far better that nothing painful be done from (that of) hatred
Kurals.Couplet: Though men disunion plan, and do thee much despite'Tis best no enmity to plan, nor evil deeds requite

Kurals.Translation: Shun the plague of enmity And win everlasting glory
Kurals.Explanation: To rid one-self of the distressing dtsease of hatred will bestow (on one) a never-decreasing imperishable fame
Kurals.Couplet: If enmity, that grievous plague, you shun,Endless undying praises shall be won
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with this text:, Hatred, Distress, Aspiration
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , , for glory/fame

Kurals.Translation: Hate-the woe of woes destroy; Then joy of joys you can enjoy
Kurals.Explanation: If hatred which is the greatest misery is destroyed, it will yield the greatest delight
Kurals.Couplet: Joy of joys abundant grows,When malice dies that woe of woes

Kurals.Translation: Who can overcome them in glory That are free from enmity?
Kurals.Explanation: Who indeed would think of conquering those who naturally shrink back from hatred ?
Kurals.Couplet: If men from enmity can keep their spirits free,Who over them shall gain the victory
Kurals.EmotionDetail: from being free from enmity and hatred, towards those who avoid hatred, in recognizing greatness in those without hostility

Kurals.Translation: His fall and ruin are quite near Who holds enmity sweet and dear
Kurals.Explanation: Failure and ruin are not far from him who says it is sweet to excel in hatred
Kurals.Couplet: The life of those who cherished enmity hold dear,To grievous fault and utter death is near
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , a sense of impending doom or despair

Kurals.Translation: They cannot see the supreme Truth Who hate and injure without ruth
Kurals.Explanation: Who only know to work men woe, fulfilled of enmity
Kurals.Couplet: The very truth that greatness gives their eyes can never see,Who only know to work men woe, fulfilled of enmity

Kurals.Translation: To turn from enmity is gain Fomenting it brings fast ruin
Kurals.Explanation: Shrinking back from hatred will yield wealth; indulging in its increase will hasten ruin
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis gain to turn the soul from enmity;Ruin reigns where this hath mastery
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 emotions associated with the text:, Hostility, Caution, Wisdom

Kurals.Translation: Fortune favours when hate recedes Hatred exceeding ruin breeds
Kurals.Explanation: At the approach of wealth one will not think of hatred (but) to secure one's ruin, one will look to its increase
Kurals.Couplet: Men think not hostile thought in fortune's favouring hour,They cherish enmity when in misfortune's power

Kurals.Translation: All evils come from enmity All goodness flow from amity
Kurals.Explanation: All calamities are caused by hatred; but by the delight (of friendship) is caused the great wealth of good virtues
Kurals.Couplet: From enmity do all afflictive evils flow;But friendliness doth wealth of kindly good bestow
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Hatred, Friendship, Joy/Happiness

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Hatred, Hostility, Joy.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Turn from strife with foes too strong With the feeble for battle long
Kurals.Explanation: Avoid offering resistance to the strong; (but) never fail to cherish enmity towards the weak
Kurals.Couplet: With stronger than thyself, turn from the strife away;With weaker shun not, rather court the fray
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Caution, towards strength, towards weakness

Kurals.Translation: Loveless, aidless, powerless king Can he withstand an enemy strong?
Kurals.Explanation: How can he who is unloving, destitute of powerful aids, and himself without strength overcome the might of his foe ?
Kurals.Couplet: No kinsman's love, no strength of friends has he;How can he bear his foeman's enmity
Kurals.emotions: Vulnerability – The text expresses a sense of weakness and exposure due to lack of love

Kurals.Translation: Unskilled, timid, miser, misfit He is easy for foes to hit
Kurals.Explanation: In the estimation of foes miserably weak is he, who is timid, ignorant, unsociable and niggardly
Kurals.Couplet: A craven thing! knows nought, accords with none, gives nought away;To wrath of any foe he falls an easy prey

Kurals.Translation: The wrathful restive man is prey To any, anywhere any day
Kurals.Explanation: He who neither refrains from anger nor keeps his secrets will at all times and in all places be easily conquered by all
Kurals.Couplet: His wrath still blazes, every secret told; each dayThis man's in every place to every foe an easy prey

Kurals.Translation: Crooked, cruel, tactless and base Any foe can fell him with ease
Kurals.Explanation: (A) pleasing (object) to his foes is he who reads not moral works, does nothing that is enjoined by them cares not for reproach and is not possessed of good qualities
Kurals.Couplet: No way of right he scans, no precepts bind, no crimes affright,No grace of good he owns; such man's his foes' delight
Kurals.emotions: Contempt – towards a person who lacks moral values and good qualities., Disapproval – of someone who disregards moral teachings and is indifferent to reproach., Scorn –

Kurals.Translation: Blind in rage and mad in lust To have his hatred is but just
Kurals.Explanation: Highly to be desired is the hatred of him whose anger is blind, and whose lust increases beyond measure
Kurals.Couplet: Blind in his rage, his lustful passions rage and swell;If such a man mislikes you, like it well
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Anger

Kurals.Translation: Pay and buy his enmity Who muddles chance with oddity
Kurals.Explanation: It is indeed necessary to obtain even by purchase the hatred of him who having begun (a work) does what is not conductive (to its accomplishment)
Kurals.Couplet: Unseemly are his deeds, yet proffering aid, the man draws nigh:His hate- 'tis cheap at any price- be sure to buy

Kurals.Translation: With no virtue but full of vice He loses friends and delights foes
Kurals.Explanation: He will become friendless who is without (any good) qualities and whose faults are many; (such a character) is a help to (his) foes
Kurals.Couplet: No gracious gifts he owns, faults many cloud his fame;His foes rejoice, for none with kindred claim
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Loneliness,

Kurals.Translation: The joy of heroes knows no bounds When timid fools are opponents
Kurals.Explanation: There will be no end of lofty delights to the victorious, if their foes are (both) ignorant and timid
Kurals.Couplet: The joy of victory is never far removed from thoseWho've luck to meet with ignorant and timid foes
Kurals.EmotionDetail: derived from "The joy of heroes knows no bounds" and "lofty delights",

Kurals.Translation: Glory's light he will not gain Who fails to fight a fool and win
Kurals.Explanation: The light (of fame) will never be gained by him who gains not the trifling reputation of having fought an unlearned (foe)
Kurals.Couplet: The task of angry war with men unlearned in virtue's loreWho will not meet, glory shall meet him never more
Kurals.emotions: Ambition – The desire for glory and fame through overcoming challenges., Determination – The necessity to take action and confront obstacles

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Prudence, Respect, Contempt Caution.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

chapters.Chapter: Knowing the Strength of an Enemy

Kurals.Translation: Let not one even as a sport The ill-natured enmity court
Kurals.Explanation: The evil of hatred is not of a nature to be desired by one even in sport
Kurals.Couplet: For Hate, that ill-conditioned thing not e'en in jestLet any evil longing rule your breast

Kurals.Translation: Incur the hate of bow-ploughers But not the hate of word-ploughers
Kurals.Explanation: Though you may incur the hatred of warriors whose ploughs are bows, incur not that of ministers whose ploughs are words
Kurals.Couplet: Although you hate incur of those whose ploughs are bows,Make not the men whose ploughs are words your foes
Kurals.EmotionDetail: of incurring the hatred of powerful or influential people, advising prudence in choosing whom to offend,

Kurals.Translation: Forlorn, who rouses many foes The worst insanity betrays
Kurals.Explanation: He who being alone, incurs the hatred of many is more infatuated than even mad men
Kurals.Couplet: Than men of mind diseased, a wretch more utterly forlorn,Is he who stands alone, object of many foeman's scorn
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , of enmity or isolation,

Kurals.Translation: This world goes safely in his grace Whose heart makes friends even of foes
Kurals.Explanation: Whose worthy rule can change his foes to friends
Kurals.Couplet: The world secure on his dexterity depends,Whose worthy rule can change his foes to friends

Kurals.Translation: Alone, if two foes you oppose Make one of them your ally close
Kurals.Explanation: He who is alone and helpless while his foes are two should secure one of them as an agreeable help (to himself)
Kurals.Couplet: Without ally, who fights with twofold enemy o'ermatched,Must render one of these a friend attached
Kurals.emotions: Vulnerability – The person is described as "alone and helpless

Kurals.Translation: Trust or distrust; during distress Keep aloof; don't mix with foes
Kurals.Explanation: Though (one's foe is) aware or not of one's misfortune one should act so as neither to join nor separate (from him)
Kurals.Couplet: Whether you trust or not, in time of sore distress,Questions of diff'rence or agreement cease to press
Kurals.emotions: **Caution** – The advice reflects a careful awareness of potential dangers and the need to avoid hasty decisions., **Distrust** – There is a theme of war

Kurals.Translation: To those who know not, tell not your pain Nor your weakness to foes explain
Kurals.Explanation: Relate not your suffering even to friends who are ignorant of it, nor refer to your weakness in the presence of your foes
Kurals.Couplet: To those who know them not, complain not of your woes;Nor to your foeman's eyes infirmities disclose

Kurals.Translation: Know how and act and defend well The pride of enemies shall fall
Kurals.Explanation: The joy of one's foes will be destroyed if one guards oneself by knowing the way (of acting) and securing assistance
Kurals.Couplet: Know thou the way, then do thy part, thyself defend;Thus shall the pride of those that hate thee have an end
Kurals.emotions: Confidence – in knowing how to act

Kurals.Translation: Cut off thorn-trees when young they are; Grown hard, they cut your hands beware
Kurals.Explanation: A thorny tree should be felled while young, (for) when it is grown it will destroy the hand of the feller
Kurals.Couplet: Destroy the thorn, while tender point can work thee no offence;Matured by time, 'twill pierce the hand that plucks it thence
Kurals.emotions: **Caution** – The advice warns to act early to avoid harm

Kurals.Translation: To breathe on earth they are not fit Defying foes who don't defeat
Kurals.Explanation: Those who do not destroy the pride of those who hate (them) will certainly not exist even to breathe
Kurals.Couplet: But breathe upon them, and they surely die,Who fail to tame the pride of angry enemy
Kurals.emotions: **Vulnerability** – The text expresses the risk of not confronting or overpowering one's enemies

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Caution, Fear, Hatred.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Traitorous kinsmen will make you sad As water and shade do harm when bad
Kurals.Explanation: Shade and water are not pleasant, (if) they cause disease; so are the qualities of (one's) relations not agreeable, (if) they cause pain
Kurals.Couplet: Water and shade, if they unwholesome prove, will bring you painAnd qualities of friends who treacherous act, will be your bane

Kurals.Translation: You need not sword-like kinsmen fear Fear foes who feign as kinsmen dear
Kurals.Explanation: Fear not foes (who say they would cut) like a sword; (but) fear the friendship of foes (who seemingly
Kurals.Couplet: Dread not the foes that as drawn swords appear;Friendship of foes, who seem like kinsmen, fear

Kurals.Translation: The secret foe in days evil Will cut you, beware, like potters' steel
Kurals.Explanation: Fear internal enmity and guard yourself; (if not) it will destroy (you) in an evil hour, as surely as the tool which cuts the potter's clay
Kurals.Couplet: Of hidden hate beware, and guard thy life;In troublous time 'twill deeper wound than potter's knife

Kurals.Translation: The evil-minded foe within Foments trouble, spoils kinsmen!
Kurals.Explanation: The secret enmity of a person whose mind in unreformed will lead to many evils causing disaffection among (one's) relations
Kurals.Couplet: If secret enmities arise that minds pervert,Then even kin unkind will work thee grievous hurt

Kurals.Translation: A traitor among kinsmen will Bring life-endangering evil
Kurals.Explanation: If there appears internal hatred in a (king's) family; it will lead to many a fatal crime
Kurals.Couplet: Amid one's relatives if hidden hath arise,'Twill hurt inflict in deadly wise

Kurals.Translation: Discord in kings' circle entails Life-destroying deadly evils
Kurals.Explanation: If hatred arises among (one's) own people, it will be hardly possible (for one) to escape death
Kurals.Couplet: If discord finds a place midst those who dwelt at one before,'Tis ever hard to keep destruction from the door
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with the provided text are:, Hatred, Fear, Distrust

Kurals.Translation: A house hiding hostiles in core Just seems on like the lid in jar
Kurals.Explanation: Never indeed will a family subject to internal hatred unite (really) though it may present an apparent union like that of a casket and its lid
Kurals.Couplet: As casket with its cover, though in one they live alway,No union to the house where hate concealed hath sway

Kurals.Translation: By secret spite the house wears out Like gold crumbling by file's contact
Kurals.Explanation: A family subject to internal hatred will wear out and lose its strength like iron that has been filed away
Kurals.Couplet: As gold with which the file contends is worn away,So strength of house declines where hate concealed hath sway
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions identified in the text:, Hatred, Distrust, Anxiety

Kurals.Translation: Ruin lurks in enmity As slit in sesame though it be
Kurals.Explanation: Although internal hatred be as small as the fragment of the sesamum (seed), still does destruction dwell in it
Kurals.Couplet: Though slight as shred of 'seasame' seed it be,Destruction lurks in hidden enmity
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with the provided text are:, Hatred, Distrust, Fear

Kurals.Translation: Dwell with traitors that hate in heart Is dwelling with snake in selfsame hut
Kurals.Explanation: Living with those who do not agree (with one) is like dwelling with a cobra (in the same) hut
Kurals.Couplet: Domestic life with those who don't agree,Is dwelling in a shed with snake for company

Summary: Highlights fear of wrongdoing as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Distrust, Fear, Hatred.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Not to spite the mighty ones Safest safeguard to living brings
Kurals.Explanation: Not to disregard the power of those who can carry out (their wishes) is more important than all the watchfulness of those who guard (themselves against evil)
Kurals.Couplet: The chiefest care of those who guard themselves from ill,Is not to slight the powers of those who work their mighty will

Kurals.Translation: To walk unmindful of the great Shall great troubles ceaseless create
Kurals.Explanation: To behave without respect for the great (rulers) will make them do (us) irremediable evils
Kurals.Couplet: If men will lead their lives reckless of great men's will,Such life, through great men's powers, will bring perpetual ill
Kurals.emotions: Fear – Concern about the consequences of offending those in power., Respect – Emphasis on the need to show proper regard for authority., Anxiety – Worry about potential

Kurals.Translation: Heed not and do, if ruin you want Offence against the mighty great
Kurals.Explanation: If a person desires ruin, let him not listen to the righteous dictates of law, but commit crimes against those who are able to slay (other sovereigns)
Kurals.Couplet: Who ruin covet let them shut their ears, and do despiteTo those who, where they list to ruin have the might

Kurals.Translation: The weak who insult men of might Death with their own hands invite
Kurals.Explanation: The weak doing evil to the strong is like beckoning Yama to come (and destroy them)
Kurals.Couplet: When powerless man 'gainst men of power will evil deeds essay,Tis beck'ning with the hand for Death to seize them for its prey
Kurals.emotions: Fear – The text evokes a sense of danger and consequence for the weak who offend the strong., Caution – There is a clear warning

Kurals.Translation: Where can they go and thrive where Pursued by powerful monarch's ire?
Kurals.Explanation: Those who have incurred the wrath of a cruel and mighty potentate will not prosper wherever they may go
Kurals.Couplet: Who dare the fiery wrath of monarchs dread,Where'er they flee, are numbered with the dead
Kurals.emotions: Fear – Fear of incurring the wrath of a powerful ruler and the consequences that follow., Helplessness – Feeling powerless or unable to escape the reach of authority., Anxiety

Kurals.Translation: One can escape in fire caught The great who offends escapes not
Kurals.Explanation: Though burnt by a fire (from a forest), one may perhaps live; (but) never will he live who has shown disrespect to the great (devotees)
Kurals.Couplet: Though in the conflagration caught, he may escape from thence:He 'scapes not who in life to great ones gives offence
Kurals.emotions: Fear – The text evokes fear regarding the consequences of offending great or respected individuals., Respect – There is an emphasis on the necessity of showing respect towards the great and revered.

Kurals.Translation: If holy mighty sages frown Stately gifts and stores who can own?
Kurals.Explanation: If a king incurs the wrath of the righteous great, what will become of his government with its splendid auxiliaries and (all) its untold wealth ?
Kurals.Couplet: Though every royal gift, and stores of wealth your life should crown,What are they, if the worthy men of mighty virtue frown
Kurals.EmotionDetail: of incurring the wrath of the righteous/sages, towards the "holy mighty sages" or the "righteous great",

Kurals.Translation: When hill-like sages are held small The firm on earth lose home and all
Kurals.Explanation: If (the) hill-like (devotees) resolve on destruction, those who seemed to be everlasting will be destroyed root and branch from the earth
Kurals.Couplet: If they, whose virtues like a mountain rise, are light esteemed;They die from earth who, with their households, ever-during seemed
Kurals.emotions: Fear – The threat of destruction and loss creates a sense of fear or dread., Respect – The reference to “hill-like sages” invokes reverence or awe for powerful

Kurals.Translation: Before the holy sage's rage Ev'n Indra's empire meets damage
Kurals.Explanation: Kings even fall from high estate and perish in the flame
Kurals.Couplet: When blazes forth the wrath of men of lofty fame,Kings even fall from high estate and perish in the flame
Kurals.emotions: Fear – of angering someone powerful or sacred., Respect – for the greatness or authority of the "holy sage.", Caution – awareness of the consequences that follow offending the

Kurals.Translation: Even mighty aided men shall quail If the enraged holy seers will
Kurals.Explanation: Though in possession of numerous auxiliaries, they will perish who are-exposed to the wrath of the noble whose penance is boundless
Kurals.Couplet: Though all-surpassing wealth of aid the boast,If men in glorious virtue great are wrath, they're lost
Kurals.emotions: Fear – Of incurring the wrath of powerful or holy figures., Respect – For those with immense power

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Respect, Fear, Caution.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Who dote on wives lose mighty gain That lust, dynamic men disdain
Kurals.Explanation: Those who lust after their wives will not attain the excellence of virtue; and it is just this that is not desired by those who are bent on acquiring wealth
Kurals.Couplet: Who give their soul to love of wife acquire not nobler gain;Who give their soul to strenuous deeds such meaner joys disdain
Kurals.EmotionDetail: lust, , drive for virtue/wealth

Kurals.Translation: Who dotes, unmanly, on his dame His wealth to him and all is shame
Kurals.Explanation: The wealth of him who, regardless (of his manliness), devotes himself to his wife's feminine nature will cause great shame (to ali men) and to himself;
Kurals.Couplet: Who gives himself to love of wife, careless of noble nameHis wealth will clothe him with o'erwhelming shame

Kurals.Translation: Who's servile to his wife always Shy he feels before the wise
Kurals.Explanation: The frailty that stoops to a wife will always make (her husband) feel ashamed among the good
Kurals.Couplet: Who to his wife submits, his strange, unmanly moodWill daily bring him shame among the good

Kurals.Translation: Fearing his wife salvationless The weaklings' action has no grace
Kurals.Explanation: The undertaking of one, who fears his wife and is therefore destitute of (bliss), will never be applauded
Kurals.Couplet: No glory crowns e'en manly actions wroughtBy him who dreads his wife, nor gives the other world a thought

Kurals.Translation: Who fears his wife fears always Good to do to the good and wise
Kurals.Explanation: He that fears his wife will always be afraid of doing good deeds (even) to the good
Kurals.Couplet: Who quakes before his wife will ever tremble too,Good deeds to men of good deserts to do
Kurals.emotions: Fear – The text explicitly discusses fear in relation to one's wife and actions., Anxiety – There is a sense of unease or worry about the consequences of behavior., Restra

Kurals.Translation: Who fear douce arms of their wives Look petty even with god-like lives
Kurals.Explanation: They that fear the bamboo-like shoulders of their wives will be destitute of manliness though they may flourish like the Gods
Kurals.Couplet: Though, like the demi-gods, in bliss they dwell secure from harm,Those have no dignity who fear the housewife's slender arm
Kurals.emotions: **Fear** – The text highlights the fear men have regarding their wives

Kurals.Translation: Esteemed more is women bashful Than man servile unto her will
Kurals.Explanation: Even shame faced womanhood is more to be esteemed than the shameless manhood that performs
Kurals.Couplet: The dignity of modest womanhood excelsHis manliness, obedient to a woman's law who dwells
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Respect,

Kurals.Translation: By fair-browed wives who are governed Help no friends nor goodness tend
Kurals.Explanation: Those who yield to the wishes of their wives will neither relieve the wants of (their) friends nor perform virtuous deeds
Kurals.Couplet: Who to the will of her with beauteous brow their lives conform,Aid not their friends in need, nor acts of charity perform
Kurals.emotions: Frustration — The text expresses dissatisfaction with yielding to others' desires

Kurals.Translation: No virtue riches nor joy is seen In those who submit to women
Kurals.Explanation: From those who obey the commands of their wives are to be expected neither deeds of virtue, nor those of wealth nor (even) those of pleasure
Kurals.Couplet: No virtuous deed, no seemly wealth, no pleasure, restsWith them who live obedient to their wives' behests
Kurals.emotions: Disdain – The text expresses a lack of respect or contempt towards those who

Kurals.Translation: Thinkers strong and broad of heart By folly on fair sex do not dote
Kurals.Explanation: The foolishness that results from devotion to a wife will never be found in those who possess a reflecting mind and a prosperity (flowing) therefrom
Kurals.Couplet: Where pleasures of the mind, that dwell in realms of thought, abound,Folly, that springs from overweening woman's love, is never found
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom (or Thoughtfulness): Emphasized through the value placed on reflection and a discerning mind., Foolishness: Referenced as a negative trait connected to unwise devotion.

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Shame, Desire, Disdain.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: For gold, not love their tongue cajoles Men are ruined by bangled belles
Kurals.Explanation: The sweet words of elegant braceleted (prostitutes) who desire (a man) not from affection but from avarice, will cause sorrow
Kurals.Couplet: Those that choice armlets wear who seek not thee with love,But seek thy wealth, their pleasant words will ruin prove
Kurals.EmotionDetail: avarice, ,

Kurals.Translation: Avoid ill-natured whores who feign Love only for their selfish gain
Kurals.Explanation: One must ascertain the character of the ill-natured women who after ascertaining the wealth (of a man) speak (as if they were) good natured-ones, and avoid intercourse (with them)
Kurals.Couplet: Who weigh the gain, and utter virtuous words with vicious heart,Weighing such women's worth, from their society depart

Kurals.Translation: The false embrace of whores is like That of a damned corpse in the dark
Kurals.Explanation: The false embraces of wealth-loving women are like (hired men) embracing a strange corpse in a dark room
Kurals.Couplet: As one in darkened room, some stranger corpse inarms,Is he who seeks delight in mercenary women's charms

Kurals.Translation: The wise who seek the wealth of grace Look not for harlots' low embrace
Kurals.Explanation: The wise who seek the wealth of grace will not desire the base favours of those who regard wealth (and not pleasure) as (their) riches
Kurals.Couplet: Their worthless charms, whose only weal is wealth of gain,From touch of these the wise, who seek the wealth of grace, abstain
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions:, Wisdom, Disdain, Self-control

Kurals.Translation: The lofty wise will never covet The open charms of a vile harlot
Kurals.Explanation: Those whose knowledge is made excellent by their (natural) sense will not covet the trffling delights
Kurals.Couplet: From contact with their worthless charms, whose charms to all are free,The men with sense of good and lofty wisdom blest will flee;
Kurals.emotions: Disdain – The text conveys a sense of contempt or disapproval toward "vile harlots" and their "trifling delights.", Wisdom – There's an underlying respect

Kurals.Translation: Those who guard their worthy fame Shun the wanton's vaunting charm
Kurals.Explanation: Those who would spread (the fame of) their own goodness will not desire the shoulders of those,who rejoice in their accomplishments and bestow their despicable favours (on all who pay)
Kurals.Couplet: From touch of those who worthless charms, with wanton arts, display,The men who would their own true good maintain will turn away
Kurals.emotions: Disgust – toward "wanton's vaunting charm" and "despicable favours.", Pride – in guarding one’s own worthy fame and goodness., Contempt

Kurals.Translation: Hollow hearts alone desire The arms of whores with hearts elsewere
Kurals.Explanation: Those who are destitute of a perfectly (reformed) mind will covet the shoulders of those who embrace (them) while their hearts covet other things
Kurals.Couplet: Who cherish alien thoughts while folding in their feigned embrace,These none approach save those devoid of virtue's grace
Kurals.emotions: **Desire** – There is a clear sense of longing or wanting

Kurals.Translation: Senseless fools are lured away By arms of sirens who lead astray
Kurals.Explanation: The wise say that to such as are destitute of discerning sense the embraces of faithless women are (as ruinous as those of) the celestail female
Kurals.Couplet: As demoness who lures to ruin woman's treacherous loveTo men devoid of wisdom's searching power will prove
Kurals.emotions: Temptation — The text describes being lured or attracted

Kurals.Translation: The soft jewelled arms of whores are hell Into which the degraded fall
Kurals.Explanation: The delicate shoulders of prostitutes with excellent jewels are a hell into which are plunged the ignorant base
Kurals.Couplet: The wanton's tender arm, with gleaming jewels decked,Is hell, where sink degraded souls of men abject

Kurals.Translation: Double-minded whores, wine and dice Are lures of those whom fortune flies
Kurals.Explanation: Treacherous women, liquor, and gambling are the associates of such as have forsaken by Fortune
Kurals.Couplet: Women of double minds, strong drink, and dice; to these giv'n o'er,Are those on whom the light of Fortune shines no more

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Deception, Greed, Sorrow Avarice.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Avoid gambling, albeit you win Gulping bait-hook what does fish gain?
Kurals.Explanation: Though able to win, let not one desire gambling; (for) even what is won is like a fish swallowing the iron in fish-hook
Kurals.Couplet: Seek not the gamester's play; though you should win,Your gain is as the baited hook the fish takes in
Kurals.emotions: Caution – The text warns against the dangers of gambling

Kurals.Translation: Can gamblers in life good obtain Who lose a hundred one to gain?
Kurals.Explanation: That they may good obtain, and see a prosperous day? Is there indeed a means of livelihood that can bestow happiness on gamblers who gain one and lose a hundred?
Kurals.Couplet: Is there for gamblers, too, that gaining one a hundred lose, some wayThat they may good obtain, and see a prosperous day
Kurals.emotions: Uncertainty – reflecting the unpredictability and risk inherent in gambling., Hope – the desire or longing for prosperity and happiness despite the odds., Doubt – questioning whether true happiness

Kurals.Translation: If kings indulge in casting dice All their fortune will flow to foes
Kurals.Explanation: If the king is incessantly addicted to the rolling dice in the hope of gain, his wealth and the resources thereof will take their departure and fall into other's hands
Kurals.Couplet: If prince unceasing speak of nought but play,Treasure and revenue will pass from him away
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 emotions associated with the text:, Warning (Caution) – The text carries a strong sense of advising against gambling

Kurals.Translation: Nothing will make you poor like game Which adds to woes and ruins fame
Kurals.Explanation: There is nothing else that brings (us) poverty like gambling which causes many a misery and destroys (one's) reputation
Kurals.Couplet: Gaming brings many woes, and ruins fair renown;Nothing to want brings men so surely down

Kurals.Translation: The game, game-hall and gambler's art Who sought with glee have come to nought
Kurals.Explanation: Penniless are those who by reason of their attachment would never forsake gambling, the gamblingplace and the handling (of dice)
Kurals.Couplet: The dice, and gaming-hall, and gamester's art, they eager sought,Thirsting for gain- the men in other days who came to nought

Kurals.Translation: Men swallowed by the ogress, dice Suffer grief and want by that vice
Kurals.Explanation: Those who are swallowed by the goddess called "gambling" will never have their hunger satisfied, but suffer the pangs of hell in the next world
Kurals.Couplet: Gambling's Misfortune's other name: o'er whom she casts her veil,They suffer grievous want, and sorrows sore bewail

Kurals.Translation: If men their time in game-den spend Ancestral wealth and virtues end
Kurals.Explanation: To waste time at the place of gambling will destroy inherited wealth and goodness of character
Kurals.Couplet: Ancestral wealth and noble fame to ruin haste,If men in gambler's halls their precious moments waste
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Caution,

Kurals.Translation: Game ruins wealth and spoils grace Leads to lies and wretched woes
Kurals.Explanation: Gambling destroys property, teaches falsehood, puts an end to benevolence, and brings in misery (here and hereafter)
Kurals.Couplet: Gambling wastes wealth, to falsehood bends the soul: it drives awayAll grace, and leaves the man to utter misery a prey
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , or Dishonesty

Kurals.Translation: Dress, wealth, food, fame, learning-these five In gambler's hand will never thrive
Kurals.Explanation: The habit of gambling prevents the attainment of these five: clothing, wealth, food, fame and learning
Kurals.Couplet: Clothes, wealth, food, praise, and learning, all departFrom him on gambler's gain who sets his heart

Kurals.Translation: Love for game grows with every loss As love for life with sorrows grows
Kurals.Explanation: As the gambler loves (his vice) the more he loses by it, so does the soul love (the body) the more it suffers through it
Kurals.Couplet: Howe'er he lose, the gambler's heart is ever in the play;E'en so the soul, despite its griefs, would live on earth alway

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Regret, Misery, Caution.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Wind, bile and phlegm three cause disease So doctors deem it more or less
Kurals.Explanation: If (food and work are either) excessive or deficient, the three things enumerated by (medical) writers, flatulence, biliousness, and phlegm, will cause (one) disease
Kurals.Couplet: The learned books count three, with wind as first; of these,As any one prevail, or fail; 'twill cause disease
Kurals.emotions: Concern – The text discusses causes of disease

Kurals.Translation: After digestion one who feeds His body no medicine needs
Kurals.Explanation: No medicine is necessary for him who eats after assuring (himself) that what he has (already) eaten has been digested
Kurals.Couplet: No need of medicine to heal your body's pain,If, what you ate before digested well, you eat again
Kurals.EmotionDetail: careful judgment and wisdom about health, satisfaction with well-being and routine,

Kurals.Translation: Eat food to digestive measure Life in body lasts with pleasure
Kurals.Explanation: If (one's food has been) digested let one eat with moderation; (for) that is the way to prolong the life of an embodied soul
Kurals.Couplet: Who has a body gained may long the gift retain,If, food digested well, in measure due he eat again
Kurals.emotions: Contentment – Emphasizing the pleasure and satisfaction that comes from proper

Kurals.Translation: Know digestion; with keen appetite Eat what is suitable and right
Kurals.Explanation: (First) assure yourself that your food has been digested and never fail to eat, when very hungry, whatever is not disagreeable (to you)
Kurals.Couplet: Knowing the food digested well, when hunger prompteth thee,With constant care, the viands choose that well agree
Kurals.emotions: Caution – Emphasized by the advice to "assure yourself that your food has been digested" and to be careful about what and when to eat., Responsibility – Suggested

Kurals.Translation: With fasting adjusted food right Cures ills of life and makes you bright
Kurals.Explanation: There will be no disaster to one's life if one eats with moderation, food that is not disagreeable
Kurals.Couplet: With self-denial take the well-selected meal;So shall thy frame no sudden sickness feel
Kurals.emotions: Caution – advocating for moderation and mindful choices in eating habits., Hope – suggesting that proper habits can prevent disasters and improve life., Contentment – implying satisfaction

Kurals.Translation: Who eats with clean stomach gets health With greedy glutton abides ill-health
Kurals.Explanation: As pleasure dwells with him who eats moderately, so disease (dwells) with the glutton who eats voraciously
Kurals.Couplet: On modest temperance as pleasures pure,So pain attends the greedy epicure

Kurals.Translation: who glut beyond the hunger's fire Suffer from untold diseases here
Kurals.Explanation: He will be afflicted with numberless diseases, who eats immoderately, ignorant (of the rules of health)
Kurals.Couplet: Who largely feeds, nor measure of the fire within maintains,That thoughtless man shall feel unmeasured pains
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Suffering, Ignorance, Regret

Kurals.Translation: Test disease, its cause and cure And apply remedy that is sure
Kurals.Explanation: Let the physician enquire into the (nature of the) disease, its cause and its method of cure and treat it faithfully according to (medical rule)
Kurals.Couplet: Disease, its cause, what may abate the ill:Let leech examine these, then use his skill
Kurals.emotions: Responsibility (or Duty/Care) – The text conveys the importance of a physician taking their duty seriously in investigating and treating diseases., Trust – There is an implicit trust in the competence

Kurals.Translation: Let the skilful doctor note The sickmen, sickness, season and treat
Kurals.Explanation: The learned (physician) should ascertain the condition of his patient; the nature of his disease, and the season (of the year) and (then) proceed (with his treatment)
Kurals.Couplet: The habitudes of patient and disease, the crises of the illThese must the learned leech think over well, then use his skill
Kurals.emotions: Responsibility – The text emphasizes the duty of the physician to carefully assess and treat patients., Concern – There is an underlying sense of care for the well-being of the sick patient. 3

Kurals.Translation: Patient, doctor, medicine and nurse Are four-fold codes of treating course
Kurals.Explanation: Medical science consists of four parts, viz, patient, physician, medicine and compounder; and each of these (again) contains four sub-divisions
Kurals.Couplet: For patient, leech, and remedies, and him who waits by patient's side,The art of medicine must fourfold code of laws provide
Kurals.emotions: Trust – The text discusses the roles of patient

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Contentment, Wisdom About Health, Routine.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Right-sense and bashfulness adorn By nature only the noble-born
Kurals.Explanation: Consistency (of thought, word and deed) and fear (of sin) are conjointly natural only to the high-born
Kurals.Couplet: Save in the scions of a noble house, you never findInstinctive sense of right and virtuous shame combined
Kurals.EmotionDetail: in noble qualities, for virtue and moral conduct, towards those possessing civic virtue and high birth

Kurals.Translation: The noble-born lack not these three: Good conduct, truth and modesty
Kurals.Explanation: The high-born will never deviate from these three; good manners, truthfulness and modesty
Kurals.Couplet: In these three things the men of noble birth fail not:In virtuous deed and truthful word, and chastened thought
Kurals.EmotionDetail: for good conduct and virtue, from the emphasis on truthfulness, reflecting modesty

Kurals.Translation: Smile, gift, sweet words and courtesy These four mark true nobility
Kurals.Explanation: A cheerful countenance, liberality, pleasant words, and an unreviling disposition, these four are said to be the proper qualities of the truly high-born
Kurals.Couplet: The smile, the gift, the pleasant word, unfailing courtesyThese are the signs, they say, of true nobility

Kurals.Translation: Even for crores, the noble mood Cannot bend to degrading deed
Kurals.Explanation: Though blessed with immense wealth, the noble will never do anything unbecoming
Kurals.Couplet: Millions on millions piled would never winThe men of noble race to soul-degrading sin

Kurals.Translation: The means of gift may dwindle; yet Ancient homes guard their noble trait
Kurals.Explanation: Though their means fall off, those born in ancient families, will not lose their character (for liberality)
Kurals.Couplet: Though stores for charity should fail within, the ancient raceWill never lose its old ancestral grace
Kurals.EmotionDetail: in noble heritage and family virtue, liberality as a lasting character trait, maintaining virtues despite dwindling means

Kurals.Translation: Who guard their family prestige pure Stoop not to acts of cunning lure
Kurals.Explanation: Those who seek to preserve the irreproachable honour of their families will not viciously do what is detrimental thereto
Kurals.Couplet: Whose minds are set to live as fits their sire's unspotted fame,Stooping to low deceit, commit no deeds that gender shame
Kurals.EmotionDetail: in family honor, , or vigilance

Kurals.Translation: The faults of nobly-born are seen Like on the sky the spots of moon
Kurals.Explanation: As spots on her bright orb that walks sublime the evening sky
Kurals.Couplet: The faults of men of noble race are seen by every eye,As spots on her bright orb that walks sublime the evening sky
Kurals.emotions: Disappointment, Shame, Admiration Reasoning: The text discusses how even small faults in noble individuals are easily noticed

Kurals.Translation: If manners of the good are rude People deem their pedigree crude
Kurals.Explanation: If one of a good family betrays want of affection, his descent from it will be called in question
Kurals.Couplet: If lack of love appear in those who bear some goodly name,'Twill make men doubt the ancestry they claim
Kurals.emotions: Disappointment – in seeing good lineage betrayed by poor manners or lack of affection., Shame – the embarrassment or dishonor felt when one’s conduct causes people to question their pedigree or family

Kurals.Translation: Soil's nature is seen in sprout The worth of birth from words flow out
Kurals.Explanation: As the sprout indicates the nature of the soil, (so) the speech of the noble indicates (that of one's birth)
Kurals.Couplet: Of soil the plants that spring thereout will show the worth:The words they speak declare the men of noble birth
Kurals.emotions: Wisdom – The text conveys an appreciation for discernment and understanding

Kurals.Translation: All gain good name by modesty Nobility by humility
Kurals.Explanation: He who desires a good name must desire modesty; and he who desires (the continuance of) a family greatness must be submissive to all
Kurals.Couplet: Who seek for good the grace of virtuous shame must know;Who seek for noble name to all must reverence show
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , for a good name or greatness through virtuous behavior

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Pride, Integrity, Reverence.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Though needed for your life in main, From mean degrading acts refrain
Kurals.Explanation: Actions that would degrade (one's) family should not be done; though they may be so important that not doing them would end in death
Kurals.Couplet: Though linked to splendours man no otherwise may gain,Reject each act that may thine honour's clearness stain
Kurals.emotions: Shame – the avoidance of actions that would bring disgrace or dishonour., Honor/Pride – the value placed on maintaining family dignity and reputation., Fear – concern about

Kurals.Translation: Who seek honour and manly fame Don't do mean deeds even for name
Kurals.Explanation: Those who desire (to maintain their) honour, will surely do nothing dishonourable, even for the sake of fame
Kurals.Couplet: Who seek with glory to combine honour's untarnished fame,Do no inglorious deeds, though men accord them glory's name

Kurals.Translation: Be humble in prosperity In decline uphold dignity
Kurals.Explanation: In great prosperity humility is becoming; dignity, in great adversity
Kurals.Couplet: Bow down thy soul, with increase blest, in happy hour;Lift up thy heart, when stript of all by fortune's power

Kurals.Translation: Like hair fallen from head are those Who fall down from their high status
Kurals.Explanation: They who have fallen from their (high) position are like the hair which has fallen from the head
Kurals.Couplet: Like hairs from off the head that fall to earth,When fall'n from high estate are men of noble birth
Kurals.emotions: Shame – The comparison of fallen status to fallen hair suggests a sense of disgrace or loss of dignity., Regret – There is an implied feeling of wishing the fall from high

Kurals.Translation: Even hill-like men will sink to nought With abrus-grain-like small default
Kurals.Explanation: Even those who are exalted like a hill will be thought low, if they commit deeds that are debasing
Kurals.Couplet: If meanness, slight as 'abrus' grain, by men be wrought,Though like a hill their high estate, they sink to nought
Kurals.emotions: Shame – The text discusses the negative impact of dishonourable deeds

Kurals.Translation: Why fawn on men that scorn you here It yields no fame, heaven's bliss neither
Kurals.Explanation: Why follow men who scorn, and at their bidding wait?
Kurals.Couplet: It yields no praise, nor to the land of Gods throws wide the gate:Why follow men who scorn, and at their bidding wait

Kurals.Translation: Better it is to die forlorn Than live as slaves of those who scorn
Kurals.Explanation: It is better for a man to be said of him that he died in his usual state than that he eked out his life by following those who disgraced him
Kurals.Couplet: Better 'twere said, 'He's perished!' than to gainThe means to live, following in foeman's train
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with the provided text are:, **Pride** – Emphasizing the importance of maintaining one’s honour and dignity., **Shame** – Highlighting the negative feeling associated with living under the

Kurals.Translation: Is nursing body nectar sweet Even when one's honour is lost?
Kurals.Explanation: For the high-born to keep their body in life when their honour is gone will certainly not prove a remedy against death
Kurals.Couplet: When high estate has lost its pride of honour meet,Is life, that nurses this poor flesh, as nectar sweet

Kurals.Translation: Honour lost, the noble expire Like a yak that loses its hair
Kurals.Explanation: Those who give up (their) life when (their) honour is at stake are like the yark which kills itself at the loss of (even one of) its hairs
Kurals.Couplet: Like the wild ox that, of its tuft bereft, will pine away,Are those who, of their honour shorn, will quit the light of day
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with the provided text are:, Shame – The text discusses the loss of honour

Kurals.Translation: Their light the world adores and hails Who will not live when honour fails
Kurals.Explanation: The world will (always) praise and adore the fame of the honourable who would rather die than suffer indignity
Kurals.Couplet: Who, when dishonour comes, refuse to live, their honoured memoryWill live in worship and applause of all the world for aye
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , to uphold values even at great personal cost

Encourages cultivating emotions such as Pride, Respect, Dignity.

Kurals.Translation: A heart of courage lives in light Devoid of that one's life is night
Kurals.Explanation: One's light is the abundance of one's courage; one's darkness is the desire to live destitute of such (a state of mind.)
Kurals.Couplet: The light of life is mental energy; disgrace is hisWho says, 'I 'ill lead a happy life devoid of this.'

Kurals.Translation: All beings are the same in birth But work decides their varied worth
Kurals.Explanation: All human beings agree as regards their birth but differ as regards their characteristics, because of the different qualities of their actions
Kurals.Couplet: All men that live are one in circumstances of birth;Diversities of works give each his special worth
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Equality – The recognition of the sameness of all beings at birth., Determination – The idea that actions and effort shape one's worth., Acceptance – Understanding and embracing the

Kurals.Translation: Ignoble high not high they are The noble low not low they fare
Kurals.Explanation: Though (raised) above, the base cannot become great; though (brought) low, the great cannot become base
Kurals.Couplet: The men of lofty line, whose souls are mean, are never greatThe men of lowly birth, when high of soul, are not of low estate
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Humility – The passage emphasizes remaining grounded and not being prideful or arrogant., Respect – There is recognition of the value in both high and low positions

Kurals.Translation: Greatness like woman's chastity Is guarded by self-varacity
Kurals.Explanation: Exists while to itself is true
Kurals.Couplet: Like single-hearted women, greatness too,Exists while to itself is true

Kurals.Translation: Great souls when their will is active Do mighty deeds rare to achieve
Kurals.Explanation: (Though reduced) the great will be able to perform, in the proper way, deeds difficult (for others to do)
Kurals.Couplet: The man endowed with greatness true,Rare deeds in perfect wise will do

Kurals.Translation: The petty-natured ones have not The mind to seek and befriend the great
Kurals.Explanation: It is never in the nature of the base to seek the society of the great and partake of their nature
Kurals.Couplet: 'As votaries of the truly great we will ourselves enroll,'Is thought that enters not the mind of men of little soul

Kurals.Translation: The base with power and opulence Wax with deeds of insolence
Kurals.Explanation: Even nobility of birth, wealth and learning, if in (the possession of) the base, will (only) produce everincreasing pride
Kurals.Couplet: Whene'er distinction lights on some unworthy head,Then deeds of haughty insolence are bred

Kurals.Translation: Greatness bends with modesty Meanness vaunts with vanity
Kurals.Explanation: The great will always humble himself; but the mean will exalt himself in self-admiration
Kurals.Couplet: Greatness humbly bends, but littleness alwaysSpreads out its plumes, and loads itself with praise

Kurals.Translation: Greatness is free from insolence Littleness swells with that offence
Kurals.Explanation: Freedom from conceit is (the nature of true) greatness; (while) obstinacy therein is (that of) meanness
Kurals.Couplet: Greatness is absence of conceit; meanness, we deem,Riding on car of vanity supreme
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , or Conceit,

Kurals.Translation: Weakness of others greatness screens Smallness defects alone proclaims
Kurals.Explanation: The great hide the faults of others; the base only divulge them
Kurals.Couplet: Greatness will hide a neighbour's shame;Meanness his faults to all the world proclaim
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – The great show understanding and kindness by hiding the faults of others., Humility – Acknowledgment that everyone has defects

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Pride, Respect, Admiration.

Kurals.Translation: All goodness is duty to them Who are dutiful and sublime
Kurals.Explanation: It is said that those who are conscious of their duty and behave with a perfect goodness will regard as natural all that is good
Kurals.Couplet: All goodly things are duties to the men, they sayWho set themselves to walk in virtue's perfect way
Kurals.EmotionDetail: a sense of responsibility, moral virtue/kindness, deep respect for high moral standards

Kurals.Translation: Good in the great is character Than that there is nothing better
Kurals.Explanation: The only delight of the perfect is that of their goodness; all other (sensual) delights are not to be included among any (true) delights
Kurals.Couplet: The good of inward excellence they claim,The perfect men; all other good is only good in name
Kurals.emotions: Contentment — deriving satisfaction from goodness and virtue., Pride — taking pleasure in one’s own moral character., Serenity — a peaceful state resulting from focusing on true

Kurals.Translation: Love, truth, regard, modesty, grace These five are virtue's resting place
Kurals.Explanation: Affection, fear (of sin), benevolence, favour and truthfulness; these are the five pillars on which perfect goodness rests
Kurals.Couplet: Love, modesty, beneficence, benignant grace,With truth, are pillars five of perfect virtue's resting-place

Kurals.Translation: Not to kill is penance pure Not to slander virtue sure
Kurals.Explanation: Penance consists in the goodness that kills not , and perfection in the goodness that tells not others' faults
Kurals.Couplet: The type of 'penitence' is virtuous good that nothing slays;To speak no ill of other men is perfect virtue's praise
Kurals.emotions: The top 3 human emotions associated with the provided text are:, Compassion, Restraint, Virtue

Kurals.Translation: Humility is valour's strength A force that averts foes at length
Kurals.Explanation: Stooping (to inferiors) is the strength of those who can accomplish (an undertaking); and that is the weapon with which the great avert their foes
Kurals.Couplet: Submission is the might of men of mighty acts; the sageWith that same weapon stills his foeman's rage
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Inner Strength / Confidence,

Kurals.Translation: To bear repulse e'en from the mean Is the touch-stone of worthy men
Kurals.Explanation: The touch-stone of perfection is to receive a defeat even at the hands of one's inferiors
Kurals.Couplet: What is perfection's test? The equal mindTo bear repulse from even meaner men resigned

Kurals.Translation: Of perfection what is the gain If it returns not joy for pain?
Kurals.Explanation: Of what avail is perfect goodness if it cannot do pleasing things even to those who have pained (it) ?
Kurals.Couplet: What fruit doth your perfection yield you, say!Unless to men who work you ill good repay

Kurals.Translation: No shame there is in poverty To one strong in good quality
Kurals.Explanation: Poverty is no disgrace to one who abounds in good qualities
Kurals.Couplet: To soul with perfect virtue's strength endued,Brings no disgrace the lack of every earthly good
Kurals.emotions: Dignity – The text emphasizes self-respect and the inherent worth of a person

Kurals.Translation: Aeons may change but not the seer Who is a sea of virtue pure
Kurals.Explanation: Those who are said to be the shore of the sea of perfection will never change, though ages may change
Kurals.Couplet: Call them of perfect virtue's sea the shore,Who, though the fates should fail, fail not for evermore
Kurals.emotions: Reverence – The text expresses deep respect and admiration for the enduring qualities of the "seer.", Awe – There is wonder at the timeless

Kurals.Translation: The world will not more bear its weight If from high virtue fall the great
Kurals.Explanation: If there is a defect in the character of the perfect, (even) the great world cannot bear (its) burden
Kurals.Couplet: The mighty earth its burthen to sustain must cease,If perfect virtue of the perfect men decrease
Kurals.emotions: Disappointment – The idea that great people losing virtue leads to hardship implies a sense of letdown if virtue fails., Responsibility – The expectation that virtuous people hold up the world’s

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Compassion, Humility, Duty.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: To the polite free of access Easily comes courteousness
Kurals.Explanation: If one is easy of access to all, it will be easy for one to obtain the virtue called goodness
Kurals.Couplet: Who easy access give to every man, they say,Of kindly courtesy will learn with ease the way

Kurals.Translation: Humanity and noble birth Develop courtesy and moral worth
Kurals.Explanation: Affectionateness and birth in a good family, these two constitute what is called a proper behaviour to all
Kurals.Couplet: Benevolence and high born dignity,These two are beaten paths of courtesy

Kurals.Translation: Likeness in limbs is not likeness It's likeness in kind courteousness
Kurals.Explanation: Resemblance of bodies is no resemblance of souls; true resemblance is the resemblance of qualities that attract
Kurals.Couplet: Men are not one because their members seem alike to outward view;Similitude of kindred quality makes likeness true
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , a sense of connection or likeness

Kurals.Translation: The world applauds those helpful men Whose actions are just and benign
Kurals.Explanation: The world applauds the character of those whose usefulness results from their equity and charity
Kurals.Couplet: Of men of fruitful life, who kindly benefits dispense,The world unites to praise the 'noble excellence.'

Kurals.Translation: The courteous don't even foes detest For contempt offends even in jest
Kurals.Explanation: Reproach is painful to one even in sport; those (therefore) who know the nature of others exhibit (pleasing) qualities even when they are hated
Kurals.Couplet: Contempt is evil though in sport They who man's nature know,E'en in their wrath, a courteous mind will show
Kurals.emotions: Respect – The emphasis on treating others with courtesy

Kurals.Translation: The world rests with the mannered best Or it crumbles and falls to dust
Kurals.Explanation: The (way of the) world subsists by contact with the good; if not, it would bury itself in the earth and perish
Kurals.Couplet: The world abides; for 'worthy' men its weight sustainWere it not so, 'twould fall to dust again

Kurals.Translation: The mannerless though sharp like file Are like wooden blocks indocile
Kurals.Explanation: He who is destitute of (true) human qualities (only) resembles a tree, though he may possess the sharpness of a file
Kurals.Couplet: Though sharp their wit as file, as blocks they must remain,Whose souls are void of 'courtesy humane'
Kurals.EmotionDetail: toward lack of courtesy or human qualities, for those who are outwardly sharp but inwardly lacking,

Kurals.Translation: Discourtesy is mean indeed E'en to a base unfriendly breed
Kurals.Explanation: It is wrong (for the wise) not to exhibit (good) qualities even towards those who bearing no friendship (for them) do only what is hateful
Kurals.Couplet: Though men with all unfriendly acts and wrongs assail,'Tis uttermost disgrace in 'courtesy' to fail
Kurals.emotions: Disapproval – There is a clear sense of judging discourtesy or mean behavior as wrong., Moral Duty – The text evokes a sense of responsibility to act rightly

Kurals.Translation: To those bereft of smiling light Even in day the earth is night
Kurals.Explanation: To those who cannot rejoice, the wide world is buried darkness even in (broad) day light
Kurals.Couplet: To him who knows not how to smile in kindly mirth,Darkness in daytime broods o'er all the vast and mighty earth

Kurals.Translation: The wealth heaped by the churlish base Is pure milk soured by impure vase
Kurals.Explanation: The great wealth obtained by one who has no goodness will perish like pure milk spoilt by the
Kurals.Couplet: Like sweet milk soured because in filthy vessel poured,Is ample wealth in churlish man's unopened coffers stored

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Respect, Kindness, Disapproval.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Dead is he with wealth in pile Unenjoyed, it is futile
Kurals.Explanation: He who does not enjoy the immense riches he has heaped up in his house, is (to be reckoned as) dead, (for) there is nothing achieved (by him)
Kurals.Couplet: Who fills his house with ample store, enjoying none,Is dead Nought with the useless heap is done
Kurals.emotions: Emptiness – The text reflects a sense of emptiness in merely amassing wealth without enjoyment or purpose., Regret – There is an undertone of regret for not

Kurals.Translation: The niggard miser thinks wealth is all He hoards, gives not is born devil
Kurals.Explanation: He who knows that wealth yields every pleasure and yet is so blind as to lead miserly life will be born a demon
Kurals.Couplet: Who giving nought, opines from wealth all blessing springs,Degraded birth that doting miser's folly brings
Kurals.emotions: Greed – The miser's obsessive hoarding of wealth reflects intense greed., Blindness/Ignorance – The text notes the miser is "blind" to the true use of

Kurals.Translation: A burden he is to earth indeed Who hoards without a worthy deed
Kurals.Explanation: A burden to the earth are men bent on the acquisition of riches and not (true) fame
Kurals.Couplet: Who lust to heap up wealth, but glory hold not dear,It burthens earth when on the stage of being they appear
Kurals.emotions: Disapproval – The text conveys a critical attitude towards those who hoard wealth without performing worthy deeds., Contempt – There is a sense of looking down upon or scorn for people

Kurals.Translation: What legacy can he leave behind Who is for approach too unkind
Kurals.Explanation: What will the miser who is not liked (by any one) regard as his own (in the world to come) ?
Kurals.Couplet: Whom no one loves, when he shall pass away,What doth he look to leave behind, I pray
Kurals.emotions: Contempt – towards the miser who is unkind and uncaring., Regret – about the lack of meaningful legacy due to selfishness., Isolation – reflecting on the miser

Kurals.Translation: What is the good of crores they hoard To give and enjoy whose heart is hard
Kurals.Explanation: Those who neither give (to others) nor enjoy (their property) are (truly) destitute, though possessing immense riches
Kurals.Couplet: Amid accumulated millions they are poor,Who nothing give and nought enjoy of all they store

Kurals.Translation: Great wealth unused for oneself nor To worthy men is but a slur
Kurals.Explanation: He who enjoys not (his riches) nor relieves the wants of the worthy is a disease to his wealth
Kurals.Couplet: Their ample wealth is misery to men of churlish heart,Who nought themselves enjoy, and nought to worthy men impart
Kurals.EmotionDetail: selfish withholding of wealth, condemnation of failing to help the worthy,

Kurals.Translation: Who loaths to help have-nots, his gold Is like a spinster-belle grown old
Kurals.Explanation: The wealth of him who never bestows anything on the destitute is like a woman of beauty growing old without a husband
Kurals.Couplet: Like woman fair in lonelihood who aged grows,Is wealth of him on needy men who nought bestows
Kurals.emotions: **Selfishness** – The text describes a person who withholds charity

Kurals.Translation: The idle wealth of unsought men Is poison-fruit-tree amidst a town
Kurals.Explanation: The wealth of him who is disliked (by all) is like the fruit-bearing of the etty tree in the midst of a town
Kurals.Couplet: When he whom no man loves exults in great prosperity,'Tis as when fruits in midmost of the town some poisonous tree
Kurals.emotions: Disdain – The text conveys that wealth belonging to a person who is generally disliked brings about a sense of contempt or disregard from others., Isolation – The reference to a person being "

Kurals.Translation: Others usurp the shining gold In loveless, stingy, vicious hold
Kurals.Explanation: To heap up glittering wealth, their hoards shall others take
Kurals.Couplet: Who love abandon, self-afflict, and virtue's way forsakeTo heap up glittering wealth, their hoards shall others take
Kurals.emotions: Greed – The desire to amass and hoard wealth., Lovelessness/Indifference – Lack of compassion or charity towards others., Loss – The inevitability of

Kurals.Translation: The brief want of the rich benign Is like rainclouds growing thin
Kurals.Explanation: The short-lived poverty of those who are noble and rich is like the clouds becoming poor (for a while)
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis as when rain cloud in the heaven grows day,When generous wealthy man endures brief poverty

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Blindness, Uncaring., Refusal To Share..
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: To shrink from evil deed is shame The rest is blush of fair-faced dame
Kurals.Explanation: True modesty is the fear of (evil) deeds; all other modesty is (simply) the bashfulness of virtuous maids
Kurals.Couplet: To shrink abashed from evil deed is 'generous shame';Other is that of bright-browed one of virtuous fame

Kurals.Translation: Food, dress and such are one for all Modesty marks the higher soul
Kurals.Explanation: Food, clothing and the like are common to all men but modesty is peculiar to the good
Kurals.Couplet: Food, clothing, and other things alike all beings own;By sense of shame the excellence of men is known

Kurals.Translation: All lives have their lodge in flesh Perfection has its home in blush
Kurals.Explanation: As the body is the abode of the spirit, so the excellence of modesty is the abode of perfection
Kurals.Couplet: All spirits homes of flesh as habitation claim,And perfect virtue ever dwells with shame

Kurals.Translation: Shame is the jewel of dignity Shameless swagger is vanity
Kurals.Explanation: Is not the modesty ornament of the noble ? Without it, their haughtiness would be a pain (to others)
Kurals.Couplet: And is not shame an ornament to men of dignity?Without it step of stately pride is piteous thing to see

Kurals.Translation: In them resides the sense of shame Who blush for their and other's blame
Kurals.Explanation: The world regards as the abode of modesty him who fear his own and other's guilt
Kurals.Couplet: As home of virtuous shame by all the world the men are known,Who feel ashamed for others, guilt as for their own

Kurals.Translation: The great refuse the wonder-world Without modesty's hedge and shield
Kurals.Explanation: The great make modesty their barrier (of defence) and not the wide world
Kurals.Couplet: Unless the hedge of shame inviolate remain,For men of lofty soul the earth's vast realms no charms retain

Kurals.Translation: For shame their life the shame-sensed give Loss of shame they won't outlive
Kurals.Explanation: The modest would rather lose their life for the sake of modesty than lose modesty for the sake of life
Kurals.Couplet: The men of modest soul for shame would life an offering make,But ne'er abandon virtuous shame for life's dear sake
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , connected to self-respect and honor

Kurals.Translation: Virtue is much ashamed of him Who shameless does what others shame
Kurals.Explanation: Virtue is likely to forsake him who shamelessly does what others are ashamed of
Kurals.Couplet: Though know'st no shame, while all around asha med must be:Virtue will shrink away ashamed of thee

Kurals.Translation: Lapse in manners injures the race Want of shame harms every good grace
Kurals.Explanation: Want of manners injures one's family; but want of modesty injures one's character
Kurals.Couplet: 'Twill race consume if right observance fail;'Twill every good consume if shamelessness prevail
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , for reputation and family, for modesty and good character

Kurals.Translation: Movements of the shameless in heart Are string-led puppet show in fact
Kurals.Explanation: The actions of those who are without modesty at heart are like those of puppet moved by a string
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis as with strings a wooden puppet apes life's functions, whenThose void of shame within hold intercourse with men

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Shame, Modesty, Humility.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: No greatness is grander like Saying \"I shall work without slack\"
Kurals.Explanation: There is no higher greatness than that of one saying I will not cease in my effort (to raise my family)
Kurals.Couplet: Who says 'I'll do my work, nor slack my hand',His greatness, clothed with dignity supreme, shall stand

Kurals.Translation: These two exalt a noble home Ardent effort and ripe wisdom
Kurals.Explanation: One's family is raised by untiring perseverance in both effort and wise contrivances
Kurals.Couplet: The manly act and knowledge full, when these combineIn deed prolonged, then lengthens out the race's line
Kurals.emotions: Determination, Wisdom (reflected in "ripe wisdom" and "wise contrivances") 3
Kurals.EmotionDetail: due to "untiring perseverance" and "ardent effort",

Kurals.Translation: When one resolves to raise his race Loin girt up God leads his ways
Kurals.Explanation: The Deity will clothe itself and appear before him who resolves on raising his family
Kurals.Couplet: 'I'll make my race renowned,' if man shall say,With vest succinct the goddess leads the way
Kurals.EmotionDetail: the resolve to raise one’s race/family, belief that God will guide and support, respect and faith in the divine

Kurals.Translation: Who raise their races with ceaseless pain No need for plan; their ends will gain
Kurals.Explanation: Those who are prompt in their efforts (to better their family) need no deliberation, such efforts will of themselves succeed
Kurals.Couplet: Who labours for his race with unremitting pain,Without a thought spontaneously, his end will gain
Kurals.emotions: Determination – The text highlights persistent effort and prompt actions towards improving the family

Kurals.Translation: Who keeps his house without a blame People around, his kinship claim
Kurals.Explanation: People will eagerly seek the friendship of the prosperous soul who has raised his family without foul means
Kurals.Couplet: With blameless life who seeks to build his race's fame,The world shall circle him, and kindred claim
Kurals.EmotionDetail: in maintaining a blameless household and conduct, from others and kin for good conduct,

Kurals.Translation: Who raise their race which gave them birth Are deemed as men of manly worth
Kurals.Explanation: A man's true manliness consists in making himself the head and benefactor of his family
Kurals.Couplet: Of virtuous manliness the world accords the praiseTo him who gives his powers, the house from which he sprang to raise

Kurals.Translation: Like dauntless heroes in battle field The home-burden rests on the bold
Kurals.Explanation: Amid his kindred so the burthen rests upon the strong
Kurals.Couplet: The fearless hero bears the brunt amid the warrior throng;Amid his kindred so the burthen rests upon the strong

Kurals.Translation: No season have they who raise their race Sloth and pride will honour efface
Kurals.Explanation: As a family suffers by (one's) indolence and false dignity there is to be so season (good or bad) to those who strive to raise their family
Kurals.Couplet: Wait for no season, when you would your house uprear;'Twill perish, if you wait supine, or hold your honour dear
Kurals.emotions: Responsibility – Emphasizing the duty to uplift and care for one’s family, Pride – Referencing both positive family honor and the negative aspect of false dignity, Shame –

Kurals.Translation: Is not his frame a vase for woes Who from mishaps shields his house?
Kurals.Explanation: Is it only to suffering that his body is exposed who undertakes to preserve his family from evil ?
Kurals.Couplet: Is not his body vase that various sorrows fill,Who would his household screen from every ill
Kurals.emotions: Responsibility – The sense of duty felt by someone who protects their family., Sacrifice – The willingness to endure pain or hardship for the sake of loved ones., Suffering –

Kurals.Translation: A house will fall by a mishap With no good man to prop it up
Kurals.Explanation: If there are none to prop up and maintain a family (in distress), it will fall at the stroke of the axe of misfortune
Kurals.Couplet: When trouble the foundation saps the house must fall,If no strong hand be nigh to prop the tottering wall
Kurals.emotions: **Anxiety** – There is a sense of worry or concern about the wellbeing and stability of the family in the face of adversity., **Responsibility** – The text evokes a

Summary: Highlights the sanctity of family life as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Determination, Responsibility, Pride.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Farming though hard is foremost trade Men ply at will but ploughmen lead
Kurals.Explanation: Agriculture, though laborious, is the most excellent (form of labour); for people, though they go about (in search of various employments), have at last to resort to the farmer
Kurals.Couplet: Howe'er they roam, the world must follow still the plougher's team;Though toilsome, culture of the ground as noblest toil esteem
Kurals.emotions: Respect – The passage expresses high regard for the work of farmers

Kurals.Translation: Tillers are linch-pin of mankind Bearing the rest who cannot tend
Kurals.Explanation: Agriculturists are (as it were) the linch-pin of the world for they support all other workers who cannot till the soil
Kurals.Couplet: The ploughers are the linch-pin of the world; they bearThem up who other works perform, too weak its toils to share
Kurals.emotions: **Respect** – The text expresses deep admiration for agriculturists

Kurals.Translation: They live who live to plough and eat The rest behind them bow and eat
Kurals.Explanation: They alone live who live by agriculture; all others lead a cringing, dependent life
Kurals.Couplet: Who ploughing eat their food, they truly live:The rest to others bend subservient, eating what they give
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Pride – The text expresses a sense of pride and honor in living by agriculture., Superiority – There is a feeling of being superior to others who are dependent and “cringing

Kurals.Translation: Who have the shade of cornful crest Under their umbra umbrellas rest
Kurals.Explanation: Patriotic farmers desire to bring all other states under the control of their own king
Kurals.Couplet: O'er many a land they 'll see their monarch reign,Whose fields are shaded by the waving grain
Kurals.emotions: Pride – Farmers are portrayed with admiration and dignity

Kurals.Translation: Who till and eat, beg not; nought hide But give to those who are in need
Kurals.Explanation: Those whose nature is to live by manual labour will never beg but give something to those who beg
Kurals.Couplet: They nothing ask from others, but to askers give,Who raise with their own hands the food on which they live

Kurals.Translation: Should ploughmen sit folding their hands Desire-free monks too suffer wants
Kurals.Explanation: If the farmer's hands are slackened, even the ascetic state will fail
Kurals.Couplet: For those who 've left what all men love no place is found,When they with folded hands remain who till the ground
Kurals.emotions: Concern – Worry about the consequences if farmers stop working., Urgency – A pressing need for farmers to continue their labor., Appreciation – Recognition of the importance and value of

Kurals.Translation: Moulds dried to quarter-dust ensure Rich crops without handful manure
Kurals.Explanation: If the land is dried so as to reduce one ounce of earth to a quarter, it will grow plentifully even without a handful of manure
Kurals.Couplet: Reduce your soil to that dry state, When ounce is quarter-ounce's weight;Without one handful of manure, Abundant crops you thus secure
Kurals.emotions: Hopefulness – The prospect that proper land preparation will yield rich crops

Kurals.Translation: Better manure than plough; then weed; Than irrigating, better guard
Kurals.Explanation: Manuring is better than ploughing; after weeding, watching is better than watering (it)
Kurals.Couplet: To cast manure is better than to plough;Weed well; to guard is more than watering now
Kurals.EmotionDetail: focus on effective actions,

Kurals.Translation: If landsmen sit sans moving about The field like wife will sulk and pout
Kurals.Explanation: If the owner does not (personally) attend to his cultivation, his land will behave like an angry wife and yield him no pleasure
Kurals.Couplet: When master from the field aloof hath stood;Then land will sulk, like wife in angry mood
Kurals.emotions: Neglect – The feeling of being ignored or uncared for

Kurals.Translation: Fair good earth will laugh to see Idlers pleading poverty
Kurals.Explanation: The maiden, Earth, will laugh at the sight of those who plead poverty and lead an idle life
Kurals.Couplet: The earth, that kindly dame, will laugh to see,Men seated idle pleading poverty
Kurals.EmotionDetail: from the Earth laughing at the idlers, felt by the idlers who plead poverty,

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as “Cringing, Dignity  Generosity, Dignity.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
