Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: What gives more pain than scarcity? No pain pinches like poverty
Kurals.Explanation: There is nothing that afflicts (one) like poverty
Kurals.Couplet: You ask what sharper pain than poverty is known;Nothing pains more than poverty, save poverty alone

Kurals.Translation: The sinner Want is enemy dire Of joys of earth and heaven there
Kurals.Explanation: When cruel poverty comes on, it deprives one of both the present and future (bliss)
Kurals.Couplet: Malefactor matchless! poverty destroysThis world's and the next world's joys

Kurals.Translation: The craving itch of poverty Kills graceful words and ancestry
Kurals.Explanation: Hankering poverty destroys at once the greatness of (one's) ancient descent and (the dignity of one's) speech
Kurals.Couplet: Importunate desire, which poverty men name,Destroys both old descent and goodly fame

Kurals.Translation: Want makes even good familymen Utter words that are low and mean
Kurals.Explanation: Even in those of high birth, poverty will produce the fault of uttering mean words
Kurals.Couplet: From penury will spring, 'mid even those of noble race,Oblivion that gives birth to words that bring disgrace

Kurals.Translation: The pest of wanton poverty Brings a train of misery
Kurals.Explanation: The misery of poverty brings in its train many (more) miseries
Kurals.Couplet: From poverty, that grievous woe,Attendant sorrows plenteous grow

Kurals.Translation: The poor men's words are thrown away Though from heart good things they say
Kurals.Explanation: The words of the poor are profitless, though they may be sound in thought and clear in expression
Kurals.Couplet: Though deepest sense, well understood, the poor man's words convey,Their sense from memory of mankind will fade away
Kurals.emotions: Sadness – The text reflects sorrow over the disregard of the poor’s voices., Frustration – There is a sense of frustration that wise or sincere words are ignored due to poverty

Kurals.Translation: Even the mother looks as stranger The poor devoid of character
Kurals.Explanation: He that is reduced to absolute poverty will be regarded as a stranger even by his own mother
Kurals.Couplet: From indigence devoid of virtue's grace,The mother e'en that bare, estranged, will turn her face

Kurals.Translation: The killing Want of yesterday Will it pester me even to-day?
Kurals.Explanation: Is the poverty that almost killed me yesterday, to meet me today too ?
Kurals.Couplet: And will it come today as yesterday,The grief of want that eats my soul away
Kurals.emotions: Anxiety — The speaker worries whether yesterday’s suffering (poverty) will continue into today., Fear — There is apprehension and dread that poverty will persist and cause further harm. 3

Kurals.Translation: One may sleep in the midst of fire In want a wink of sleep is rare
Kurals.Explanation: One may sleep in the midst of fire; but by no means in the midst of poverty
Kurals.Couplet: Amid the flames sleep may men's eyelids close,In poverty the eye knows no repose
Kurals.emotions: Desperation – The text highlights the profound distress experienced during poverty

Kurals.Translation: Renounce their lives the poor must Or salt and gruel go to waste
Kurals.Explanation: The destitute poor, who do not renounce their bodies, only consume their neighbour's salt and water
Kurals.Couplet: Unless the destitute will utterly themselves deny,They cause their neighbour's salt and vinegar to die
Kurals.emotions: Despair – The text reflects the hopelessness and suffering of the destitute poor., Resignation – There is a sense of acceptance of harsh circumstances

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Sadness, Despair, Shame.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Demand from those who can supply Default is theirs when they deny
Kurals.Explanation: If you meet with those that may be begged of, you may beg; (but) if they withhold (their gift) it is their blame and not yours
Kurals.Couplet: When those you find from whom 'tis meet to ask,- for aid apply;Theirs is the sin, not yours, if they the gift deny
Kurals.emotions: Hope – The act of begging or requesting implies hope that the person addressed will fulfill the need., Vulnerability – The one who begs places themselves in a position of dependence and exposure.

Kurals.Translation: Even demand becomes a joy When the things comes without annoy
Kurals.Explanation: Even begging may be pleasant, if what is begged for is obtained without grief (to him that begs)
Kurals.Couplet: Even to ask an alms may pleasure give,If what you ask without annoyance you receive

Kurals.Translation: Request has charm form open hearts Who know the duty on their part
Kurals.Explanation: There is even a beauty in standing before and begging of those who are liberal in their gifts and understand their duty (to beggars)
Kurals.Couplet: The men who nought deny, but know what's due, before their faceTo stand as suppliants affords especial grace

Kurals.Translation: Like giving even asking seems From those who hide not even in dreams
Kurals.Explanation: To beg of such as never think of withholding (their charity) even in their dreams, is in fact the same as giving (it oneself);
Kurals.Couplet: Like giving alms, may even asking pleasant seem,From men who of denial never even dream

Kurals.Translation: The needy demand for help because The world has men who don't refuse
Kurals.Explanation: As there are in the world those that give without refusing, there are (also) those that prefer to beg by simply standing before them
Kurals.Couplet: Because on earth the men exist, who never say them nay,Men bear to stand before their eyes for help to pray
Kurals.emotions: Neediness: The text centers on those who are in want and seek help

Kurals.Translation: The pain of poverty shall die Before the free who don't deny
Kurals.Explanation: All the evil of begging will be removed at the sight of those who are far from the evil of refusing
Kurals.Couplet: It those you find from evil of 'denial' free,At once all plague of poverty will flee
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – recognizing the pain of poverty and the suffering of those who beg., Hope – the idea that suffering can be alleviated through kindness and generosity., Relief – the

Kurals.Translation: When givers without scorn impart A thrill of delight fills the heart
Kurals.Explanation: Beggars rejoice exceedingly when they behold those who bestow (their alms) with kindness and courtesy
Kurals.Couplet: If men are found who give and no harsh words of scorn employ,The minds of askers, through and through, will thrill with joy
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated:, Joy, Gratitude, Hope

Kurals.Translation: This grand cool world shall move to and fro Sans Askers like a puppet show
Kurals.Explanation: If there were no beggars, (the actions done in) the cool wide world would only resemble the movement of a puppet
Kurals.Couplet: If askers cease, the mighty earth, where cooling fountains flow,Will be a stage where wooden puppets come and go
Kurals.emotions: Empathy – The text draws attention to the condition of beggars

Kurals.Translation: Where stands the glory of givers Without obligation seekers?
Kurals.Explanation: What (praise) would there be to givers (of alms) if there were no beggars to ask for and reveive (them)
Kurals.Couplet: What glory will there be to men of generous soul,When none are found to love the askers' role
Kurals.emotions: Gratitude – Highlighting appreciation for the mutual roles of giver and receiver., Humility – Recognizing the value and necessity of those who seek help., Compassion – Emphas

Kurals.Translation: The needy should not scowl at \"No\" His need another's need must show * Saint valluvar talks of two kinds of Asking:() Asking help for public causes or enterprises () Begging when one is able to work and this is condemned
Kurals.Explanation: He who begs ought not to be angry (at a refusal); for even the misery of (his own) poverty should be a sufficient reason (for so doing)
Kurals.Couplet: Askers refused from wrath must stand aloof;The plague of poverty itself is ample proof
Kurals.emotions: Humility – The text discusses the importance of not reacting with anger or pride when refused help and emphasizes an attitude of humility in the context of need and asking., Compassion – There is

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Gratitude, Humility, Generosity.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Not to beg is billions worth E'en from eye-like friends who give with mirth
Kurals.Explanation: Not to beg (at all) even from those excellent persons who cheerfully give without refusing, will do immense good
Kurals.Couplet: Ten million-fold 'tis greater gain, asking no alms to live,Even from those, like eyes in worth, who nought concealing gladly give
Kurals.emotions: **Dignity** – Emphasizing self-respect and the value of not having to beg., **Pride** – The positive feeling derived from self-reliance and

Kurals.Translation: Let World-Maker loiter and rot If \"beg and live\" be human fate
Kurals.Explanation: If the Creator of the world has decreed even begging as a means of livelihood, may he too go abegging and perish
Kurals.Couplet: If he that shaped the world desires that men should begging go,Through life's long course, let him a wanderer be and perish so
Kurals.emotions: Resentment – There is a strong feeling of bitterness directed at the Creator for decreeing begging as a means of livelihood., Hopelessness – The text expresses despair about

Kurals.Translation: Nothing is hard like hard saying \"We end poverty by begging\"
Kurals.Explanation: There is no greater folly than the boldness with which one seeks to remedy the evils of poverty by begging (rather than by working)
Kurals.Couplet: Nothing is harder than the hardness that will say,'The plague of penury by asking alms we'll drive away.'
Kurals.emotions: Shame – The reference to begging and the emotional burden attached to it conveys a sense of shame., Despair – The recognition of poverty and the difficulty in escaping it by begging

Kurals.Translation: All space is small before the great Who beg not e'en in want acute
Kurals.Explanation: Even the whole world cannot sufficiently praise the dignity that would not beg even in the midst of destitution
Kurals.Couplet: Who ne'er consent to beg in utmost need, their worthHas excellence of greatness that transcends the earth

Kurals.Translation: Though gruel thin, nothing is sweet Like the food earned by labour's sweat
Kurals.Explanation: Even thin gruel is ambrosia to him who has obtained it by labour
Kurals.Couplet: Nothing is sweeter than to taste the toil-won cheer,Though mess of pottage as tasteless as the water clear
Kurals.emotions: Pride – derived from earning one’s sustenance through hard work., Contentment – satisfaction with what one has

Kurals.Translation: It may be water for the cow Begging tongue is mean anyhow
Kurals.Explanation: There is nothing more disgraceful to one's tongue than to use it in begging water even for a cow
Kurals.Couplet: E'en if a draught of water for a cow you ask,Nought's so distasteful to the tongue as beggar's task

Kurals.Translation: If beg they must I beg beggers Not to beg from shrinking misers
Kurals.Explanation: I beseech all beggars and say, "If you need to beg, never beg of those who give unwillingly."
Kurals.Couplet: One thing I beg of beggars all, 'If beg ye may,Of those who hide their wealth, beg not, I pray.'
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions in the text:, Dread – A strong feeling of fear or apprehension

Kurals.Translation: The hapless bark of beggary splits On the rock of refusing hits
Kurals.Explanation: The unsafe raft of begging will split when it strikes on the rock of refusal
Kurals.Couplet: The fragile bark of beggaryWrecked on denial's rock will lie

Kurals.Translation: The heart at thought of beggars melts; It dies at repulsing insults
Kurals.Explanation: To think of (the evil of) begging is enough to melt one's heart; but to think of refusal is enough to break it
Kurals.Couplet: The heart will melt away at thought of beggary,With thought of stern repulse 'twill perish utterly
Kurals.emotions: Compassion – The thought of beggars and their plight evokes a sense of empathy and caring for their suffering., Dread – There is a strong feeling of fear or anxiety at

Kurals.Translation: The word \"No\" kills the begger's life Where can the niggard's life be safe?
Kurals.Explanation: Saying "No" to a beggar takes away his life. (but as that very word will kill the refuser) where then would the latter's life hide itself ?
Kurals.Couplet: E'en as he asks, the shamefaced asker dies;Where shall his spirit hide who help denies

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Shame, Fear, Dignity.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The mean seem men only in form We have never seen such a sham
Kurals.Explanation: The base resemble men perfectly (as regards form); and we have not seen such (exact) resemblance (among any other species)
Kurals.Couplet: The base resemble men in outward form, I ween;But counterpart exact to them I've never seen
Kurals.emotions: Disdain (contempt) – The text expresses a sense of scorn toward "the mean" or "base

Kurals.Translation: The base seem richer than the good For no care enters their heart or head
Kurals.Explanation: The low enjoy more felicity than those who know what is good; for the former are not troubled with anxiety (as to the good)
Kurals.Couplet: Than those of grateful heart the base must luckier be,Their minds from every anxious thought are free
Kurals.emotions: Envy – There is a sense of envying the carefree state of those considered 'base' compared to the troubled state of the 'good'., Resignation – The explanation conveys a

Kurals.Translation: The base are like gods; for they too As prompted by their desire do
Kurals.Explanation: The base resemble the Gods; for the base act as they like
Kurals.Couplet: The base are as the Gods; they tooDo ever what they list to do
Kurals.emotions: Contempt – The text conveys a sense of disapproval and disdain towards "the base" by comparing them ironically to gods in their self-serving actions., Cynicism – The explanation suggests

Kurals.Translation: When the base meets a rake so vile Him he will exceed, exult and smile
Kurals.Explanation: The base feels proud when he sees persons whose acts meaner than his own
Kurals.Couplet: When base men those behold of conduct vile,They straight surpass them, and exulting smile
Kurals.emotions: Pride – The base feels proud when seeing others worse than himself., Superiority – There is a sense of feeling above or better than others who are more "vile."

Kurals.Translation: Fear forms the conduct of the low Craving avails a bit below
Kurals.Explanation: (The principle of) behaviour in the mean is chiefly fear; if not, hope of gain, to some extent
Kurals.Couplet: Fear is the base man's virtue; if that fail,Intense desire some little may avail
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Craving/Desire for gain, due to fear or hope of small gain

Kurals.Translation: The base are like the beaten drum Since other's secrets they proclaim
Kurals.Explanation: The base are like a drum that is beaten, for they unburden to others the secrets they have heard
Kurals.Couplet: The base are like the beaten drum; for, when they hearThe sound the secret out in every neighbour's ear
Kurals.EmotionDetail: towards those who reveal others’ secrets, resulting from secret-telling,

Kurals.Translation: The base their damp hand will not shake But for fists clenched their jaws to break
Kurals.Explanation: The mean will not (even) shake off (what sticks to) their hands (soon after a meal) to any but those who would break their jaws with their clenched fists
Kurals.Couplet: From off their moistened hands no clinging grain they shake,Unless to those with clenched fist their jaws who break
Kurals.emotions: **Meanness (or Baseness)** – The text reflects a lack of generosity or kindness

Kurals.Translation: The good by soft words profits yield The cane-like base when crushed and killed
Kurals.Explanation: The great bestow (their alms) as soon as they are informed; (but) the mean, like the sugar-cane, only when they are tortured to death
Kurals.Couplet: The good to those will profit yield fair words who use;The base, like sugar-cane, will profit those who bruise
Kurals.emotions: Generosity – Highlighted by the praise of those who bestow alms willingly., Contempt/Disdain – Directed toward the mean/baseness of those who give only under

Kurals.Translation: Faults in others the mean will guess On seeing how they eat and dress
Kurals.Explanation: The base will bring an evil (accusation) against others, as soon as he sees them (enjoying) good food
Kurals.Couplet: If neighbours clothed and fed he see, the baseIs mighty man some hidden fault to trace

Kurals.Translation: The base hasten to sell themselves From doom to flit and nothing else
Kurals.Explanation: The base will hasten to sell themselves as soon as a calamity has befallen them. For what else are they fitted ?
Kurals.Couplet: For what is base man fit, if griefs assail?Himself to offer, there and then, for sale
Kurals.emotions: Desperation – The text conveys a sense of urgency and loss

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Fear, Greed, Caution.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Is it an angel? A fair peacock Or jewelled belle? To my mind a shock!
Kurals.Explanation: Is this jewelled female a celestial, a choice peahen, or a human being ? My mind is perplexed
Kurals.Couplet: Goddess? or peafowl rare? She whose ears rich jewels wear,Is she a maid of human kind? All wildered is my mind
Kurals.emotions: Awe – The speaker marvels at the subject’s beauty

Kurals.Translation: The counter glances of this belle Are armied dart of the Love-Angel
Kurals.Explanation: This female beauty returning my looks is like a celestial maiden coming with an army to contend against me
Kurals.Couplet: She of the beaming eyes, To my rash look her glance replies,As if the matchless goddess' hand Led forth an armed band
Kurals.EmotionDetail: at the overwhelming beauty, , feeling defenseless or overtaken by love

Kurals.Translation: Not known before -I spy Demise In woman's guise with battling eyes
Kurals.Explanation: I never knew before what is called Yama; I see it now; it is the eyes that carry on a great fight with (the help of) female qualities
Kurals.Couplet: Death's form I formerly Knew not; but now 'tis plain to me;He comes in lovely maiden's guise, With soul-subduing eyes
Kurals.EmotionDetail: at the power and beauty of love,

Kurals.Translation: This artless dame has darting eyes That drink the life of men who gaze
Kurals.Explanation: These eyes that seem to kill those who look at them are as it were in hostilities with this feminine simplicity
Kurals.Couplet: In sweet simplicity, A woman's gracious form hath she;But yet those eyes, that drink my life, Are with the form at strife
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – for the woman's captivating beauty., Awe – at the powerful effect her eyes have on those who behold them., Fascination – with the contrast between her simplicity and

Kurals.Translation: Is it death, eye or doe? All three In winsome woman's look I see
Kurals.Explanation: Is it Yama, (a pair of) eyes or a hind ?- Are not all these three in the looks of this maid ?
Kurals.Couplet: The light that on me gleams, Is it death's dart? or eye's bright beams?Or fawn's shy glance? All three appear In form of maiden here
Kurals.emotions: Fascination – The speaker is captivated by the mysterious and enchanting qualities in the woman's gaze., Desire – There is a sense of longing and attraction toward the beauty described in the woman's look

Kurals.Translation: If cruel brows unbent, would screen Her eyes won't cause me trembling pain
Kurals.Explanation: Her eyes will cause (me) no trembling sorrow, if they are properly hidden by her cruel arched eyebrows
Kurals.Couplet: If cruel eye-brow's bow, Unbent, would veil those glances now;The shafts that wound this trembling heart Her eyes no more would dart
Kurals.emotions: Longing – The speaker expresses desire for relief from the pain caused by someone's gaze., Pain/Sorrow – There is an acknowledgment of "trembling sorrow" inflicted by the beloved

Kurals.Translation: Vest on the buxom breast of her Looks like rutting tusker's eye-cover
Kurals.Explanation: The cloth that covers the firm bosom of this maiden is (like) that which covers the eyes of a rutting elephant
Kurals.Couplet: As veil o'er angry eyes Of raging elephant that lies,The silken cincture's folds invest This maiden's panting breast
Kurals.emotions: Desire – The description emphasizes physical attraction and admiration

Kurals.Translation: Ah these fair brows shatter my might Feared by foemen yet to meet
Kurals.Explanation: On her bright brow alone is destroyed even that power of mine that used to terrify the most fearless
Kurals.Couplet: Ah woe is me my might, That awed my foemen in the fight,By lustre of that beaming brow Borne down, lies broken now
Kurals.EmotionDetail: at the beauty and power of the beloved's brows, feeling powerless despite previous strength, deep appreciation of the beloved's excellence

Kurals.Translation: Which jewel can add to her beauty With fawn-like looks and modesty?
Kurals.Explanation: Of what use are other jewels to her who is adorned with modesty, and the meek looks of a hind ?
Kurals.Couplet: Like tender fawn's her eye; Clothed on is she with modesty;What added beauty can be lent; By alien ornament
Kurals.emotions: Admiration — The text expresses admiration for the woman's beauty

Kurals.Translation: To the drunk alone is wine delight Nothing delights like love at sight
Kurals.Explanation: Unlike boiled honey which yields delight only when it is drunk, love gives pleasure even when looked at
Kurals.Couplet: The palm-tree's fragrant wine, To those who taste yields joys divine;But love hath rare felicity For those that only see

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Desire, Fear At The Power, Beauty Of Love.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Her painted eyes, two glances dart One hurts; the other heals my heart
Kurals.Explanation: There are two looks in the dyed eyes of this (fair one); one causes pain, and the other is the cure thereof
Kurals.Couplet: A double witchery have glances of her liquid eye;One glance is glance that brings me pain; the other heals again
Kurals.emotions: Love/Attraction – Reflected in the focus on the beloved’s eyes and their effect on the heart., Pain/Suffering – One glance is described as hurtful

Kurals.Translation: Her furtive lightning glance is more Than enjoyment of sexual lore
Kurals.Explanation: A single stolen glance of her eyes is more than half the pleasure (of sexual embrace)
Kurals.Couplet: The furtive glance, that gleams one instant bright,Is more than half of love's supreme delight

Kurals.Translation: She looked; looking bowed her head And love-plant was with water fed
Kurals.Explanation: She has looked (at men) and stooped (her head); and that (sign) waters as it were (the corn of) our love
Kurals.Couplet: She looked, and looking drooped her head:On springing shoot of love 'its water shed
Kurals.EmotionDetail: implied by her bowing/stopping her head after looking,

Kurals.Translation: I look; she droops to earth awhile I turn; she looks with gentle smile
Kurals.Explanation: When I look, she looks down; when I do not, she looks and smiles gently
Kurals.Couplet: I look on her: her eyes are on the ground the while:I look away: she looks on me with timid smile

Kurals.Translation: No direct gaze; a side-long glance She darts at me and smiles askance
Kurals.Explanation: She not only avoids a direct look at me, but looks as it were with a half-closed eye and smiles
Kurals.Couplet: She seemed to see me not; but yet the maidHer love, by smiling side-long glance, betrayed

Kurals.Translation: Their words at first seem an offence But quick we feel them friendly ones
Kurals.Explanation: Though they may speak harshly as if they were strangers, the words of the friendly are soon understood
Kurals.Couplet: Though with their lips affection they disown,Yet, when they hate us not, 'tis quickly known

Kurals.Translation: Harsh little words; offended looks, Are feigned consenting love-lorn tricks
Kurals.Explanation: Little words that are harsh and looks that are hateful are (but) the expressions of lovers who wish to act like strangers
Kurals.Couplet: The slighting words that anger feign, while eyes their love revealAre signs of those that love, but would their love conceal
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with the text are:, Offense (hurt/pride) – Indicated by "offended looks" and "harsh little words", Pretense/Playfulness – The actions are described as

Kurals.Translation: What a grace the slim maid has! As I look she slightly smiles
Kurals.Explanation: When I look, the pitying maid looks in return and smiles gently; and that is a comforting sign for me
Kurals.Couplet: I gaze, the tender maid relents the while;And, oh the matchless grace of that soft smile
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions in the text:, Hope, Affection, Comfort

Kurals.Translation: Between lovers we do discern A stranger's look of unconcern
Kurals.Explanation: Both the lovers are capable of looking at each other in an ordinary way, as if they were perfect strangers
Kurals.Couplet: The look indifferent, that would its love disguise,Is only read aright by lovers' eyes

Kurals.Translation: The words of mouth are of no use When eye to eye agrees the gaze
Kurals.Explanation: The words of the mouths are of no use whatever, when there is perfect agreement between the eyes (of lovers)
Kurals.Couplet: When eye to answering eye reveals the tale of love,All words that lips can say must useless prove

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Love, Anticipation, Shyness.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: In this bangled beauty dwell The joys of sight sound touch taste smell
Kurals.Explanation: The (simultaneous) enjoyment of the five senses of sight, hearing, taste, smell and touch can only be found with bright braceleted (women)
Kurals.Couplet: All joys that senses five- sight, hearing, taste, smell, touch- can give,In this resplendent armlets-bearing damsel live
Kurals.emotions: Joy – The passage emphasizes "rejoicing" and "enjoyment" of the senses

Kurals.Translation: The cure for ailment is somewhere For fair maid's ill she is the cure
Kurals.Explanation: The remedy for a disease is always something different (from it); but for the disease caused by this jewelled maid, she is herself the cure
Kurals.Couplet: Disease and medicine antagonists we surely see;This maid, to pain she gives, herself is remedy

Kurals.Translation: Is lotus-eyed lord's heaven so sweet As sleep in lover's arms so soft?
Kurals.Explanation: Can the lotus-eyed Vishnu's heaven be indeed as sweet to those who delight to sleep in the delicate arms of their beloved ?
Kurals.Couplet: Than rest in her soft arms to whom the soul is giv'n,Is any sweeter joy in his, the Lotus-eyed-one's heaven

Kurals.Translation: Away it burns and cools anear Wherefrom did she get this fire?
Kurals.Explanation: From whence has she got this fire that burns when I withdraw and cools when I approach ?
Kurals.Couplet: Withdraw, it burns; approach, it soothes the pain;Whence did the maid this wondrous fire obtain
Kurals.emotions: Curiosity – Wondering about the mysterious source of the changing feelings., Longing – Desire and yearning implied by the effect of the speaker’s presence and absence., Fascination

Kurals.Translation: The arms of my flower-tressed maid Whatever I wish that that accord
Kurals.Explanation: Each varied form of joy the soul can wish is found
Kurals.Couplet: In her embrace, whose locks with flowery wreaths are bound,Each varied form of joy the soul can wish is found

Kurals.Translation: My simple maid has nectar arms Each embrace brings life-thrilling charms
Kurals.Explanation: The shoulders of this fair one are made of ambrosia, for they revive me with pleasure every time I embrace them
Kurals.Couplet: Ambrosia are the simple maiden's arms; when I attainTheir touch, my withered life puts forth its buds again

Kurals.Translation: Ah the embrace of this fair dame Is like sharing one's food at home
Kurals.Explanation: The embraces of a gold-complexioned beautiful female are as pleasant as to dwell in one's own house and live by one's own (earnings) after distributing (a portion of it in charity)
Kurals.Couplet: As when one eats from household store, with kindly graceSharing his meal: such is this golden maid's embrace
Kurals.emotions: Joy – The text revolves around the concept of "rejoicing

Kurals.Translation: Joy is the fast embrace that doth Not admit e'en air between both
Kurals.Explanation: To ardent lovers sweet is the embrace that cannot be penetrated even by a breath of breeze
Kurals.Couplet: Sweet is the strict embrace of those whom fond affection binds,Where no dissevering breath of discord entrance finds

Kurals.Translation: Sulking, feeling and clasping fast These three are sweets of lover's tryst
Kurals.Explanation: Love quarrel, reconciliation and intercourse - these are the advantages reaped by those who marry for lust
Kurals.Couplet: The jealous variance, the healing of the strife, reunion gained:These are the fruits from wedded love obtained
Kurals.emotions: Longing/Desire, Joy/Rejoicing, Reconciliation These emotions are reflected in the themes of sulking
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , longing/desire

Kurals.Translation: As knowledge reveals past ignorance So is the belle as love gets close
Kurals.Explanation: As (one's) ignorance is discovered the more one learns, so does repeated intercourse with a welladorned female (only create a desire for more)
Kurals.Couplet: The more men learn, the more their lack of learning they detect;'Tis so when I approach the maid with gleaming jewels decked

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Joy, Love, Longing.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

chapters.Chapter: Praising the Beauty of the Beloved

Kurals.Translation: Soft blessed anicha flower, hail On whom I dote is softer still
Kurals.Explanation: May you flourish, O Anicham! you have a delicate nature But my beloved is more delicate than you
Kurals.Couplet: O flower of the sensitive plant! than theeMore tender's the maiden beloved by me
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – The speaker expresses deep appreciation for the beauty and delicacy of the beloved., Love – The text conveys strong affection and endearment towards the beloved. 3

Kurals.Translation: You can't liken flowers by many eyed, To her bright eyes, O mind dismayed
Kurals.Explanation: O my soul, fancying that flowers which are seen by many can resemble her eyes, you become confused at the sight of them
Kurals.Couplet: You deemed, as you saw the flowers, her eyes were as flowers, my soul,That many may see; it was surely some folly that over you stole
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – The speaker is expressing deep appreciation for the beloved's beauty., Awe – The comparison of the beloved’s eyes to flowers

Kurals.Translation: The bamboo-shouldered has pearl-like smiles Fragrant breath and lance-like eyes
Kurals.Explanation: The complexion of this bamboo-shouldered one is that of a shoot; her teeth, are pearls; her breath,
Kurals.Couplet: As tender shoot her frame; teeth, pearls; around her odours blend;Darts are the eyes of her whose shoulders like the bambu bend
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions associated with this text are:, Admiration, Love, Awe

Kurals.Translation: Lily droops down to ground and says I can't equal the jewelled-one's eyes
Kurals.Explanation: If the blue lotus could see, it would stoop and look at the ground saying, "I can never resemble the eyes of this excellent jewelled one."
Kurals.Couplet: The lotus, seeing her, with head demiss, the ground would eye,And say, 'With eyes of her, rich gems who wears, we cannot vie.'
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – Deep appreciation and praise for the beauty of the beloved., Humility – A sense of modesty or recognizing one's own limitations compared to the beloved's beauty.

Kurals.Translation: Anicha flower with stem she wears To her breaking waist sad-drum-blares!
Kurals.Explanation: No merry drums will be beaten for the (tender) waist of her who has adorned herself with the anicham without having removed its stem
Kurals.Couplet: The flowers of the sensitive plant as a girdle around her she placed;The stems she forgot to nip off; they 'll weigh down the delicate waist
Kurals.emotions: **Sadness** – The phrase "sad-drum-blares" and reference to a "breaking waist" evoke a sense of sorrow and fragility., **Longing** –

Kurals.Translation: Stars are confused to know which is The moon and which is woman's face
Kurals.Explanation: The stars have become confused in their places not being able to distinguish between the moon and the maid's countenance
Kurals.Couplet: The stars perplexed are rushing wildly from their spheres;For like another moon this maiden's face appears
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – Expressing deep respect and appreciation for the beloved’s beauty., Awe – A sense of wonder and amazement at the beloved’s radiance

Kurals.Translation: Are there spots on the lady's face Just as in moon that changes phase?
Kurals.Explanation: Could there be spots in the face of this maid like those in the bright full moon ?
Kurals.Couplet: In moon, that waxing waning shines, as sports appear,Are any spots discerned in face of maiden here
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – expressing wonder at the beauty of the beloved., Awe – likening the beloved’s face to the bright full moon evokes a sense of awe., Cur

Kurals.Translation: Like my lady's face if you shine All my love to you; hail O moon!
Kurals.Explanation: If you can indeed shine like the face of women, flourish, O moon, for then would you be worth loving ?
Kurals.Couplet: Farewell, O moon! If that thine orb could shineBright as her face, thou shouldst be love of mine

Kurals.Translation: Like the face of my flower-eyed one If you look, then shine alone O moon!
Kurals.Explanation: O moon, if you wish to resemble the face of her whose eyes are like (these) flowers, do not appear so as to be seen by all
Kurals.Couplet: If as her face, whose eyes are flowers, thou wouldst have charms for me,Shine for my eyes alone, O moon, shine not for all to see
Kurals.emotions: Admiration – Expressing deep appreciation and reverence for the beauty of the beloved., Longing – Conveying a yearning or desire for the beloved’s presence and incomparable beauty. 3

Kurals.Translation: The soft flower and the swan's down are Like nettles to the feet of the fair
Kurals.Explanation: The anicham and the feathers of the swan are to the feet of females, like the fruit of the (thorny) Nerunji
Kurals.Couplet: The flower of the sensitive plant, and the down on the swan's white breast,As the thorn are harsh, by the delicate feet of this maiden pressed
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions in the text:, Admiration – Expressed by praising the delicate beauty and sensitivity of the beloved., Awe – In response to the extraordinary tenderness attributed to the beloved's feet., Tenderness – Ref

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Admiration, Love, Awe.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Like milk and honey the dew is sweet From her white teeth whose word is soft
Kurals.Explanation: The water which oozes from the white teeth of this soft speeched damsel is like a mixture of milk and honey
Kurals.Couplet: The dew on her white teeth, whose voice is soft and low,Is as when milk and honey mingled flow

Kurals.Translation: Love between me and this lady Is like bond between soul and body
Kurals.Explanation: The love between me and this damsel is like the union of body and soul
Kurals.Couplet: Between this maid and me the friendship kindIs as the bonds that soul and body bind

Kurals.Translation: Depart image in my pupil Giving room to my fair-browed belle!
Kurals.Explanation: O you image in the pupil (of my eye)! depart; there is no room for (my) fair-browed beloved
Kurals.Couplet: For her with beauteous brow, the maid I love, there place is none;To give her image room, O pupil of mine eye, begone
Kurals.emotions: Love – The focus on the beloved and the act of making room for her in one’s heart and eyes underscores deep affection., Longing – The plea for the image to

Kurals.Translation: Life with my jewel is existence Death it is her severance
Kurals.Explanation: My fair-jewelled one resembles the living soul (when she is in union with me), the dying soul when she leaves me
Kurals.Couplet: Life is she to my very soul when she draws nigh;Dissevered from the maid with jewels rare, I die

Kurals.Translation: Can I forget? I recall always The charms of her bright battling eyes
Kurals.Explanation: If I had forgotten her who has bright battling eyes, I would have remembered (thee); but I never forget her (Thus says he to her maid)
Kurals.Couplet: I might recall, if I could once forget; but from my heartHer charms fade not, whose eyes gleam like the warrior's dart

Kurals.Translation: So subtle is my lover's form Ever in my eyes winking, no harm
Kurals.Explanation: My lover would not depart from mine eyes; even if I wink, he would not suffer (from pain); he is so ethereal
Kurals.Couplet: My loved one's subtle form departs not from my eyes;I wink them not, lest I should pain him where he lies

Kurals.Translation: My lover in my eyes abides I paint them not lest he hides
Kurals.Explanation: As my lover abides in my eyes, I will not even paint them, for he would (then) have to conceal himself
Kurals.Couplet: My love doth ever in my eyes reside;I stain them not, fearing his form to hide
Kurals.emotions: Love – The text centers on deep affection and attachment toward the lover., Devotion – There is a sense of dedication and carefulness

Kurals.Translation: My lover abides in my heart I fear hot food lest he feels hot
Kurals.Explanation: As my lover is in my heart, I am afraid of eating (anything) hot, for I know it would pain him
Kurals.Couplet: Within my heart my lover dwells; from food I turnThat smacks of heat, lest he should feel it burn

Kurals.Translation: My eyes wink not lest he should hide And him as cruel the townsmen chide
Kurals.Explanation: I will not wink, knowing that if I did, my lover would hide himself; and for this reason, this town says, he is unloving
Kurals.Couplet: I fear his form to hide, nor close my eyes:'Her love estranged is gone!' the village cries
Kurals.emotions: Love – The speaker’s devotion and care toward their lover is central., Anxiety – There is worry or fear that the lover might disappear or “hide” if the speaker is

Kurals.Translation: He abides happy in my heart But people mistake he is apart
Kurals.Explanation: My lover dwells in my heart with perpetual delight; but the town says he is unloving and (therefore) dwells afar
Kurals.Couplet: Rejoicing in my very soul he ever lies;'Her love estranged is gone far off!' the village cries

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Love, Longing, Devotion.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Pangs of passion find no recourse Except riding *`palmyra horse' * Palmyra horse or 'Madal' is a torture expressive of the burning passion of the lover to the beloved The lover's body is laid on a rough pricking palmyra bed and he is carried along the street with songs of love pangs The parents of the lovers first reproach them and then consent to their marriage
Kurals.Explanation: To those who after enjoyment of sexual pleasure suffer (for want of more), there is no help so efficient as the palmyra horse
Kurals.Couplet: To those who 've proved love's joy, and now afflicted mourn,Except the helpful 'horse of palm', no other strength remains
Kurals.emotions: Passion — The text revolves around intense desire and longing

Kurals.Translation: Pining body and mind lose shame And take to riding of the palm
Kurals.Explanation: Having got rid of shame, the suffering body and soul save themselves on the palmyra horse
Kurals.Couplet: My body and my soul, that can no more endure,Will lay reserve aside, and mount the 'horse of palm'
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 emotions in the text:, Longing, Shame, Suffering
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Pining, and the loss or overcoming of it, both body and soul

Kurals.Translation: Once I was modest and manly My love has now Madal only
Kurals.Explanation: Modesty and manliness were once my own; now, my own is the palmyra horse that is ridden by the lustful
Kurals.Couplet: I once retained reserve and seemly manliness;To-day I nought possess but lovers' 'horse of palm'
Kurals.EmotionDetail: or Loss of Modesty, especially passionate or unrestrained,

Kurals.Translation: Rushing flood of love sweeps away The raft of shame and firmness, aye!
Kurals.Explanation: The raft of modesty and manliness, is, alas, carried-off by the strong current of lust
Kurals.Couplet: Love's rushing tide will sweep away the raftOf seemly manliness and shame combined
Kurals.EmotionDetail: intense love or lust, referencing the raft of shame and modesty,

Kurals.Translation: Palm-ride and pangs of eventide Are gifts of wreath-like bracelet maid
Kurals.Explanation: She with the small garland-like bracelets has given me the palmyra horse and the sorrow that is endured at night
Kurals.Couplet: The maid that slender armlets wears, like flowers entwined,Has brought me 'horse of palm,' and pangs of eventide

Kurals.Translation: Madal I ride at midnight for My eyes sleep not seeing this fair
Kurals.Explanation: Mine eyes will not close in sleep on your mistress's account; even at midnight will I think of mounting the palmyra horse
Kurals.Couplet: Of climbing 'horse of palm' in midnight hour, I think;My eyes know no repose for that same simple maid
Kurals.emotions: Love – The speaker declares a deep and powerful love

Kurals.Translation: Her sea-like lust seeks not Madal! Serene is woman's self control
Kurals.Explanation: There is nothing so noble as the womanly nature that would not ride the palmyra horse, though plunged a sea of lust
Kurals.Couplet: There's nought of greater worth than woman's long-enduring soul,Who, vexed by love like ocean waves, climbs not the 'horse of palm'
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , restrained or subdued

Kurals.Translation: Lust betrays itself in haste Though women are highly soft and chaste
Kurals.Explanation: Even the Lust (of women) transgresses its secrecy and appears in public, forgetting that they are too chaste and liberal (to be overcome by it)
Kurals.Couplet: In virtue hard to move, yet very tender, too, are we;Love deems not so, would rend the veil, and court publicity
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Lust, ,

Kurals.Translation: My perplexed love roves public street Believing that none knows its secret
Kurals.Explanation: And thus, in public ways, perturbed will rove
Kurals.Couplet: 'There's no one knows my heart,' so says my love,And thus, in public ways, perturbed will rove
Kurals.emotions: Love – The passage repeatedly refers to love

Kurals.Translation: Fools laugh at me before my eyes For they feel not my pangs and sighs
Kurals.Explanation: Even strangers laugh (at us) so as to be seen by us, for they have not suffered
Kurals.Couplet: Before my eyes the foolish make a mock of me,Because they ne'er endured the pangs I now must drie
Kurals.emotions: Shame (or vulnerability) – Expressed in the sense of being laughed at and exposed., Pain (emotional pain/suffering) – Highlighted by "my pangs

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Shame, Longing, Love.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Rumour sustains my existence Good luck! many know not its sense
Kurals.Explanation: My precious life is saved by the raise of rumour, and this, to my good luck no others are aware of
Kurals.Couplet: By this same rumour's rise, my precious life stands fast;Good fortune grant the many know this not
Kurals.emotions: Relief – The speaker feels relieved that the rumour is keeping their secret safe and protecting them., Gratitude – There is a sense of thankfulness for the "good luck" that

Kurals.Translation: Rumour gives me the flower-like belle People know not what rare angel
Kurals.Explanation: Not knowing the value of her whose eyes are like flowers this town has got up a rumour about me
Kurals.Couplet: The village hath to us this rumour giv'n, that makes her mine;Unweeting all the rareness of the maid with flower-like eyne
Kurals.emotions: Love – The speaker refers to being in love with someone whose worth is unrecognized by others., Frustration – The speaker feels misunderstood or frustrated that others spread rumors

Kurals.Translation: I profit by this public rumour Having not, I feel, I have her
Kurals.Explanation: Will I not get a rumour that is known to the (whole) town ? For what I have not got is as if I had got it (already)
Kurals.Couplet: The rumour spread within the town, is it not gain to me?It is as though that were obtained that may not be
Kurals.emotions: **Desire** – The speaker longs for love and feels a yearning for the beloved., **Hopefulness** – The speaker anticipates that even though they have not yet attained their

Kurals.Translation: Rumour inflames the love I seek Or else it becomes bleak and weak
Kurals.Explanation: Rumour increases the violence of my passion; without it it would grow weak and waste away
Kurals.Couplet: The rumour rising makes my love to rise;My love would lose its power and languish otherwise

Kurals.Translation: Drink delights as liquor flows Love delights as rumour grows
Kurals.Explanation: As drinking liquor is delightful (to one) whenever one is in mirth, so is lust delightful to me whenever it is the subject of rumour
Kurals.Couplet: The more man drinks, the more he ever drunk would be;The more my love's revealed, the sweeter 'tis to me
Kurals.emotions: Delight, Pleasure, Anticipation (or Excitement) These emotions are reflected in the joy of drinking

Kurals.Translation: One lasting day we met alone Lasting rumours eclipse our moon
Kurals.Explanation: It was but a single day that I looked on (my lover); but the rumour thereof has spread like the seizure of the moon by the serpent
Kurals.Couplet: I saw him but one single day: rumour spreads soonAs darkness, when the dragon seizes on the moon
Kurals.emotions: Secrecy – The need and effort to conceal love from others., Anxiety – Worry over public scrutiny and the spread of rumours., Longing – The powerful

Kurals.Translation: Scandal manures; mother's refrain Waters the growth of this love-pain
Kurals.Explanation: This malady (of lust) is manured by the talk of women and watered by the (harsh) words of my mother
Kurals.Couplet: My anguish grows apace: the town's reportManures it; my mother's word doth water it
Kurals.emotions: Shame — The need to conceal love from outsiders and references to "scandal" suggest feelings of embarrassment or disgrace., Pain/Sorrow — The phrase "love-pain" and the

Kurals.Translation: To quench the lust by rumour free Is to quench fire by pouring ghee
Kurals.Explanation: To say that one could extinguish passion by rumour is like extinguishing fire with ghee
Kurals.Couplet: With butter-oil extinguish fire! 'Twill proveHarder by scandal to extinguish love
Kurals.emotions: Desire (or Passion) – reflecting the strong feelings of love or lust that are difficult to suppress., Frustration – stemming from the futile attempt to conceal or quell these

Kurals.Translation: Who said \"fear not\" flared up rumour Why then should I blush this clamour?
Kurals.Explanation: When the departure of him who said "fear not" has put me to shame before others, why need I be ashamed of scandal
Kurals.Couplet: When he who said 'Fear not!' hath left me blamed,While many shrink, can I from rumour hide ashamed
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , Concealed/Hidden Love

Kurals.Translation: Town raising this cry, I desire Consent is easy from my sire
Kurals.Explanation: The rumour I desire is raised by the town (itself); and my lover would if desired consent (to my following him)
Kurals.Couplet: If we desire, who loves will grant what we require;This town sends forth the rumour we desire

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Longing, Protecting Them., Passion.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Tell me if you but do not leave, Your quick return to those who live
Kurals.Explanation: If it is not departure, tell me; but if it is your speedy return, tell it to those who would be alive then
Kurals.Couplet: If you will say, 'I leave thee not,' then tell me so;Of quick return tell those that can survive this woe
Kurals.emotions: **Sorrow** – The chapter title and content convey a deep sense of sadness due to separation., **Longing** – There is a yearning for the return of someone who is

Kurals.Translation: His sight itself was pleasing, near Embrace pains now by partings fear
Kurals.Explanation: His very look was once pleasing; but (now) even intercourse is painful through fear of separation
Kurals.Couplet: It once was perfect joy to look upon his face;But now the fear of parting saddens each embrace
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Sadness,

Kurals.Translation: On whom shall I lay my trust hence While parting lurks in knowing ones?
Kurals.Explanation: As even the lover who understands (everything) may at times depart, confidence is hardly possible
Kurals.Couplet: To trust henceforth is hard, if ever he depart,E'en he, who knows his promise and my breaking heart

Kurals.Translation: He parts whose love told me -fear not Is my trust in him at default?
Kurals.Explanation: If he who bestowed his love and said "fear not" should depart, will it be the fault of those who believed in (his) assuring words ?
Kurals.Couplet: If he depart, who fondly said, 'Fear not,' what blame's incurredBy those who trusted to his reassuring word
Kurals.emotions: Sorrow – Reflecting on the pain and sadness of separation., Betrayal – Feeling let down by someone who once offered love and reassurance., Doubt – Questioning

Kurals.Translation: Stop his parting -my life to save Meeting is rare if he would leave
Kurals.Explanation: If you would save (my life), delay the departure of my destined (husband); for if he departs, intercourse will become impossible
Kurals.Couplet: If you would guard my life, from going him restrainWho fills my life! If he depart, hardly we meet again
Kurals.EmotionDetail: due to separation and the title itself, desire to be with the loved one,

Kurals.Translation: His hardness says, \"I leave you now\" Is there hope of his renewed love?
Kurals.Explanation: Is hard, when he could stand, and of departure speak to me
Kurals.Couplet: To cherish longing hope that he should ever gracious be,Is hard, when he could stand, and of departure speak to me

Kurals.Translation: Will not my gliding bangles' cry The parting of my lord betray?
Kurals.Explanation: Do not the rings that begin to slide down my fingers forebode the separation of my lord ?
Kurals.Couplet: The bracelet slipping from my wrist announced beforeDeparture of the Prince that rules the ocean shore
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Sadness, , Worry

Kurals.Translation: Bitter is life in friendless place; Worse is parting love's embrace!
Kurals.Explanation: Painful is it to live in a friendless town; but far more painful is it to part from one's lover
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis sad to sojourn in the town where no kind kinsmen dwell;'Tis sadder still to bid a friend beloved farewell

Kurals.Translation: Can fire that burns by touch burn like Parting of the hearts love-sick?
Kurals.Explanation: Fire burns when touched; but, like the sickness of love, can it also burn when removed ?
Kurals.Couplet: Fire burns the hands that touch; but smart of loveWill burn in hearts that far away remove

Kurals.Translation: Many survive pangs of parting Not I this sore so distressing
Kurals.Explanation: As if there were many indeed that can consent to the impossible, kill their pain, endure separation and yet continue to live afterwards
Kurals.Couplet: Sorrow's sadness meek sustaining, Driving sore distress away,Separation uncomplaining Many bear the livelong day

Summary: Highlights fear of wrongdoing as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Sorrow, Longing, Fear Sadness.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: It swells out like baled out spring How to bear this pain so writhing?
Kurals.Explanation: I would hide this pain from others; but it (only) swells like a spring to those who drain it
Kurals.Couplet: I would my pain conceal, but see! it surging swells,As streams to those that draw from ever-springing wells

Kurals.Translation: I can't conceal this nor complain For shame to him who caused this pain
Kurals.Explanation: I cannot conceal this pain, nor can I relate it without shame to him who has caused it
Kurals.Couplet: I cannot hide this pain of mine, yet shame restrainsWhen I would tell it out to him who caused my pains

Kurals.Translation: In life -poles of this wearied frame Are poised the weights of lust and shame
Kurals.Explanation: (Both) lust and shame, with my soul for their shoulder pole balance themselves on a body that cannot bear them
Kurals.Couplet: My soul, like porter's pole, within my wearied frame,Sustains a two-fold burthen poised, of love and shame
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Lust,

Kurals.Translation: My lust is a sea; I do not see A raft to go across safely
Kurals.Explanation: There is indeed a flood of lust; but there is no raft of safety to cross it with
Kurals.Couplet: A sea of love, 'tis true, I see stretched out before,But not the trusty bark that wafts to yonder shore
Kurals.emotions: Longing / Desire, Helplessness / Despair, Anxiety / Fear

Kurals.Translation: What wilt they prove when they are foes Who in friendship bring me woes!
Kurals.Explanation: What will they prove when angry tempests lower?
Kurals.Couplet: Who work us woe in friendship's trustful hour,What will they prove when angry tempests lower

Kurals.Translation: The pleasure in love is oceanful But its pangs are more painful
Kurals.Explanation: The pleasure of lust is (as great as) the sea; but the pain of lust is far greater
Kurals.Couplet: A happy love 's sea of joy; but mightier sorrows rollFrom unpropitious love athwart the troubled soul
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Pining / Desire, associated with love/lust, Suffering / Distress

Kurals.Translation: Wild waves of love I swim shoreless Pining alone in midnight hush
Kurals.Explanation: I have swam across the terrible flood of lust, but have not seen its shore; even at midnight I am alone; still I live
Kurals.Couplet: I swim the cruel tide of love, and can no shore descry,In watches of the night, too, 'mid the waters, only I
Kurals.EmotionDetail: Pining, , Endurance despite difficulty

Kurals.Translation: Night's mercy lulls all souls to sleep Keeping but me for companionship
Kurals.Explanation: The night which graciously lulls to sleep all living creatures, has me alone for her companion
Kurals.Couplet: All living souls in slumber soft she steeps;But me alone kind night for her companing keeps

Kurals.Translation: Crueller than that cruel he Are midnight hours gliding slowly
Kurals.Explanation: The long nights of these days are far more cruel than the heartless one who is torturing me
Kurals.Couplet: More cruel than the cruelty of him, the cruel one,In these sad times are lengthening hours of night I watch alone
Kurals.EmotionDetail: pining, emotional pain, sorrow due to separation or unrequited love

Kurals.Translation: Like heart, if my sight reaches him It won't in floods of tears swim!
Kurals.Explanation: Could mine eyes travel like my thoughts to the abode (of my absent lord), they would not swim in this flood of tears
Kurals.Couplet: When eye of mine would as my soul go forth to him,It knows not how through floods of its own tears to swim

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Longing, Helplessness, Desire.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: The eye pointed him to me; why then They weep with malady and pine?
Kurals.Explanation: As this incurable malady has been caused by my eyes which showed (him) to me, why should they now weep for (him)
Kurals.Couplet: They showed me him, and then my endless painI saw: why then should weeping eyes complain
Kurals.emotions: **Regret** – The speaker laments the role their own eyes played in causing pain

Kurals.Translation: Why should these dyed eyes grieve now sans Regrets for their thoughtless glance?
Kurals.Explanation: The dyed eyes that (then) looked without foresight, why should they now endure sorrow, without feeling sharply (their own fault)
Kurals.Couplet: How glancing eyes, that rash unweeting looked that day,With sorrow measureless are wasting now away

Kurals.Translation: Eyes darted eager glance that day It's funny that they weep today
Kurals.Explanation: They themselves looked eagerly (on him) and now they weep. Is not this to be laughed at ?
Kurals.Couplet: The eyes that threw such eager glances round erewhileAre weeping now. Such folly surely claims a smile
Kurals.emotions: Regret – There is a sense of sorrow over past actions

Kurals.Translation: These eyes left me to endless grief Crying adry without relief
Kurals.Explanation: These painted eyes have caused me a lasting mortal disease; and now they can weep no more, the tears having dried up
Kurals.Couplet: Those eyes have wept till all the fount of tears is dry,That brought upon me pain that knows no remedy

Kurals.Translation: My eyes causing lust more than sea Suffer that torture sleeplessly
Kurals.Explanation: Mine eyes have caused me a lust that is greater than the sea and (they themselves) endure the torture of sleeplessness
Kurals.Couplet: The eye that wrought me more than sea could hold of woes,Is suffering pangs that banish all repose
Kurals.EmotionDetail: lust, , or sleeplessness/anxiety

Kurals.Translation: Lo! eyes that wrought this love-sickness Are victims of the same themselves
Kurals.Explanation: The eyes that have given me this disease have themselves been seized with this (suffering) Oh! I am much delighted
Kurals.Couplet: Oho! how sweet a thing to see! the eyeThat wrought this pain, in the same gulf doth lie

Kurals.Translation: Let tears dry up pining pining In eyes that eyed him longing longing
Kurals.Explanation: The eyes that became tender and gazed intently on him, may they suffer so much as to dry up the fountain of their tears
Kurals.Couplet: Aching, aching, let those exhaust their stream,That melting, melting, that day gazed on him

Kurals.Translation: Ther's he whose lips loved, not his heart Yet my eyes pine seeing him not
Kurals.Explanation: He is indeed here who loved me with his lips but not with his heart but mine eyes suffer from not seeing him
Kurals.Couplet: Who loved me once, onloving now doth here remain;Not seeing him, my eye no rest can gain

Kurals.Translation: He comes; no sleep; he goes; no sleep This is the fate of eyes that weep
Kurals.Explanation: When he is away they do not sleep; when he is present they do not sleep; in either case, mine eyes endure unbearable agony
Kurals.Couplet: When he comes not, all slumber flies; no sleep when he is there;Thus every way my eyes have troubles hard to bear

Kurals.Translation: Like drum beats eyes declare my heart; From people who could hide his secret?
Kurals.Explanation: It is not difficult for the people of this place to understand the secret of those whose eyes, like mine, are as it were beaten drums
Kurals.Couplet: It is not hard for all the town the knowledge to obtain,When eyes, as mine, like beaten tambours, make the mystery plain

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Longing, Sorrow, Despair.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: My lover's parting, I allowed Whom to complain my hue pallid?
Kurals.Explanation: I who (then) consented to the absence of my loving lord, to whom can I (now) relate the fact of my having turned sallow
Kurals.Couplet: I willed my lover absent should remain;Of pining's sickly hue to whom shall I complain

Kurals.Translation: Claiming it is begot through him Pallor creeps and rides over my frame
Kurals.Explanation: Sallowness, as if proud of having been caused by him, would now ride on my person
Kurals.Couplet: 'He gave': this sickly hue thus proudly speaks,Then climbs, and all my frame its chariot makes

Kurals.Translation: He seized my beauty and modesty Leaving pangs and Pallor to me
Kurals.Explanation: He has taken (away) my beauty and modesty, and given me instead disease and sallowness
Kurals.Couplet: Of comeliness and shame he me bereft,While pain and sickly hue, in recompense, he left
Kurals.emotions: Grief – The speaker mourns the loss of her beauty and modesty

Kurals.Translation: He is my thought, his praise my theme Yet this pallor steals over my frame
Kurals.Explanation: I think (of him); and what I speak about is but his excellence; still is there sallowness; and this is deceitful
Kurals.Couplet: I meditate his words, his worth is theme of all I say,This sickly hue is false that would my trust betray
Kurals.emotions: Longing — There is a deep sense of yearning and continual thinking about someone beloved., Melancholy — The mention of "pallor

Kurals.Translation: My lover departed me there And pallor usurped my body here
Kurals.Explanation: Just as my lover departed then, did not sallowness spread here on my person ?
Kurals.Couplet: My lover there went forth to roam;This pallor of my frame usurps his place at home

Kurals.Translation: Just as darkness waits for light-off Pallor looks for lover's arms-off
Kurals.Explanation: Just as darkness waits for the failing light; so does sallowness wait for the laxity of my husband's intercourse
Kurals.Couplet: As darkness waits till lamp expires, to fill the place,This pallor waits till I enjoy no more my lord's embrace
Kurals.emotions: **Longing** – The text conveys a deep yearning for the lover’s presence and intimacy., **Melancholy** – There’s a pervasive sense of sadness and languishing due

Kurals.Translation: From his embrace I turned a nonce This pallor swallowed me at once
Kurals.Explanation: I who was in close embrace just turned aside and the moment I did so, sallowness came on me like something to be seized on
Kurals.Couplet: I lay in his embrace, I turned unwittingly;Forthwith this hue, as you might grasp it, came on me
Kurals.emotions: Sadness – The descriptions of "sallowness" and "pallor" evoke a sense of melancholy and emotional low., Longing – The turning from an embrace and

Kurals.Translation: On my pallor they cast a slur But none says \"lo he parted her\"
Kurals.Explanation: Besides those who say "she has turned sallow" there are none who say "he has forsaken her"
Kurals.Couplet: On me, because I pine, they cast a slur;But no one says, 'He first deserted her.'
Kurals.emotions: Sadness – The mention of “pallor” and being “forsaken” suggests deep sorrow or melancholy., Loneliness – The speaker feels isolated

Kurals.Translation: Let all my body become pale If he who took my leave fares well
Kurals.Explanation: If he is clear of guilt who has conciliated me (to his departure) let my body suffer its due and turn sallow
Kurals.Couplet: Well let my frame, as now, be sicklied o'er with pain,If he who won my heart's consent, in good estate remain
Kurals.emotions: **Longing** – The speaker expresses a yearning for someone who has left., **Resignation** – There is an acceptance of suffering or pale appearance as a consequence of the separation

Kurals.Translation: Let people call me all pallid But my lover let them not deride
Kurals.Explanation: It would be good to be said of me that I have turned sallow, if friends do not reproach with unkindness him who pleased me (then)
Kurals.Couplet: 'Tis well, though men deride me for my sickly hue of pain;If they from calling him unkind, who won my love, refrain
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions in the text:, Vulnerability – The speaker exposes their fragile state

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Longing, Despair, Sadness.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: Stoneless fruit of love they have Who are beloved by those they love
Kurals.Explanation: The women who are beloved by those whom they love, have they have not got the stone-less fruit of sexual delight ?
Kurals.Couplet: The bliss to be beloved by those they love who gains,Of love the stoneless, luscious fruit obtains

Kurals.Translation: The lover-and-beloved's self-givings Are like rains to living beings
Kurals.Explanation: The bestowal of love by the beloved on those who love them is like the rain raining (at the proper season) on those who live by it
Kurals.Couplet: As heaven on living men showers blessings from above,Is tender grace by lovers shown to those they love

Kurals.Translation: The pride of living is for those Whose love is returned by love so close
Kurals.Explanation: The pride that says "we shall live" suits only those who are loved by their beloved (husbands)
Kurals.Couplet: Who love and are beloved to them aloneBelongs the boast, 'We've made life's very joys our own.'
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , , or yearning

Kurals.Translation: Whose love is void of love in turn Are luckless with all esteems they earn
Kurals.Explanation: Even those who are esteemed (by other women) are devoid of excellence, if they are not loved by their beloved
Kurals.Couplet: Those well-beloved will luckless prove,Unless beloved by those they love

Kurals.Translation: What can our lover do us now If he does not requite our love?
Kurals.Explanation: He who is beloved by me, what will he do to me, if I am not beloved by him ?
Kurals.Couplet: From him I love to me what gain can be,Unless, as I love him, he loveth me

Kurals.Translation: One sided pains; love in both souls Poises well like shoulder poles
Kurals.Explanation: Lust, like the weight of the KAVADI, pains if it lies in one end only but pleases if it is in both
Kurals.Couplet: Love on one side is bad; like balanced loadBy porter borne, love on both sides is good
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , due to unreciprocated feelings, when love is mutual

Kurals.Translation: This cupid aims at me alone; Knows he not my pallor and pain?
Kurals.Explanation: Would not cupid who abides and contends in one party (only) witness the pain and sorrow (in that party)?
Kurals.Couplet: While Kaman rushes straight at me alone,Is all my pain and wasting grief unknown

Kurals.Translation: None is so firm as she who loves Without kind words from whom she dotes
Kurals.Explanation: There is no one in the world so hard-hearted as those who can live without receiving (even) a kind word from their beloved
Kurals.Couplet: Who hear from lover's lips no pleasant word from day to day,Yet in the world live out their life,- no braver souls than they

Kurals.Translation: The lover accords not my desires And yet his words sweeten my ears
Kurals.Explanation: Though my beloved bestows no love on one, still are his words sweet to my ears
Kurals.Couplet: Though he my heart desires no grace accords to me,Yet every accent of his voice is melody

Kurals.Translation: You tell your grief to listless he Bless my heart! rather fill up sea!
Kurals.Explanation: Live, O my soul, would you who relate your great sorrow to strangers, try rather to fill up your own sea (of sorrow)
Kurals.Couplet: Tell him thy pain that loves not thee?Farewell, my soul, fill up the sea

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Longing, Desire, Sadness.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

chapters.Chapter: Lamenting the Absence of the Beloved

Kurals.Translation: Love is sweeter than wine; for vast Is its delight at very thought
Kurals.Explanation: Sexuality is sweeter than liquor, because when remembered, it creates a most rapturous delight
Kurals.Couplet: From thought of her unfailing gladness springs,Sweeter than palm-rice wine the joy love brings
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Rapture/Delight,

Kurals.Translation: Pains are off at the lover's thought In all aspects this love is sweet
Kurals.Explanation: Even to think of one's beloved gives one no pain Sexuality, in any degree, is always delightful
Kurals.Couplet: How great is love! Behold its sweetness past belief!Think on the lover, and the spirit knows no grief
Kurals.EmotionDetail: , Delight,

Kurals.Translation: To sneeze I tried hence but could not Me he tried to think but did not
Kurals.Explanation: I feel as if I am going to sneeze but do not, and (therefore) my beloved is about to think (of me) but does not
Kurals.Couplet: A fit of sneezing threatened, but it passed away;He seemed to think of me, but do his fancies stray
Kurals.emotions: Longing — There is a strong sense of yearning for the absent beloved., Frustration — The inability to sneeze or be thought of creates a feeling of irritation and unrest.

Kurals.Translation: Have I a place within his heart? Ah from mine he will never depart
Kurals.Explanation: He continues to abide in my soul, do I likewise abide in his ?
Kurals.Couplet: Have I a place within his heart!From mine, alas! he never doth depart

Kurals.Translation: Shame! My heart often he enters Banning me entry into his
Kurals.Explanation: He who has imprisoned me in his soul, is he ashamed to enter incessantly into mine
Kurals.Couplet: Me from his heart he jealously excludes:Hath he no shame who ceaseless on my heart intrudes
Kurals.emotions: Top 3 human emotions in the text:, Longing – There is a deep yearning for the presence of the beloved., Shame – The text references "shame" both directly and as a sense of regret or self-consciousness.

Kurals.Translation: Beyond the thought of life with him What else of life can I presume?
Kurals.Explanation: I live by remembering my (former) intercourse with him; if it were not so, how could I live ?
Kurals.Couplet: How live I yet? I live to ponder o'erThe days of bliss with him that are no more

Kurals.Translation: What will happen if I forget When his memory burns my heart?
Kurals.Explanation: I have never forgotten (the pleasure); even to think of it burns my soul; could I live, if I should ever forget it ?
Kurals.Couplet: If I remembered not what were I then? And yet,The fiery smart of what my spirit knows not to forget

Kurals.Translation: I bring him to ceaseless memory He chides not; and thus honours me
Kurals.Explanation: He will not be angry however much I may think of him; is it not so much the delight my beloved
Kurals.Couplet: My frequent thought no wrath excites It is not so?This honour doth my love on me bestow

Kurals.Translation: Dear life ebbs away by thought Of him who said we are one heart
Kurals.Explanation: My precious life is wasting away by thinking too much on the cruelty of him who said we were not different
Kurals.Couplet: Dear life departs, when his ungracious deeds I ponder o'er,Who said erewhile, 'We're one for evermore'
Kurals.emotions: **Sadness** – The text expresses sorrow over separation and the loss of a beloved., **Longing** – There is a deep yearning for the person who is absent

Kurals.Translation: Hail moon! Set not so that I find Him who left me but not my mind
Kurals.Explanation: May you live, O Moon! Do not set, that I mine see him who has departed without quitting my soul
Kurals.Couplet: Set not; so may'st thou prosper, moon! that eyes may seeMy love who went away, but ever bides with me

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Longing, Sadness, Love.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

Kurals.Translation: How shall I feast this dream-vision That brings the beloved's love-mission?
Kurals.Explanation: Where with shall I feast the dream which has brought me my dear one's messenger ?
Kurals.Couplet: It came and brought to me, that nightly vision rare,A message from my love,- what feast shall I prepare

Kurals.Translation: I beg these fish-like dark eyes sleep To tell my lover how life I keep
Kurals.Explanation: If my fish-like painted eyes should, at my begging, close in sleep, I could fully relate my sufferings to my lord
Kurals.Couplet: If my dark, carp-like eye will close in sleep, as I implore,The tale of my long-suffering life I'll tell my loved one o'er

Kurals.Translation: In wakeful hours who sees me not I meet in dreams and linger yet
Kurals.Explanation: My life lasts because in my dream I behold him who does not favour me in my waking hours
Kurals.Couplet: Him, who in waking hour no kindness shows,In dreams I see; and so my lifetime goes

Kurals.Translation: In dreams I enjoy his love-bliss Who in wakeful hours I miss
Kurals.Explanation: There is pleasure in my dream, because in it I seek and obtain him who does not visit me in my wakefulness
Kurals.Couplet: Some pleasure I enjoy when him who loves not meIn waking hours, the vision searches out and makes me see

Kurals.Translation: Dream-sight of him delights at once Awake-What of seeing him -hence
Kurals.Explanation: I saw him in my waking hours, and then it was pleasant; I see him just now in my dream, and it is (equally) pleasant
Kurals.Couplet: As what I then beheld in waking hour was sweet,So pleasant dreams in hour of sleep my spirit greet
Kurals.emotions: Delight – Experiencing pleasure both in dreams and in waking moments upon seeing the person., Love – The joy derived from seeing the loved one

Kurals.Translation: If wakeful hours come to nought My lov'r in dreams would nev'r depart
Kurals.Explanation: Were there no such thing as wakefulness, my beloved (who visited me) in my dream would not depart from me
Kurals.Couplet: And if there were no waking hour, my loveIn dreams would never from my side remove

Kurals.Translation: Awake he throws my overtures Adream, ah cruel! he tortures!
Kurals.Explanation: The cruel one who would not favour me in my wakefulness, what right has he to torture me in my dreams?
Kurals.Couplet: The cruel one, in waking hour, who all ungracious seems,Why should he thus torment my soul in nightly dreams

Kurals.Translation: Asleep he embraces me fast; Awake he enters quick my heart
Kurals.Explanation: When I am asleep he rests on my shoulders, (but) when I awake he hastens into my soul
Kurals.Couplet: And when I sleep he holds my form embraced;And when I wake to fill my heart makes haste

Kurals.Translation: In dreams who don't discern lovers Rue their missing in wakeful hours
Kurals.Explanation: They who have no dear ones to behold in their dreams blame him who visits me not in my waking hours
Kurals.Couplet: In dreams who ne'er their lover's form perceive,For those in waking hours who show no love will grieve
Kurals.emotions: Longing – The text expresses a deep yearning for the presence of a loved one

Kurals.Translation: The townsmen say he left me thus In dreams failing to see him close
Kurals.Explanation: The women of this place say he has forsaken me in my wakefulness I think they have not seen him visit me in my dreams
Kurals.Couplet: They say, that he in waking hours has left me lone;In dreams they surely see him not,- these people of the town;
Kurals.emotions: Longing – A deep desire to see or be with someone who is absent., Sadness – A sense of sorrow or loss due to separation., Hope – Retaining some

Summary: Highlights enduring moral values and higher truths as guiding virtues.
Encourages cultivating emotions such as Longing, Love, Hope.
Emphasizes moral and spiritual discipline through the ten couplets.
Inspires reflection on righteous living and the pursuit of higher values.

chapters.Chapter: Grieving at the Sight of the Beloved’s Haunts

Kurals.Translation: Bless you! you are not eventide But killing dart to wedded bride!
Kurals.Explanation: Live, O you evening are you (the former) evening? No, you are the season that slays (married) women
Kurals.Couplet: Thou art not evening, but a spear that doth devourThe souls of brides; farewell, thou evening hour
Kurals.emotions: **Grief** — The text speaks of grieving at places associated with the beloved

Kurals.Translation: Hail sad eventide dim and grim Has your mate like mine, cruel whim!
Kurals.Explanation: A long life to you, O dark evening! You are sightless Is your help-mate (also) as hard-hearted as mine
Kurals.Couplet: Thine eye is sad; Hail, doubtful hour of eventide!Of cruel eye, as is my spouse, is too thy bride

Kurals.Translation: Wet eve came pale and trembling then Now it makes bold with growing pain
Kurals.Explanation: The evening that (once) came in with trembling and dimness (now) brings me an aversion for life and increasing sorrow
